title: Unit 1 — Who We Are: Pronouns & People
desc: Free pronouns (singular, dual, plural) and all singular, dual & plural people nouns

lessons.title: Singular Pronouns
lessons.sub: The five singular positions
intro: Kanien’kéha has three sets of pronouns — singular (one person), dual (two people), and plural (three or more). English uses a single ‘you’ for all of these. Kanien’kéha does not.

This lesson covers the five singular pronouns and introduces one of the most important patterns in the language: Ra-/Ro- marks male; I-/Iak-/A- marks female. This prefix pattern unlocks vocabulary across every unit.

A note on dialect: pronouns here follow Kanehsatà:ke / Kahnawà:ke spelling. In Six Nations and Tyendinaga (Ontario), you will see y where this dialect uses i. In Akwesasne, r becomes l. All community forms are accepted as correct in this app.
lessons.ped: Why singular first? Following Freire's (1970) generative theme principle: we begin with who we are and who we speak to before we can speak about anything else. The pronoun system is a map of how Kanien'kéha encodes social position.

exercises.prompt: What does í:se' mean?
exercises.sub: Choose the correct answer
exercises.correct: You (singular)
options: You (singular)
I / Me
He
She (respectful)
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! í:se' — 'you' (one person). In Six Nations/Tyendinaga this appears as ý:se' — both are correct.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does í:'i mean?
exercises.correct: I (singular)
options: I (singular)
You (singular)
We two
She
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! í:'i — 'I'. The glottal stop (') between the vowels is a full consonant, not punctuation.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Which pronoun is used for a respected woman — a mother or elder?
exercises.sub: Think about the formal vs. informal distinction
exercises.correct: akaónha
options: aónha
akaónha
raónha
í:se'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! akaónha is the formal, respectful female pronoun. Kanien'kéha encodes respect directly into grammar.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: aónha has three uses. Which of these is NOT one of them?
exercises.correct: A respected elder woman
options: A female animal
A familiar female (sister/friend)
An inanimate object
A respected elder woman
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! akaónha is for respected women. aónha is for familiar females, animals, and objects.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match each pronoun to its meaning
exercises.sub: Tap a word on the left, then its match on the right
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Ra- marks male, A-/Iak- marks female — this pattern runs through the whole language.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the pronoun
exercises.correct: ra
exercises.hint: r
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! raónha — 'he'. The Ra- prefix marks male. In Akwesasne: laónha.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — the prefix starts with 'r' (or 'l' in Akwesasne).

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'You (singular)'?
exercises.sub: Dialect variants accepted
exercises.correct: í:se'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! í:se' — or ý:se' in Ontario dialects. Both accepted.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again. Try: í:se'

exercises.prompt: Say this pronoun aloud
exercises.sub: Record, listen back, and self-assess
exercises.hint: Two syllables: Í-se'. Stress on the first. The apostrophe at the end is a glottal stop — a brief closure of the throat.

exercises.prompt: Why does Kanien'kéha have separate pronouns for 'formal she' (akaónha) vs 'informal she' (aónha)?
exercises.sub: A deeper reflection on language and social structure
exercises.correct: Because respect and familiarity are grammatically encoded — the language requires you to decide your relationship before you speak
options: Because respect and familiarity are grammatically encoded — the language requires you to decide your relationship before you speak
Because the language has too many pronouns
To make sentences longer
For historical reasons only
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is García & Li Wei's (2014) point about translanguaging: each language carries a unique architecture of social meaning. English has lost this distinction; Kanien'kéha has kept it.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.sub: A moment of reflection
exercises.storyPrompt: This pronoun is reserved for women who command respect — mothers, elders, people of authority. In Kanien'kéha, respect isn't just a feeling; it's built into grammar. Who in your life would you address with this pronoun? Take a moment to bring them to mind.

lessons.title: Dual Pronouns
lessons.sub: Speaking about two
intro: Kanien’kéha keeps a three-way distinction — singular (one), dual (two), plural (three or more) — throughout the whole grammar. This lesson covers the four dual pronouns.
The dual is not a small detail. It reflects a world where ‘just us two’ and ‘a whole group’ are genuinely different things grammatically. English collapses them into the same ‘you’.
Notice the te- prefix in every dual pronoun. You will see te- again in verb forms that describe two people acting together. Learning it now means you will recognise it later.
lessons.ped: Why separate dual and plural? Following Nation's (2001) pattern-based learning principle: introducing the te- dual prefix now prepares learners to recognise it in dualic verbs (te-s-aráhtat, you run) later. Grammatical patterns, not just vocabulary lists.

exercises.prompt: What does teseniiáhshe mean?
exercises.correct: You two (dual)
options: You two (dual)
You (singular)
We all
Two females
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! teseniiáhshe — 'you two'. Note the te- prefix marking duality.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Which dual pronoun means 'you and I together'?
exercises.sub: First person dual inclusive
exercises.correct: teiakeniiáhshe
options: teseniiáhshe
teiakeniiáhshe
teteniiáhshe
tehniiáhshe
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! teiakeniiáhshe — you and I. The teia- prefix marks the inclusive 'we two'.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match each dual pronoun to its meaning
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! All dual pronouns carry the te- prefix. This marks duality throughout the grammar.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the dual pronoun for 'two males'
exercises.correct: nii
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! tehniiáhshe. The tehni- marks male dual — compare tekeniiáhshe (female dual).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — the missing part starts with 'n'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'you two'?
exercises.sub: Type the dual pronoun
exercises.correct: teseniiáhshe
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! teseniiáhshe. The te- prefix marks duality.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Starts with te-.

exercises.prompt: What distinguishes teteniiáhshe from teseniiáhshe?
exercises.sub: A structural question about the dual system
exercises.correct: teteniiáhshe is for two females; teseniiáhshe is 'you two' regardless of gender
options: teteniiáhshe is for two females; teseniiáhshe is 'you two' regardless of gender
They mean the same thing
teteniiáhshe is plural; teseniiáhshe is dual
teseniiáhshe is older
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The dual system encodes gender — the same precision we saw in the singular pronouns carries through.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this pronoun aloud
exercises.sub: Record yourself, listen back, and self-assess
exercises.hint: Four syllables: te-se-ni-ÁH-she. The te- is quick and unstressed; the stress falls on the áh syllable.

exercises.prompt: The te- prefix appears in all dual pronouns AND in dualic verbs (like te-s-aráhtat, 'you run'). What does this tell you about Kanien'kéha grammar?
exercises.sub: A language-structure reflection
exercises.correct: Grammatical meaning is carried by prefixes that recur consistently — learning one prefix unlocks many words
options: Grammatical meaning is carried by prefixes that recur consistently — learning one prefix unlocks many words
te- is just a coincidence
Verbs and pronouns are unrelated
Only pronouns use te-
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is the power of Kanien'kéha's prefix system. As Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) note, understanding the architecture of an Indigenous language — not just its vocabulary — is itself an act of reclamation.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: 'You and I' — teiakeniiáhshe — is a single word that holds a relationship. Think of someone with whom you would use this word. A partner, a sibling, a friend, a collaborator in this language work. Who comes to mind?

lessons.title: Plural Pronouns
lessons.sub: Speaking about groups
intro: The third set of pronouns covers groups of three or more. Even in plural forms, Kanien’kéha keeps the gender distinction — there are separate pronouns for all-female groups, all-male or mixed groups, and inclusive ‘we all’.
The -kwé:kon ending signals ‘all’ or ‘everyone’ and will reappear in other contexts across the language.
After this lesson you will have all fifteen pronouns: five singular, four dual, six plural.
lessons.ped: Completing the paradigm: Research in morphology acquisition (Mithun, 1989) shows that polysynthetic language learners benefit from seeing complete paradigms — all forms of a category — before moving to nouns and verbs. This allows pattern recognition rather than rote memorisation.

exercises.prompt: Which pronoun means 'you all' (plural)?
exercises.correct: sewakwé:kon
options: sewakwé:kon
iakwakwé:kon
kontikwé:kon
ratikwé:kon
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! sewakwé:kon — 'you all'. The sewa- prefix marks 'you' in plural.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the plural pronouns
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Notice that -kwé:kon marks plurality in all these forms — a consistent plural suffix.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the inclusive plural pronoun
exercises.correct: kwé:kon
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! iakwakwé:kon — 'we all'. The iakwa- marks inclusive we; -kwé:kon marks plurality.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — the suffix starts with 'k'.

exercises.prompt: kontikwé:kon refers to all females. What prefix family does konti- belong to?
exercises.sub: Connect to what you learned in Lessons 1 and 2
exercises.correct: The female plural prefix family (Konti-, Kon-, Teken- mark female groups)
options: The female plural prefix family (Konti-, Kon-, Teken- mark female groups)
The male prefix family
The dual prefix family
The negation family
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Konti- is the plural extension of the female prefix family. You saw Kon- in plural person nouns (Konón:kwe = women) and Teken- in dual (Tekenón:kwe = two women).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'all males / everyone' (plural pronoun)?
exercises.correct: ratikwé:kon
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ratikwé:kon. The rati- marks male/mixed plural — the same Ra- root you saw in singular raónha and in plural person nouns (Ronón:kwe).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Starts with rati-.

exercises.prompt: What is special about iakwakwé:kon compared to ratikwé:kon?
exercises.sub: A structural question
exercises.correct: iakwakwé:kon is inclusive — it includes the person being spoken to; ratikwé:kon excludes the speaker
options: iakwakwé:kon is inclusive — it includes the person being spoken to; ratikwé:kon excludes the speaker
They are identical in meaning
iakwakwé:kon is only for women
ratikwé:kon is dual not plural
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The inclusive/exclusive distinction is another example of Kanien'kéha encoding relational meaning — who is in the group — directly into the pronoun.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this pronoun aloud
exercises.sub: Record, listen, and self-assess
exercises.hint: Four syllables: ra-ti-KWÉ-kon. The stress falls on kwé. The long vowel (:) is held slightly longer.

exercises.prompt: You now know all 15 Kanien'kéha pronouns. How does this compare to English?
exercises.sub: A comparative reflection
exercises.correct: English has about 7 distinct pronouns; Kanien'kéha has 15, encoding gender, number (singular/dual/plural), and formality
options: English has about 7 distinct pronouns; Kanien'kéha has 15, encoding gender, number (singular/dual/plural), and formality
Kanien'kéha and English have the same number of pronouns
Kanien'kéha has fewer pronouns because it is simpler
Kanien'kéha pronouns are identical to French
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kanien'kéha's 15-pronoun system encodes social information English has lost. This is not complexity — it is precision. As Flores & Rosa (2015) argue, calling a language 'complex' often reflects unfamiliarity, not objective difficulty.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: kontikwé:kon — all the women. Think of the women in your life as a group, not as individuals. The women who shaped you, who surround you, who came before you. Kanien'kehá:ka society organises much of its life around female collective — the clan mothers, the longhouse. What does holding all of them in a single word feel like?

lessons.title: Singular People Nouns
lessons.sub: Naming the people in our world
intro: Kanien’kéha has eight core singular people nouns: four female, four male. Each covers a life stage — child, teenager, adult, elder.
The I-/Iak- prefix marks female; R- marks male. In Akwesasne, R- becomes L-. Both are correct. Once you recognise this pattern, it opens up dozens of words across the language.

Notice that this lesson includes words for ‘elder woman’ and ‘elder man’ — not simply ‘old person’. That difference matters. Elders in Kanien’kehá:ka culture are knowledge keepers and leaders, and the language recognises that.
lessons.ped: Semantic set learning: Presenting vocabulary in age-graded pairs (child/teen/adult/elder × female/male) follows Nation's (2001) principle of meaningful grouping. The R/I prefix rule acts as a generative pattern learners can extend to new words they encounter.

exercises.prompt: What does Ieksá:'a mean?
exercises.correct: Girl
options: Girl
Woman
Teenage girl
Elder woman
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ieksá:'a — girl. The I- prefix marks female. The glottal stop (') before the final 'a is part of the word.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Rón:kwe mean?
exercises.correct: Man
options: Boy
Teenage boy
Man
Elder man
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Rón:kwe — man. R- marks male. In Akwesasne: Lón:kwe. Same -kwe root as Iakón:kwe (woman).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the female person nouns
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Four female life stages — a complete picture of the community across generations.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the male person nouns
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Compare the male and female sets — same age stages, different gender prefixes.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Woman'?
exercises.sub: Dialect variants accepted
exercises.correct: Iakón:kwe
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iakón:kwe. The Iak- prefix marks adult female. The long vowel (:) matters.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Iakón:kwe

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Man'? (Akwesasne form also accepted)
exercises.correct: Rón:kwe
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Rón:kwe (Kanehsatà:ke) — or Lón:kwe in Akwesasne. Both correct.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Rón:kwe

exercises.prompt: Complete the word
exercises.correct: ha
exercises.hint: h
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Akokstén:ha. The -stén:ha suffix marks eldership. Same root in Rokstén:ha (elder man).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — the suffix starts with 'h'.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Two syllables: RÓN-kwe. The colon (:) means the n-vowel is held longer. The kwe rhymes with 'way'.

exercises.prompt: Akokstén:ha means 'elder woman'. In Kanien'kehá:ka society, what role do elder women hold?
exercises.correct: Central authority — clan mothers select and depose chiefs; elder women are ceremony keepers
options: Central authority — clan mothers select and depose chiefs; elder women are ceremony keepers
Primarily domestic roles only
The same roles as elder men, separately
No formal role
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! In Haudenosaunee political structure, clan mothers hold enormous authority. The word Akokstén:ha is not merely descriptive — it names a position of power.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: In Kanien'kéha, the word for elder woman carries authority, not just age. Think of an elder woman in your life, your family, or your community. What did she carry? What has she passed forward?

lessons.title: Dual & Plural People Nouns
lessons.sub: Groups of two and groups of many
intro: Just as pronouns come in singular, dual, and plural, so do people nouns. The te-/tekeni- prefix marks two people; the konti-/rati- prefix marks three or more.

The singular noun roots stay the same across all three forms — what changes is the prefix at the front. This is the prefix system doing what it always does: adding meaning without changing the core word.
The pattern here is the same one you learned with pronouns. Once you see it, you can start predicting how any people noun will look in its dual and plural forms.
lessons.ped: Pattern completion: By this point learners have seen the singular/dual/plural distinction in pronouns. Applying it to person nouns is spaced repetition of the grammatical pattern (not just the vocabulary), which research consistently shows improves retention (Nation, 2001; Webb, 2020).

exercises.prompt: What does Tekenón:kwe mean?
exercises.correct: Two women
options: Two women
Women (plural)
Three women
One woman
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tekenón:kwe — two women. Teken- marks female dual. Compare singular Iakón:kwe and plural Konón:kwe.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Konón:kwe mean?
exercises.correct: Women (three or more)
options: Two women
Women (three or more)
One woman
Elder women
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Konón:kwe — women (plural, 3+). Kon- marks female plural. The -kwe root is consistent across all three: Iakón:kwe / Tekenón:kwe / Konón:kwe.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the number forms of 'man/men'
exercises.sub: Singular, dual, and plural
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Same -kwe root throughout: Rón:kwe → tehnón:kwe → Ronón:kwe. The prefix carries the number.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: tekeniksá:'a means 'two girls'. What prefix marks female dual in this word?
exercises.correct: tekeni-
options: tekeni-
konti-
rati-
te-
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! tekeni- is the female dual prefix. Compare tehni- for male dual (tehniksá:'a = two boys).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the plural form for boys
exercises.correct: okón:'a
exercises.hint: o
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ratiksa'okón:'a — boys (plural). Rati- marks male plural. In Akwesasne: Latiksa'okón:'a.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — the suffix starts with 'o'.

exercises.prompt: You know Rón:kwe (one man) and tehnón:kwe (two men). What prefix marks male dual?
exercises.correct: teh- / tehni-
options: teh- / tehni-
ron-
rati-
roti-
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The male dual prefix is tehni- (tehniksá:'a = two boys, tehninekénhteron = two teenage boys, tehnón:kwe = two men). This is the male counterpart to female tekeni-.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Women' (plural, 3+)?
exercises.correct: Konón:kwe
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Konón:kwe. Kon- marks female plural.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Konón:kwe

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: ro-NÓN-kwe. Stress on the second syllable. The colon (:) marks a slightly longer vowel.

exercises.prompt: The prefix system for person nouns follows a consistent three-number pattern. Fill in the gap: I-/Iak- (singular female) → Teken- (dual female) → ___- (plural female)
exercises.correct: Kon-
options: Kon-
Rati-
Ron-
Te-
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kon- is the female plural prefix. The full female paradigm: I-/Iak- (one) → Teken- (two) → Kon- (many). The male paradigm: R- (one) → Tehni- (two) → Rati-/Ron- (many).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Konón:kwe — women, the group. Kanien'kehá:ka society organises around female collective: the longhouse, the clan, the decision-making circle. What does it mean to you to have a single word for all the women, as a group? Who does that word hold?

lessons.title: Pronouns in Action: Putting Them to Work
lessons.sub: Using what you know in real sentences
intro: You now know all fifteen pronouns. This lesson moves from recognising them to using them in short sentences.

A pronoun in Kanien’kéha does not sit beside a verb the way ‘I’ sits beside ‘sit’ in English. Instead, the pronoun’s meaning is built into the verb through prefixes. But in everyday speech, the free pronoun is often said alongside the verb for emphasis or clarity.
This lesson practises the most common combinations: í:’i (I) + an action, í:se’ (you) + an action, and third-person pronouns with actions you have already learned.
lessons.ped: Pronoun-verb collocation: Nation (2001) identifies collocational knowledge as essential for productive vocabulary use. This lesson moves learners from knowing isolated pronouns to pairing them with known verbs. LaFever Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: In Kanien’kéha you can say í:’i ká:tiens — I am sitting. What is í:’i doing in this sentence?
exercises.correct: Adding emphasis or clarity — the verb already carries ‘I’ in its prefix, but the pronoun makes it explicit
options: Adding emphasis or clarity — the verb already carries ‘I’ in its prefix, but the pronoun makes it explicit
Replacing the verb prefix
Making the sentence a question
Marking the past tense
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The verb ká:tiens already contains ‘I’ in its k- prefix. Adding í:’i emphasises the subject — it is I who am sitting, not someone else.

exercises.prompt: Match the pronoun + verb pair to its meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Pronoun + verb. The same sitting verb, four different people.

exercises.prompt: í:se’ sá:tien means ‘you sit’ as a command. What prefix in the verb marks ‘you’?
exercises.correct: s- — the s- prefix marks second person (you) on A-stem verbs
options: s- — the s- prefix marks second person (you) on A-stem verbs
k- — that marks I
ra- — that marks he
te- — that marks two people
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! s- on an A-stem verb marks ‘you’. This is why the command ‘sit’ is sá:tien — s- + á:tien. The pronoun í:se’ can be added for emphasis.

exercises.prompt: Complete: ‘He is running’ — raónha ___árhtat
exercises.correct: r
exercises.hint: r
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! raónha rárhtat — he is running. The r- prefix marks male third person on this verb. raónha and r-árhtat share the same Ra- origin.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which prefix marks male third person on verbs?

exercises.prompt: How do you say ‘I am listening’ (I + the listening verb)?
exercises.correct: í:’i katkáhthons
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! í:’i katkáhthons — I am listening. The k- in katkáhthons already marks ‘I’; the pronoun í:’i adds emphasis.
exercises.wm: Please try again — I is í:’i, and the listening verb starts with katk-.

exercises.prompt: Say this sentence aloud
exercises.hint: Í:’i (2 syllables) — ká:-tiens (2 syllables). Say it clearly and mean it — you are sitting somewhere right now.

exercises.prompt: I
exercises.storyPrompt: í:’i — I. A two-syllable word with a glottal stop in its heart. Think about what it means to say ‘I’ in a language that was taken from families. To say í:’i in Kanien’kéha is to locate yourself in this language. Not as a guest, not as a student only — as someone who exists in this language. Who are you, in this language, right now?

lessons.title: Inclusive and Exclusive We
lessons.sub: Who is inside the ‘we’ — and who isn’t
intro: English has one word for ‘we’. Kanien’kéha has several, and they make an important distinction: inclusive we (you and I, we all including you) and exclusive we (we, but not you).

You already know this distinction from the pronouns you learned. teiakeniiáhshe — you and I (inclusive dual). iakwakwé:kon — we all including you. These pronouns say explicitly whether the person being spoken to is inside the group or outside it.
This lesson deepens your understanding of this distinction and practises it in the contexts where it matters most: community, family, and ceremony.
lessons.ped: Inclusive/exclusive distinction as relational grammar: The inclusive/exclusive distinction in Kanien’kéha encodes social information English cannot. This lesson names the distinction plainly and explores its social and cultural significance. LaFever Emotional domain: being inside or outside the ‘we’ is a matter of belonging.

exercises.prompt: teiakeniiáhshe means ‘you and I’. What makes this an inclusive pronoun?
exercises.correct: It includes the person you are speaking to — they are inside the ‘we’
options: It includes the person you are speaking to — they are inside the ‘we’
It excludes the speaker
It refers to a group of three
It is only used formally
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Inclusive means the listener is part of the group. teiakeniiáhshe — you and I — always includes both speaker and listener.

exercises.prompt: Match the pronoun to whether it includes or excludes the listener

pairs.english: Inclusive plural — we all, including you
Exclusive plural — all males, not including the listener necessarily

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The inclusive/exclusive distinction means the language requires the speaker to decide, before speaking, whether the listener is inside or outside the group being talked about.

exercises.prompt: Why might it matter grammatically whether your listener is inside or outside the ‘we’?
exercises.correct: Because it tells the listener whether they are being included in what you are saying — they know immediately if you mean them
options: Because it tells the listener whether they are being included — they know immediately if you mean them
It is a historical accident
The distinction is never important in practice
It only matters in ceremonies
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! When someone says iakwakwé:kon they are telling you: you are in this group. When they say ratikwé:kon they may be talking about others. The language builds that relational clarity into every plural.

exercises.prompt: Say these two contrasting pronouns aloud
exercises.meaning: You and I — All males / everyone (exclusive sense)
exercises.hint: te-ia-ke-nii-ÁH-she (6 syllables). ra-ti-KWÉ-kon (4 syllables). Feel the difference between ‘us together’ and ‘them as a group’.

exercises.prompt: You and I
exercises.storyPrompt: teiakeniiáhshe — you and I. A single word that holds a relationship between two specific people. Think of the person you are learning this language alongside, or the person you most want to speak it with. teiakeniiáhshe is the pronoun for that relationship — not ‘we’ in some vague sense, but the two of you specifically, together. Who is in your teiakeniiáhshe?

lessons.title: Formal Pronouns and Addressing Elders
lessons.sub: How respect is built into the pronouns themselves
intro: You learned that Kanien’kéha has two pronouns for ‘she’: akaónha (respectful, for mothers and elders) and aónha (familiar, for peers and animals). This distinction is not just politeness — it is grammatically required.

Choosing the wrong pronoun for a respected person is not just impolite. It is grammatically incorrect in Kanien’kéha. The language requires you to decide your relationship to someone before you can refer to them.
This lesson extends that understanding and practises using the respectful forms in the contexts where they appear most often: talking about elder women, clan mothers, and respected community figures.
lessons.ped: Respect encoded in grammar: The akaónha/aónha distinction is one of the clearest examples of social structure encoded in Kanien’kéha grammar. This lesson names that clearly and practises it in socially appropriate contexts. LaFever Emotional and Spiritual domains: respect for elders is a core cultural value.

exercises.prompt: akaónha is the respectful pronoun for ‘she’. When is it required?
exercises.correct: When referring to an elder woman, a clan mother, or any woman who commands respect
options: When referring to an elder woman, a clan mother, or any woman who commands respect
Only in ceremonies
Whenever talking about any woman
Only in formal writing
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! akaónha — the respectful she. Used for Akokstén:ha (elder woman), for Iakoiánehr (clan mother), for any woman whose role or age commands recognition. The choice of pronoun is an act of acknowledgement.

exercises.prompt: aónha is used for familiar females, female animals, and objects. What would it mean to use aónha when talking about a clan mother?
exercises.correct: It would be grammatically incorrect and disrespectful — the wrong pronoun signals the wrong relationship
options: It would be grammatically incorrect and disrespectful — the wrong pronoun signals the wrong relationship
It is a casual alternative
It is acceptable in informal speech
The distinction does not matter in practice
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Using aónha for a clan mother is not just impolite — it is wrong in the grammatical sense. The language requires you to mark the relationship correctly. This is how Kanien’kéha encodes social structure in every sentence.

exercises.prompt: Match the person to the correct pronoun
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The pronoun selection maps social relationship. Elder women and clan mothers — akaónha. Familiar peers — aónha. Elder men — raónha.

exercises.prompt: Complete the respectful pronoun for ‘she’: ___ónha
exercises.correct: aka
exercises.hint: a
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! akaónha — the aka- prefix marks the respectful female form. You first learned this in Lesson 1 of this unit.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which prefix marks the respectful she?

exercises.prompt: Say both female pronouns and feel the difference
exercises.hint: a-ÓN-ha (3 syllables) — a-ka-ÓN-ha (4 syllables). The aka- prefix adds a syllable and a level of respect. Say them side by side and notice what changes.

exercises.prompt: Respect in grammar
exercises.storyPrompt: akaónha — she, with respect. In Kanien’kéha, you cannot talk about an elder woman without the grammar itself acknowledging who she is. The language builds the relationship into the sentence. English has only one ‘she’ for everyone. Kanien’kéha has one for an elder and one for a peer, and choosing between them is not optional. Who in your life would you address with akaónha?

exercises.prompt: A community gathering
exercises.sub: Use what you know
exercises.scene: You are at a community gathering with your cousin. An elder man — Rokstén:ha — approaches and greets you both. He introduces the woman standing beside him, then turns and asks you a question.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Rokstén:ha
turns.gloss: Greetings.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Greet him back.
options: Shé:kon.
Niawenhkó:wa.
Tho niioht.
Onen'.
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon is the standard greeting — you return it in kind.
turns.wm: When someone greets you with Shé:kon, you return the same greeting.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Rokstén:ha
turns.gloss: Greetings. She is known to us — she is an elder woman.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He is speaking about the elder woman beside him. How do you refer to her to your cousin?
options: Akaónha kanónhsa tsi ionkwarihó:ten.
Aónha kanónhsa tsi ionkwarihó:ten.
Raónha kanónhsa tsi ionkwarihó:ten.
Í:se' kanónhsa tsi ionkwarihó:ten.
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! akaónha — the respectful she. An Akokstén:ha requires akaónha. aónha would be grammatically wrong here.
turns.wm: She is an elder woman — which pronoun encodes respect for a woman of her standing?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Rokstén:ha
turns.gloss: Where are you going?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Tell him where you are going — to your grandmother's house.
options: Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke.
Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' non nontà:ke.
Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' wà:ke.
Ka' nontáhse'?
turns.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke — I am going to my grandmother's house. wà:ke marks going; nontà:ke marks coming from.
turns.wm: You are going — which verb is for going, not coming from? And whose house are you going to?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Rokstén:ha
turns.line: Tho niioht. Aksótha — akaónha ká:tiens kanónhsa.
turns.gloss: I see. Your grandmother — she is sitting at home.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He has told you your grandmother is home. Take your leave.
options: Niawenhkó:wa. Onen'.
Shé:kon. Onen'.
Tho niioht. Ka' wáhse'?
Én. í:'i.
turns.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Thank you — and then Onen', meaning 'now' or 'that's it for now.' A natural way to close a conversation.
turns.wm: You want to thank him and say goodbye. Niawenhkó:wa is thank you; Onen' closes the exchange.

exercises.closing: You moved through a real community exchange — greeting, recognising an elder, and saying where you are going. The pronoun akaónha is not optional here. The language requires you to see who she is before you can speak about her.
exercises.dialect_note: In Akwesasne, raónha becomes laónha and Rokstén:ha becomes Lokstén:ha. All community forms are accepted.

lessons.title: Pronoun Review: All Fifteen Together
lessons.sub: Seeing the full system at once
intro: This lesson brings together all fifteen pronouns you have learned across this unit. Seeing them together shows the full shape of the system — how singular, dual, and plural build on the same prefixes, how the gender distinction runs through every level.

This is not just review. When you can hold the full fifteen-pronoun system in your head as a shape rather than a list, you start to read new words you have never seen before. The pattern does that work for you.
Use this lesson to consolidate. Return to it whenever you feel uncertain about a pronoun.
lessons.ped: Paradigm completion as structural knowledge: Mithun (1989) and Nation (2001) both identify complete paradigm knowledge as the foundation for productive morphological control in polysynthetic languages. This consolidation lesson provides the full paradigm view before learners move to kinship and verbs.

exercises.prompt: Singular pronouns — all five
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four of the five singular pronouns. The fifth — aónha — covers familiar she, female animals, and objects.

exercises.prompt: Dual pronouns — all four
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! All four dual pronouns. Every one carries the te- prefix marking duality.

exercises.prompt: Plural pronouns — four of six
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Four of the six plural pronouns. All carry -kwé:kon, the plural marker. You have now seen all fifteen.

exercises.prompt: The inclusive/exclusive distinction only exists in the plural and dual. English has no equivalent. What does this mean for someone translating between Kanien’kéha and English?
exercises.correct: Some meaning is always lost in translation — the English ‘we’ cannot carry the inclusive/exclusive information that Kanien’kéha builds in
options: Some meaning is always lost in translation — the English ‘we’ cannot carry the inclusive/exclusive information
Translation is perfect between the two languages
English has an equivalent in ‘us’
The distinction does not matter outside the classroom
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! This is one of the clearest examples of what García & Li Wei (2014) mean by translanguaging — each language carries architecture that cannot be fully reproduced in another. Kanien’kéha’s ‘we’ knows things English’s ‘we’ does not.

exercises.prompt: Fifteen pronouns
exercises.storyPrompt: ratikwé:kon — everyone. You now know fifteen pronouns: five singular, four dual, six plural. Each one encodes a specific social relationship — who you are, who you are speaking to, who you are speaking about, whether they are male or female, respected or familiar, two or many, inside or outside the group. English uses maybe seven pronouns to do all of this. Kanien’kéha uses fifteen. That is not complexity. That is precision. The language sees relationships carefully.

exercises.prompt: At your grandmother's door
exercises.sub: Three conversations at once
exercises.scene: You arrive at your grandmother's house. Two women are sitting on the step. Your grandmother calls from inside. Three people want to speak with you.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Aksótha (from inside)
turns.gloss: Greetings! Is that you?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Confirm it's you — just you, alone.
options: Ó:nen, í:'i.
Ó:nen, teiakeniiáhshe.
Ó:nen, iakwakwé:kon.
Ó:nen, raónha.
turns.cm: Tho niioht! í:'i — I, me. You are alone at the door. In Ontario dialects this appears as ý:'i — both accepted.
turns.wm: You are alone. Which pronoun means 'I' — just you, singular?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Younger woman on the step
turns.line: Shé:kon. Teiakeniiáhshe wà:ke — í:se' wáhse' nahe'?
turns.gloss: Greetings. You and I are going — are you coming along?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: She wants you both to go somewhere together. Agree — say 'you and I, yes.'
options: Én, teiakeniiáhshe.
Én, iakwakwé:kon.
Én, í:'i.
Én, raónha.
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! teiakeniiáhshe — you and I, the inclusive dual. She invited you in, and you accepted with the same pronoun.
turns.wm: She used teiakeniiáhshe — you and I, two people together. Which pronoun matches that?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Elder woman on the step
turns.gloss: Where are you going?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Tell her where you are going.
turns.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! wà:ke — I am going. Short, complete, correct.
turns.wm: The verb 'I am going' — it starts with w. Try: wà:ke

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Elder woman on the step
turns.gloss: I see. Thank you — take care.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Thank her in return.
options: Niawenhkó:wa, akaónha.
Niawenhkó:wa, aónha.
Ka' wáhse', akaónha?
Shé:kon, akaónha.
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Niawenhkó:wa — thank you. And akaónha, the respectful address, because she is an elder woman. The grammar of respect holds even in a single closing line.
turns.wm: Thank her — and remember which pronoun an elder woman requires.

exercises.closing: Singular, dual, and plural all appeared in one scene. The pronoun system is not a list to memorise — it is a map of who is here, and who belongs to whom.
exercises.dialect_note: In Akwesasne: í:'i stays the same. raónha becomes laónha. All are accepted.

title: Unit 2 — Family, Kinship & Movement
desc: All 22 kinship nouns with possessive forms, plus direction verbs for everyday navigation

lessons.title: Female Kinship Nouns
lessons.sub: The women in our relational world
intro: In Kanien’kéha, kinship words change their form depending on who is speaking. My mother, your mother, her mother, his mother are all different words. The relationship is built directly into the grammar.

This lesson covers the female kinship nouns: mother, older sister, grandmother, younger sister, daughter, step-daughter, granddaughter, sister-in-law, mother-in-law, female friend, and girlfriend — each with the forms for ‘my’ and ‘your’.

Kanien’kehá:ka society is matrilineal — clan membership, land rights, and social identity pass through the mother’s line. This vocabulary is not just grammar. It is a map of how the community sustains itself.
lessons.ped: Kinship as cultural grammar: McCarty & Lee (2014) argue that culturally sustaining/revitalizing pedagogy must treat language as carrying a worldview, not just vocabulary. Presenting kinship terms within their matrilineal context honours this principle.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'My mother'?
exercises.sub: Remember: the possessive prefix changes the base word
exercises.correct: Ake'nisténha
options: Sa'nisténha
Ake'nisténha
Ro'nisténha
ista
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ake'nisténha — my mother. The ake- prefix marks 'my' for this female kinship term.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Which word means 'My grandmother'?
exercises.correct: Aksótha
options: Aksótha
Sasótha
tota
Rosótha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Aksótha — my grandmother. In a sentence: 'I am going to my grandmother's house' = Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke'.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the possessive forms of 'Mother'
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four different words for one kinship relationship — the possessor is built into the word itself.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the form 'My older sister'
exercises.correct: tsí:'a
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Akhtsí:'a — my older sister. The akh- marks 'my' in this female sibling term. Compare Sahtsí:'a (your older sister).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — the suffix starts with 'ts'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'My daughter'?
exercises.correct: aksen'á:a
options: aksen'á:a
saksén'a
akonsen'á:a
rosen'á:a
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! aksen'á:a — my daughter. The ak- marks 'my/first person' in this kinship term.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'My grandmother'?
exercises.correct: Aksótha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Aksótha. The full base form is tota; with 'my' it becomes Aksótha.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Aksótha

exercises.prompt: Why do the forms Sahtsí:'a (your older sister) and Akhtsí:'a (my older sister) share the -htsí:'a root?
exercises.sub: A structural question
exercises.correct: The root -htsí:'a means 'older sister'; the prefix (sa-/akh-) changes who is speaking
options: The root -htsí:'a means 'older sister'; the prefix (sa-/akh-) changes who is speaking
They are different words with the same sound by coincidence
The prefix changes the meaning of 'older'
The -htsí:'a is a suffix, not a root
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is the core of Kanien'kéha kinship grammar — the root carries the relationship category, the prefix carries the possessor. Learning the root unlocks all four possessive forms.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this phrase aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: ak-SÓ-tha. Stress on the second. A warm, close word.

exercises.prompt: Kanien'kehá:ka society is matrilineal. What does this mean for how 'mother' functions in the kinship system?
exercises.correct: Clan membership, land rights, and identity pass through the mother's line — the word 'mother' is structurally central to social organisation
options: Clan membership, land rights, and identity pass through the mother's line — the word 'mother' is structurally central to social organisation
Mothers are important but have no formal role
It means only women can speak Kanien'kéha
Matrilineal means fathers are not acknowledged
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Tuck & Yang (2012) remind us that decolonization restores what colonization disrupted. Learning these kinship terms — in their matrilineal context — is itself a small act of that restoration.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Aksótha — my grandmother. A word you say in a sentence when you're going to her house, when you're asking about her, when you're thinking of her. What memory or person does this word call up for you? Take a moment with it.

lessons.title: Male Kinship Nouns
lessons.sub: The men in our relational world
intro: This lesson covers the male kinship nouns: father, older brother, grandfather, younger brother, son, step-son, grandson, brother-in-law, father-in-law, male friend, and boyfriend — each with their possessive forms.
Notice how the female and male kinship nouns are parallel in structure. The same prefix families work across both sets: sa- = your, ake-/rake- = my.
Compare: Sahtsí:’a (your older sister) ↔ Iahtsí:’a (your older brother). Same -htsí:’a root, different prefix. The parallelism is consistent and learnable.
lessons.ped: Contrastive learning: Presenting male kinship immediately after female kinship exploits the parallel structure for contrastive learning (Tinkham, 1997) — learners can map the prefix differences rather than memorising each form independently.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'My father'?
exercises.correct: Rake'níha
options: Ia'níha
Rake'níha
Ronwa'níha
Ro'níha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Rake'níha — my father. The rake- prefix marks 'my' for paternal kin.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'My grandfather'?
exercises.correct: Rake'sótha
options: Rake'sótha
Sa'sótha
Ro'sótha
Ako'sótha
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Rake'sótha — my grandfather. In a sentence: 'I am going to my grandfather's house' = Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' wà:ke'.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the possessive forms of 'Father'
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four possessive forms of one kinship relationship — the possessor is built in.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Your older brother'?
exercises.correct: Iahtsí:'a
options: Iahtsí:'a
Rakhtsí:'a
Ronwahtsí:'a
Rohtsí:'a
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iahtsí:'a — your older brother. Compare Sahtsí:'a (your older sister) — same -htsí:'a root, different possessor prefix.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete 'His son'
exercises.correct: a
exercises.hint: a
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! ro'sén'a — his son. The ro- prefix marks 'his' in this kinship term.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — the final syllable is 'a'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'My grandfather' (the possessed form used in sentences)?
exercises.correct: raksótha
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! raksótha. Compare aksótha (my grandmother). The ra- marks male possession in elder kin terms.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: raksótha

exercises.prompt: Compare Sahtsí:'a (your older sister) and Iahtsí:'a (your older brother). What does this parallel structure tell you?
exercises.correct: The -htsí:'a root means 'older sibling'; sa- marks female-possessed 'your', ia- marks male-possessed 'your'
options: The -htsí:'a root means 'older sibling'; sa- marks female-possessed 'your', ia- marks male-possessed 'your'
They are completely different words
Only the ending changes
The words are from different dialects
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! This is noun-root consistency — the same root -htsí:'a carries 'older sibling' in both terms. The prefix changes to encode both the gender of the sibling and the gender of the possessor.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this phrase aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: rak-SÓ-tha. Stress on the second. A word of respect.

exercises.prompt: The Kontinónhstats curriculum (Mary McDonald) teaches 22 kinship nouns each with 4 possessive forms. How many total forms does that represent?
exercises.correct: 88 unique kinship word forms
options: 88 unique kinship word forms
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! 22 × 4 = 88. This is why vocabulary lists alone are not enough — learning the prefix system means you can generate all 88 forms from a much smaller set of roots and prefixes.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Rake'sótha — my grandfather. Think of a grandfather figure in your life: a grandfather, a great-uncle, an elder man who played that role. What knowledge did he carry? What do you carry from him?

lessons.title: Direction Verbs & Going Places
lessons.sub: Where are you going? Where did you come from?
intro: Kanien’kéha has a rich system for expressing direction and movement. This lesson introduces three essential direction question-and-answer pairs that connect kinship vocabulary to real daily life.
Ka’ wáhse’? Where are you going? → wà:ke I am going (there).
Ka’ nontáhse’? Where did you come from? → non nontà:ke I came from (there).
Ka’ iéhse’skwe’? Where did you go? → iè:ke’skwe’ I went (there).
These three frames also give you your first real sentences combining kinship nouns with verbs.
lessons.ped: Functional language learning: Freire (1970) argued that generative vocabulary should be rooted in the learner's world. Direction verbs connected to family homes and community places make language immediately usable in daily life.

exercises.prompt: What does Ka' wáhse' mean?
exercises.correct: Where are you going?
options: Where are you going?
Where did you come from?
Where did you go?
Who is going?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ka' wáhse' — where are you going? Ka' = where; wáhse' = you go.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am going (somewhere)'?
exercises.correct: wà:ke
options: wà:ke
nontà:ke
iè:ke'skwe'
Ka' wáhse'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! wà:ke — I am going. Used with a destination: 'Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke'' = I am going to my grandmother's house.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the direction question to its meaning
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Three distinct verb forms: going (present), coming from (origin), went (past). The tense is built into the verb, not expressed with separate words like 'did' in English.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the direction question
exercises.correct: wá:
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ka' wáhse'. The stress on wá: is important.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — starts with 'w'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am going'?
exercises.correct: wà:ke
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! wà:ke — I am going. Use it with a destination.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: wà:ke

exercises.prompt: Ka' iéhse'skwe' uses the suffix -skwe'. What does this suffix signal?
exercises.correct: Past tense — the action already happened
options: Past tense — the action already happened
Future tense
Habitual action
Plural subject
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The -skwe' suffix marks past tense in this direction verb. This is your first encounter with tense marking in Kanien'kéha — tense is encoded as a suffix on the verb itself.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: In the sentence 'Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke'', what does Aksótha mean?
exercises.sub: You learned this in Lesson 6
exercises.correct: My grandmother
options: My grandmother
My grandfather
My mother
My home
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Aksótha = my grandmother. This is spaced repetition in action — kinship vocabulary from Lesson 6 reappearing in a direction verb sentence.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — think back to the female kinship nouns.

exercises.prompt: Say this phrase aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: ka'-WÁH-se'. The stress is on the second syllable. The ' after Ka' is a glottal stop.

exercises.prompt: Ka' nontáhse' (where did you come from) and Ka' iéhse'skwe' (where did you go) both ask about the past, but are they asking the same thing?
exercises.correct: No — Ka' nontáhse' asks about origin/source; Ka' iéhse'skwe' asks about destination in the past
options: No — Ka' nontáhse' asks about origin/source; Ka' iéhse'skwe' asks about destination in the past
Yes, they mean the same thing
Ka' nontáhse' is more formal
Ka' iéhse'skwe' is only used for long trips
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! This distinction — where you came FROM vs where you WENT — is encoded as separate verb forms in Kanien'kéha. English uses the same 'where did you...' frame for both; Kanien'kéha does not.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: 'I am going' — wà:ke — is one of the first complete verbs you have learned. Think of a place in your life you regularly go to: a family member's home, a community space, somewhere that matters. Try saying it in your mind: '[Name of that place] wà:ke' — I am going to [that place]. What does it feel like to hold that destination in this language?

exercises.prompt: Walking through the community
exercises.sub: A morning exchange
exercises.scene: You are walking through the community on a quiet morning. A neighbour stops to talk. The conversation moves through where you are going, where they came from, and where someone else went yesterday.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Neighbour
turns.gloss: Greetings. Where are you going?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: You are going to your grandmother's house. Tell them.
options: Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke.
Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' wà:ke.
Ake'nisténha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke.
Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' non nontà:ke.
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Aksótha = my grandmother; wà:ke = I am going. nontà:ke would mean coming from, not going to.
turns.wm: You are going — not coming from. And it is your grandmother's house, not your grandfather's or mother's.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Neighbour
turns.line: Tho niioht. Í:'i, Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' non nontà:ke.
turns.gloss: I see. I — I came from my grandfather's house.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: They came from their grandfather's house. Say the Kanien'kéha word for 'my grandfather.'
options: Raksótha
Aksótha
Rake'sótha
Rake'níha
turns.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Raksótha — my grandfather, used in a sentence. Compare Aksótha (my grandmother) — same -sótha root, ra- marks male.
turns.wm: The ra- prefix marks male elder kin. Aksótha is grandmother. Which form has ra-?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Neighbour
turns.gloss: Where did your father go?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Type the question they just asked: 'Where did (someone) go?'
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Ka' iéhse'skwe' — where did (someone) go? The -skwe' suffix marks past tense.
turns.wm: The past-tense direction question — starts with Ka' and ends with -skwe'. Try: Ka' iéhse'skwe'

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Your father went to your grandfather's house. Type: 'I went (there).'
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! iè:ke'skwe' — I went. Same -skwe' suffix as the question. All three direction pairs complete: wà:ke / nontà:ke / iè:ke'skwe'.
turns.wm: The past-tense answer — shares the -skwe' suffix with the question. Try: iè:ke'skwe'

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Neighbour
turns.line: Tho niioht. Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' wà:ke ó:nen.
turns.gloss: I see. I'm going to my grandfather's house now.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He is leaving. Say goodbye — type the Kanien'kéha word for 'now / that's all.'
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Onen' — now, that's all, goodbye for now. A simple and complete closing.
turns.wm: The word that closes a Kanien'kéha conversation — short, two syllables. Try: Onen'

exercises.closing: Ka' wáhse' — Ka' nontáhse' — Ka' iéhse'skwe'. Three questions, three tenses, built into the verb itself. Where you go and who you go to are not separate in Kanien'kéha. The language holds them together.
exercises.dialect_note: wà:ke, nontà:ke, and iè:ke'skwe' are stable across community dialects — no r→l alternation in these forms.

lessons.title: Extended Family and Social Kin
lessons.sub: Cousins, friends, and the people who are family without sharing blood
intro: In Kanien’kehá:ka society, family extends far beyond the nuclear household. Your mother’s sisters are also your mothers. Your clan members are your siblings. Friends held close over a lifetime may be family in every practical sense.
This lesson introduces extended kinship vocabulary: cousins, friends, and some of the kinship terms that came into use through social relationship rather than blood.
The glossary has these forms in the possessed form — onkyara’sé:’a (my cousin), onkyatén:ron (my male friend), rakenohá:’a (my uncle). They are ready to use in sentences.
lessons.ped: Extended kinship as cultural knowledge: The Iroquois kinship system — in which parallel cousins are called by the same terms as siblings — is linguistically and culturally significant. This lesson introduces it plainly without anthropological framing. Nation (2001): kinship vocabulary in semantic sets. LaFever Emotional domain: family as identity.

exercises.prompt: Match the extended kin term to its meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Extended kin and close friends. In Kanien’kehá:ka life, the line between family and close friend is often fluid — the language reflects that.

exercises.prompt: Kahwatsíre’ means ‘family / extended family’. In Kanien’kehá:ka society, the extended family often includes clan members you have never met. What does this tell you?
exercises.correct: Clan membership creates family relationships with people across the community and beyond — you have siblings everywhere your clan exists
options: Clan membership creates family relationships with people across the community and beyond
Family only means immediate blood relatives
The extended family is a modern concept
Clan members are not considered family
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Kahwatsíre’ — your family includes your clan family. When you meet a Bear Clan person from a different community, they are your family in the deepest sense. The language encodes this with the same word.

exercises.prompt: In Kanien’kehá:ka kinship, your mother’s sister’s children are your siblings, not your cousins. Your father’s brother’s children are also your siblings. Who then is a cousin?
exercises.correct: Your father’s sister’s children and your mother’s brother’s children — those who cross to the other clan line
options: Your father’s sister’s children and your mother’s brother’s children — those who cross to the other clan line
All children of your aunts and uncles equally
Only children of your mother’s sisters
Cousins do not exist in the system
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! This is the Iroquois kinship system: parallel cousins (same-sex sibling’s children) are siblings. Cross cousins (opposite-sex sibling’s children) are cousins — potential marriage partners in traditional practice. The kinship system and the clan exogamy rule fit together precisely.

exercises.prompt: Complete: my cousin — onkyara’sé:___
exercises.correct: ’a
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! onkyara’sé:’a — my cousin. The glottal stop before the final ‘a is part of the word.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends with a glottal stop and ‘a.

exercises.prompt: Say the family word aloud
exercises.hint: Ka-hwat-SÍ-re’ — four syllables. The stress falls on SÍ. Glottal stop at the end.

exercises.prompt: Family
exercises.storyPrompt: Kahwatsíre’ — family. In residential schools, children were separated from their families and forbidden from speaking to siblings who were placed in different dormitories. The destruction of family connection was deliberate policy. Kahwatsíre’ names what was targeted. It also names what survived — the extended family networks that kept knowledge, language, and connection alive even under that pressure. Who is your Kahwatsíre’?

lessons.title: Kinship in Sentences
lessons.sub: Using kinship terms in real speech
intro: Knowing kinship words is one thing. Using them in sentences is another. This lesson puts the kinship vocabulary from Lessons 6, 7, and 9 into sentences you would actually say.
The most common kinship sentences are: going to someone’s house, talking about what someone is doing, and making introductions. All of these combine kinship nouns with vocabulary you already have.
You already know the direction sentence frames from Lesson 8. This lesson adds more family members to those frames.
lessons.ped: Kinship vocabulary in sentence frames: Nation (2001): known vocabulary (kinship terms) placed in new sentence structures (direction, activity) produces efficient consolidation. Krashen (+1): new structure (kinship + verb) built on known vocabulary. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Match the direction sentence to its meaning
exercises.sub: These are full sentences you can use

pairs.english: I am going to my mother’s house
I am going to my father’s house
I am going to my grandmother’s house
I am going to my grandfather’s house

exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Four complete sentences combining kinship and direction. The pattern: kinship term + tsi + [their house form] + wà:ke’.

exercises.prompt: In Ake’nistén:ha tsi tiakonónhsote’ wà:ke’, what does tsi do?
exercises.correct: It connects the destination (her house) to the direction verb — ‘where her house is’
options: It connects the destination to the direction verb — ‘where her house is’
It marks the past tense
It is the word for ‘my’
It negates the sentence
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsi connects a destination to the action of going. You first saw tsi as a locative particle in Unit 3. Here it says ‘to the place where her house is’.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does tsi do?

exercises.prompt: Complete: I am going to my grandmother’s house — Aksóhtha tsi tiakonónhsote’ ___
exercises.correct: wà:ke’
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! wà:ke’ — I am going. The direction verb that ends every ‘I am going to...’ sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which direction verb means I am going?

exercises.prompt: Write the sentence: I am going to my father’s house
exercises.correct: Rake’ní:ha tsi thonónhsote’ wà:ke’
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Rake’ní:ha tsi thonónhsote’ wà:ke’ — I am going to my father’s house. A complete Kanien’kéha sentence you can use.
exercises.wm: Please try again — my father + tsi + his house + I am going.

exercises.prompt: Say this sentence aloud
exercises.meaning: I am going to my mother’s house
exercises.hint: Ake’-nis-TÉN:-ha (5 syllables) — tsi (1) — tia-ko-NÓN-hso-te’ (5) — wà:-ke’ (2). Thirteen syllables in one complete sentence. Say it like you are actually heading out the door.

exercises.prompt: Going home
exercises.storyPrompt: Ake’nistén:ha tsi tiakonónhsote’ wà:ke’ — I am going to my mother’s house. Such a simple sentence. Such a complete world. Think about the last time you said that in any language. Where was her house? What would you find there? For the children who were taken to residential schools, this sentence was the one they could not say for years. Some never went back. Saying it in Kanien’kéha is saying the thing that was taken.

lessons.title: The Matrilineal System
lessons.sub: How identity and family pass through the mother’s line
intro: Kanien’kehá:ka society is matrilineal — clan membership, longhouse identity, and social belonging all pass through the mother. You belong to your mother’s clan, not your father’s.

This is not just cultural background. It shapes the kinship vocabulary you have been learning: why there are separate terms for mother’s side and father’s side, why the clan mother holds political authority, why the longhouse is organised the way it is.
This lesson explores that connection between the kinship vocabulary and the social system it names.
lessons.ped: Matrilineality as linguistic and social context: McCarty & Lee (2014): culturally sustaining pedagogy treats language as carrying a worldview. The matrilineal system is not background to the kinship vocabulary — it is what the vocabulary is built to express. LaFever Spiritual and Emotional domains.

exercises.prompt: In the matrilineal system, a child belongs to their mother’s clan. What does this mean for the word Ake’nistén:ha — my mother?
exercises.correct: Your mother is the source of your clan identity — knowing who your mother is tells you who you are in the community
options: Your mother is the source of your clan identity
The mother’s role is primarily domestic
The father’s clan matters more
Clan identity is chosen by the child
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! In Kanien’kehá:ka society, your mother’s line is your identity line. Her clan is your clan. Her longhouse is your longhouse. Ake’nistén:ha is not just a word for mother — it names the person who gave you your place in the world.

exercises.prompt: Iakoiánéhr — clan mother. She is a woman who has earned the recognition of her clan family. What makes her authority legitimate?
exercises.correct: Her authority comes from her clan family’s recognition — she is nominated and acknowledged by the women of her clan, not appointed by outside authority
options: Her authority comes from clan family recognition — not outside appointment
She is elected by the whole community
Her authority comes from the Canadian government
She inherits the role from her mother automatically
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! The clan mother’s authority is internal to the clan family — it is recognised by the women around her. This is a form of governance that predates and is structurally different from anything the Indian Act tried to impose.

exercises.prompt: Your mother is Bear Clan. Your father is Turtle Clan. What clan are you?
exercises.correct: Bear Clan — you take your mother’s clan
options: Bear Clan — you take your mother’s clan
Turtle Clan — you take your father’s clan
Both clans
You choose your own clan
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! You are Bear Clan because your mother is Bear Clan. The clan line passes through her. This is what matrilineal means in practice: the mother’s identity determines the child’s social position.
exercises.wm: Please try again — in a matrilineal system, which parent’s clan do you take?

exercises.prompt: Match the kinship term to its role in the matrilineal system

pairs.english: The keeper of clan memory and knowledge
The political authority of the clan family
The extended clan family that surrounds you

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four roles in the matrilineal system, four kinship words. They work together to hold the clan family in place.

exercises.prompt: Grandmother
exercises.storyPrompt: Aksótha — my grandmother. In the matrilineal system, the grandmother is the keeper of the family’s clan knowledge, oral history, and language. Residential schools targeted this deliberately — by removing children, they broke the chain between grandmother and grandchild, disrupting the most direct transmission line of culture, language, and identity. Many Kanien’kehá:ka grandmothers today are working to restore what was taken. Some are learning the language alongside their grandchildren. Aksótha.

lessons.title: Asking About Family
lessons.sub: Questions you can ask and answer about people’s families
intro: Being able to ask about someone’s family is a fundamental social skill in Kanien’kehá:ka community life. When you meet someone, you often talk about family — who your mother is, where your grandmother is from, how many siblings you have.
This lesson combines the kinship vocabulary with question words and answer frames. You already know most of the pieces: Ónhka (who), Nahò:ten (what), Ka’ (where), and the kinship terms themselves.
The goal is to build enough to participate in a real family conversation, even briefly.
lessons.ped: Question-answer pairs as conversational entry: Nation (2001): question-answer pairs around high-salience topics (family) provide immediate communicative function. Physical domain: producing real conversational turns. Emotional domain: family questions are personally significant.

exercises.prompt: Ónhka ne raónha? means ‘Who is he?’ You learned this in Unit 12. How would you ask ‘Who is she?’ using the respectful pronoun?
exercises.correct: Ónhka ne akaónha?
options: Ónhka ne akaónha?
Ónhka ne aónha?
Ónhka ne raónha?
Ónhka iken’?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ónhka ne akaónha? — Who is she (respectful)? The question word Ónhka + ne + the correct pronoun for the person being asked about.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which pronoun is respectful for ‘she’?

exercises.prompt: Ka’ (where) is the question word for location. How would you ask ‘Where is my grandmother?’
exercises.correct: Ka’ aksótha? — where is my grandmother?
options: Ka’ aksótha? — where is my grandmother?
Ónhka aksótha? — who is my grandmother?
Nahò:ten aksótha? — what is my grandmother?
Ka’ Ónhka aksótha?
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ka’ aksótha? — where is my grandmother? Ka’ (where) + the kinship term. Short, complete, usable.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which question word asks ‘where’?

exercises.prompt: Match the family question to its answer

pairs.english: Iakoiánéhr — she is the clan mother

exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Four family question-and-answer pairs. These are the exchanges you would have meeting someone in the community.

exercises.prompt: Complete the question: Who is he? — ___ ne raónha?
exercises.correct: Ónhka
exercises.hint: Ó
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ónhka ne raónha? — Who is he? The question word Ónhka starts every ‘who’ question.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which question word means ‘who’?

exercises.prompt: Ask and answer about your mother
exercises.hint: Ask the question with genuine curiosity. Answer firmly. These two short sentences carry a conversation.

exercises.prompt: Talking about family
exercises.storyPrompt: Ake’nistén:ha — my mother. In Kanien’kehá:ka communities, introducing yourself often means naming your mother, your grandmother, your clan. ‘Who is your mother?’ is not just a family question — it is a social location question. It places you in the web of community relationships. When you can answer it in Kanien’kéha, you are placing yourself in that web in the language it belongs to.

lessons.title: Direction Vocabulary Extended
lessons.sub: More ways to say where you are going and coming from
intro: Lesson 8 introduced the three core direction question-answer pairs. This lesson adds the full set of direction sentences from the glossary, covering going to each family member’s home and returning.

Direction verbs in Kanien’kéha are among the most frequently used verbs in daily conversation. Being able to say where you are going, where you came from, and where someone else went covers a huge proportion of everyday exchanges.
This lesson consolidates all the direction vocabulary so you have full fluency with these essential frames.
lessons.ped: Direction vocabulary as daily-life core: Direction sentences are among the highest-frequency sentence types in any language. Nation (2001): high-frequency functional vocabulary should be practised to automaticity. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Match the direction question to its answer
exercises.sub: The full question-answer pairs

pairs.target: Ka’ wáhse’? / Where are you going?
Ka’ nontáhse’? / Where did you come from?
Ka’ iéhse’skwe’? / Where did you go?

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Three question-answer pairs that cover going, coming, and where you went. These three frames carry most daily direction conversation.

exercises.prompt: When you say ‘I am going to the longhouse’, you use the article thi with the destination. Why thi and not ne?
exercises.correct: Thi marks something as a destination in motion — you are going toward it; ne marks something stationary
options: Thi marks something as a destination in motion — you are going toward it; ne marks a stationary reference
They are interchangeable
Ne is used with all places
Thi is only for the longhouse
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Thi is the article of direction and motion. When you are heading toward something, it gets thi. When you are pointing at something that is just there, it gets ne. You have been seeing this since Unit 3.

exercises.prompt: How do you say ‘I am going (somewhere)’?
exercises.correct: wà:ke
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! wà:ke — I am going. The most basic direction verb. Combine it with a destination to say where.
exercises.wm: Please try again — a short two-syllable verb.

exercises.prompt: Ka’ iéhse’skwe’? asks where you went — it uses a past-completed form. How do you know it refers to the past?
exercises.correct: The -skwe’ ending marks a completed past event — something that happened and is finished
options: The -skwe’ ending marks a completed past event
The word order marks the past
There is no past marker — context tells you
The iéhse root is past tense
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! -skwe’ is the completed past marker in Kanien’kéha. Ka’ iéhse’skwe’? — where did you go (and it is done)? You will study this in depth in Unit 19.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does the -skwe’ ending signal?

exercises.prompt: Complete the question: Where did you come from? — Ka’ non___?
exercises.correct: táhse’
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ka’ nontáhse’? — Where did you come from? The -táhse’ root is the coming-from direction root.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what root follows non in this coming-from question?

exercises.prompt: Say the going question and answer aloud
exercises.meaning: Where are you going? — I am going (there).
exercises.hint: Ka’ WÁH-se’? (3 syllables, rising). WÀ:-ke’ (2 syllables, settled). Ask with genuine curiosity. Answer with direction.

exercises.prompt: Going
exercises.storyPrompt: Wà:ke’ — I am going. Such a small verb. It names one of the most constant acts of a life — moving toward something. For Kanien’kehá:ka people who cross the Canada-US border at Akwesasne daily, who travel between Kanehsatà:ke and Kahnawà:ke and Six Nations, who go to ceremonies and councils and workplaces and family homes — wà:ke’ is a verb they say constantly. Where are you going, in this language, from here?

lessons.title: Unit 2 Review: Family and Movement
lessons.sub: Bringing it all together
intro: This lesson reviews the key vocabulary and sentence patterns from Unit 2: kinship terms, possessive forms, direction verbs, and the sentences that combine them.

Review is not just repetition. Each time you see a word in a new context, a new combination, or alongside a new question, it goes a little deeper. This lesson creates new combinations from words you already know.
By the end you should be able to say where you are going, name your family members, and ask basic questions about someone else’s family.
lessons.ped: Spaced retrieval review: Nation (2001) identifies spaced retrieval — encountering known vocabulary in new contexts after an interval — as the most effective vocabulary consolidation strategy. This review lesson creates new pairings of kinship and direction vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Match the kinship term to its meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four core kinship terms. The Ake-/Rake- prefix pair marks my for female/male kinship respectively.

exercises.prompt: Ka’ wáhse’? You are about to visit your grandmother. What is the most natural answer?
exercises.correct: Aksóhtha tsi tiakonónhsote’ wà:ke’ — I am going to my grandmother’s house
options: Aksóhtha tsi tiakonónhsote’ wà:ke’ — I am going to my grandmother’s house
Ka’ nontáhse’? — that is a different question
Wà:ke’ only — no destination needed
Ónhka aksótha?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Aksóhtha tsi tiakonónhsote’ wà:ke’ — the full destination sentence. Question + complete answer is a full conversational exchange.

exercises.prompt: Someone introduces themselves and you want to ask about their family. Which question asks ‘Who is your grandmother?’
exercises.correct: Ónhka ne sahsó:tha? — Who is your grandmother?
options: Ónhka ne sahsó:tha? — Who is your grandmother?
Ka’ sahsó:tha? — Where is your grandmother?
Nahò:ten sahsó:tha? — What is your grandmother?
To nihá:ti sahsó:tha?
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Ónhka ne sahsó:tha? — Ónhka (who) + ne + sahsó:tha (your grandmother — sa- prefix for ‘your’ on the grandmother root).
exercises.wm: Please try again — which question word asks about a person?

exercises.prompt: Complete: my mother — Ake’nistén:___
exercises.correct: ha
exercises.hint: h
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ake’nistén:ha — my mother. The -ha ending closes the word.

exercises.prompt: Say this full sentence aloud
exercises.meaning: I am going to my mother’s house
exercises.hint: Say it like you are heading out the door right now. This is a sentence you can use today.

exercises.prompt: Onen’ ki’ wáhi — Unit 2
exercises.storyPrompt: Kahwatsíre’ — family. You have now learned how to name your family in Kanien’kéha, how to say you are going to their homes, how to ask who someone is and where their people are from. These are some of the first things any speaker of any language learns. You are doing that learning now, in this language. The vocabulary of family is the vocabulary of who you are. Onen’ ki’ wáhi.

title: Unit 3 — Objects, Verbs & Everyday Language
desc: A-class nouns, A-stem verbs, articles, greetings, and negation

lessons.title: A-Class Nouns & Noun Incorporation
lessons.sub: Naming the objects in our world
intro: Kanien’kéha groups nouns by their first sound. A-class nouns begin with the vowel ‘a’. This lesson introduces eight common A-class nouns from everyday life.

Some A-class nouns have been part of the language so long that their original meaning is no longer obvious — like à:there’ (basket). Others still show their roots clearly — like atià:tawi (shirt), which comes from a root meaning ‘body cover’. Both are simply A-class nouns.

The most important thing to notice here is noun incorporation — in Kanien’kéha, a noun can be folded directly into a verb to make one word. áhta’ (shoe) + big → watahkowá:nen (a big shoe). One word does what English takes three words to say.
lessons.ped: Noun incorporation awareness: Mithun (1984) identifies noun incorporation as a core feature of polysynthetic languages. Introducing it early — even before learners can use it — creates conceptual readiness (Krashen's comprehensible input +1) for later grammar lessons.

exercises.prompt: What does áhta' mean?
exercises.correct: Shoe / Shoes
options: Shoe / Shoes
Pants
Chair
Ball
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! áhta' — shoe. This word was used in the noun incorporation example: watahkowá:nen = a big shoe.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the A-class nouns to their meanings
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! All these words begin with 'a' — the defining feature of A-class nouns.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: atià:tawi (shirt/dress/coat) is a 'fake' A-class noun. What does 'fake' mean in this context?
exercises.correct: The root meaning (body cover) is still identifiable — it derives from a verb root
