options: The root meaning (body cover) is still identifiable — it derives from a verb root
The word is borrowed from another language
It is only used in dialects
It is not a real Kanien'kéha word
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! A 'fake' noun's root is still recognisably verbal — atià:tawi = body cover (verb root). A 'true' noun like à:there' (basket) has a root whose original meaning has been lost over time.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Shoe'?
exercises.correct: áhta'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! áhta'. The apostrophe at the end is a glottal stop — part of the word.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: áhta'

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'chair'
exercises.correct: ra
exercises.hint: r
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! ani'tskwá:ra. The long vowel (:) before the final syllable is important.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — ends in 'ra'.

exercises.prompt: à:there' (basket) is a 'true' A-class noun. What does 'true' mean here?
exercises.correct: The original root meaning has been lost — the word survives but its verb origin is no longer identifiable
options: The original root meaning has been lost — the word survives but its verb origin is no longer identifiable
It is more grammatically correct than 'fake' nouns
It is the only correct form
It comes from a different language
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! In Kanien'kéha, 'true' A-class nouns are ancient words whose verbal origins have been lost to time. This is how languages evolve — words outlive their roots.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: The example 'áhta' + -owá:nen → watahkowá:nen (a big shoe)' demonstrates what grammatical process?
exercises.correct: Noun incorporation — a noun is merged with a verb suffix to form a single complex word
options: Noun incorporation — a noun is merged with a verb suffix to form a single complex word
Simple adjective agreement
A borrowing from English
Verb conjugation only
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Noun incorporation is one of Kanien'kéha's most distinctive features. What English says in 2–3 words, Kanien'kéha can express in one. This is part of what Mary McDonald calls building a 'base vocabulary' — knowing nouns prepares you to incorporate them.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Two syllables: ÁH-ta'. Stress on the first syllable. The ' at the end is a glottal stop — hold it briefly.

exercises.prompt: Match these A-class nouns
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! A-class nouns cover body coverings, tools, and traditional items — the vocabulary of daily life.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: à:there' — basket. This word is a 'true' noun: ancient enough that its original verb root has been forgotten. The word exists, carries meaning, but its history is partially obscured. What in your own life feels like that — present and meaningful, but with roots that are partially hidden or lost? How does reclaiming a language connect to recovering those roots?

lessons.title: Possessed Nouns: My and Your
lessons.sub: Adding 'my' and 'your' to the nouns you know
intro: You have been using possessed nouns since Unit 2 — Ake'nistén:ha (my mother), Ak-áhta' (my shoe). This lesson makes the possessive system explicit for the A-class, O-class, and Ka-class nouns from Unit 3.
The prefix that marks 'my' changes slightly depending on the noun class. Ak- works for many nouns. 'Your' is usually sa-.
You do not need to memorise every form now. You need to recognise the pattern: a short prefix before the noun root signals possession.
lessons.ped: Possessive prefixes in noun classes: Nation (2001): possession is among the highest-frequency grammatical functions. Krashen (+1): known nouns in a new structural frame (possessive).

exercises.prompt: Possessed forms — My (Ak-)
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ak- marks 'my'. Sa- marks 'your'. The prefix attaches to the front of the noun root.

exercises.prompt: Sa-kahiatónhsera means 'your book'. What prefix marks 'your'?
exercises.correct: Sa- — the your prefix
options: Sa-
Ak-
Ro-
Ko-
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Sa- marks 'your'. The same sa- you saw in the kinship terms.

exercises.prompt: Complete: my water — ak-Ónhk___
exercises.correct: a
exercises.hint: a
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ak-Ónhka — my water. The ak- prefix attaches to the water noun.

exercises.prompt: My shoe
exercises.storyPrompt: Ak-áhta' — my shoe. The prefix that marks 'my shoe' is the same family that marks 'my mother' and 'my grandmother'. The shoe and the mother are held the same way in the grammar. Both are mine. Both are kin to the prefix Ak-.

lessons.title: A-Stem Verbs: Commands & First Person
lessons.sub: Actions — sitting, running, listening
intro: Kanien’kéha verbs are organised into six groups based on the first sound of the verb root. The first group is A-stem verbs — verbs whose root begins with ‘a’.

Some A-stem verbs describe a simple action — like sitting. Others describe a two-phase action — like running, where you go from still to moving. The two-phase ones take a te- prefix — the same te- you already know from dual pronouns.

This lesson teaches you how to give a command (telling someone to do something) and how to say ‘I do something regularly’. Commands have no extra ending. Regular or repeated actions take the -s ending.
lessons.ped: Verb-stem classes as generative grammar: Mary McDonald's curriculum explicitly teaches verb stems as classes because knowing the stem class lets learners predict how pronouns attach. This is deductive grammar instruction within a communicative framework.

exercises.prompt: What does sá:tien mean?
exercises.correct: You sit (command)
options: You sit (command)
I am sitting
We two sit
Sitting
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! sá:tien — you sit (command). Structure: s- (you) + á:tien (A-stem root). No suffix — it's a command.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does ká:tiens mean?
exercises.correct: I am sitting (habitual)
options: You sit
I am sitting (habitual)
She sits
Two people sit
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! ká:tiens — I am sitting. Structure: k- (I) + á:tien (root) + -s (habitual suffix).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the A-stem verb forms
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Notice: s- = you, k- = I; no suffix = command, -s = habitual.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: tesaráhtat (you run) has the prefix te-. Why?
exercises.sub: Think back to what te- means
exercises.correct: Running is a dualic verb — it requires two phases (standing still → moving), so te- marks the dual action
options: Running is a dualic verb — it requires two phases (standing still → moving), so te- marks the dual action
te- marks plural subjects
te- is required before all verbs
te- marks a different dialect
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Dualic verbs require two phases of action. Mary McDonald's explanation: to run, you are first standing still, then you move forward. Two actions → dualic prefix te-. You already know te- from dual pronouns — the same morpheme carries 'two-ness' in both contexts.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the habitual suffix
exercises.correct: s
exercises.hint: s
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ká:tiens. The -s suffix marks habitual/repetitive action. It appears when the action is done regularly.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — the habitual suffix is a single letter.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'You sit' (command)?
exercises.correct: sá:tien
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! sá:tien. s- (you) + á:tien (root). No suffix — commands are bare.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: sá:tien

exercises.prompt: satkáhthons means 'you listen' (habitual). What is the pronoun prefix for 'you' in this form?
exercises.correct: s-
options: s-
k-
r-
te-
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! s- is the 'you' subjective pronoun prefix for A-stem verbs. You see it in sá:tien (you sit), tesaráhtat (you run), satkáhthons (you listen). The same s- across all three.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: katkáhthons means 'I listen'. Which part is the verb root?
exercises.correct: -káhthon-
options: -káhthon-
kat-
-s
k-
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! -káhthon- is the verb root meaning 'listen'. k- is the pronoun (I), at- is the middle-voice infix (body-related), and -s is the habitual suffix.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this verb aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: te-sa-RÁH-tat. Stress on the third syllable. The te- prefix is quick and unstressed.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: sá:tien — sit. ká:tiens — I am sitting. These are your first Kanien'kéha verbs. They are humble, everyday actions. In Freire's (1970) pedagogy, literacy begins with the words of daily life — not abstractions. What everyday actions in your life would you most want to be able to name in Kanien'kéha? Think of three and write them here.

lessons.title: Articles, Greetings & Negation
lessons.sub: The glue of everyday conversation
intro: This lesson covers three things you need to hold any real conversation: articles (the, a), common greetings and expressions, and negation (how to say ‘no’ or ‘not’).

The articles ne and thi are not quite like English ‘the’. Ne points to a specific thing. Thi points to something in motion or being moved toward: Wá:s skó:ha thi áhta’ — go get the shoe — uses thi because the shoe is being fetched.
Negation in Kanien’kéha uses the word Iáh before the verb: Iáh + ká:tiens = I am not sitting. It is a separate word, not attached to the verb.
lessons.ped: Communicative chunks: García & Li Wei (2014) emphasise that translanguaging learners benefit from acquiring functional phrases as wholes before analysing them. Shé:kon, Niawenhkó:wa, Tho niioht, and Tó:ka' are essential communicative chunks that appear throughout this course.

exercises.prompt: What does Shé:kon mean?
exercises.correct: Hello / Greetings
options: Hello / Greetings
Thank you
Goodbye
Well done
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon — hello. More literally 'still / yet' — an expression of ongoing life and presence.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Niawenhkó:wa mean?
exercises.correct: Thank you very much
options: Thank you very much
Hello
Well done
Wonderful
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! (And that's the word itself!) It carries more weight than a simple thank-you — it connects to the spirit of the Thanksgiving Address.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the expression to its meaning
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! These four expressions have appeared throughout this course — you've already been learning them in context.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you form negation in Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: Place the particle Iáh before the verb: Iáh ká:tiens = I am not sitting
options: Place the particle Iáh before the verb: Iáh ká:tiens = I am not sitting
Add -iáh to the end of the verb
Change the verb prefix to ia-
Kanien'kéha has no negation
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Iáh is a free particle — it stands before the verb to negate it. This is different from languages (like French) where negation wraps around the verb.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the negated sentence 'I am not sitting'
exercises.correct: Iáh
exercises.hint: I
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iáh ká:tiens. The particle Iáh negates the verb that follows it.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — the negation particle starts with 'I'.

exercises.prompt: In 'Wá:s skó:ha thi áhta'' (Go get the shoe), what does thi signal?
exercises.correct: The article thi is used before nouns in directional/motion contexts
options: The article thi is used before nouns in directional/motion contexts
thi means 'the' in all contexts
thi is a verb prefix
thi signals plural
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! thi is the directional article — it appears when you're going to get or interact with something. ne is used for simple identification.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Hello'?
exercises.correct: Shé:kon
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! (Exactly.) You just greeted yourself.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Shé:kon

exercises.prompt: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne literally means 'it makes my mind happy'. What does this tell you about how Kanien'kéha expresses emotion?
exercises.correct: Emotions are described as embodied experiences — what happens to the mind/body — rather than abstract states
options: Emotions are described as embodied experiences — what happens to the mind/body — rather than abstract states
Kanien'kéha has no words for emotions
This expression is borrowed from English
The word is only used in formal contexts
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is exactly the point. Kanien'kéha describes internal states as physical events — 'it makes the mind happy', not 'I am happy'. This reflects a different ontology of emotion and experience. As Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) note, reclaiming the language also means reclaiming these ways of knowing.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this greeting aloud
exercises.hint: Two syllables: SHÉ-kon. Stress on the first. The é is a mid front vowel with a long-ish quality. Warm and open.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne — it makes the mind happy. You have now completed Unit 3. What, in this course so far, has made your mind happy? What has surprised you? What has felt like coming home to something? Write freely — this reflection is just for you.

lessons.title: O-Class Nouns: Nature & Objects
lessons.sub: Nouns beginning with O
intro: O-class nouns begin with the vowel ‘o’. They include many words from the natural world — water, feathers, flowers, leaves — alongside everyday objects.
Like A-class nouns, some O-class words have visible roots and some don’t. What matters more is recognising the class by its opening ‘o’ — that recognition will serve you across the whole vocabulary.

Many O-class nature nouns appear in the Thanksgiving Address — water, flowers, and feathers are all acknowledged as living beings to be thanked. Learning them here connects the vocabulary to that larger practice.
lessons.ped: Nature vocabulary and the Thanksgiving Address: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) argue that Indigenous language pedagogies should root vocabulary in the worldview of the language. Introducing O-class nature nouns alongside their presence in the Thanksgiving Address does exactly this — these are not merely 'vocabulary items' but beings the language acknowledges.

exercises.prompt: What does Ónhka mean?
exercises.correct: Water
options: Water
Flower
Feather
Glass
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ónhka — water. Water is the first being acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address after the people themselves.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Ohwísta mean?
exercises.correct: Money
options: Money
Sugar
Necklace
Feather
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ohwísta — money. This word appears in the Kontinónhstats curriculum text describing funding for language revitalization.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the O-class nature nouns
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! These four nature nouns all appear in the Thanksgiving Address — they are not just vocabulary but acknowledged members of the world.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the O-class everyday nouns
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! O-class nouns cover both the natural world and everyday life.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'sugar/candy'
exercises.correct: ta
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Otsikhè:ta. This word appeared in the very first Kontinónhstats example sentence: 'Shní:non thi otsikhè:ta' (You buy the candy).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — ends in 'ta'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Water'?
exercises.correct: Ónhka
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ónhka. Water is central to Kanien'kehá:ka life and ceremony.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Ónhka

exercises.prompt: Ohón:ta means 'feather'. Why might feathers have particular significance in the vocabulary you are learning?
exercises.correct: Eagle feathers and other feathers carry ceremonial importance in Haudenosaunee culture
options: Eagle feathers and other feathers carry ceremonial importance in Haudenosaunee culture
Feathers are only used for writing
No particular reason
Feathers are only in the O-class by accident
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Feathers — especially eagle feathers — carry deep ceremonial significance. The language does not separate 'everyday vocabulary' from 'sacred vocabulary'; all of creation is present in the words.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Two syllables: ÓN-hka. The Ó is stressed and slightly long. The -nhka ending is a nasal + stop.

exercises.prompt: Ohwén:tsia (flower) appears in the Thanksgiving Address. What does this tell you about how Kanien'kéha treats the natural world?
exercises.correct: The natural world is addressed directly — flowers are beings to be thanked, not objects to be named
options: The natural world is addressed directly — flowers are beings to be thanked, not objects to be named
Flowers are only decorative
The Thanksgiving Address is just poetry
O-class nouns are less important than A-class
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Thanksgiving Address acknowledges every element of creation — water, plants, animals, winds, sun, moon, stars — as beings in ongoing relationship with the people. Learning these words means learning a relational ontology.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Ónhka — water. In the Thanksgiving Address, water is the first being acknowledged after the people themselves. Without water, nothing lives. Think about water in your life: where it comes from, who it connects you to, what it sustains. What does holding this word in Kanien'kéha feel like?

lessons.title: Ka-Class Nouns: Tools & Community
lessons.sub: Nouns beginning with Ka
intro: Ka-class nouns begin with the syllable ‘ka’. They include a wide range of everyday tools, vehicles, musical instruments, and traditional items.

Some Ka-class nouns show how Kanien’kéha names new things: Kà:sere’ (car) and Kahiatónhsera (paper/book) use the Ka-class system for concepts that did not exist centuries ago. The language does not freeze — it grows.

One culturally significant Ka-class noun: Ka’nikonhrhà:wi (flint/arrowhead). The Kanien’kehá:ka — the People of the Flint — take their identity from this stone. The word for who you are as a people is in the same class as your most essential tool.
lessons.ped: Loanwords and language vitality: A living language adapts. García & Li Wei (2014) note that translanguaging communities continuously add new vocabulary. Kanien'kéha has named cars, books, and clocks using its own noun class system — this is evidence of vitality, not corruption.

exercises.prompt: What does Kà:sere' mean?
exercises.correct: Car / Automobile
options: Car / Automobile
Boat
Book
Drum
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kà:sere' — car. A modern concept named using the Ka-class system. The language adapts.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Kahiatónhsera means 'paper or book'. What does the root -hiatónhsera suggest about this word?
exercises.correct: The root is related to writing — kahiatónhsera literally means 'writing-thing'
options: The root is related to writing — kahiatónhsera literally means 'writing-thing'
It is a pure loanword with no Kanien'kéha root
The root means 'tree'
Ka- is the only meaningful part
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! -hiatónhsera is a verb-derived root meaning 'writing'. Kanien'kéha names modern objects by describing their function — 'paper' is 'the writing thing'. This is how the language grows.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the Ka-class nouns
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Ka-class nouns range from modern inventions (car, book) to traditional instruments (drum). The same noun class holds all of them.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Kaná:tsihs mean?
exercises.correct: Boat / Canoe
options: Boat / Canoe
Car
Key
Drum
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kaná:tsihs — boat/canoe. Watercraft is historically central to Kanien'kehá:ka life on the St. Lawrence and Lake of Two Mountains.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'drum'
exercises.correct: tara
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kanén:tara — drum. The drum opens and closes ceremony and social dance throughout Haudenosaunee culture.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — ends in 'tara'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Car'?
exercises.correct: Kà:sere'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kà:sere'. The Ka- prefix marks the class; the rest is the noun root.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Kà:sere'

exercises.prompt: Ka'nikonhrhà:wi means 'flint/arrowhead'. The Kanien'kehá:ka are known as 'People of the Flint' (Kanien'kéha = language of the flint). What does it mean that flint appears in the Ka-class — the same class as 'car' and 'drum'?
exercises.correct: The noun class system is not a hierarchy — traditional tools and modern inventions belong in the same grammatical family
options: The noun class system is not a hierarchy — traditional tools and modern inventions belong in the same grammatical family
Cars are more important than flint in the modern world
Flint is less important than a drum
The Ka-class only holds modern loanwords
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The noun class system is grammatical, not hierarchical. Flint — the stone that names the people — and car — a modern adaptation — share Ka-class grammar. The language holds both without ranking one above the other.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Four syllables: ka-NÉN-ta-ra. Stress on the second. The colon (:) after én means the vowel is held slightly longer.

exercises.prompt: Kanien'kéha has adapted its noun class system to name modern objects. How does this compare to how English handles new technology vocabulary?
exercises.correct: English often borrows foreign words (telephone, internet); Kanien'kéha creates new words from existing roots using its own grammar
options: English often borrows foreign words (telephone, internet); Kanien'kéha creates new words from existing roots using its own grammar
Kanien'kéha borrows more than English
English never borrows words
They do exactly the same thing
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kanien'kéha's strategy of naming new objects by describing their function (kahiatónhsera = 'writing-thing' for paper) is different from English's tendency to borrow Greek/Latin or foreign terms. Both are valid — they reflect different approaches to language growth.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Ka'nikonhrhà:wi — flint. The Kanien'kehá:ka are the People of the Flint. What object, tool, or material defines the people you come from? What would you be called if your identity were named after the most essential thing your community works with?

lessons.title: Questions About Nouns
lessons.sub: What is it? Where is it? Asking about the objects around you
intro: This lesson applies the question words you know to the noun vocabulary from Unit 3. Asking what something is called, where it is, and whose it is covers most daily-life noun questions.
The three most useful noun questions are: Nahò:ten (what is it?), Ka' (where is it?), and E'tho ká:yen (it is there — the standard answer).
Every question here uses vocabulary you already know. The new skill is putting it together.
lessons.ped: Question formation with nouns: Nation (2001): question-answer pairs built on known vocabulary. Physical domain: producing questions about everyday objects.

exercises.prompt: Nahò:ten ne ní:'i? — what is this? You know ne (specific article) and ní:'i (pointer). What does the full question ask?
exercises.correct: What is this/that? — asking for the name of something being pointed to
options: What is this/that?
What do you want?
What time is it?
What is your name?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Nahò:ten ne ní:'i? — what is that one? Ne + ní:'i point to a specific thing. Nahò:ten asks what it is called.

exercises.prompt: Match the question to its answer

pairs.english: Kà:sere' ne ní:'i — that is a car

pairs.target: Ka' ne áhta'? (where is the shoe?)

pairs.english: Ónhka ne ní:'i — that is water

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! What and where questions, answered with naming and existence sentences.

exercises.prompt: Complete the what question: ___ò:ten ne ní:'i?
exercises.correct: Nah
exercises.hint: N
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Nahò:ten ne ní:'i? — what is this?

exercises.prompt: What is this?
exercises.storyPrompt: Nahò:ten ne ní:'i? — what is this? The question every child asks constantly. Every time you point at something in the world and ask its name in Kanien'kéha, you are doing what the language has always been for — naming the world.

lessons.title: Kanehsatà:ke: Our Place on the Land
lessons.sub: Place names, directions, and the land
intro: This lesson introduces the name of the community itself — Kanehsatà:ke — and the place and direction vocabulary connected to it. Language and land are inseparable in Kanien’kéha.

Kanehsatà:ke means ‘where the crust of snow on top of the earth is’ — a description of the land at a particular time of year. Indigenous place names almost always describe the land’s actual qualities. The name carries the memory of what the land looks like.

The community is also called ‘Oka’ in English — a colonial name. The 1990 Oka Crisis centred on this community’s defence of their land and burial grounds against a golf course expansion on a sacred pine grove. Using Kanehsatà:ke instead of Oka is not just a preference. It is using the name that belongs there.
This lesson also introduces Karáhkwa (the sun) and Awé:ri, a directional term encoding local geography.
lessons.ped: Place names as decolonization: Tuck & Yang (2012) argue that decolonization involves restoring land and relationships to Indigenous peoples. Place names are one site of that restoration — 'Kanehsatà:ke' rather than 'Oka' is not just a preference but an assertion of presence.

exercises.prompt: What does Kanehsatà:ke mean?
exercises.correct: Where the crust of snow on top of the earth is
options: Where the crust of snow on top of the earth is
People of the Flint
The village by the water
Place of many pines
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanehsatà:ke — 'where the crust of snow on top of the earth is'. The community often called 'Oka' in English — a name that erases Indigenous presence entirely.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: What does Karáhkwa mean?
exercises.correct: Sun / The sun
options: Sun / The sun
Moon
Star
Fire
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Karáhkwa — the sun. Acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address: 'Look up and see the sun, our eldest brother.'
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the community name
exercises.sentence: The community's name in Kanien'kéha is Kanehsatà:___
exercises.correct: ke
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kanehsatà:ke. Not 'Oka' — the Kanien'kéha name asserts this community's existence in its own language.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — ends in 'ke'.

exercises.prompt: How do you spell the community's name in Kanien'kéha?
exercises.sub: Accents optional for this answer
exercises.correct: Kanehsatà:ke
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kanehsatà:ke. The grave accent (à) marks the stressed syllable; the colon (:) marks vowel length. Every mark carries meaning.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Kanehsatà:ke

exercises.prompt: The 1990 Oka Crisis involved Kanehsatà:ke. What was at stake?
exercises.correct: The community's defence of their land, including a sacred pine grove, against a golf course expansion
options: The community's defence of their land, including a sacred pine grove, against a golf course expansion
A dispute over fishing rights
A language policy disagreement
A boundary between two provinces
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Oka Crisis — Kanehsatà:ke from the community's perspective — was a 78-day standoff in which Kanien'kehá:ka people defended their land and sacred sites. The community's name in Kanien'kéha is itself a form of that defence.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Karáhkwa (the sun) is acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address. How does including this word in a language lesson connect to the critical pedagogies this course draws on?
exercises.correct: Language is not separate from culture and ceremony — learning Karáhkwa means entering a worldview where the sun is a being, not just a word
options: Language is not separate from culture and ceremony — learning Karáhkwa means entering a worldview where the sun is a being, not just a word
It is just vocabulary
The Thanksgiving Address is not related to language learning
Only ceremonial words belong in ceremonial contexts
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! This is precisely McCarty & Lee's (2014) point: culturally sustaining/revitalizing pedagogy treats language as inseparable from its cultural and spiritual dimensions. Karáhkwa is not just 'sun' — it is a being in ongoing relationship with the people.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say the community's name aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: ka-neh-sa-TÀ-ke. The stress falls on the 'ta' (fourth) syllable. The grave accent (à) marks this stress. The final 'ke' is short and quick.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.meaning: Where the crust of snow on top of the earth is
exercises.storyPrompt: Kanehsatà:ke — a name that describes what the land looks like at a particular time of year. Indigenous place names are almost always descriptions of the land's actual qualities, not commemorations of people. Think about the place you are from or call home. What would its name be if it were named for what the land actually looks, feels, or sounds like?

exercises.prompt: Kanehsatà:ke is described as ‘where the crust of snow on top of the earth is’. What does it tell you about Kanien’kéha place names that they describe what the land looks and feels like?
exercises.correct: Place names are descriptions — the land is named for what it actually is, not for a person or an event imposed on it
options: Place names are descriptions — the land is named for what it actually is, not for a person or an event imposed on it
Place names are chosen randomly
The name refers to a building in the community
All Kanien’kéha place names use snow vocabulary
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanien’kéha place names describe the land. Kanehsatà:ke is the snow-crust on the earth. Kahnawà:ke means ‘at the rapids’. Akwesasne means ‘where the partridge drums’. Each name is a description of what is actually there.

exercises.prompt: Complete the name of the sun: Karáh___
exercises.correct: kwa
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Karáhkwa — the sun. A Ka-class noun from the same class as the tools in Lesson 13. The sun, the canoe, the drum — all Ka-class. The class cuts across the natural and the made world.
exercises.wm: Please try again — the sun’s name ends in ‘kwa’.

lessons.title: A Living Language Grows
lessons.sub: How Kanien'kéha names new things — and why that matters
intro: You have seen throughout Unit 3 that Kanien'kéha names new things using its own word-formation strategies. A car is Kà:sere'. A book is Kahiatónhsera. A clock is Karihtón:ni.
This is what a living language does. It adapts to the world its speakers live in, without losing itself.
A language that can name everything its speakers encounter is a language that will survive.
lessons.ped: Language vitality through productive naming: García & Li Wei (2014): translanguaging communities use their language's productive capacity to name new things. LaFever Emotional domain: the living language as source of pride.

exercises.prompt: Karihtón:ni — clock. The name is built from the Ka-class system. What does it describe?
exercises.correct: Something like 'the thing that counts the hours' — the Ka-class names the object by its function
options: The thing that counts the hours
A borrowed French word
An old word for sundial
It has no meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Karihtón:ni — the counting/measuring thing. Kanien'kéha named the clock using its own roots. This is the productive naming strategy.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehón:we means 'Indigenous person / original person'. What does the word itself say?
exercises.correct: It names Indigenous people as the original people of the land — the identity is built into the word
options: It names Indigenous people as the original people
It is only a political term
It means 'first settler'
It is borrowed
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Onkwehón:we — original people. The identity is built into the word itself. This is what it means to have a language that names your world from the inside.

exercises.prompt: Write the phrase 'I am studying Kanien'kéha'
exercises.correct: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am studying Kanien'kéha. A sentence about itself, in itself.

exercises.prompt: Original people
exercises.storyPrompt: Onkwehón:we — original person. The people who spoke this language first, who gave names to every river and hill and animal. Onkwehón:we. The language remembers who was here first. Not because someone wrote it down. Because the word itself says so.

lessons.title: Unit 3 Review
lessons.sub: Nouns, verbs, articles, greetings, and place names together
intro: Unit 3 covered three noun classes, A-stem verbs, articles and greetings, negation, and place names. This review brings it all together.
You can now name objects in three classes, command someone to do something, say hello and thank you, say no, and name the community where this language lives.
Use this lesson to consolidate before moving forward.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation review: Nation (2001): end-of-unit review consolidates learning through new combinations.

exercises.prompt: Match the noun to its class
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A-, O-, Ka- — three classes, identified by their first sound.

exercises.prompt: Sá:tien means 'sit' (command). Ká:tiens means 'I am sitting'. What changes?
exercises.correct: The prefix changes — s- for you (command), k- for I — and the habitual -s suffix is added
options: The prefix changes from s- to k-, and -s suffix is added
Only the suffix changes
Only the prefix changes
Nothing changes
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! S- (command) → k- + -s (I habitually). Two pieces: the person prefix and the habitual suffix. The verb root stays the same.

exercises.prompt: Complete: the (specific article) — ___
exercises.correct: ne
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ne — the specific article. Points to a particular thing.

exercises.prompt: Iáh means not. Iáh ká:tiens means what?
exercises.correct: I am not sitting
options: I am not sitting
I am sitting
Are you sitting?
You sit (command)
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iáh + ká:tiens = I am not sitting. Iáh flips the meaning.

exercises.prompt: The community
exercises.storyPrompt: Kanehsatà:ke — where the crust is on the sand. This is where the curriculum this app is built on was created. Every word in this app has passed through that community's knowledge. You are learning the language of a specific place, with a specific name, held by specific people. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 4 — Colours, Body & Numbers
desc: Colour nouns, body parts, and counting 1–10

lessons.title: Colour, Body and Number: What This Unit Covers
lessons.sub: Colours, body, and numbers — the building blocks of description
intro: Unit 4 gives you three of the most useful categories of vocabulary: colours, body parts, and numbers.

These three sets do more than just name things. Colours connect to how Kanien’kéha names the natural world. Body parts prepare you for verbs that incorporate them — brushing teeth, washing hands. Numbers open up time, age, and counting.
Start where you are. If some words come easily and others need more time — that is how language learning works. Every word you carry from this unit will come back in later lessons.
lessons.ped: Unit 4 scope: Colours, body parts, and numbers 1–10 are introduced here as foundational vocabulary that recurs throughout the course. Colours reappear in Unit 23 (describing things). Body parts reappear in Unit 13 (daily routine verbs). Numbers reappear in Unit 17 (numbers in depth). Nation (2001): spaced repetition of foundational vocabulary across units is more effective than intensive coverage in one place. LaFever Mental and Physical domains primary.

exercises.prompt: Colours, body parts, and numbers are in the same unit. What do these three things have in common as starting vocabulary?
exercises.correct: They are all high-frequency — you use them to describe almost anything in daily life
options: They are all high-frequency — you use them to describe almost anything in daily life
They are the easiest vocabulary in the language
They are only used in formal speech
They have no connection to each other
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Colour, body, and number vocabulary turns up everywhere — in descriptions, in daily routines, in time-telling, in ceremony. Starting here gives you tools you will use constantly.

exercises.prompt: What you bring to this unit
exercises.storyPrompt: Onekwénhtara — red. The colour of blood, of certain berries, of the east at dawn. Before you start this unit, take a moment. You already know pronouns, kinship terms, nouns and verbs, articles and greetings, and direction vocabulary. You have been building a foundation. The words you learn in this unit will go on top of it. What colour would you say this language is, so far?

lessons.title: Colours
lessons.sub: Naming what we see
intro: Kanien’kéha colour words are mostly O-class nouns — many are descriptive in origin, naming the colour by what is that colour. Óhonte’ (green) is related to grass and growing things. Oròn:ia (blue) connects to the sky. Onekwénhtara (red) is related to the word for blood.

Colours in Kanien’kéha are used with the question Ónhka ohsawé:non? — which colour does it wear? The verb for colour uses the image of wearing, not being. Colour is something a thing carries, not something it permanently is.
This lesson covers eight core colours that appear throughout the vocabulary and in the natural world descriptions you will build in later units.
lessons.ped: Culturally embedded vocabulary: Flores & Rosa (2015) warn against treating colour terms as 'neutral vocabulary'. The fact that Kanien'kéha colour words are often descriptive — green = plant-coloured, red = blood-coloured — reflects a different way of categorising perception. Learning these is learning an ontology.

exercises.prompt: What does Onekwénhtara mean?
exercises.correct: Red
options: Red
Blue
Green
Brown
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onekwénhtara — red. This colour word is related to the word for blood.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Óhonte' means green. The root is related to grass and growing things. This is an example of what type of word formation?
exercises.correct: Descriptive naming — the colour is named by what is that colour
options: Descriptive naming — the colour is named by what is that colour
Borrowing from another language
Pure coincidence
The root has no meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Óhonte' = green = the colour of growing plants. This descriptive naming strategy is consistent across Kanien'kéha vocabulary.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the colours
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four of the eight colours — notice that red, blue, and green are all O-class nouns.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match more colours
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Notice that brown (Athéhsa) begins with A — it is an A-class noun, not O-class. Colours span noun classes.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Red'?
exercises.correct: Onekwénhtara
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onekwénhtara. The long stress on wén is important — it carries the primary stress of the word.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Onekwénhtara

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Green'?
exercises.correct: Óhonte'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Óhonte'. The glottal stop (') at the end is part of the word.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Óhonte'

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'blue'
exercises.correct: ia
exercises.hint: i
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Oròn:ia — blue, the colour of sky. The grave accent (ò) marks a falling tone on the 'o'.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — ends in 'ia'.

exercises.prompt: Say this colour word aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: o-nek-wÉN-hta-ra. Stress on the third syllable (wén). The -htara ending is a soft 'hta-ra'.

exercises.prompt: Oròn:ia (blue) is related to the word for sky. How does this connect to the broader pattern you have seen in Kanien'kéha vocabulary?
exercises.correct: Many Kanien'kéha words describe properties or functions rather than giving arbitrary names — the language encodes how things relate to the world
options: Many Kanien'kéha words describe properties or functions rather than giving arbitrary names — the language encodes how things relate to the world
This is a coincidence in one word only
Blue and sky are unrelated in Kanien'kéha
All languages do this identically
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This descriptive naming is a pattern: blue = sky-coloured, green = plant-coloured, red = blood-coloured, writing-thing = paper. Kanien'kéha consistently encodes relationship and function into word roots.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Óhonte' — green, the colour of growing things. Think of something green that matters to you: a plant, a place, a season. Now think of what 'green' means beyond just a colour — growth, renewal, land, life. Holding the word Óhonte' in your mind, what does it bring forward?

lessons.title: Colours in the Natural World
lessons.sub: Using colour words to describe what you see around you
intro: Now that you know the colour words, this lesson puts them to work describing the natural world — animals, plants, the sky, the land. These are the contexts where colour words appear most often in Kanien’kéha.
You already know many of the natural world nouns from Units 3 and 6. Pairing them with colour words creates real descriptions. A brown bear. A white feather. A green leaf.
Colour words in Kanien’kéha are describing verbs — they say what state something is in. You will build on this in Unit 23.
lessons.ped: Colour in naturalistic context: Nation (2001): vocabulary retention improves when words are encountered in multiple meaningful contexts. Colour words learned in Unit 15 reappear here in natural world descriptions, and again in Unit 23. This lesson is the first contextual reuse.

exercises.prompt: Match the natural world description
exercises.sub: Noun + colour describing verb
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Noun + colour describing verb. A complete description in two words each time.

exercises.prompt: Óhonte’ means green. Its root connects to plants and growing things. What does it mean to describe a leaf as Óhonte’?
exercises.correct: The leaf is in the state of being plant-coloured — green like living vegetation
options: The leaf is in the state of being plant-coloured — green like living vegetation
The leaf is painted green
The word is unrelated to plants
Only certain leaves are Óhonte’
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Óhonte’ carries the green of living plants in its root. When you say the leaf is Óhonte’, you are connecting its colour to its living nature.

exercises.prompt: Onekwénhtara means red — related to the word for blood. The strawberry is red — Ken’ niiohontésha’ Onekwénhtara. What do red things share in Kanien’kéha colour logic?
exercises.correct: Red is the colour of living blood — the word names the colour by its most vivid living source
options: Red is the colour of living blood
Red is named after a plant
The colour has no named origin
Red only describes blood
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Onekwénhtara — blood-coloured. Strawberries, berries, certain birds, the dawn sky — all share this quality. The colour word carries the living thing it originally named.

exercises.prompt: The snow is white — Óniehte ___
exercises.correct: Ó:wira’
exercises.hint: Ó
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Óniehte Ó:wira’ — the snow is white. A simple, true sentence about the winter landscape.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which colour word means white?

exercises.prompt: How do you say ‘the night is black’? (iohsiénhaon is nighttime)
exercises.correct: iohsiénhaon Kayé:ri
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! iohsiénhaon Kayé:ri — the night is black. Two words, a complete description of the darkest time of day.
exercises.wm: Please try again — night + the black colour word.

exercises.prompt: The green world
exercises.storyPrompt: Óhonte’ — green. The colour of the growing season, of the corn pushing up, of the forest in summer, of the land waking in spring. In Sé:ske — spring — the world becomes Óhonte’ again after the white of winter. This colour word holds a whole season’s return. What is the first green thing you notice when spring comes where you live?

lessons.title: Body Parts
lessons.sub: Naming the self
intro: Body part vocabulary in Kanien’kéha matters for two reasons. First, it gives you the words needed for verbs that fold the body part directly into the action — brushing your teeth, washing your hands, combing your hair are all single words in Kanien’kéha, with the body part built in.

Second, the body in Kanien’kéha is not just anatomical. Many body part words carry connections to related actions. The word for nose relates to smelling. The word for teeth appears inside the verb for brushing them.
This lesson covers eight core body parts — the foundation for the grooming and daily routine vocabulary you will build in Unit 13.
lessons.ped: Noun incorporation preview: You first encountered noun incorporation in Unit 3 (áhta' + -owá:nen → 'a big shoe'). Body part vocabulary sets you up for the next level — verbs that incorporate body part roots to express actions: wash-face, brush-teeth, comb-hair all exist as single Kanien'kéha words.

exercises.prompt: What does Ononkwa' mean?
exercises.correct: Head
options: Head
Hair
Face
Nose
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ononkwa' — head. The glottal stop (') at the end is significant.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the body parts — face
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! The vocabulary of the face — head, eye, nose, lips.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match the body parts — upper body
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Upper body vocabulary — hair, teeth, hand, arm.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Onékahrha means 'teeth'. How does this connect to the A-stem verb lesson you completed?
exercises.sub: Think about what verb uses the teeth root
exercises.correct: The verb 'to brush one's teeth' incorporates the tooth root — the body part is built into the verb
options: The verb 'to brush one's teeth' incorporates the tooth root — the body part is built into the verb
Teeth and verbs are unrelated
You need teeth to speak — that is the connection
Onékahrha is only used in medical contexts
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Noun incorporation: the verb for 'to brush one's teeth' embeds -nekáhrha- (teeth root) directly into the verb. One Kanien'kéha word = brush + teeth + I + habitual. This is spaced repetition of a key grammatical concept.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'hand'
exercises.correct: a
exercises.hint: a
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onenhstà:'a — hand. This root appears in the verb 'to wash one's hands' from the A-stem lesson.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — ends in 'a'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Eye'?
exercises.correct: Ó:nen
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ó:nen — eye. The long vowel on Ó: is important.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Ó:nen

exercises.prompt: Ononkwatón:'a (hair) literally relates to 'on the head' (ononkwa = head). This pattern of body part words being location-based is common in Kanien'kéha. What does this tell you about how the language conceptualises the body?
exercises.correct: Body parts are named relationally — by their position on or in relation to other body parts
options: Body parts are named relationally — by their position on or in relation to other body parts
Body part names are arbitrary
Hair and head are unrelated in the grammar
Only a few body parts use this pattern
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Relational naming extends to anatomy — the body is described as a set of spatial relationships, not isolated parts. This reflects the same relational ontology you saw in kinship nouns (where 'my mother' is a different word from 'your mother').
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: o-nenh-STÀ-'a. Stress on the third syllable. The glottal stop (') before the final 'a creates a brief pause.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Onenhstà:'a — hand. Hands that bead, that drum, that plant, that write, that hold. The Kontinónhstats video shows a grandmother's hands teaching beading. What do the hands in your family carry? What do your hands know how to do that your voice does not yet know how to name?

lessons.title: Extended Body Parts
lessons.sub: Lower body, back, and full-body vocabulary
intro: Lesson 16 covered the upper body: head, hair, eyes, nose, lips, teeth, hands, arms. This lesson adds the lower body and back, completing the picture.
Knowing the full set of body part words matters for daily conversation about health, movement, and the incorporated body-action verbs you will use in Unit 13.
The lower body words follow the same O-class pattern as the upper body words — they all begin with O-.
lessons.ped: Completing the body vocabulary set: Nation (2001): semantic sets are best learned completely rather than partially — a partial body vocabulary creates gaps that are frustrating in real use. Lower body vocabulary prepares learners for the health and daily routine units. Physical domain: production of body part vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Match the lower body part to its name
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four lower body and torso words. Notice the O- opening on all of them — body part words are O-class nouns.

exercises.prompt: More body parts
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Forehead, neck, elbow, finger — four more body parts. The O- pattern holds across all of them.

exercises.prompt: Otsìtà:’a (leg) and Ohsì:’a (foot) share the same -sì- root. What does this suggest?
exercises.correct: The leg and foot share a root because they are part of the same limb — the language groups related body parts
options: The leg and foot share a root because they are part of the same limb
It is a coincidence
They have completely unrelated meanings
Only the foot is a body part
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Kanien’kéha often groups related body parts through shared roots. When you notice a shared root, you are reading the morphology of the language. Otsìtà:’a and Ohsì:’a — leg and foot — are related.

exercises.prompt: Complete: back — Ohrák___
exercises.correct: wens
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohrákwens — back. Three syllables: oh-RÁK-wens.

exercises.prompt: Say the foot and leg aloud
exercises.hint: oh-SÌ:-’a (3 syllables) — ot-sì-TÀ:-’a (4 syllables). Both have glottal stops before the final ‘a. Both have falling-pitch grave accents.

exercises.prompt: The whole body
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohràkwens — back. You now know words for most of the body in Kanien’kéha. The head, the hair, the eyes, the nose, the lips, the teeth, the hands, the arms, the legs, the feet, the back. These words allow you to talk about health, about movement, about care. They are the words for describing how a person is — or how they hurt. They are also the words that appear in the incorporated body-action verbs of Unit 13. The body is not separate from the language. The language knows the body.

lessons.title: Numbers 1–10
lessons.sub: Counting in Kanien'kéha
intro: Kanien’kéha counts from 1 to 10 with its own unique words. From 10 onwards, numbers combine: eleven = ten + one (Óia’k énska), twenty = two tens (Tékeni óia’k).

You have already seen the number Tékeni (two) — it is the root in the dual prefix tekeni- (two people). The grammatical dual is built directly on the number two. The number and the grammar are the same.
Numbers appear throughout this course — in age expressions, in time-telling, in counting at a gathering. Learning 1–10 here gives you the foundation for all of that.
lessons.ped: Numeracy and language: The eCampusOntario OER for beginner Mohawk language learning includes numbers as an early-intermediate topic, reflecting their centrality to daily communication. Tékeni also demonstrates spaced repetition — a number root you've seen grammatically now appears as standalone vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: What does Énska mean?
exercises.correct: One (1)
options: One (1)
Two (2)
Three (3)
Five (5)
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Énska — one. You will see this root in compound numbers: eleven = Óia'k énska (ten-one).
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: You already know Tékeni from earlier in the course. Where did you see this root before?
exercises.correct: In the dual prefix tekeni- (two people) — tekeniksá:'a = two girls
options: In the dual prefix tekeni- (two people) — tekeniksá:'a = two girls
In the word for grandmother
In the A-stem verb lesson
It has not appeared before
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Tékeni (two) is the root of the tekeni- dual prefix. The number two and the grammatical dual are explicitly connected — this is the prefix system made visible as standalone vocabulary.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match numbers 1–5
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Numbers 1–5. Say them aloud in order if you can: Énska, Tékeni, Áhsen, Kaié:ri, Wísk.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Match numbers 6–10
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Numbers 6–10. Sha'té:kon (eight) has a glottal stop (') — do not drop it.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'One'?
exercises.correct: Énska
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Énska — one. Stress on the first syllable.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Énska

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Ten'?
exercises.correct: Óia'k
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Óia'k — ten. All numbers above ten are built from this root: eleven = Óia'k Énska.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — try again. Try: Óia'k

exercises.prompt: Complete the number six
exercises.correct: k
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ià:ia'k — six. The Haudenosaunee Confederacy has six member nations — Ia:ia'k Onkwehonwe.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — ends in 'k'.

exercises.prompt: The Haudenosaunee Confederacy consists of six nations. How is Ià:ia'k (six) related to Haudenosaunee identity?
exercises.correct: 'Six Nations' in Kanien'kéha is Ià:ia'k Onkwehonwe — the number is embedded in the confederacy's self-description
options: 'Six Nations' in Kanien'kéha is Ià:ia'k Onkwehonwe — the number is embedded in the confederacy's self-description
Numbers are only used for counting objects
The number six has no cultural meaning
Only the English name 'Six Nations' carries meaning
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ià:ia'k Onkwehonwe — six peoples. The political identity of the confederacy is encoded in the language itself. Learning to count is also learning to name the political reality.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Say these numbers aloud in sequence
exercises.sub: Record yourself counting 1 to 5
exercises.meaning: Five — try counting: Énska, Tékeni, Áhsen, Kaié:ri, Wísk
exercises.hint: Short, distinct words. Énska (ÉN-ska), Tékeni (TÉ-ke-ni), Áhsen (ÁH-sen), Kaié:ri (ka-IÉ-ri), Wísk (single syllable, short and sharp).

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.meaning: Two — and the dual prefix tekeni-
exercises.storyPrompt: Tékeni — two. You have now seen this number as a standalone word AND as a grammatical prefix (tekeniksá:'a = two girls). In Kanien'kéha, the number two and the grammatical dual are the same root. What other pairs exist in your life that belong together — people, places, ideas? Who is your tékeni?

lessons.title: Numbers in Daily Life
lessons.sub: Using numbers 1–10 in sentences you actually need
intro: Knowing numbers as a list is different from using them. This lesson practises numbers 1–10 in the sentence contexts where they come up most often: counting things, saying how many, and the beginning of time and age expressions.
You already know all ten numbers. This lesson gives them somewhere to live in real sentences.
Numbers appear again in Unit 17 — the full numbers unit — where counting people, telling time, and the months are all covered in depth. What you practise here is the foundation for all of that.
lessons.ped: Number vocabulary in context: Nation (2001): numbers are best consolidated through functional use in multiple sentence types. This lesson provides those contexts. Physical domain: productive number use. Numbers from this unit recur in Unit 17 (numbers in depth) and throughout the course.

exercises.prompt: Numbers 1–10 — all of them
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! One through five. The foundation of the whole number system.

exercises.prompt: Numbers 6–10
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Six through ten. Óia’k — ten — is the base for all compound numbers above ten.

exercises.prompt: To nihá:ti? means ‘how many?’ Someone asks: To nihá:ti ne Ohkwá:ri? How many bears are there? There are three. What do you say?
exercises.correct: Áhsen — three
options: Áhsen — three
Tékeni — two
Wísk — five
Óia’k — ten
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! To nihá:ti? — Áhsen. Three bears. The question and the number, a complete exchange.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which number is three?

exercises.prompt: There are two drums. Complete: ___ Kanén:tara
exercises.correct: Tékeni
exercises.hint: T
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tékeni Kanén:tara — two drums. Number + noun. You also know Tékeni as the root of the dual pronoun and the te- dual prefix. The number two runs through the whole grammar.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which number means two?

exercises.prompt: Write the number five in Kanien’kéha
exercises.correct: Wísk
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wísk — five. Also appears as part of Wísk óia’k — fifty. The same root across all uses.

exercises.prompt: Count from one to ten aloud
exercises.word: Énska — Tékeni — Áhsen — Kaié:ri — Wísk — À:ia’k — Tsà:ta — Sha’té:kon — Tióhton — Óia’k
exercises.hint: Say each number clearly before moving to the next. When you can count to ten without a pause, you own these numbers.

exercises.prompt: Three
exercises.storyPrompt: Áhsen — three. Three clans. Three sisters. The number three structures so much of Kanien’kehá:ka life. When you say three in this language, you are using the same word that names the Bear Clan, the Turtle Clan, and the Wolf Clan together. You are using the number that counts the corn and the beans and the squash in the same breath. Áhsen is not just three. It is wholeness.

lessons.title: Unit 4 Review
lessons.sub: Colours, body parts, and numbers — all together
intro: This lesson brings together the colour, body part, and number vocabulary from Unit 4 in new combinations and sentence types.

By now you have learned 11 colours, over 12 body part words, and all 10 numbers. This review creates new connections between them: what colour are the eyes? How many fingers? Describe a body part using a colour.
Every combination you can make is a sentence in Kanien’kéha.
lessons.ped: Cross-category consolidation: Nation (2001): combining vocabulary from different semantic categories in review produces deeper retention than reviewing each category separately. This lesson creates colour-body, number-body, and colour-number combinations.

exercises.prompt: Ó:nen means eyes. To say ‘the eyes are dark / black’, which colour word do you use?
exercises.correct: Kayé:ri — black / dark
options: Kayé:ri — black / dark
Óhonte’ — green
Ó:wira’ — white
Oròn:ia — blue
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ó:nen Kayé:ri — the eyes are dark. Noun + colour describing verb. The same pattern you have been practising.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which colour word means dark or black?

exercises.prompt: Colour + body part descriptions
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Four colour + body part descriptions. Each one is a complete Kanien’kéha sentence.

exercises.prompt: To nihá:ti ne Onén:ta’shon? How many fingers? The answer is ten. What do you say?
exercises.correct: Óia’k — ten
options: Óia’k — ten
Wísk — five
Tékeni — two
Áhsen — three
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Óia’k — ten fingers. The same number that is the base for all compound numbers is also the number of fingers on two hands.
exercises.wm: Please try again — how many fingers does a person have?

exercises.prompt: Complete: the head — Ononk___
exercises.correct: wa’
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ononkwa’ — head. The glottal stop (') at the end is part of the word.

exercises.prompt: Write the word for white
exercises.correct: Ó:wira’
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ó:wira’ — white. Long vowel on Ó:, glottal stop at the end.
exercises.wm: Please try again — long vowel and glottal stop.

exercises.prompt: Describing the world
exercises.storyPrompt: Ó:wira’, Kayé:ri, Óhonte’ — white, black, green. Three of the most basic colours, and already you can describe so much with them: the snow is white, the night is black, the leaf is green. Colour vocabulary is not just decoration. It is description, and description is how you tell someone what you see. You are learning to see the world in Kanien’kéha. What do you see right now that you could describe in this language?

title: Unit 5 — The Thanksgiving Address
desc: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words before all else

lessons.title: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen: The Words Before All Else
lessons.sub: The words that come before all else
intro: Unit 5 introduces the Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the Thanksgiving Address. It is the most important oral text in Kanien’kéha.

The Address opens every gathering, every ceremony, every school day in Haudenosaunee communities. It is not a prayer in the colonial religious sense. It is an acknowledgement — of every being in the world, thanked in order, from the earth up to the Creator.
You do not need to understand every word to begin here. You need to know what it is, why it matters, and what it feels like to hear your first phrases from it. That is what this unit gives you.
lessons.ped: Unit 5 scope: The Thanksgiving Address is introduced in Unit 5 rather than deferred as ‘advanced material’ because McCarty & Lee (2014) argue that culturally sustaining pedagogy must centre oral traditions from the beginning. Learners who encounter the Address early have a ceremonial frame for the vocabulary they are building. LaFever Spiritual domain primary throughout this unit.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address is called ‘the words that come before all else’. Before all else — before any meeting, any ceremony, any school day. What does this tell you about how Haudenosaunee gatherings are structured?
exercises.correct: The world is acknowledged before any business begins — gratitude comes first, then work
options: The world is acknowledged before any business begins — gratitude comes first, then work
The Address is the final part of any gathering
It is only spoken by chiefs
It is only used in formal ceremonies
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — before all else. The world is acknowledged before any conversation, any decision, any lesson. Everything else comes after.

exercises.prompt: Say the name of the Thanksgiving Address aloud
exercises.sub: You will say this name many times across this course
exercises.meaning: The words that come before all else
exercises.hint: OH-én:-ton (3 syllables) — Ka-ri-hwa-TÉH-kwen (5 syllables). Eight syllables total. Say it slowly. This name will become familiar.

exercises.prompt: Before all else
exercises.meaning: The words that come before all else
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. Every single gathering in Haudenosaunee life begins this way. Before the council meets. Before the ceremony begins. Before the school day starts. The earth, the waters, the plants, the animals, the birds, the winds, the thunderers, the sun, the moon, the stars, the Creator — each acknowledged, each thanked. Then the meeting can begin. What would it feel like to begin every day that way?

lessons.title: The Thanksgiving Address
lessons.sub: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen
intro: The Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — ‘the words that come before all else’ — is the most important oral text in Kanien’kéha. It opens and closes every gathering, every ceremony, every school day in Haudenosaunee communities.

The Address acknowledges every element of creation in order: the people, the earth, the waters, the plants, the animals, the trees, the birds, the winds, Grandfather Thunderers, the sun, the moon, the stars, and finally the Creator. After each section comes: Ionkhi’nikonhraé:tase’ — our minds are made one, we give thanks.
Listening to the full Address in Kanien’kéha — even without understanding every word — is a form of learning. The sound and the rhythm carry meaning before the words do.
lessons.ped: Oral tradition as curriculum: McCarty & Lee (2014) argue that culturally sustaining/revitalizing pedagogy must centre the oral traditions and knowledge systems of the community. The Thanksgiving Address is not 'advanced material' to be deferred — it is the beginning of meaning in Kanien'kéha. Starting learners here, even at a vocabulary level, grounds the language in its living context.

exercises.prompt: What does Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen mean?
exercises.correct: The words that come before all else (Thanksgiving Address)
options: The words that come before all else (Thanksgiving Address)
The end of the gathering
A prayer to the Creator only
A greeting between two people
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — 'the words before all else'. It opens and closes every gathering in Haudenosaunee communities.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehshón:'a — 'the people' — is the first group acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address. What does it mean that humans come first?
exercises.correct: Acknowledging the people first establishes their presence and responsibility before anything else can be named
options: Acknowledging the people first establishes their presence and responsibility before anything else can be named
Humans are more important than all other beings
The order is arbitrary
Humans come first because they are the speakers
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The people are named first not as a hierarchy but as an acknowledgement of presence and relationship. We are here. Then the Address proceeds to acknowledge everything we are in relationship with.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' is the closing refrain of each section. It shares a root with Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne ('it makes my mind happy') but is in first person plural. What does this shift signal?
exercises.correct: The Thanksgiving Address is a collective act — 'our minds' not 'my mind' — gratitude as communal practice
options: The Thanksgiving Address is a collective act — 'our minds' not 'my mind' — gratitude as communal practice
It is just a grammatical accident
The plural form is more polite
Only elders say the plural form
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! You just used the singular form — I. The Thanksgiving Address uses the plural — we. Gratitude, in this tradition, is not a private feeling but a collective orientation toward the world.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Complete the name of the Thanksgiving Address
exercises.correct: kwen
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen. The full name: 'the words that come before all else'.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — ends in 'kwen'.

exercises.prompt: Ohwén:tsia ne Ionkwarihó:ten means 'the earth that sustains us'. What does Ionkwarihó:ten literally mean?
exercises.correct: It takes care of us / it is our responsibility — a two-way relationship
options: It takes care of us / it is our responsibility — a two-way relationship
It is just an object below our feet
The earth belongs to humans
The earth is passive
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ionkwarihó:ten encodes reciprocity — the earth takes care of us AND it is our responsibility. The Thanksgiving Address is not passive gratitude but an acknowledgement of mutual obligation.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Listen to the Thanksgiving Address
exercises.sub: Visit the Resources tab to watch the Kontinónhstats animated version on YouTube. For now, try saying the title aloud.
exercises.hint: Six syllables: o-HÉN-ton ka-rih-wa-TÉH-kwen. Two stressed syllables — HÉN and TÉH. The Address begins with these words at every gathering.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address acknowledges beings in order: people, earth, waters, plants, animals, trees, birds, winds, thunderers, sun, moon, stars, Creator. What is significant about this sequence?
exercises.correct: The sequence moves from immediate/earthly to distant/celestial — it maps the world as it is experienced from the ground up
options: The sequence moves from immediate/earthly to distant/celestial — it maps the world as it is experienced from the ground up
The order is arbitrary
The Creator comes first in all Haudenosaunee traditions
The sequence is borrowed from European religion
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Address moves outward from the community — people first, then the ground beneath them, then the waters, then plants and animals, then up through the sky to the stars. It is a phenomenological map of the world as it is lived, from the earth up.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: An animated version of the Thanksgiving Address was produced by Kontinónhstats in Kanehsatà:ke. Why does a language revitalization organization invest in animated media?
exercises.correct: Media in the language creates new domains of use — children and families can access the language through familiar formats
options: Media in the language creates new domains of use — children and families can access the language through familiar formats
Animation is only for entertainment
The words are too difficult to teach directly
Animated media is required by law
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! This is García & Li Wei's (2014) point about expanding language domains. When Kanien'kéha appears in animation, YouTube, podcasts, and apps, it gains new contexts of use — which is essential for revitalization.
exercises.wm: That answer is incorrect — please try again.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: 'The words that come before all else' — the idea that before any meeting, any learning, any gathering, we first acknowledge the world we are part of. What would it mean in your life to begin that way? What would you acknowledge, in your own words, before you began something that mattered?

lessons.title: The People and the Earth
lessons.sub: The opening of the Thanksgiving Address — people and the earth that holds us
intro: The Thanksgiving Address begins by acknowledging the people — Onkwehshón:’a — and then the earth — Ohwén:tsia ne Ionkwarihó:ten — the earth that sustains us.
These first two acknowledgements set the tone for everything that follows. The people first, because they are the ones gathered. Then the earth, because without it nothing else exists.
This lesson introduces the vocabulary of these opening sections and explores what it means to begin with the earth.
lessons.ped: Opening sections of the Address as primary vocabulary: Beginning with the people and the earth follows the Address’s own sequence. Learners who know these opening sections understand the structure of the whole. LaFever Spiritual domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehshón:’a — the people / human beings. The Address acknowledges the people first. Why do you think the people come before the earth in the sequence?
exercises.correct: The people are the ones gathered — acknowledging them first affirms that everyone is present before turning to the wider world
options: The people are the ones gathered — acknowledging them first affirms that everyone is present
People are more important than the earth
The order is random
The earth is acknowledged last
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Address moves from the immediate — the people in the room — to the vast. Acknowledging the people first says: I see you. You are here. Now let us look at the world together.

exercises.prompt: Ohwén:tsia ne Ionkwarihó:ten — the earth that sustains us. What does the word Ionkwarihó:ten tell you about the earth?
exercises.correct: It is actively sustaining us right now — the earth is not just a place but a living provider
options: It is actively sustaining us right now
The earth is a historical concept
It means the earth is dangerous
Ionkwarihó:ten means beautiful
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Ionkwarihó:ten — that which sustains us. The word describes an ongoing action, not a fixed state. The earth is sustaining you right now, as you read this.

exercises.prompt: Match the opening Address vocabulary to its meaning

pairs.english: Water (the waters acknowledged after the earth)
On the earth / at ground level

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The opening of the Address: people, earth, waters. Each acknowledged in turn.

exercises.prompt: Complete: the people — Onkwehshón:___
exercises.correct: ’a
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onkwehshón:’a — the people. The glottal stop before the final ‘a is part of the word and part of every saying of this name.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends with glottal stop + a.

exercises.prompt: Say this phrase from the Thanksgiving Address aloud
exercises.hint: oh-WÉN:-tsia (3 syllables) — ne (1) — ion-kwa-ri-HÓ:-ten (5 syllables). Nine syllables in one phrase. Say it slowly. This phrase has been said at the start of every gathering in Haudenosaunee communities for generations.

exercises.prompt: The earth that sustains us
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohwén:tsia ne Ionkwarihó:ten — the earth that sustains us. Not the earth as a resource. Not the earth as property. The earth as the thing that sustains — actively, presently, continuously. This phrase has been spoken at the start of every gathering for as long as anyone can remember. It has been spoken while the earth was being taken by colonial governments. It was spoken during the Oka Crisis, when Kanien’kehá:ka people stood to defend their land. The earth is still sustaining. The words are still being said.

lessons.title: Waters, Plants, and Animals
lessons.sub: The middle sections of the Thanksgiving Address
intro: After acknowledging the people and the earth, the Thanksgiving Address moves to the waters, then the plants, then the animals. Each group is named and thanked for its specific gifts.
You already know vocabulary from all three of these categories — Ónhka (water), Onón:ta (corn), Ohkwá:ri (bear). This lesson connects that vocabulary to its place in the Address.
When you know the vocabulary the Address uses, hearing it becomes a different experience. You begin to understand what is being said, not just that something is being said.
lessons.ped: Address vocabulary as vocabulary review: Krashen (+1): known vocabulary (water, corn, bear) reappears in the new context of the Thanksgiving Address, deepening understanding without new cognitive load. Nation (2001): contextual reuse of known vocabulary. LaFever Spiritual domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi’nikonhraién:tase’ — it makes our minds happy / we give thanks — comes after each group is acknowledged. What does this phrase do in the Address?
exercises.correct: It closes each acknowledgement with a collective expression of gratitude — the minds of everyone gathered are made one in thanks
options: It closes each acknowledgement with collective gratitude
It is only said by the speaker
It means the section is complete only
It is a question asking if people are grateful
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ionkhi’nikonhraién:tase’ — our minds are made happy. Not just I give thanks, but we give thanks together. The Address is collective. Every person present is participating in the gratitude.

exercises.prompt: Match the being to its gift in the Thanksgiving Address

pairs.english: They quench thirst, carry life, and keep the world clean
They feed the people through the growing season
They give food, medicine, and material for living
They give wood, shelter, medicine, and clean the air

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Each being thanked in the Address gives something specific. The Address is precise — it does not thank ‘nature in general’, it thanks each being by what it does.

exercises.prompt: Onón:ta (corn) is one of the Three Sisters acknowledged in the Address. What makes the Three Sisters — corn, beans, squash — a specific group worth naming together?
exercises.correct: They grow together, support each other, and together provide complete nutrition — they are acknowledged as a unit because they work as a unit
options: They grow together, support each other, and provide complete nutrition as a unit
They are the only plants in Kanien’kehá:ka territory
They are named together only for convenience
Each is thanked separately in the Address
