exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! The Three Sisters are companion plants — corn provides a stalk for the beans to climb, beans fix nitrogen for the corn and squash, squash leaves shade the soil. They grow together and are complete together. The Address honours that relationship.

exercises.prompt: Complete the phrase of gratitude: Ionkhi’nikonhraién:___
exercises.correct: tase’
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ionkhi’nikonhraién:tase’ — it makes our minds happy. The phrase that closes each section of the Address. -tase’ is the habitual suffix on this complex form.

exercises.prompt: Say the phrase of gratitude aloud
exercises.meaning: It makes our minds happy / We give thanks
exercises.hint: ion-khi’-ni-kon-hraién:-ta-se’ — seven syllables. The stress falls on hraién:. Say it slowly. This phrase closes every section of the Address. You will learn to say it like breathing.

exercises.prompt: The animals
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohkwá:ri — bear. In the Thanksgiving Address, the animals are thanked for what they give. Not just food — though the bear gives that — but presence. The animals are our companions on the land. They were here before us. They will be here after. Some are our clan names. Some are our medicines. The bear is all of these things. When you say Ohkwá:ri in the context of the Address, you are not naming an animal. You are acknowledging a relative.

lessons.title: The Sky Beings
lessons.sub: Winds, Thunderers, sun, moon, and stars
intro: The upper sections of the Thanksgiving Address acknowledge the beings of the sky: the winds, the Thunderers, the sun, the moon, and the stars. You met all of these in Unit 14.
This lesson connects that sky vocabulary to its place in the Address and practises the full sky section as a sequence of acknowledgements.
By the end of this lesson you will be able to follow the sky section of the Address as it is spoken — recognising each being as it is named.
lessons.ped: Sky vocabulary in Address context: Nation (2001): known vocabulary (sky beings from Unit 14) reappears in the ceremonial frame of the Thanksgiving Address. This deepens retention and connects language to ceremony. LaFever Spiritual domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Match the sky being to what the Address thanks them for

pairs.english: Bringing rain and clearing harmful forces
Giving light and warmth to help living things grow
Guiding tides and cycles, regulating time
Lighting the night and guiding travellers

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four sky beings, four gifts. Each thanked for what they actually do. The Address is specific — not ‘thanks for everything’ but ‘thanks for this particular thing you do.

exercises.prompt: Ratiwéras — the Thunderers. In the Thanksgiving Address they are thanked by name. What does it mean that they have a specific name rather than just being called ‘thunder’?
exercises.correct: They are beings with a specific identity and role — naming them acknowledges who they are, not just what they do
options: They are beings with a specific identity and role
The name is a coincidence
All weather phenomena have names
Only humans are given specific names
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Ratiwéras is a name, not just a weather description. In Haudenosaunee understanding, the Thunderers are real beings who travel the sky. Naming them in the Address is acknowledging who is being thanked, not just what is happening.

exercises.prompt: Karáhkwa — the sun. You have known this word since Unit 3. What is different about knowing it in the context of the Thanksgiving Address?
exercises.correct: In the Address, Karáhkwa is not just a Ka-class noun — it is a being being thanked for specific gifts given every day
options: In the Address, Karáhkwa is a being being thanked for specific gifts given every day
Nothing is different
The Address uses a different word for sun
Karáhkwa is only used in the Address
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The same word — two contexts. In Unit 3, Karáhkwa is a Ka-class noun to learn. In the Address, Karáhkwa is a being to acknowledge and thank. Both are true. The word holds both.

exercises.prompt: Complete: Grandmother Moon — Tsí:te___
exercises.correct: wen
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsí:tewen — Grandmother Moon. Acute accent and long vowel on Tsí:, then tewen.

exercises.prompt: Say the Thunderers’ name aloud — with respect
exercises.hint: Ra-ti-WÉ-ras — four syllables. Stress on WÉ. This is a name. Say it with the weight a name deserves.

exercises.prompt: Grandmother Moon in the Address
exercises.storyPrompt: Tsí:tewen — Grandmother Moon. In the Thanksgiving Address she is thanked for guiding cycles, for lighting the night, for the rhythms she maintains that the living world depends on. Every month, every tide, every planting season carries her influence. She has been doing this work since before the language had a writing system. She will be doing it long after. The Address notices her work and says: we see you, we are grateful, Ionkhi’nikonhraién:tase’.

lessons.title: The Creator and Closing
lessons.sub: Shonkwaiá:tison — the Creator at the end of the Address
intro: The Thanksgiving Address closes by acknowledging the Creator — Shonkwaiá:tison. After all the beings of the earth, water, and sky have been thanked, the Creator is named last.

The order is significant. The Address moves from the immediate and earthly to the vast and spiritual — it arrives at the Creator after passing through everything that came from the Creator. The Creator is thanked for all of it.
The final phrase of the Address is the same closing you already know: Onen’ ki’ wáhi — that is all, I am finished.
lessons.ped: The Address as complete arc: This lesson closes the Thanksgiving Address sequence begun in Lesson 18. Learners who have followed the whole sequence now understand the Address as a whole — people to earth to water to plants to animals to sky to Creator. LaFever Spiritual domain primary. The closing with Onen’ ki’ wáhi connects ceremony to daily speech.

exercises.prompt: Shonkwaiá:tison — the Creator. The Address acknowledges the Creator last, after all the other beings. What does this order suggest?
exercises.correct: You move through all of creation before arriving at its source — the gratitude builds from the immediate to the vast
options: You move through all of creation before arriving at its source
The Creator is less important than the other beings
The order is random
The Creator is only acknowledged in some versions
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! The Address moves from the ground up — from the earth under your feet to the sky above to the Creator who made it all. By the time you reach Shonkwaiá:tison, you have already acknowledged everything that came from the Creator. The gratitude has been building to this.

exercises.prompt: Onen’ ki’ wáhi closes the Thanksgiving Address. You have said this at the end of every unit in this course. What does it feel like to say it now, knowing it is the closing phrase of the most important text in Kanien’kéha?
exercises.correct: The phrase carries more weight — knowing it closes the Thanksgiving Address connects everyday speech to ceremony
options: The phrase carries more weight — it connects everyday speech to ceremony
Nothing changes — it is just a phrase
The phrase means something different in the Address
Onen’ ki’ wáhi is only ceremonial
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onen’ ki’ wáhi is the same phrase whether it closes a unit in this app or a section of the Thanksgiving Address. Every time you have said it, you have been using ceremonial language. That connection was always there.

exercises.prompt: The full arc of the Address — match the section to what it acknowledges

pairs.english: Ohwén:tsia — the earth that sustains us

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! People → earth → waters → plants → animals → trees → birds → winds → thunderers → sun → moon → stars → Creator. The Address names everything. You now know many of the beings it names.

exercises.prompt: Write the Kanien’kéha word for the Creator
exercises.correct: Shonkwaiá:tison
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shonkwaiá:tison — the Creator. Shon-kwa-iá:-ti-son — five syllables. Stress on IÁ:.

exercises.prompt: Close the Address — say the final phrase aloud
exercises.meaning: That is all / I am finished
exercises.hint: O-nen’ (2) — ki’ (1) — WÁ-hi (2). You have said this many times. Now say it knowing it closes the most important oral text in Kanien’kéha. Let it land.

exercises.prompt: The Creator
exercises.storyPrompt: Shonkwaiá:tison — the Creator. After the earth, the waters, the plants, the animals, the birds, the winds, the thunderers, the sun, the moon, the stars — after all of that — the Creator. You have learned vocabulary from every section of the Thanksgiving Address across this unit. You have not learned the full Address — that takes years of listening and practice. But you have been introduced to the beings it names. And introduction is the beginning of relationship. Onen’ ki’ wáhi.

lessons.title: The Address in Daily Life
lessons.sub: When and where the Thanksgiving Address is spoken
intro: The Thanksgiving Address is not reserved for big ceremonies. It is spoken at the beginning of every gathering — school days, council meetings, community events, family gatherings.
In many Kanien’kehá:ka schools, children begin each day with the Address. In longhouse councils, nothing proceeds without it. In community events, the organiser or an elder opens with it.
This lesson explores when and how the Address is used in daily community life, and what it means for a language when its most important text is also its most everyday text.
lessons.ped: The Address as living daily practice: McCarty & Lee (2014): language must sustain community life, not just preserve ceremonial forms. The Address is both ceremonial and quotidian — this lesson shows both dimensions. LaFever Spiritual and Emotional domains.

exercises.prompt: In Haudenosaunee schools, children begin each day with the Thanksgiving Address. What does this practice do for language revitalization?
exercises.correct: It makes the most important ceremonial text part of daily life — children who hear and say it daily internalise both the language and the worldview it carries
options: It makes the most important text part of daily life
It is only a formality
It replaces regular schooling
It is required by the Canadian government
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Daily repetition of the Address is language immersion in the most meaningful possible content. Children who grow up saying the Address know the names of the beings it acknowledges, the structure of gratitude, and the vocabulary of the natural world — all at once.

exercises.prompt: Ionteriwahrónnion — school / place of learning. In Kanien’kehá:ka immersion schools, the day begins with the Thanksgiving Address spoken in Kanien’kéha. What kind of language learning is this?
exercises.correct: Immersion learning — the language is used as the medium of daily life, not just as a subject of study
options: Immersion learning — the language is used as the medium of daily life
Translation practice
Grammar drilling
Vocabulary memorisation only
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Immersion — the language is the environment, not the lesson. Starting each school day with the Address in Kanien’kéha means children are immersed in the language before any other lesson begins.

exercises.prompt: At kanonhséhsne — at the longhouse. Every gathering at the longhouse begins with the Thanksgiving Address. What does this tell you about the relationship between the Address and the longhouse?
exercises.correct: The Address and the longhouse are inseparable — you cannot have a longhouse gathering without the Address opening it
options: The Address and the longhouse are inseparable
The Address is optional at longhouse gatherings
Only the chief speaks the Address at the longhouse
The Address is only spoken at ceremonies, not council meetings
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! The longhouse and the Address are paired. Rotinonhsyón:ni — People of the Longhouse — open their longhouse with Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen. The building and the words go together.

exercises.prompt: Complete the name of the Thanksgiving Address: Ohén:ton ___
exercises.correct: Karihwatéhkwen
exercises.hint: K
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. You have said this name many times. It has become familiar. That is what happens when you say something every day.
exercises.wm: Please try again — the second word of the Address name begins with K.

exercises.prompt: The Address as daily practice
exercises.meaning: The words that come before all else
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — every day, at every gathering. In Kanien’kehá:ka schools, children who have been speaking this Address since they were four years old know it as well as they know their own name. They know the beings it names. They know the order. They know the feeling of saying Ionkhi’nikonhraién:tase’ at the end of each section. What would it mean to you to say the Thanksgiving Address every morning? What would it change in how you begin your day?

lessons.title: Unit 5 Review
lessons.sub: The Thanksgiving Address — from the opening to the closing
intro: This review lesson brings together all the Thanksgiving Address vocabulary from Unit 5: the opening acknowledgement of the people, the earth section, the waters, the plants and animals, the sky beings, and the Creator.

You do not know the full Address. Learning it in its entirety takes years of listening and practice with speakers who carry it. But you now know enough of its vocabulary to follow along when it is spoken.
That is what this unit has given you: not the Address itself, but the ability to hear it and recognise what is being named.
lessons.ped: Vocabulary recognition as the outcome: The goal of this unit is not memorisation of the full Address but recognition of its vocabulary and structure. Nation (2001): receptive knowledge (recognising words in context) is the first and most achievable learning goal. This review tests that recognition.

exercises.prompt: Match the Address vocabulary to its section

pairs.english: Earth — the earth that sustains us

exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! People → earth → sky → Creator. The arc of the Address in four steps.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi’nikonhraién:tase’ — it makes our minds happy. This phrase appears after each being is acknowledged. What does its repeated use do for the structure of the Address?
exercises.correct: It creates a rhythm and a collective pause — each section closes with shared gratitude before moving to the next
options: It creates a rhythm and collective pause between sections
It means the section was particularly important
It is said only once at the very end
It signals a change of speaker
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ionkhi’nikonhraién:tase’ is the rhythmic heartbeat of the Address — it closes each acknowledgement and gathers everyone’s minds together before the next one begins. Collective, shared, repeated.

exercises.prompt: You are listening to the Thanksgiving Address and you hear Karáhkwa. Which section are you in?
exercises.correct: The sky beings section — the sun is acknowledged after the Thunderers
options: The sky beings section — the sun comes after the Thunderers
The earth section
The opening
The closing
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! When you hear Karáhkwa, you are in the upper sky section. The Thunderers come first, then the sun, then Grandmother Moon, then the stars. You now know the order.

exercises.prompt: Complete the gratitude phrase: Ionkhi’nikonhraién:___
exercises.correct: tase’
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ionkhi’nikonhraién:tase’. You have seen and said this phrase many times now. It is becoming yours.

exercises.prompt: Say the full name and closing phrase of the Address
exercises.meaning: The words that come before all else — That is all
exercises.hint: Two phrases that bracket the whole Address. The opening name and the closing phrase. Say them together, with a pause between.

exercises.prompt: What the Address gives you
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen. You have now spent a whole unit with the Thanksgiving Address. You know the people, the earth, the waters, the plants, the animals, the sky beings, the Creator. You know the phrase that closes each section. You know the phrase that closes the whole thing. You do not know the full Address — that takes years of listening and practice. But you know enough to hear it and recognise what is being said. That recognition is a kind of belonging. You are inside the language now, a little further in. Onen’ ki’ wáhi.

title: Unit 6 — Animals, Shapes & Clans
desc: Shapes, wild animals, domestic animals, and the Mohawk clan system

lessons.title: Shapes
lessons.sub: The geometry of Kanien'kéha
intro: Kanien'kéha has names for shapes — and many of them are descriptive compounds using vocabulary you already know. Triangle is literally Áhsen nikahióhsake — 'three sides'. Diamond is Kaié:ri nikahióhsake — 'four sides'.
This demonstrates one of Kanien'kéha's most productive features: new vocabulary is built from existing roots. You do not need to memorise a new arbitrary word — you combine pieces you already know.
The lesson also reviews the number words Áhsen (three) and Kaié:ri (four) from the Numbers lesson, showing spaced repetition in action.
lessons.ped: Productive morphology: Mary McDonald explicitly highlights that shape vocabulary shows 'Mohawk is a productive language'. Productiveness — the ability to generate new words from known parts — is a key feature of language vitality (Mithun, 1999).

exercises.prompt: Áhsen nikahióhsake means 'triangle'. You know Áhsen from the Numbers lesson. What does Áhsen mean?
exercises.correct: Three
options: Three
Four
Five
Six
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Áhsen = three. Triangle = three sides. Shape names in Kanien'kéha describe the shape using number roots you already know.

exercises.prompt: Kaié:ri nikahióhsake means 'diamond'. What does Kaié:ri mean?
exercises.correct: Four
options: Three
Four
Five
Six
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kaié:ri = four. Diamond = four sides (in a rhombus). Knowing the numbers unlocks the shape names.

exercises.prompt: Match the shape names
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four shapes matched.

exercises.prompt: Teionteriwaién:te' (circle) uses the te- prefix you know from dual pronouns and dualic verbs. What does te- signal in circle?
exercises.correct: The te- prefix marks continuous/dual movement — a circle has no beginning or end
options: The te- prefix marks continuous/dual movement — a circle has no beginning or end
te- means the shape is large
te- is only for verbs
te- signals an O-class noun
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The te- prefix signals dualic or continuous action. A circle is a shape that continuously returns to its starting point — the prefix captures that quality.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'square'
exercises.hint: The word ends at te' — no suffix needed
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Teiotokerón:te' — square.
exercises.wm: The word is complete as written — Teiotokerón:te'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Star'?
exercises.correct: Otsísto
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Otsísto — star. The stars are the last beings acknowledged before the Creator in the Thanksgiving Address.

exercises.prompt: You have now seen Karáhkwa (sun) in three different lessons. Where else did it appear?
exercises.correct: In the Kanehsatà:ke place names lesson and as a shape in this lesson
options: In the Kanehsatà:ke place names lesson and as a shape in this lesson
Only in this lesson
In the kinship lesson
In the greetings lesson
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Karáhkwa has appeared as a place-name vocabulary word (Lesson 14), as a shape (this lesson), and in the Thanksgiving Address context. This is how vocabulary deepens — one word, many contexts.

exercises.prompt: Triangle and diamond are both named using number words + nikahióhsake (sides). What does this word-formation strategy tell you about Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: The language generates new words from existing parts — knowing roots unlocks vocabulary you have never explicitly learned
options: The language generates new words from existing parts — knowing roots unlocks vocabulary you have never explicitly learned
These are borrowed words from French
Shapes are too complex to name originally
Only shapes use this strategy
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is what Mary McDonald means by 'productive language'. Knowing Áhsen, Kaié:ri, and nikahióhsake gives you triangle and diamond without memorising arbitrary new words.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Four syllables: o-tsís-to. Stress on the second syllable (tsís). Short and clear.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Teionteriwaién:te' — circle. The circle is a recurring symbol in Haudenosaunee tradition: the circle of the Confederacy, the circular design of the longhouse, the cycle of ceremonies through the year. What circles exist in your life — relationships, routines, returns? What keeps coming back around?

lessons.title: Shapes in Context
lessons.sub: Using shape words to describe the world
intro: You learned the shape words in Lesson 19. This lesson puts them to work — describing the shapes of animals, natural objects, and cultural items.

Shape words in Kanien’kéha are often descriptive in origin. The word for circle — Teionteriwaién:te’ — describes something that is round and continuous. The word for star — Otsísto — is the same word used as a shape name.
This lesson connects shape vocabulary to the vocabulary you have built across the course.
lessons.ped: Shape vocabulary in context: Nation (2001): contextual reuse of known vocabulary (shapes) with new vocabulary (animals, objects) deepens retention. Physical domain: learners produce shape + noun description sentences.

exercises.prompt: Match the shape word to what it describes

pairs.english: Star shape — also the word for star in the sky
Sun shape — also the word for the sun

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Shape words and the natural things they describe. Otsísto is a star — the shape word and the sky word are the same. Karáhkwa is the sun — the shape and the being.

exercises.prompt: Teionteriwaién:te’ — circle. Wampum belts often have circular patterns. What does it mean to use a shape word to describe a ceremonial object?
exercises.correct: Shape vocabulary connects the everyday description of form to the ceremonial objects that carry specific meaning
options: Shape vocabulary connects everyday description to ceremonial objects
Shape words are only for geometry
Wampum belts are not described using shape words
The circle has no significance in Haudenosaunee design
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The circle in Haudenosaunee design often represents continuity, cycles, and wholeness. When you describe something as Teionteriwaién:te’, you are naming a shape and, in ceremonial context, evoking those meanings.

exercises.prompt: A’nó:wara — turtle. A turtle’s shell is covered in sections. Which shape word best describes the individual sections of a turtle shell?
exercises.correct: Ka’neróhkwes — rectangle / section shape
options: Ka’neróhkwes — rectangle / section shape
Teionteriwaién:te’ — circle
Otsísto — star
Áhsen nikahióhsake — triangle
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! The sections of a turtle shell are roughly rectangular or hexagonal. Ka’neróhkwes — rectangle. The turtle, the creation story, the shape of the shell — all connected through vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Complete: circle — Teionteriwaién:___
exercises.correct: te’
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Teionteriwaién:te’ — circle. The glottal stop closes the word.
exercises.wm: Please try again — ends in te’.

exercises.prompt: The star shape
exercises.storyPrompt: Otsísto — star. The same word names the shape and the being in the sky. In beadwork and design, the star pattern is one of the most beloved — eight points radiating from a centre, appearing on moccasins, on pouches, on bandolier bags. When someone makes an Otsísto in beadwork, they are making a star. The shape carries the sky into the hands. What shape do you see most often in the Kanien’kehá:ka designs you have encountered?

lessons.title: Wild Animals
lessons.sub: The animals that share our territory
intro: Wild animals are central to Kanien'kehá:ka life — in traditional diet, in the clan system, in ceremony, and in oral tradition. Bear (Ohkwá:ri), Turtle (A'nó:wara), and Wolf (Okwáho) are the three founding clans of the Kanien'kehá:ka.

Recognising the clan animals in Kanien'kéha is politically and socially important: when you know which clan someone belongs to, you understand their kinship obligations, their ceremonial roles, and their relational position in the community.
This lesson draws from Lesson 22 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum. The bear Ohkwá:ri is also the main character in the Tóta tánon Ohkwá:ri puppet series produced in Kahnawà:ke.
lessons.ped: Animals and clan identity: Presenting wild animals in the context of the clan system follows Freire's (1970) principle of generative themes — the animals are not vocabulary items but social identifiers. Learning Ohkwá:ri (bear) also means learning who the Bear Clan people are.

exercises.prompt: What does Ohkwá:ri mean?
exercises.correct: Bear
options: Bear
Wolf
Turtle
Beaver
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri — bear. The Bear Clan (Rotihskeré:wake) takes its name from this animal. Ohkwá:ri also appears in the Tóta tánon Ohkwá:ri puppet series from Kahnawà:ke.

exercises.prompt: A'nó:wara means turtle. In Haudenosaunee creation story, what role does the turtle play?
exercises.correct: North America (Turtle Island) is formed on the back of a great turtle
options: North America (Turtle Island) is formed on the back of a great turtle
The turtle carries the sun across the sky
The turtle is the fastest animal
The turtle guards the longhouse
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Turtle Island — the Haudenosaunee name for North America — rests on the back of a great turtle. A'nó:wara is not just a vocabulary word but a cosmological reference.

exercises.prompt: Match the three clan animals to their Kanien'kéha names
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The three founding clans of the Kanien'kehá:ka: Bear, Turtle, Wolf.

exercises.prompt: Match the other wild animals
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Beaver, rabbit, deer, moose — animals of the Kanehsatà:ke territory.

exercises.prompt: Tsaní:to (beaver) was historically important to Kanien'kehá:ka political life. Why?
exercises.correct: The fur trade in beaver pelts dramatically affected Kanien'kehá:ka alliances and territory in the 17th–18th centuries
options: The fur trade in beaver pelts dramatically affected Kanien'kehá:ka alliances and territory in the 17th–18th centuries
Beavers were pets
Beavers were used in ceremony only
Beaver is only eaten in winter
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The beaver pelt trade was a primary driver of the Beaver Wars of the 17th century, in which Haudenosaunee nations competed for access to trade networks. Language, animals, and history are inseparable here.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Bear'?
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ohkwá:ri. Three syllables: oh-KWÁ-ri.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'wolf'
exercises.correct: ho
exercises.hint: h
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Okwáho — wolf. The Wolf Clan (Ronathahí:non) are associated with pathfinding.

exercises.prompt: Ahwénsion means porcupine. Before European contact, what were porcupine quills used for?
exercises.correct: Traditional quillwork — an art form predating European glass beads
options: Traditional quillwork — an art form predating European glass beads
Making weapons
Only food
Ceremonial drums
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Porcupine quillwork is one of the oldest Haudenosaunee art traditions. When Europeans arrived with glass beads, quillwork gradually shifted to beadwork — though both traditions continue.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: oh-KWÁ-ri. The stress falls on the second syllable. The long vowel (:) after kwá is slightly extended.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohkwá:ri — bear. One of the three founding clan animals. In Haudenosaunee tradition, knowing someone's clan means knowing your relationship to them — you cannot marry within your clan, and you have specific ceremonial obligations to your clan members. If you were to belong to a clan named after an animal, what animal would it be, and why? What qualities of that animal do you carry?

lessons.title: More Wild Animals
lessons.sub: Extending the wild animal vocabulary beyond the clan animals
intro: Unit 6 introduced four wild animals in Lesson 20. The glossary has many more — foxes, skunks, muskrats, chipmunks, squirrels, raccoons. These are the animals of the Kanien’kehá:ka landscape, each with their own name.
Wild animals in Kanien’kéha are not just vocabulary items. They are beings acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address, present in oral tradition, and part of the living world the language names.
This lesson extends your wild animal vocabulary with animals that appear in everyday observation and in the stories.
lessons.ped: Extended animal vocabulary: Nation (2001): semantic set vocabulary is best learned in multiple encounters. This lesson extends the animal set from Lesson 20 and provides new context for known animals. LaFever Spiritual domain: animals as beings, not objects.

exercises.prompt: Match the animal to its name
exercises.sub: Animals of the Kanien’kehá:ka landscape
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Skunk, fox, muskrat, chipmunk — four animals of the woodland and waterway. Each has its name in Kanien’kéha.

exercises.prompt: Four more animals
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Squirrel, raccoon, rabbit, deer. The full landscape of animals the Kanien’kehá:ka have lived alongside for generations.

exercises.prompt: Oskenhón:ton — deer. Deer appear in the Thanksgiving Address as part of the animal section. Beyond food, what role do deer play in Kanien’kehá:ka material culture?
exercises.correct: Deer hide is used for clothing, moccasins, drums, and other important objects — the whole animal is used with respect
options: Deer hide is used for clothing, moccasins, drums, and other objects
Only deer meat is used
Deer are not used in material culture
Deer are only in the stories
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The deer gives hide for clothing and moccasins, sinew for thread, bone for tools, and meat for food. In the Thanksgiving Address, the animals are thanked for all of this. Oskenhón:ton is not just vocabulary — it names a relationship.

exercises.prompt: Complete: fox — Tsinón:___
exercises.correct: we
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsinón:we — fox. The fox appears in stories as a clever and sometimes tricky character.

exercises.prompt: Say this animal name aloud
exercises.hint: a-TÍ-hron — three syllables. Stress on TÍ. The raccoon is a familiar presence at the edge of the forest and the water.

exercises.prompt: The muskrat
exercises.storyPrompt: Tanón:kereh — muskrat. In the Haudenosaunee creation story, it was the muskrat who dove to the bottom of the water and brought up the mud that Earth Woman built the world from. Every other animal had tried and failed. The muskrat was the smallest and the last. And it was the muskrat who succeeded. The smallest creature in the story becomes the one who made the earth. Tanón:kereh.

lessons.title: Domestic Animals
lessons.sub: Animals that arrived with contact
intro: When European settlers arrived in North America, they brought animals the Kanien'kehá:ka had never seen: horses, cows, sheep, pigs. Rather than borrowing European names, Kanien'kéha created new words using its own word-formation system.

Akohsá:tens (horse) is related to 'it carries on its back'. Teiotina'karontón:'a (sheep) is 'it has wool on its back'. These descriptive names demonstrate that the language did not need to borrow — it described.
This is exactly what Mary McDonald means when she says Kanien'kéha is a 'productive and creative language' in her Lesson 23 introduction.
lessons.ped: Language contact and creativity: García & Li Wei (2014) note that all living languages encounter new words and adapt. Kanien'kéha's strategy of descriptive naming (rather than borrowing) shows a language with strong internal generative capacity — a mark of vitality.

exercises.prompt: Akohsá:tens (horse) is related to 'it carries on its back'. What does this tell you about how Kanien'kéha names new animals?
exercises.correct: New animals are named by describing their defining function or appearance — not by borrowing foreign words
options: New animals are named by describing their defining function or appearance — not by borrowing foreign words
The word was borrowed from French
All animal names come from clan animals
Horses had Kanien'kéha names before contact
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Descriptive naming: horse = 'the thing that carries on its back', sheep = 'the thing with wool on its back'. The language generates new vocabulary from within its own system.

exercises.prompt: What does Takós mean?
exercises.correct: Cat
options: Cat
Dog
Horse
Cow
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Takós — cat.

exercises.prompt: Match the domestic animals
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Four domestic animals — all named using Kanien'kéha's own word-formation rules.

exercises.prompt: Teiotina'karontón:'a (sheep) means 'it has wool on its back'. What other animal name uses the same descriptive strategy?
exercises.sub: Think about what Akohsá:tens means
exercises.correct: Akohsá:tens (horse = it carries on its back)
options: Akohsá:tens (horse = it carries on its back)
Takós (cat)
Ohkwá:ri (bear)
A'nó:wara (turtle)
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Both sheep and horse are named by describing a physical characteristic or function. Descriptive compound naming is the consistent strategy for post-contact animals.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Cat'?
exercises.correct: Takós
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Takós — one of the shorter domestic animal names.

exercises.prompt: Tiohnhónhskwaron (cow) contains the root 'hónhskwar'. The bull is Tiohnhónhskwaron rá:tsi'n. What does rá:tsi'n likely indicate?
exercises.correct: It marks the male form — rá:tsi'n is a masculine suffix/modifier
options: It marks the male form — rá:tsi'n is a masculine suffix/modifier
It means 'large'
It means 'old'
It is a separate unrelated word
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! rá:tsi'n marks the male form of the animal. This parallels the Ra-/I- prefix system you know from person nouns — Kanien'kéha consistently marks gender.

exercises.prompt: Kanien'kéha responded to European contact by creating new words from within its own grammatical system rather than borrowing. How does this compare to what English did with new concepts?
exercises.correct: English typically borrows words from other languages (e.g. 'potato' from Taíno, 'chocolate' from Nahuatl); Kanien'kéha describes new things using existing roots
options: English typically borrows words from other languages (e.g. 'potato' from Taíno, 'chocolate' from Nahuatl); Kanien'kéha describes new things using existing roots
Both languages do exactly the same thing
Kanien'kéha borrows more than English
There is no difference
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Different languages have different strategies for new vocabulary. Kanien'kéha's descriptive compounding strategy reflects a different philosophy of naming — you describe what something is, rather than adopting a foreign sound.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: a-koh-SÁ-tens. Stress on the third syllable. The colon (:) after sá extends the vowel slightly.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Akohsá:tens — horse — named for what it does: it carries. When Europeans arrived with horses, the Kanien'kehá:ka did not call them by a European name. They described what the animal does. Think of something new in your own life that you have had to find a name for. How did you name it — by borrowing someone else's word, or by describing what it is?

lessons.title: Birds as Clan Symbols and Ceremonial Beings
lessons.sub: Birds beyond the sky — clan identity and ceremony
intro: You learned eight birds in Lesson 33. This lesson connects the bird vocabulary to clan identity and ceremony. Some birds are clan symbols beyond the Kanien’kehá:ka — the Snipe Clan and the Eel Clan both take their names from specific beings of the land and water.
Birds are also acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address — their songs are said to please us daily, and they are thanked for that gift.
Knowing birds by name in Kanien’kéha means knowing more than vocabulary. It means knowing who has been singing over this territory since before the language was written.
lessons.ped: Birds in ceremony and clan: Bird vocabulary connects to clan identity (Snipe Clan) and to the Thanksgiving Address. This deepens the cultural grounding of what might otherwise be seen as simple vocabulary. LaFever Spiritual domain.

exercises.prompt: Roti’nehí:io — Snipe Clan. The Snipe (Tkaréhton) is one of the clan animals of the broader Haudenosaunee Confederacy. What does having a bird as a clan animal suggest?
exercises.correct: Birds are as central to Haudenosaunee identity as the wolf, bear, and turtle — the living world organises the social world
options: Birds are central to identity as well as land animals
Only large animals can be clan symbols
The Snipe Clan is from a different culture
Clan animals are chosen arbitrarily
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! Clans are named after the beings of the land and water and sky. The Snipe flies the wetlands and shorelines of Kanien’kehá:ka territory. The people who carry its name carry a relationship to those places.

exercises.prompt: Match the bird to its name
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Eagle, goose, hawk, horned owl. Four birds from the Thanksgiving Address’s bird section.

exercises.prompt: Á:kwe’ks — eagle. The eagle sits at the top of the Great Tree of Peace in Haudenosaunee tradition. What is the eagle’s role there?
exercises.correct: The eagle watches from above for any threat to the peace — it is a sentinel, not just a symbol
options: The eagle watches for threats to the peace — a sentinel
The eagle is only decorative
The eagle represents war
Eagles are not part of the Confederacy’s symbolism
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The eagle perches at the top of the Great Tree of Peace and watches in all four directions. If it sees a threat to the Confederacy, it cries out a warning. Á:kwe’ks is not just a bird. It is a guardian.

exercises.prompt: More birds of the territory
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Robin, chickadee, crow, woodpecker. Birds you can hear and see in Kanien’kehá:ka territory today.

exercises.prompt: The geese return
exercises.storyPrompt: Káhonk — goose. The return of the Canada Geese in spring is one of the first signs that the land is waking. The Thanksgiving Address thanks the birds for the pleasure their songs and presence give to the people daily. When the geese fly over in V-formation, calling — that is a moment the Address was made for. Káhonk. They are back. It is spring. The world is continuing.

lessons.title: The Clan System
lessons.sub: Who we are to each other
intro: The Kanien'kehá:ka have three original clans: Bear (Rotihskeré:wake), Turtle (Rotiniáhton), and Wolf (Ronathahí:non). Clan membership is matrilineal — you belong to your mother's clan forever.

At Kanehsatà:ke specifically, two additional clans are present: Snipe (Roti'nehsí:io) and Eel (Rotineniotthró:non), both originally Onondaga clans. Their presence reflects the 1806 resettlement of Oswegatchie Onondaga people at Akwesasne, followed by intermarriage and migration.
The clan system governs who you can marry (never within your own clan), who attends your ceremonies, and who speaks at your funeral. The language and the social structure are inseparable.
lessons.ped: Clans as social grammar: McCarty & Lee (2014) argue that language learning must honour cultural institutions. The clan system is not background knowledge — it is the social structure that the language serves. Teaching clan names without this context produces vocabulary without meaning.

exercises.prompt: What does Rotihskeré:wake mean?
exercises.correct: Bear Clan
options: Bear Clan
Turtle Clan
Wolf Clan
Snipe Clan
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Rotihskeré:wake — Bear Clan. The Bear Clan is associated with healing in oral tradition.

exercises.prompt: Match each clan name to its animal
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The three founding Mohawk clans. You can verify your own clan by asking your mother's family.

exercises.prompt: Roti'nehsí:io (Snipe Clan) is present at Kanehsatà:ke. Why?
exercises.correct: Oswegatchie Onondaga people resettled at Akwesasne in 1806 after being driven out by US troops; intermarriage brought these clans to Kanehsatà:ke
options: Oswegatchie Onondaga people resettled at Akwesasne in 1806 after being driven out by US troops; intermarriage brought these clans to Kanehsatà:ke
Snipe Clan was always a Mohawk clan
The clan system is the same at all communities
Snipe Clan members moved to avoid the Bear Clan
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The Kontinónhstats curriculum explicitly explains this history. The presence of Snipe and Eel Clans at Kanehsatà:ke is not an irregularity — it is evidence of the community's specific history of displacement, resettlement, and kinship.

exercises.prompt: Clan membership is matrilineal. You belong to your mother's clan. What does this mean for the Kanien'kehá:ka kinship system you studied earlier?
exercises.correct: Your clan connects directly to the matrilineal kinship nouns — ista (mother), tota (grandmother) — because clan passes through the female line
options: Your clan connects directly to the matrilineal kinship nouns — ista (mother), tota (grandmother) — because clan passes through the female line
Clan membership is separate from kinship vocabulary
Fathers determine clan
Only elders know their clan
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The entire vocabulary you learned in Unit 2 (kinship nouns, matrilineal family structure) connects directly to the clan system. The language is a map of the social structure.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Turtle Clan'?
exercises.correct: Rotiniáhton
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Rotiniáhton — Turtle Clan. The Turtle Clan carries leadership responsibilities in Haudenosaunee governance.

exercises.prompt: Complete the Wolf Clan name
exercises.correct: non
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ronathahí:non — Wolf Clan. The Wolf Clan are the pathfinders, those who know the way.

exercises.prompt: 'You cannot marry within your own clan' is a fundamental rule of Haudenosaunee society. How is this reflected in the kinship vocabulary you learned?
exercises.correct: Kinship terms mark every relationship — knowing your clan tells you which people are prohibited as marriage partners and which ceremonial roles you hold
options: Kinship terms mark every relationship — knowing your clan tells you which people are prohibited as marriage partners and which ceremonial roles you hold
Marriage rules are unrelated to language
The kinship vocabulary only applies to blood relatives
Only elders must follow clan rules
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The kinship vocabulary from Unit 2 and the clan system from this lesson are the same social structure viewed from two angles. Language is social architecture.

exercises.prompt: Say this clan name aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: ro-tih-ske-RÉ-wake. Stress on the fourth syllable. A long, ceremonial-sounding word.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: The Kanien'kehá:ka have three founding clans — Bear, Turtle, Wolf. Belonging to a clan means belonging to a network of people across all Haudenosaunee communities, with specific rights and responsibilities. Many people learning this language are also discovering (or recovering) their own clan identity. Do you know your clan, or do you know where you might find out? What would it mean to know?

lessons.title: The Clan System in Practice
lessons.sub: How the clans organise daily and ceremonial life
intro: You learned the Kanien’kehá:ka clan names in Lesson 22. This lesson explores what those clans actually mean in daily life: how they organise seating in the longhouse, how they determine marriage, how they distribute political responsibility.
The clan system is not a historical curiosity. It is a living governance and social structure that Kanien’kehá:ka people navigate daily.
Understanding the clan system is part of understanding the language — because the language is built to express it.
lessons.ped: Clan system as social grammar: McCarty & Lee (2014): language must carry cultural institutions, not just vocabulary. The clan system is the social institution the kinship vocabulary is built to express. This lesson makes that explicit. LaFever Spiritual and Emotional domains.

exercises.prompt: Rotiniáhton — Turtle Clan. The Turtle Clan is the keeper of the earth in Haudenosaunee tradition — the turtle whose back Sky Woman landed on. What does it mean for a clan to be connected to the creation story?
exercises.correct: The clan carries the identity of that being from before the world existed — Turtle Clan people are the descendants of the original relationship with the turtle
options: The clan carries identity from the creation story
It is a historical title only
Turtle Clan members are not connected to real turtles
The story is unrelated to the clan
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! The turtle in the creation story is the foundation of the world. The Turtle Clan carries that. When you meet a Turtle Clan person, you are meeting someone whose clan identity reaches back to the beginning of the world as Haudenosaunee people understand it.

exercises.prompt: In the longhouse, the three clans are arranged on specific sides. Bear and Turtle face Wolf and other clans across the fire. Why does this seating arrangement matter?
exercises.correct: It reflects the clan moiety system — clans are divided into two groups that balance each other in governance and ceremony
options: It reflects the clan moiety system — two groups that balance each other
It is for convenience only
The arrangement changes every gathering
Only chiefs have assigned positions
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The longhouse has two sides facing a central fire. The moiety system — two groups of clans — is reflected in this arrangement. Each side supports and checks the other. Balance is built into the architecture.

exercises.prompt: Ronathahí:non — Wolf Clan. The Wolf Clan is responsible for keeping peace between nations in Haudenosaunee tradition. How does this connect to the Great Law?
exercises.correct: The Great Law distributes specific responsibilities to specific clans — the Wolf Clan’s role in peacemaking is built into the law
options: The Great Law distributes responsibilities to clans
It is a ceremonial role only
Wolf Clan has no political role
All clans have identical responsibilities
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ronathahí:non — Wolf Clan — carries the responsibility of the path-clearer and peacemaker between nations. The Great Law is specific about which clan carries which responsibility. This is governance, not symbolism.

exercises.prompt: Complete: Bear Clan — Rotihskeré:___
exercises.correct: wake
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Rotihskeré:wake — Bear Clan. The Bear Clan is the keeper of medicine and health in Kanien’kehá:ka society.

exercises.prompt: Bear Clan
exercises.storyPrompt: Rotihskeré:wake — Bear Clan. The Bear Clan keeps the medicine knowledge. In Kanien’kehá:ka tradition, the bear knows where the medicine plants grow. The people of the Bear Clan carry that knowledge — healing traditions, plant medicines, the care of the sick. When colonial governments and residential schools disrupted the transmission of that knowledge, Bear Clan families worked to preserve it. The name itself — Rotihskeré:wake — names a responsibility that has always existed and still exists.

lessons.title: Unit 6 Review
lessons.sub: Animals, shapes, and clans — the whole unit together
intro: This review lesson brings together the animal, shape, and clan vocabulary from Unit 6 in new combinations.

You know wild animals, domestic animals, birds, shapes, and the clan system. This lesson asks you to connect them: the bear and its clan, the shape of the turtle’s shell, the eagle at the top of the Great Tree.
Every connection you can make is a deeper understanding of the language and the world it names.
lessons.ped: Cross-category consolidation: Nation (2001): review combining multiple semantic categories produces deeper retention. This lesson creates animal-clan, animal-shape, and shape-ceremony connections.

exercises.prompt: Match the clan animal to its clan name

pairs.english: Guardian of the Great Tree of Peace

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Animal + clan. The animals are not separate from the people who carry their names. Bear Clan carries the bear’s role. Turtle Clan carries the turtle’s foundation. Wolf Clan carries the wolf’s peacemaking.

exercises.prompt: A’nó:wara Rotiniáhton — the Turtle, the Turtle Clan. In the creation story the turtle carries the world. In the clan system the Turtle Clan carries which responsibility?
exercises.correct: The Turtle Clan is often associated with the keeper of the earth and the natural world
options: The Turtle Clan keeps the earth and natural world
The Turtle Clan keeps medicine
The Turtle Clan keeps peace
The Turtle Clan keeps the stories
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraien:ta’ne! The Turtle is the foundation of the world in the creation story. The Turtle Clan carries that relationship with the earth. The animal and the clan and the story are one continuous thing.

exercises.prompt: Otsísto — star shape and sky star. Ratiwéras — the Thunderers. Both appear in the sky. The Thanksgiving Address thanks both. What do they have in common in the Address?
exercises.correct: Both are sky beings thanked for specific gifts they give — the stars for light and navigation, the Thunderers for rain
options: Both are sky beings thanked for specific gifts
They are the same being
Neither is acknowledged in the Address
Only the Thunderers are sky beings
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Stars and Thunderers — different sky beings, different gifts, both acknowledged. The Address is specific about each one. You know the vocabulary for both now.

exercises.prompt: Complete: bear — Ohkwá:___
exercises.correct: ri
exercises.hint: r
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri — bear. The Bear Clan, the medicine keeper, the large dark animal at the river.

exercises.prompt: Three clans, one people
exercises.meaning: Turtle Clan — Bear Clan — Wolf Clan
exercises.storyPrompt: Rotiniáhton, Rotihskeré:wake, Ronathahí:non — Turtle, Bear, Wolf. Three clans, one people. The earth-keeper, the medicine-keeper, the peace-keeper. Each clan carries something the others need. Together they make a complete community. When the three clans sit in the longhouse — the turtle and bear on one side, the wolf on the other — they are not competing. They are completing each other. That is what the clan system is. Onen’ ki’ wáhi.

title: Unit 7 — Food, Land & Ceremony
desc: Fruits, traditional foods, cultural items, household vocabulary, nature & seasons, and cultural heroes

lessons.title: Fruits & Traditional Foods
lessons.sub: What the land gives us
intro: Kanien'kéha has rich vocabulary for the fruits of the land. Many of these words appear in the Thanksgiving Address — the berries, plants, and fruits of the earth are acknowledged as gifts.
The Strawberry (Ken' niiohontésha') holds particular ceremonial importance as the first fruit of spring — the Strawberry Ceremony marks the return of sweetness to the world after winter.

The Three Sisters — corn (Onón:ta), beans (Ohsahéta'), and squash (Otá:ra) — are the agricultural foundation of Haudenosaunee life. They are companion plants: grown together, they sustain each other and the people.
lessons.ped: Food as cultural knowledge: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) emphasise that language revitalization must connect to land-based knowledge. Food vocabulary is not neutral — it maps the relationship between the people and their territory.

exercises.prompt: What does Ken' niiohontésha' mean?
exercises.correct: Strawberries
options: Strawberries
Cranberries
Blueberries
Cherries
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ken' niiohontésha' — strawberries. The first fruit of spring, celebrated in the Strawberry Ceremony.

exercises.prompt: Onón:ta means corn. What are the Three Sisters?
exercises.correct: Corn (Onón:ta), beans (Ohsahéta'), and squash (Otá:ra) — the three companion crops of Haudenosaunee agriculture
options: Corn (Onón:ta), beans (Ohsahéta'), and squash (Otá:ra) — the three companion crops of Haudenosaunee agriculture
Corn, wheat, and rice
Potatoes, carrots, and onions
Strawberries, blueberries, and cranberries
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The Three Sisters are companion crops: corn provides the trellis for beans, beans fix nitrogen in the soil, and squash shades the ground to retain moisture. They are grown together and named together.

exercises.prompt: Match the fruits
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Four fruits of the Kanehsatà:ke territory.

exercises.prompt: Match the Three Sisters
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Onón:ta, Ohsahéta', Otá:ra — the Three Sisters. Grow them together and name them together.

exercises.prompt: Ohsi'tá:na means apples. The apple orchards at Kanehsatà:ke were historically significant. Why?
exercises.correct: The 1990 Oka Crisis partly involved the threatened destruction of orchards and a sacred pine grove for a golf course expansion
options: The 1990 Oka Crisis partly involved the threatened destruction of orchards and a sacred pine grove for a golf course expansion
Apples were the most traded food
Apple orchards were introduced in the 1990s
Apples are not significant at Kanehsatà:ke
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The land that community defended in 1990 included orchards, a sacred pine grove, and a traditional burial ground. The word Ohsi'tá:na connects to that history.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Strawberries'?
exercises.correct: Ken' niiohontésha'
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ken' niiohontésha'. A beautiful and phonetically rich word for the first fruit of spring.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Try: Ken' niiohontésha'

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'corn'
exercises.correct: ta
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta — corn. The most sacred of the Three Sisters.

exercises.prompt: Ohsahéta' (beans), Onón:ta (corn), and Otá:ra (squash) are companion plants. How does growing them together mirror the Haudenosaunee social philosophy you have learned?
exercises.correct: Interdependence — different members of a community support each other, just as the Three Sisters support each other in the garden
options: Interdependence — different members of a community support each other, just as the Three Sisters support each other in the garden
They are grown together only for convenience
The connection to social philosophy is metaphorical only
Only one of the three is important
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Three Sisters are a living metaphor for confederacy — different nations, different strengths, working together. The clan system, the Great Law, and the Three Sisters are all expressions of the same underlying value.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: o-NÓN-ta. Stress on the second syllable. The long vowel (:) after nón is held slightly.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Ken' niiohontésha' — strawberries, the first sweetness after winter. The Strawberry Ceremony marks their return. Think of something in your life that returns each year and signals renewal — a season, a food, a person, an activity. What does its return mean to you? What would you give thanks for when it arrives?

lessons.title: Foods of the Home
lessons.sub: What we eat, and what it carries
intro: Beyond the Three Sisters and fruits, Kanien'kéha vocabulary covers the broader range of foods in modern Kanien'kehá:ka households. This lesson covers everyday food nouns from Lesson 30 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum.
Several words here demonstrate noun compounding in action: Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron (bear meat) combines Ohkwá:ri (bear, from the wild animals lesson) with o'wá:ron (meat) — you already know both halves.
Food vocabulary in Kanien'kéha also reflects the history of the community — some words are ancient, others adapted to post-contact ingredients.
lessons.ped: Compounding as review: Presenting Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron (bear meat) as a compound draws on vocabulary from two previous lessons (animals and food). This is spaced retrieval practice — encountering known words in new combinations strengthens long-term memory (Nation, 2001).

exercises.prompt: What does Ohnenná:ta mean?
exercises.correct: Potatoes
options: Potatoes
Corn
Meat
Bread
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohnenná:ta — potatoes. An important staple in the modern Kanien'kehá:ka household.

exercises.prompt: Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron means 'bear meat'. You already know both words separately. What is o'wá:ron?
exercises.correct: Meat (general term)
options: Meat (general term)
Bear
Water
Corn
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! o'wá:ron = meat. Ohkwá:ri = bear. Together: bear meat. Kanien'kéha compounds nouns by placing them side by side — the same strategy you saw in shape names.

exercises.prompt: Match the food words
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Everyday food vocabulary for the home.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for 'meat'
exercises.correct: ron
exercises.hint: r
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! O'wá:ron — meat. The glottal stop (') at the beginning is part of the word.

exercises.prompt: Kanà:taros means bread. Corn bread is Onón:ta kanà:taros. Based on this, what does the compound Onón:ta kanà:taros literally describe?
exercises.correct: Corn + bread = corn bread (a direct compound of the two noun roots)
options: Corn + bread = corn bread (a direct compound of the two noun roots)
A special ceremony name
A new word with no connection to the parts
A verb phrase
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is the same compounding strategy as bear meat (Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron). Kanien'kéha builds compound nouns by placing nouns side by side. Knowing the parts gives you the compound.

exercises.prompt: You learned corn in the previous lesson. How do you say it?
exercises.sub: Spaced review
exercises.correct: Onón:ta
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Onón:ta — corn. The most sacred of the Three Sisters.

exercises.prompt: O'wá:ron (meat) in traditional diet primarily referred to game — deer, moose, bear. How does knowing the wild animal vocabulary from the previous unit connect to food vocabulary?
exercises.correct: Wild animal names + o'wá:ron (meat) generate compound food words: Oskenhón:ton o'wá:ron (deer meat), Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron (bear meat)
options: Wild animal names + o'wá:ron (meat) generate compound food words: Oskenhón:ton o'wá:ron (deer meat), Ohkwá:ri o'wá:ron (bear meat)
Animal names and food names are unrelated
Only bear meat has a Kanien'kéha name
Food vocabulary is a separate system
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! This is the power of systematic vocabulary learning — once you know the pattern (animal + o'wá:ron = that animal's meat), you can generate new compound words yourself.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Four syllables: oh-nen-NÁ-ta. Stress on the third syllable. The long vowel (:) before 'ta' is important.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Onón:ta — corn. Sacred, central, sustaining. The Haudenosaunee Green Corn Ceremony gives thanks for the corn harvest. Think about a food that is central to your family or community — not just nutritionally but in terms of memory, identity, gathering. What would you give thanks for? What is your Onón:ta?

lessons.title: Food and Community
lessons.sub: The language of sharing food and eating together
intro: Food in Kanien'kehá:ka life is relationship. Feasts accompany ceremonies. Meals mark gatherings. Eating together is itself a form of community.
This lesson brings the food vocabulary from Lessons 23–25 into the context of sharing: offering food, accepting, and appreciating what you have been given.
You already know the key food verbs from Unit 13. This lesson adds the social language around them.
lessons.ped: Food as relational practice: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): food vocabulary carries land-based and relational knowledge. LaFever Emotional domain: sharing food as relationship.

exercises.prompt: Match the food exchange phrase to its meaning

pairs.english: Yes, I like the taste of ___

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The full arc of a food exchange — hungry, eaten, like it. These are the phrases of every shared meal.

exercises.prompt: Iawekon ken? — Is it delicious? You have just been given food by an elder. What is the most appropriate response?
exercises.correct: Hen', yawékon! — Yes, it is delicious!
options: Hen', yawékon! — Yes, it is delicious!
Iáh tewakekas — I don't like it
I have already eaten
Nothing — silence is respectful
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Hen', yawékon — yes, it is delicious. Expressing genuine appreciation for food honours the person who gave it.

exercises.prompt: Onón:ta — corn. In the Thanksgiving Address the Three Sisters are thanked. How does eating the corn connect to that gratitude?
exercises.correct: Eating receives the gift the Address thanks — the ceremony names what the meal contains
options: Eating receives the gift the Address thanks
Eating is unrelated to the Address
The Address is only about ceremony
Food is thanked separately
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Address thanks the corn, the beans, the squash. When you eat them, you receive what was thanked. The meal and the ceremony are not separate.

exercises.prompt: Delicious
exercises.storyPrompt: Yawékon — it is delicious. Three syllables that hold a whole experience: corn soup, the first strawberries of the season, something made by someone who loves you. In Kanien'kéha, you say yawékon and the person who cooked knows that what they gave was received. That is the whole loop of food and relationship, in three syllables.

lessons.title: Household Vocabulary
lessons.sub: The language of everyday home life
intro: Lesson 32 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum covers household items — the objects of daily domestic life. Like domestic animals, many household items arrived with European contact, and Kanien'kéha named them from within its own grammatical system.
Several of these words connect to the body part vocabulary you learned earlier: soap connects to washing, towel to the 'washing face' verb, bed to resting.
Knowing household vocabulary is essential for using Kanien'kéha in daily life — one of the goals of the Kontinónhstats curriculum is to make the language present in everyday domestic conversation.
lessons.ped: Domain expansion: García & Li Wei (2014) identify domestic and household domains as critical for language vitality. When Kanien'kéha can be spoken in the kitchen and bathroom as well as in ceremony, it is truly a language of daily life.

exercises.prompt: What does Atsihnhétste' mean?
exercises.correct: Soap
options: Soap
Bed
Towel
Shampoo
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Atsihnhétste' — soap. Essential for the 'washing hands' and 'washing face' verbs you learned from the A-stem lesson.

exercises.prompt: What does Ohkwáriyentha' mean?
exercises.correct: Bed
options: Bed
Chair
Table
Sofa
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ohkwáriyentha' — bed. The -yentha' locative suffix marks 'the place where one does something'. Compare ani'tskwá:ra (chair) from the A-class nouns lesson.

exercises.prompt: Match the household items
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Three household items — each connected to a daily routine.

exercises.prompt: Atsihnhétste' (soap) connects to two A-stem verbs you already learned. Which ones?
exercises.sub: Think about what soap is used for
exercises.correct: Washing hands (sato'nnenhstáhkwa's) and washing face — both body-care verbs from the A-stem lesson
options: Washing hands (sato'nnenhstáhkwa's) and washing face — both body-care verbs from the A-stem lesson
Sitting and running
Listening and looking
Coughing and sweeping
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Household objects connect to the verbs that use them. Soap → washing hands/face. This is how vocabulary networks build — each word connects to others you already know.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Soap'?
exercises.correct: Atsihnhétste'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Atsihnhétste' — soap. An A-class noun beginning with 'at'.

exercises.prompt: Atié:wen (towel/face cloth) connects to which body part vocabulary from the Body Parts lesson?
exercises.correct: Ononkwa' (head/face) and the face-washing verb — towels are used after washing
options: Ononkwa' (head/face) and the face-washing verb — towels are used after washing
Ohskenón:ni (arm) only
Onékahrha (teeth)
None — household and body vocabulary are separate
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Vocabulary networks: towel connects to face, which connects to the face-washing verb, which connects to soap. Learning in networks is more efficient than learning isolated words.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Five syllables: oh-kwá-ri-yen-tha'. The glottal stop (') at the end is the locative suffix '-yentha'' meaning 'the place of'. Stress on the second syllable.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohkwáriyentha' — the place where one rests. The -yentha' suffix turns any activity into a place: the place of resting, the place of washing, the place of sitting. Think about the spaces in your home — each one is defined by what happens there. Which space in your home would you most want to be able to name in Kanien'kéha, and why?

lessons.title: Cultural Items & Political Vocabulary
lessons.sub: The objects that hold the culture
intro: This lesson introduces vocabulary from Lesson 31 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum — cultural items and political vocabulary central to Kanien'kehá:ka identity.
The Longhouse (Kanonsesné:ha) is not only a building — it is the governing unit of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy. 'People of the Longhouse' is how the Haudenosaunee describe themselves.

The Great Law of Peace (Kaià:ner) is the constitution of the Confederacy. Peace (Skennón:'a), strength/righteousness, and good mind are its three principles. Native tobacco (Onkwatà:wen) carries prayers upward — it is ceremonial, not recreational.
The Two Dog Wampum Belt was repatriated to Kanehsatà:ke from the McCord Stewart Museum in 2024, a landmark act described on the Kontinónhstats website.
lessons.ped: Political vocabulary as critical literacy: Freire (1970) argued that reading the word means reading the world. Learning Kaià:ner (Great Law), Skennón:'a (peace), Iakoiánehr (clan mother), and Rotiiánehr (longhouse chief) is reading the Haudenosaunee political world — its institutions, its principles, its structure.

exercises.prompt: What does Kanonsesné:ha mean?
exercises.correct: Longhouse
options: Longhouse
Wampum belt
Peace
Tobacco
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanonsesné:ha — longhouse. The Haudenosaunee Confederacy is 'People of the Longhouse'. Clan mothers governed the longhouse.

exercises.prompt: What does Kaià:ner mean?
exercises.correct: The Great Law of Peace / The Good Path
options: The Great Law of Peace / The Good Path
Longhouse
Wampum belt
Clan mother
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kaià:ner — the Good Path, the Great Law of Peace. The three principles are peace (Skennón:'a), strength/righteousness, and good mind.

exercises.prompt: Match the cultural and political vocabulary
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Longhouse, Great Law, Wampum, Peace — the political architecture of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy named.

exercises.prompt: Onkwatà:wen (native tobacco) is used ceremonially, not recreationally. What is its ceremonial function?
exercises.correct: Tobacco carries prayers upward — it is placed in fire, water, or earth as an offering and expression of thanks
options: Tobacco carries prayers upward — it is placed in fire, water, or earth as an offering and expression of thanks
Tobacco is used for trade only
Tobacco is a food
Tobacco is only for men
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The ceremonial use of native tobacco (Onkwatà:wen) is distinct from commercial tobacco. It is an offering — a way of sending thanks and prayer into the world.

exercises.prompt: The Two Dog Wampum Belt (Teioháte) was returned to Kanehsatà:ke from the McCord Stewart Museum in 2024. What does wampum repatriation represent?
exercises.correct: The return of cultural records and governance documents to the community they belong to
options: The return of cultural records and governance documents to the community they belong to
A financial transaction
A museum policy change unrelated to community
Wampum has no political significance
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Wampum belts are records — of treaties, alliances, and history. Their repatriation is the return of a community's own documentary archive. The Kontinónhstats website covers this directly.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Peace'?
exercises.correct: Skennón:'a
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Skennón:'a — peace. One of the three principles of the Great Law of Peace.

exercises.prompt: Ionkwaiénhton (corn husk doll) has no face in Haudenosaunee tradition. What does the oral tradition say about why?
exercises.correct: She gave her face to the people and was taught humility — she is not to look at her own reflection
options: She gave her face to the people and was taught humility — she is not to look at her own reflection
The face was never added for practical reasons
Corn husk dolls are not ceremonial
The face was added later in history
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The corn husk doll story is a teaching about humility and service. Learning these cultural items means learning the stories that give them meaning.

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Three syllables: sken-NÓN-'a. The glottal stop (') at the end gives it a soft, complete quality. A word that ends with intention.

exercises.prompt: Connect to your life
exercises.storyPrompt: Skennón:'a — peace. One of three principles that united five warring nations under the Great Law. Peace here is not the absence of conflict — it is an active choice, a political project, a word that required a whole confederacy to embody. What does peace mean in your own life, in your own community? Is it something you have, something you build, or something you carry?

lessons.title: Cultural Objects and Their Stories
lessons.sub: The objects that carry the culture
intro: Each cultural object carries a story. The corn husk doll has its own legend about humility. The rattle drives ceremonies. The beading loom holds designs that encode clan identity.
Knowing these words means knowing what they hold — not just what they are called.
This lesson introduces key cultural objects and the stories they carry.
lessons.ped: Cultural objects as knowledge carriers: Tuck & Yang (2012): decolonization involves restoring relationships to material culture. LaFever Spiritual domain.

exercises.prompt: Match the cultural object to its role

pairs.english: A teaching object carrying a lesson about humility
Drives the rhythm of ceremony and social dance
Creates designs carrying clan and community identity

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Corn husk doll, rattle, water drum, beading loom — each carries a specific role and specific knowledge.

exercises.prompt: Ionkwaiénhton — corn husk doll. The doll's legend teaches about humility. What is the teaching?
exercises.correct: The first corn husk doll was given a beautiful face and became vain; the Creator took her face away as a lesson
options: She was given a beautiful face, became vain, and the Creator removed it
The doll was made as a game
The doll represents the corn spirit only
The legend is about harvesting
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The corn husk doll's legend: she was beautiful and knew it, always looking at her own face. The Creator took her face as a reminder. Haudenosaunee corn husk dolls are made without faces. The legend lives in the object.

exercises.prompt: Onhkwa'shón:'a — beads. Beadwork uses specific colours and patterns that carry meaning. What does knowing the vocabulary of beads mean?
exercises.correct: It connects you to the knowledge of design, clan identity, and the makers who have worked this craft for generations
options: It connects to design knowledge and generational craft
It is only practical for artists
Bead vocabulary is decorative, not meaningful
The designs have no specific meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Beadwork patterns carry clan symbols, ceremonial meanings, and family identities. The beads are a visual language. Knowing the words is knowing the vocabulary of that language.

exercises.prompt: The corn husk doll
exercises.storyPrompt: Ionkwaiénhton — corn husk doll. Made from the dried outer leaves of the corn, twisted and folded into a human form. No face. The doll is given to children and the legend is told alongside it. In residential schools, children were forbidden from making corn husk dolls. The objects carried the lessons, and the lessons had to go too. The dolls are still being made. The lessons are still being taught.

lessons.title: Sacred Medicines and Plants
lessons.sub: The four sacred medicines and plants of the territory
intro: The Haudenosaunee recognise four sacred medicines: Onkwatà:wen (native tobacco), Onen'takwenhténtsha (cedar), Ohnhónsaton (sweet grass), and Óhserón:ni (sage). Each is used specifically in healing and ceremony.
Beyond the four sacred medicines, the territory is full of plant knowledge — the maple (Wahtá:), the elm, the basswood, the birch.
Plant knowledge in Kanien'kéha is extensive and specific. This lesson introduces the most important plant vocabulary.
lessons.ped: Plant medicines as sacred and practical knowledge: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): land-based knowledge should be central to Indigenous language pedagogy. LaFever Spiritual domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Match the sacred medicine to its role

pairs.english: Opens communication with the spirit world; carries prayers
Purification and protection; used in healing
Attracts positive energy; used after cedar

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Tobacco, cedar, sweet grass, sage. Four medicines, four specific purposes.

exercises.prompt: Onkwatà:wen — native tobacco — carries prayers. What does this mean in practice?
exercises.correct: When you put tobacco down and speak a prayer, the tobacco carries that intention to the spirit world
options: Tobacco carries intentions to the spirit world
Tobacco is only used socially
Tobacco is mixed with food
It is only burned
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tobacco is put down before making a request of the land, the spirits, or the Creator. It opens the channel. This is specific and intentional.

exercises.prompt: Trees of the territory
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Maple, elm, basswood, birch — trees of Kanien'kehá:ka territory. Each has its role as food, medicine, or material.

exercises.prompt: Cedar
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen'takwenhténtsha — cedar. The tree of purification. Its smoke clears what does not belong. Its leaves are used in ceremony and healing. In Kanien'kehá:ka territory, cedar grows near the water and in the forest. The tree knows where to stand. When you learn its name in Kanien'kéha, you are learning one word in a very large vocabulary of plant relationship.

lessons.title: The Natural World: Seasons & Land
lessons.sub: The world we live in and move through
intro: This lesson covers the vocabulary of the natural world from Lesson 33 of the Kontinónhstats curriculum: the moon, the earth, trees, rivers, mountains, and the four seasons.

Many of these words appear in the Thanksgiving Address. The moon (Tsí:tewen, 'Grandmother Moon') governs tides and the female cycle. The maple (Wahta:) marks the beginning of spring with its sap. The river (Kaniatarà:ke) is the St. Lawrence — the waterway of Kanehsatà:ke's history.

The four seasons in Kanien'kéha map onto a ceremonial calendar: spring (Sé:ske) brings the Strawberry Ceremony; summer (Ohiarí:ha) is the growing season; autumn (Ken'niká:ien) brings the Green Corn and Harvest Ceremonies; winter (Kenién:ke) is a time of storytelling.
lessons.ped: Land-based vocabulary and seasonal ceremony: In Haudenosaunee pedagogy, the land is not background — it is the primary text. Learning the seasons means learning when to hold ceremonies, when to plant, when to harvest. This is what Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023) mean by decolonizing language pedagogy: the curriculum should serve the community's relationship to the land.

exercises.prompt: What does Tsí:tewen mean?
exercises.correct: Moon
options: Moon
Sun
Star
Earth
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsí:tewen — moon, our Grandmother Moon. You already know Karáhkwa (sun) from the shapes lesson. Sun and moon — two celestial bodies now named.

exercises.prompt: Match the four seasons
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The four seasons — each connected to ceremony, agriculture, and the natural world.

exercises.prompt: Sé:ske (spring) is associated with which ceremony?
exercises.correct: The Strawberry Ceremony — celebrating the first fruit of spring
options: The Strawberry Ceremony — celebrating the first fruit of spring
The Green Corn Ceremony
The Harvest Ceremony
The Midwinter Ceremony
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Sé:ske → Strawberry Ceremony (Ken' niiohontésha'). You already know the word for strawberries from the food lesson. The vocabulary connects.

exercises.prompt: Wahta: means maple tree. Why is the maple tree particularly significant at Kanehsatà:ke?
exercises.correct: The sugar bush tradition — gathering maple sap each spring — is a land-based practice connecting community to territory
options: The sugar bush tradition — gathering maple sap each spring — is a land-based practice connecting community to territory
Maple trees are only decorative
Maple was used to make longhouses
The maple tree has no special significance
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The sugar bush (tapping maple trees for sap) is a spring ceremony and a community activity. The maple leaf appears on the Canadian flag — but for the Kanien'kehá:ka, Wahta: is much more than a national symbol.

exercises.prompt: Match the nature vocabulary
exercises.cm: Shé:kon! Moon, earth, maple, river — the natural world named.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'Spring'?
exercises.correct: Sé:ske
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Sé:ske — spring. A short, clean word for renewal.

exercises.prompt: Complete the word for autumn
exercises.correct: ien
exercises.hint: i
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ken'niká:ien — autumn. The season of the Green Corn and Harvest Ceremonies.

exercises.prompt: Tsí:tewen (moon) is called 'Grandmother Moon' in Haudenosaunee tradition. You already know the word for grandmother. What is it?
exercises.sub: Spaced review from Unit 2
exercises.correct: tota (base form) / Aksótha (my grandmother)
options: tota (base form) / Aksótha (my grandmother)
ista (mother)
'sot (grandfather)
raónha (he)
