exercises.storyPrompt: À:there'. Iakoriwá:ienstha. Iakotsí:sten. A basket, a potter, a hide tanner. In Kanien'kehá:ka life, the makers have always been the carriers of knowledge. The baskets hold the food. The pots cook it. The hide clothing shelters the body. The songs open the ceremony. Making is how the culture reproduces itself. When you learn the words for what is made and who makes it, you are learning part of how the culture holds itself together. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 16 — Community Life: Governance, Ceremony and Gathering
desc: The longhouse, the clans, the chiefs and clan mothers, the Great Law, wampum, ceremony and gathering — the language of how the Kanien'kehá:ka people organise themselves

lessons.title: The Longhouse Revisited
lessons.sub: Kanonsesné:ha — the centre of everything
intro: You first met the longhouse in Unit 3 — Kanonsesné:ha — as a Ka-class noun. This lesson goes much deeper. The longhouse is not just a building. It is the political, ceremonial, and social centre of Kanien'kehá:ka life.
The word Rotinonhsyón:ni — the Haudenosaunee name for themselves — means People of the Longhouse. The longhouse is so central that the whole Confederacy is named after it.
This lesson covers the extended vocabulary of the longhouse: its roles, its organisation by clan, and its place in the community today.
lessons.ped: The longhouse as social architecture: The longhouse is not background information — it is the structural centre of Haudenosaunee political and ceremonial life. Nation (2001): known vocabulary (Kanonsesné:ha from Unit 3) reintroduced in expanded cultural context. LaFever Spiritual and Emotional domains: the longhouse is identity, not just location.

exercises.prompt: Rotinonhsyón:ni — People of the Longhouse — is the name the Haudenosaunee use for themselves. What does naming yourself after a building tell you?
exercises.correct: The longhouse is more than a building — it is the symbol of how the people live, govern, and relate to each other
options: The longhouse is more than a building — it is the symbol of how the people live, govern, and relate to each other
The Haudenosaunee primarily identify with architecture
The name is historical only and not used today
It refers to a specific longhouse in one community
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! People of the Longhouse — the name holds a whole way of life. The longhouse structure, with its central fire, its clan families on either side, its shared governance — is the model for how the Confederacy works. The name is the philosophy.

exercises.prompt: Match the longhouse word to its meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The longhouse appears in three forms: as a noun, as a variant, and as a locative (at the longhouse). And then as the name of a whole people.

exercises.prompt: Iakoiánehr — clan mother. In the longhouse, the clan mother sits at one end. What is her role?
exercises.correct: She holds political authority — she nominates, advises, and can remove longhouse chiefs; she is the senior decision-maker of her clan family
options: She holds political authority — she nominates, advises, and can remove longhouse chiefs; she is the senior decision-maker of her clan family
She cooks for the ceremonies only
She is an honorary title with no practical role
Her role is the same as a longhouse chief
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The clan mother — Iakoiánehr — holds real political power. She nominates the chief, advises him, and if he fails to serve the people well, she can remove him. This is not ceremony — it is governance. The matrilineal structure of Kanien'kehá:ka society makes the clan mother the centre of political authority.

exercises.prompt: Rotiiánehr — longhouse chief. His authority comes from his clan mother's nomination. What happens if he does not serve the people well?
exercises.correct: The clan mother can remove him — his title returns to the clan family
options: The clan mother can remove him — his title returns to the clan family
He serves for life regardless
The community votes him out
He cannot be removed once appointed
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A chief who does not serve the people — who fails to listen, who acts for himself rather than the community — can be removed by the clan mother who nominated him. This accountability is built into the system. The Great Law of Peace specifies this process.

exercises.prompt: Complete the sentence: 'The longhouse is there' — ___ e'tho ká:yen
exercises.correct: Kanonsesné:ha
exercises.hint: K
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanonsesné:ha e'tho ká:yen — the longhouse is there. The existence sentence pattern from Unit 20, naming the centre of community life.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the Kanien'kéha word for longhouse?

exercises.prompt: Say this word aloud
exercises.hint: Ka-non-ses-NÉ:-ha — five syllables. Stress and long vowel on NÉ:. Say it with the weight of everything the word holds.

exercises.prompt: People of the Longhouse
exercises.storyPrompt: Rotinonhsyón:ni — People of the Longhouse. The Haudenosaunee Confederacy — Kanien'kehá:ka (Mohawk), Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca, and later Tuscarora — are all People of the Longhouse. The Confederacy is the longest-running participatory democracy in North America, and some historians argue it influenced the framers of the US Constitution. The longhouse is the model: many families, different clans, one fire, one purpose. What would your community look like if it was organised like a longhouse?

lessons.title: Clan Mothers and Chiefs
lessons.sub: The title-holders and how to address them
intro: You learned the clan names in Unit 6 and revisited them in Unit 12. This lesson focuses on the people who hold titles within those clans — the clan mothers and the chiefs — and the formal language used to address and speak about them.
Iakoiánehr — clan mother. Rotiiánehr — longhouse chief. These are not just job titles. They are roles with specific responsibilities that come from the Great Law of Peace.
Addressing a title-holder in Kanien'kéha is different from everyday speech — the formal register changes slightly. This lesson introduces those distinctions.
lessons.ped: Political vocabulary as cultural knowledge: Understanding the clan mother and chief system is essential to understanding Kanien'kehá:ka governance. This lesson treats political vocabulary as primary content, not as cultural background. LaFever Spiritual and Emotional domains: titles carry responsibility and relationship. McCarty & Lee (2014): language learning must honour cultural institutions.

exercises.prompt: Match the title to who holds it

pairs.english: Clan mother — senior woman of a clan family
Longhouse chief — nominated by his clan mother

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Clan mother, chief, and medicine woman — three distinct roles in Kanien'kehá:ka community life. Each title carries specific responsibilities that the Great Law defines.

exercises.prompt: Kaià:ner — the Great Law of Peace / the Good Path. This is the foundational law of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy. What does the word 'law' miss about what Kaià:ner actually means?
exercises.correct: Kaià:ner means a good path or way of life — it is a guide for living together well, not a legal code enforced by punishment
options: Kaià:ner means a good path or way of life — it is a guide for living together well, not a legal code enforced by punishment
It is exactly the same as a legal code
It is only relevant to ceremonial occasions
It applies only to chiefs
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kaià:ner — the Good Path, the Great Law. The word itself suggests guidance and right living rather than a set of rules with penalties. The Peacemaker brought this path to the five nations to end the cycle of violence and establish a framework for living in peace and relationship.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look at the translation: 'the Good Path'.

exercises.prompt: The clan mother system means that political authority passes through women — clan mothers nominate and can remove chiefs. How does this compare to the political systems introduced by colonial governments?
exercises.correct: Colonial governments imposed male-dominated systems — the Indian Act's elected band council system replaced the traditional clan mother and chief system in many communities
options: Colonial governments imposed male-dominated systems — the Indian Act's elected band council system replaced the traditional clan mother and chief system in many communities
Colonial systems were identical to the traditional system
The traditional system had no role for women
The two systems have always coexisted without conflict
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Indian Act imposed elected band councils with male voters and candidates, directly undermining the matrilineal clan mother system. Many Kanien'kehá:ka communities have worked to maintain or restore traditional governance alongside — or instead of — the Indian Act system. The political vocabulary of Kanien'kéha carries that history.

exercises.prompt: Complete the title: 'clan mother' — Iak___
exercises.correct: oiánehr
exercises.hint: o
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iakoiánehr — clan mother. The Iak- prefix marks female authority. This is the I-/Iak- prefix family you first learned in Unit 1.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which prefix family marks female roles?

exercises.prompt: Write the Kanien'kéha word for longhouse chief
exercises.correct: Rotiiánehr
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Rotiiánehr — longhouse chief. The Roti- prefix marks male group; -iánehr is the title root. The same root appears in Iakoiánehr (clan mother) — the title connects them.

exercises.prompt: Say both titles aloud
exercises.hint: ia-ko-IÁ-nehr (4 syllables) — ro-ti-IÁ-nehr (4 syllables). The same -iánehr root, two different authority prefixes. Iak- for female, Roti- for male. Say them with the respect due to the roles.

exercises.prompt: The clan mother
exercises.storyPrompt: Iakoiánehr — clan mother. The women who hold this title carry enormous responsibility. They watch over their clan families, guide the selection of chiefs, manage conflicts, and maintain the social and ceremonial life of the community. Their authority comes not from conquest or election but from lineage and community recognition. The Indian Act spent over a century trying to make this role invisible. It is still here. She is still here.

lessons.title: The Great Law of Peace
lessons.sub: Kaià:ner and Kaienrekó:wa — the foundation
intro: The Great Law of Peace — Kaià:ner — is the foundational law of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, brought by the Peacemaker Tekanawí:ta and recorded in wampum.
Kaienrekó:wa — the Great Tree of Peace — is the symbol of the Confederacy. Under this tree, the five (later six) nations buried their weapons and agreed to live under one law.
The Great Law establishes the structure of the Confederacy: the clans, the chiefs, the clan mothers, the council, and the process for making decisions together.
lessons.ped: The Great Law as living document: The Great Law is not ancient history — it is the governing framework of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy today. This lesson treats it as current political reality, not as 'traditional' or past-tense. Tuck & Yang (2012): decolonization involves restoring Indigenous political structures. LaFever Spiritual domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Tekanawí:ta — the Peacemaker — brought the Great Law to the five nations. He came to end the cycle of violence and grief. What was the first nation he approached?
exercises.correct: The Kanien'kehá:ka (Mohawk) — who were among the most affected by the cycles of war and were the first to accept the message
options: The Kanien'kehá:ka (Mohawk) — who were among the most affected by the cycles of war and were the first to accept the message
The Onondaga
The Seneca — the most western nation
The Tuscarora
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Peacemaker came first to the Kanien'kehá:ka, then moved west nation by nation. Aiionwátha (Hiawatha) helped carry his message. The Kanien'kehá:ka, as the easternmost nation and the Keepers of the Eastern Door, accepted the Great Law first.

exercises.prompt: Match the Great Law word to its meaning

pairs.english: The Great Law / the Good Path

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Law, tree, peace, wampum — four words from the foundation of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy. Each one names something specific and essential.

exercises.prompt: Kaienrekó:wa — the Great Tree of Peace. The nations buried their weapons at the roots of this tree. What does this act symbolise?
exercises.correct: The permanent end of war between the nations — weapons buried means they are gone, not just set aside
options: The permanent end of war between the nations — weapons buried means they are gone, not just set aside
A temporary truce
Only the end of one specific conflict
A ceremony with no political meaning
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Burying weapons is not a pause — it is a declaration that those weapons will not be taken up again. The Great Tree stands over the buried weapons. Its roots extend in four directions to any nation that wishes to join. The Eagle at the top watches for any threat to the peace.

exercises.prompt: Teioháte — wampum belt. Wampum belts recorded the Great Law and other agreements. What does this tell you about wampum?
exercises.correct: Wampum belts are records and agreements — they carry legal and historical meaning, not just decorative value
options: Wampum belts are records and agreements — they carry legal and historical meaning, not just decorative value
Wampum is primarily used as currency
Wampum belts are decorative only
Wampum only appears in ceremonies
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wampum belts are records — the patterns of white and purple shell beads encode specific agreements, histories, and laws. The Two Row Wampum (Kaswentha) records the relationship between the Haudenosaunee and European newcomers: two vessels travelling the same river, neither interfering with the other. These are living legal documents.

exercises.prompt: Write the Kanien'kéha word for the Great Law of Peace
exercises.correct: Kaià:ner
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kaià:ner — the Great Law / the Good Path. Grave accent on ià:, long vowel after it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — grave accent on ià: followed by a colon.

exercises.prompt: Say these two foundational words aloud
exercises.hint: Ka-IÀ:-ner (3 syllables) — Sken-NÓN:-'a (3 syllables). Both carry long vowels and glottal stops. Say them with the weight they deserve.

exercises.prompt: Skennón:'a
exercises.storyPrompt: Skennón:'a — peace. The Peacemaker came to bring this to nations caught in cycles of grief and violence. He did not bring peace through war. He brought it through words — through the Great Law, through the image of the Great Tree, through the shared understanding that the people were stronger together than apart. The Haudenosaunee Confederacy has lasted for centuries on this foundation. Skennón:'a is not a wish. It is a structure. What would it take to build that structure where you live?

lessons.title: Condolence: The Language of Loss and Restoration
lessons.sub: The ceremony of grief — and how the language holds it
intro: The condolence ceremony is one of the most important ceremonies in Kanien'kehá:ka life. When a title-holder dies, the condolence ceremony restores the grieving nation and raises up a new title-holder.
The ceremony uses specific formal language — language of loss, of grief, of restoration. Some of this vocabulary is among the most moving in Kanien'kéha.
This lesson introduces the condolence vocabulary in a way that is respectful and accessible — naming what the ceremony does without trying to reproduce it.
lessons.ped: Condolence as the highest form of Kanien'kéha oratory: The condolence ceremony is described by many scholars as the most elaborate and linguistically rich of Haudenosaunee ceremonial forms. This lesson introduces it respectfully — naming its purpose and some of its vocabulary — without attempting to reproduce ceremonial content that belongs to specific knowledge keepers. LaFever Spiritual and Emotional domains primary.

exercises.prompt: The condolence ceremony has two parts: the grief of the mourning nation, and the restoration of clarity and peace. What does Skennón:'a (peace) mean in this context?
exercises.correct: The restoration of clear thinking and right relationship after grief has clouded the mind
options: The restoration of clear thinking and right relationship after grief has clouded the mind
The absence of conflict only
A formal apology
A new political agreement
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! In the condolence ceremony, the mourning nation's grief is addressed first — eyes cleared of tears, ears cleared of grief, throat cleared so speech can return. Only then can the work of raising a new title-holder proceed. Peace here means the restoration of the capacity to think and act clearly.

exercises.prompt: Wake'nikonhrén:ton — I am heavy-hearted. You learned this emotional state verb in Unit 23. Why is it particularly relevant to the condolence ceremony?
exercises.correct: Heavy-heartedness is the condition that the condolence ceremony addresses — grief makes the mind heavy, and the ceremony restores it
options: Heavy-heartedness is the condition that the condolence ceremony addresses — grief makes the mind heavy, and the ceremony restores it
It is used only for personal sadness
The condolence ceremony creates heavy-heartedness
It is not relevant to the ceremony
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The condolence ceremony begins by acknowledging that the mourning nation is heavy-hearted — Wake'nikonhrén:ton. The ceremony then, step by step, lifts that heaviness. The language of the ceremony maps directly onto the emotional states you have already learned.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what state does the condolence ceremony begin with?

exercises.prompt: When a clan mother passes, the condolence ceremony must take place before a new clan mother can be recognised. Why does governance pause until the condolence is complete?
exercises.correct: Grief clouds judgment — no decisions can be made clearly until the community has properly acknowledged the loss and restored its clarity
options: Grief clouds judgment — no decisions can be made clearly until the community has properly acknowledged the loss and restored its clarity
It is only a formality with no practical reason
The new clan mother must wait one year
The ceremony is required by Canadian law
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Great Law is clear: governance cannot proceed when grief has not been addressed. This is not just ceremony — it is political wisdom. A community acting from unacknowledged grief makes poor decisions. The condolence ceremony ensures clarity before governance resumes.

exercises.prompt: Match the emotional state to where it appears in the condolence ceremony
exercises.sub: The ceremony moves from grief toward restoration

pairs.english: I am heavy-hearted — the state the ceremony begins with
I am sad — the grief being acknowledged
Peace — the state the ceremony restores
I feel good inside — the state after restoration

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Heavy-hearted → sad → peace restored → feeling good inside. The condolence ceremony moves through these states deliberately. The vocabulary you learned in Unit 23 maps the ceremony's emotional arc.

exercises.prompt: The condolence
exercises.storyPrompt: Wake'nikonhrén:ton — I am heavy-hearted. The condolence ceremony names this state directly and addresses it directly. It does not rush past grief. It does not tell the mourning nation to be strong. It says: your eyes are filled with tears. Your ears are blocked with grief. Your throat is closed with sorrow. Let us clear each one. This is governance that understands that people are whole beings — that you cannot think clearly when you are heavy-hearted, and that a community deserves to have its grief acknowledged before it is asked to carry on.

lessons.title: Social Dances and Gathering
lessons.sub: The language of coming together — dances, songs, and invitation
intro: Social dances are one of the most joyful parts of Kanien'kehá:ka community life. They are not performances — they are participatory. Everyone is invited; everyone can join.
Ratiwennahní:ion — the singers. Katsi'á:wens — water drum. Okwéskons — rattle. These are the instruments and the people who drive the social dances.
Knowing how to invite someone to dance, how to acknowledge the singers, and how to talk about a social gathering is practical community language.
lessons.ped: Social dance as community participation: Social dances are one of the most accessible points of entry into Kanien'kehá:ka community life for learners. This lesson provides vocabulary for participation — not performance analysis. LaFever Emotional domain: belonging and community. Physical domain: the language of movement and gathering.

exercises.prompt: Ratiwennahní:ion — the singers. In Kanien'kehá:ka social dances, the singers lead and the community follows. What does this structure tell you about the relationship between the singers and the dancers?
exercises.correct: The singers are the foundation — the dances follow their rhythm and call; the singers carry the ceremony forward
options: The singers are the foundation — the dances follow their rhythm and call; the singers carry the ceremony forward
The dancers lead and the singers follow
Singers and dancers are completely separate
Only one singer is needed per ceremony
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The singers — Ratiwennahní:ion — lead the social dances. Their knowledge of the songs, the rhythms, and the protocols is what makes the gathering possible. Respecting the singers means following their lead.

exercises.prompt: Match the instrument or gathering word to its meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Singers, drum, rattle, lacrosse — four words from the gathering and celebration life of the community.

exercises.prompt: Tewaarathon — lacrosse / the Creator's game. Why is lacrosse called the Creator's game?
exercises.correct: It was given by the Creator as a gift to the people — played for the Creator's enjoyment and as a form of prayer and healing
options: It was given by the Creator as a gift to the people — played for the Creator's enjoyment and as a form of prayer and healing
Because it was invented by a man named Creator
It is called this only in English translation
Because it is played in the longhouse
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Tewaarathon is the Creator's game — played as an act of thanksgiving, as a form of medicine, and as a way of honouring the Creator. The game was also used diplomatically to resolve conflicts between communities. Lacrosse is not just a sport; it is a ceremonial and political practice.

exercises.prompt: Katsi'á:wens — the water drum. What makes the water drum different from other drums?
exercises.correct: It contains water inside, which affects the pitch and tone — the water is part of the instrument
options: It contains water inside, which affects the pitch and tone — the water is part of the instrument
It is played near water only
It is made from water animals only
It is the same as other drums
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The water drum — Katsi'á:wens — has water inside that can be adjusted to change the pitch. It is a living instrument in the sense that its voice changes with the water inside it. It is the heartbeat of many Haudenosaunee ceremonies.

exercises.prompt: Say the singers' name aloud
exercises.hint: Ra-ti-wen-nah-NÍ:-ion — six syllables. The Ra- prefix marks male group. The -wennahnÍ:- root relates to voice and song. Long vowel on NÍ:. Say it with appreciation — the singers make the gathering possible.

exercises.prompt: The Creator's game
exercises.storyPrompt: Tewaarathon — the Creator's game. At the 1990 Oka Crisis, when Kanien'kehá:ka warriors stood at the barricades defending their land and burial grounds, the women behind the lines played lacrosse. In the middle of a confrontation with the Canadian army, they played the Creator's game. That is not incidental. That is the game doing what it was given for — medicine, prayer, presence, strength. The stick and the ball and the game are still here, the way the language is still here.

lessons.title: Ceremonial Songs and Music
lessons.sub: The songs that hold the ceremonies together
intro: Songs in Kanien'kehá:ka ceremonial life are not entertainment — they are the ceremony itself. The right song at the right moment, sung correctly, is what makes the ceremony work.

Different ceremonies have different songs. Some songs belong to the longhouse; others belong to specific clans or medicine societies. The knowledge of which song goes where, and who has the right to sing it, is held by the singers and the knowledge keepers.
This lesson introduces the vocabulary of song and ceremony — not the songs themselves, but the language around them.
lessons.ped: Ceremonial songs as restricted knowledge: This lesson explicitly acknowledges that ceremonial song knowledge is restricted — it belongs to knowledge keepers and is not appropriate for a language app to reproduce. The lesson teaches the vocabulary of songs and ceremony without overstepping. This is an application of Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): decolonizing pedagogy honours community-defined boundaries around knowledge.

exercises.prompt: Some ceremonial songs in Kanien'kehá:ka life are restricted — they belong to specific knowledge keepers and are not shared publicly. Why is this boundary important?
exercises.correct: Ceremonial knowledge has power and context — shared without that context, or by those without the right to share it, it loses its integrity or causes harm
options: Ceremonial knowledge has power and context — shared without that context, or by those without the right to share it, it loses its integrity or causes harm
The songs are secret to keep outsiders away
There is no reason — all songs should be public
Restricted songs are less important than public ones
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The boundaries around ceremonial knowledge are not arbitrary secrecy — they are forms of care. Songs that belong to healing ceremonies carry specific power. Used outside that context, or by people not trained to use them, that power is not available or may cause unintended harm. Respecting the boundary is part of respecting the ceremony.

exercises.prompt: Onkwatà:wen — native tobacco. Tobacco is used to open communication with the spirit world — to send a message, to make an offering, to accompany a request. How does this differ from recreational tobacco use?
exercises.correct: Ceremonial tobacco is used intentionally and with specific protocol — its purpose is relational and spiritual, not habitual or recreational
options: Ceremonial tobacco is used intentionally and with specific protocol — its purpose is relational and spiritual, not habitual or recreational
They are the same thing
Ceremonial tobacco is stronger
Native tobacco is a different plant with no spiritual use
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Onkwatà:wen — native tobacco — is one of the four sacred medicines. It is used to open prayer, to make offerings, to carry intentions to the spirit world. This use is specific and intentional. It is entirely different from habitual recreational use of commercial tobacco.

exercises.prompt: Match the ceremonial item to its purpose

pairs.english: Native tobacco — used for prayer and offering
Rattle — drives the rhythm of ceremony
Water drum — the heartbeat of the ceremony
The singers — who carry the ceremony

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Each ceremonial element has a specific purpose. None of them is decorative. Together they make the ceremony work.

exercises.prompt: Shonkwaiá:tison — the Creator. Ceremonies in Kanien'kehá:ka life are not directed at the Creator in the same way prayer works in some other traditions. What is the relationship between ceremony and the Creator?
exercises.correct: Ceremony gives thanks and maintains the relationship — it is ongoing gratitude and recognition, not petition or request
options: Ceremony gives thanks and maintains the relationship — it is ongoing gratitude and recognition, not petition or request
Ceremonies ask the Creator for specific things
The Creator is not involved in ceremony
Only the Thanksgiving Address involves the Creator
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Haudenosaunee ceremony is primarily an act of gratitude and maintenance — giving thanks for what has been given, maintaining the relationships that keep the world alive. This is different from petitionary prayer. The Creator gave the people everything they need; ceremony is the ongoing acknowledgement of that gift.

exercises.prompt: Tobacco
exercises.storyPrompt: Onkwatà:wen — native tobacco. One of the four sacred medicines, alongside cedar, sweet grass, and sage. When you put tobacco down, you are opening a line of communication — to the spirit world, to the land, to the beings you are asking to hear you. The commercial tobacco that has caused so much illness and harm is something different. The original plant, used in the original way, is a gift for relationship. That distinction matters — and the language holds it.

lessons.title: Council Speech
lessons.sub: The formal language of speaking at a gathering
intro: Speaking formally in Kanien'kéha — at a council, at a ceremony, at a gathering — uses a different register from everyday conversation. Some phrases are specific to formal openings and closings.
You already know Onen' ki' wáhi — that is all, I am finished — which closes each section of the Thanksgiving Address and formal speeches. This lesson adds the formal opening phrases.
Formal speech in Kanien'kéha is not about using difficult words. It is about using the right words in the right sequence — acknowledging the people, the purpose, and the occasion before you speak.
lessons.ped: Register variation as community knowledge: Knowing when and how to speak formally is social knowledge as much as linguistic. This lesson introduces the formal register through the phrases learners are most likely to encounter at community gatherings. LaFever Physical domain: learners practise formal opening and closing phrases. Emotional domain: speaking formally in your language at a gathering is an act of presence and respect.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address begins by acknowledging Onkwehshón:'a — the people. Why does a formal speech in Kanien'kéha begin by acknowledging those present?
exercises.correct: You speak to and for the people — acknowledging them first affirms that they are seen and that you are speaking on their behalf
options: You speak to and for the people — acknowledging them first affirms that they are seen and that you are speaking on their behalf
It is a formality with no meaning
Only the speaker matters in formal speech
The people are acknowledged last
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Beginning with the people is an act of recognition — I see you, I know you are here, I am speaking to and for you. It is the opposite of a speech that ignores the audience. In Haudenosaunee oratory, the speaker serves the people; the speech is not for the speaker.

exercises.prompt: Onen' ki' wáhi closes a speech or section. You have said this at the end of every unit. What does it mean in a formal speech context?
exercises.correct: I have said what I came to say — this part is complete, and the next speaker or the next section may proceed
options: I have said what I came to say — this part is complete, and the next speaker or the next section may proceed
I am leaving now
I disagree with what was said
The entire gathering is over
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Onen' ki' wáhi in a formal speech marks the end of one speaker's turn or one section of an address. It is a clear, respectful signal that you are finished and the space is open for what comes next.

exercises.prompt: Put these formal speech elements in their natural order
exercises.sub: From opening to closing

pairs.english: First — you open by greeting those present
Second — the words that come before all else
Third — naming and recognising those gathered
Last — that is all, I am finished

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Greeting → Thanksgiving Address → acknowledge the people → speak your purpose → close with Onen' ki' wáhi. The sequence is the protocol.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what comes first in a formal gathering?

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — it makes our minds happy / we give thanks. Why does gratitude appear so frequently in formal Kanien'kéha speech?
exercises.correct: Gratitude is not just polite — it actively orients the speaker and listeners toward right relationship before any business begins
options: Gratitude is not just polite — it actively orients the speaker and listeners toward right relationship before any business begins
It is a formality required by protocol only
Gratitude only appears in the Thanksgiving Address
The phrase is optional and rarely used
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — saying together that our minds are made happy, that we give thanks — is an act of collective orientation. Everyone in the room is being reminded of what they share and what they are grateful for before the difficult work of governance or ceremony begins.

exercises.prompt: Say the closing phrase with the weight it carries
exercises.meaning: That is all / I am finished
exercises.hint: O-nen' — ki' — WÁ-hi. Three small words that close a speech, a ceremony, a unit, a section of the Thanksgiving Address. Say it like you mean it — not rushing to be done, but genuinely finishing.

exercises.prompt: The words that come before all else
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. Before any council meeting, any ceremony, any gathering — these words are spoken. They have been spoken before every important conversation in Kanien'kehá:ka life for as long as anyone can remember. They will be spoken after this course ends, long after you have moved on to other things. The Address is not part of the culture — it is the container the culture happens in. Every time you learn another piece of the vocabulary the Address contains, you understand a little more of what that container is made of.

lessons.title: Wampum and Records
lessons.sub: Teioháte — the records that hold the agreements
intro: Wampum belts — Teioháte — are the records of the Haudenosaunee. Specific belts record specific agreements: the Great Law, treaties with European nations, the Two Row Wampum.

The Two Row Wampum — Kaswéntha — is among the most significant: two purple rows on a white background representing two vessels travelling the same river, neither interfering with the other. It was the original agreement between the Haudenosaunee and Dutch newcomers, and its principles are still referenced in Indigenous rights discussions today.
Wampum is not money — though European colonists misunderstood and misused it as currency. Wampum is a legal and historical record.
lessons.ped: Wampum as living legal record: The Two Row Wampum is cited in contemporary Indigenous rights discourse. Teaching wampum vocabulary alongside its legal and historical significance treats it as what it is — a living legal system — not as cultural artifact. Tuck & Yang (2012): Indigenous governance systems are not historical. LaFever Spiritual and Mental domains.

exercises.prompt: Teioháte — wampum belt. European colonists treated wampum as currency. What was wrong with this understanding?
exercises.correct: Wampum is a record and agreement — not a medium of exchange. Treating it as money misunderstood and degraded its actual purpose.
options: Wampum is a record and agreement — not a medium of exchange. Treating it as money misunderstood and degraded its actual purpose.
Wampum was used as currency before colonists arrived
Wampum and currency serve identical purposes
Wampum had no value before European contact
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wampum belts encode specific agreements and histories in their patterns. Treating them as money is like treating a legal document as scrap paper because it is made of the same material. The misunderstanding was not accidental — it served colonial interests to reduce wampum to something tradeable and controllable.

exercises.prompt: The Two Row Wampum — Kaswéntha — shows two purple rows on white. The two rows represent two nations travelling the same river, each in their own vessel. What does this symbol say about the relationship between the Haudenosaunee and European settlers?
exercises.correct: They travel side by side — neither interfering with the other's laws, governance, or way of life
options: They travel side by side — neither interfering with the other's laws, governance, or way of life
One nation leads and the other follows
The two rows will eventually merge
The relationship ended when the belt was made
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kaswéntha — two rows, side by side, never crossing. The Haudenosaunee offered this as the framework for a relationship with European newcomers: you have your vessel and your laws; we have ours. We share the river but do not interfere. This principle is referenced in treaty discussions and sovereignty arguments to this day.

exercises.prompt: Many original wampum belts were taken from Haudenosaunee communities and are now held in museums and private collections in North America and Europe. What is the Haudenosaunee position on these belts?
exercises.correct: They should be returned — they are living legal documents, not museum artifacts, and their return is a matter of sovereignty
options: They should be returned — they are living legal documents, not museum artifacts, and their return is a matter of sovereignty
They are better preserved in museums
The communities have no ongoing relationship with them
Replicas are equivalent to the originals
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The repatriation of wampum belts — the return of the original belts to the communities they belong to — is an ongoing Haudenosaunee priority. The Onondaga Nation has been particularly active in recovering belts from institutions in North America and Europe. Wampum belts are not heritage objects. They are active governance documents.

exercises.prompt: Write the Kanien'kéha word for wampum belt
exercises.correct: Teioháte
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Teioháte — wampum belt. Acute accent on há:.
exercises.wm: Please try again — acute accent on há.

exercises.prompt: The record
exercises.storyPrompt: Teioháte — wampum belt. White and purple shell beads, worked into patterns that encode agreements made between nations. The belts were read aloud at councils — the reader would trace the pattern with their hands and speak the words the belt held. When a belt was in the wrong hands, the agreement it held was also in the wrong hands. The Haudenosaunee have been working for decades to bring their belts home. Each one that returns is not just an object returning — it is a piece of their legal record returning. A page of their law.

lessons.title: Lacrosse: The Creator's Game
lessons.sub: Tewaarathon — medicine, prayer, and community in a game
intro: Tewaarathon — lacrosse — was given to the Haudenosaunee by the Creator as a gift. It is played for the Creator's enjoyment, as a form of medicine and prayer, and as a way of resolving conflicts between communities.

You learned the word Tewaarathon in Unit 3 (Ka-class nouns) and it has appeared in several places since. This lesson goes deeper: the game as ceremony, the vocabulary of the game, and its role in community life today.
Lacrosse is now the most widely played field sport in North America. Its origin is in this language and this people.
lessons.ped: Lacrosse as ceremonial and political practice: Lacrosse is often taught as sport history without its ceremonial and political dimensions. This lesson restores those dimensions — not instead of the sport vocabulary but alongside it. LaFever Spiritual domain: the game is a gift and a ceremony. Emotional domain: the game as community identity.

exercises.prompt: Tewaarathon is called the Creator's game. It has also been used to settle disputes between communities without war. What does this tell you about the game's political role?
exercises.correct: The game was a form of diplomacy — communities could contest something through the game rather than through violence
options: The game was a form of diplomacy — communities could contest something through the game rather than through violence
The game has no political role
It was only played for entertainment
The game was never used diplomatically
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Lacrosse as diplomacy — a way to contest an issue, release tension, and reach resolution without bloodshed. The Creator's game substituting for war. This is not metaphor; it is documented historical practice. The game was sometimes played over days, with hundreds of players, across vast territories.

exercises.prompt: Rakwára:okweh — a lacrosse player. The Ra- prefix marks male. What would the female form look like, based on what you know about prefix patterns?
exercises.correct: The female form would use the I- or Iak- prefix family instead of Ra-
options: The female form would use the I- or Iak- prefix family instead of Ra-
Women do not play lacrosse in Kanien'kehá:ka tradition
The same word is used for both
A completely different root is used for women players
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Ra-/I- prefix pattern you learned in Unit 1 extends to role words like lacrosse player. Rakwára:okweh (male player) becomes Iakwára:okweh or similar for a female player. The prefix system is consistent throughout the language.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which prefix family marks female roles and nouns?

exercises.prompt: At the 1990 Oka Crisis, Kanien'kehá:ka women played lacrosse behind the barricades while warriors defended the land. Why was this significant?
exercises.correct: Playing the Creator's game in a moment of crisis was an act of prayer and cultural affirmation — the game connects the community to the Creator in difficulty
options: Playing the Creator's game in a moment of crisis was an act of prayer and cultural affirmation — the game connects the community to the Creator in difficulty
It was a distraction tactic
Lacrosse has no connection to ceremony or prayer
It was coincidental
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! In the middle of a confrontation with the Canadian army, playing Tewaarathon was not incidental. It was ceremony. It was prayer. It was an assertion that the community's spiritual and cultural life continued — even under pressure. The Creator's game, played for the Creator, in the middle of defending what the Creator gave them.

exercises.prompt: Write the Kanien'kéha word for lacrosse — the Creator's game
exercises.correct: Tewaarathon
exercises.hint: T
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tewaarathon — the Creator's game. You have seen this word since Unit 3. Now you know more of what it holds.
exercises.wm: Please try again — starts with Tew.

exercises.prompt: The game
exercises.storyPrompt: Tewaarathon. The game that is played across North America — in high schools, in universities, in the Olympics — began here. In this territory. With this people. With sticks made from wood bent over fire and bags made from hide. Played for the Creator. Played as medicine. Played as prayer. The word in English — lacrosse — came from French missionaries who thought the stick looked like a bishop's staff. The word in Kanien'kéha names what the game actually is. The Creator's game. Tewaarathon.

lessons.title: Invitation and Welcome
lessons.sub: How to invite and welcome someone in Kanien'kéha
intro: Hospitality is central to Kanien'kehá:ka culture. Welcoming a guest, inviting someone to a gathering, feeding a visitor — these are not just social conventions. They are expressions of the values the Great Law is built on.
This lesson covers the language of invitation and welcome: the phrases you need to invite someone to a gathering, to welcome them when they arrive, and to acknowledge their presence.
You already have many of the pieces: Shé:kon (hello / greeting), Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne (I am pleased), Niawenhkó:wa (thank you). This lesson puts them in the context of welcome.
lessons.ped: Hospitality as cultural value expressed in language: The vocabulary of welcome and invitation is where everyday language and cultural values meet most directly. Nation (2001): functional communicative vocabulary (inviting, welcoming) is among the most transferable to real use. LaFever Emotional domain primary: welcome is a relational act.

exercises.prompt: Shé:kon, skennenkó: ken? — Hello, is everything peaceful? This is the full formal greeting. How is it different from the everyday Shé:kon, tó:kens?
exercises.correct: It asks about peace and wellbeing in a broader sense — not just 'how are you' but 'is all well with you and your community'
options: It asks about peace and wellbeing in a broader sense — not just 'how are you' but 'is all well with you and your community'
They are the same question
The formal greeting is only used in ceremonies
Skennenkó: means hungry
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon, skennenkó: ken? — the formal greeting asks about peace — skennón:'a. You learned this word as one of the foundational words of the Great Law. Using it in a greeting asks: are you at peace? Is your world in good order?
exercises.wm: Please try again — what root do you recognise in skennenkó:?

exercises.prompt: Match the welcome phrase to its meaning

pairs.english: Great peace for me / I am well (full form)
I am pleased / it makes my mind happy

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! A full welcome exchange: peace greeting → peace response → and you? → I am pleased. These four phrases carry a complete welcome in Kanien'kéha.

exercises.prompt: Skennen'kó:wa ní:'i — great peace for me. The -kó:wa suffix means great/large. Where have you seen this suffix before?
exercises.correct: In Niawenhkó:wa (great thanks) and Skennón:wa (great peace) — it adds greatness to whatever it follows
options: In Niawenhkó:wa (great thanks) and Skennón:wa (great peace) — it adds greatness to whatever it follows
Only in this greeting
In number words only
It has no meaning — just a sound
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! -kó:wa: you have seen it in Niawenhkó:wa, in Skennón:wa, in Kaià:ner'kó:wa. It amplifies. Skennen'kó:wa — not just peace, but great peace, fullness of peace. This is the state you are saying you are in when you give the full greeting response.
exercises.wm: Please try again — where have you seen -kó:wa before?

exercises.prompt: Complete the follow-up after greeting: 'And you?' — Nok ___
exercises.correct: ní:se?
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Nok ní:se? — and you? The simplest follow-up after a greeting. You have responded to how you are; now you turn the question back. Conversation is reciprocal.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the word for 'you' with a long vowel?

exercises.prompt: Say the full formal greeting exchange aloud — both sides
exercises.word: Shé:kon, skennenkó: ken? — Skennen'kó:wa ní:'i. Nok ní:se?
exercises.meaning: Hello, is everything peaceful? — Great peace for me. And you?
exercises.hint: Three exchanges, six beats. Shé:kon (warm open), skennenkó: ken? (genuine inquiry, rising). Skennen'kó:wa ní:'i (settled, confident). Nok ní:se? (turning it back, gentle). Practice both sides.

exercises.prompt: Is everything peaceful?
exercises.storyPrompt: Shé:kon, skennenkó: ken? — Hello, is everything peaceful? This is not a casual greeting. It is an inquiry. When you ask this, you are asking: is your world in balance? Is your family well? Is your community at peace? Are you carrying something heavy? The greeting opens the door to a real answer. The answer Skennen'kó:wa ní:'i — great peace for me — says: yes, I am whole. When that is the honest answer, saying it in Kanien'kéha makes it more real.

lessons.title: Community Roles and Responsibilities
lessons.sub: Beyond chief and clan mother — the many roles that keep a community alive
intro: A community needs more than chiefs and clan mothers to function. This lesson covers the wider vocabulary of community roles: the singers, the medicine people, the basket makers, the potters, the hide tanners.
In Kanien'kehá:ka society, these roles are not just occupations — they are identities, often inherited through clan lineage, practiced with specific protocols and responsibilities.
Knowing the vocabulary of these roles means being able to talk about who does what in a community — and why it matters.
lessons.ped: Community roles as social vocabulary: Nation (2001): vocabulary of social roles is among the most culturally significant to learn — it names how a community organises itself. This lesson draws on the wide range of role vocabulary in the glossary. LaFever Emotional domain: role and identity. Spiritual domain: roles carry ceremonial responsibility.

exercises.prompt: Match the community role to who fills it

pairs.english: The singers — who lead the ceremonies

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Singers, medicine woman, hide tanner, basket makers — four roles that have kept Kanien'kehá:ka communities alive for generations. Each one is a specific identity with specific knowledge.

exercises.prompt: Iakoiánehr tsi oiontátkon — she makes medicine / medicine woman. In Kanien'kehá:ka society, medicine knowledge is typically held by specific individuals and families. Why might this be?
exercises.correct: Medicine knowledge is powerful and requires training — not everyone who administers it will do so correctly or safely without proper preparation
options: Medicine knowledge is powerful and requires training — not everyone who administers it will do so correctly or safely without proper preparation
Medicine is kept secret to restrict access
Anyone can be a medicine person
Medicine knowledge is not important
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Traditional medicine knowledge is not casual — it is specific, precise, and powerful. The wrong plant, the wrong dose, the wrong ceremony can cause harm. Holding this knowledge responsibly means knowing its limits as well as its capacities.

exercises.prompt: Ronataierénhthon — basket makers (plural). The Ra- prefix marks male. What does this tell you about basket making as a role?
exercises.correct: Men also make baskets in Kanien'kehá:ka tradition — basket making is not exclusively a female practice
options: Men also make baskets in Kanien'kehá:ka tradition — basket making is not exclusively a female practice
The prefix is wrong
Only men make baskets
Basket making is not a traditional role
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ronataierénhthon — the male (or mixed group) basket makers. Basket making in Haudenosaunee tradition is practiced by both men and women, though specific styles and types may be associated with particular groups. The Ra- prefix in the word simply reflects who is being named in this particular form.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does the Ra- prefix mark?

exercises.prompt: Iakotsí:sten — she tans hides. Hide tanning is a skilled and labour-intensive process. What does the existence of a specific role word for this tell you?
exercises.correct: It is important enough to name — it is a specific skill that is recognised as a community role, not just a household task
options: It is important enough to name — it is a specific skill that is recognised as a community role, not just a household task
It is an unusual or rare activity
The word is no longer used
Hide tanning is a male role
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! When a language has a specific word for a role, that role is significant enough to be named. Iakotsí:sten — she tans hides — is a recognised skill, a community contribution, an identity. The language does not take it for granted.

exercises.prompt: The potter
exercises.storyPrompt: Iakoriwá:ienstha — she makes pots. A person who makes pots. Before metal cookware, before plastic, before stores — pots made from clay were what food was cooked in and water was carried in. The potter held knowledge of clay, of firing, of form. That knowledge fed the community. It is still held by people today. Iakoriwá:ienstha — her work is not in the past. When you say her name in Kanien'kéha, you are naming a role that has always existed and still exists. The language remembers.

lessons.title: A Full Gathering: Opening, Address, and Closing
lessons.sub: Putting it all together — the language of a community gathering from start to finish
intro: This is the final lesson of Unit 16. It brings together the vocabulary and phrases of the whole unit into the arc of a real community gathering: opening with greeting and the Thanksgiving Address, naming who is present, conducting the gathering's business, and closing with Onen' ki' wáhi.
You will not build every sentence of a full gathering here — that is the work of a lifetime of practice. But you can now recognise and use the main structural pieces.
Every gathering in Kanien'kehá:ka life follows this arc, from a small family meeting to a full council of the Confederacy.
lessons.ped: Integration as unit endpoint: Nation (2001): productive synthesis of all unit vocabulary in a meaningful, realistic context. The gathering arc is the most authentic possible context for community vocabulary — it is how all this vocabulary is actually used. LaFever all four domains: knowing the language (Mental), speaking it in context (Physical), belonging to the community it serves (Emotional), participating in the ceremonial life it holds (Spiritual).

exercises.prompt: A gathering begins. What is the first thing that happens?
exercises.correct: Greeting — Shé:kon — and then the Thanksgiving Address
options: Greeting — Shé:kon — and then the Thanksgiving Address
The chief speaks first
The business of the gathering begins immediately
The closing phrase Onen' ki' wáhi
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Greeting first, then the Thanksgiving Address — the words that come before all else. No gathering begins with business. The people are acknowledged, the world is acknowledged, and then the work begins.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what comes before all else?

exercises.prompt: Put the gathering arc in order
exercises.sub: Match each element to its position

pairs.english: The Thanksgiving Address — before all else
Closing — that is all, I am finished

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Greeting → Thanksgiving Address → acknowledge the people → conduct the gathering → Onen' ki' wáhi. This arc has structured Kanien'kehá:ka life for as long as people have been gathering.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the correct sequence?

exercises.prompt: At a council gathering, the clan mother and the chief are both present. Who opens the formal proceedings?
exercises.correct: Protocol varies — but the clan mother's authority frames everything; the chief speaks with her support and guidance
options: Protocol varies — but the clan mother's authority frames everything; the chief speaks with her support and guidance
The chief always speaks first
Whoever arrives first speaks first
Guests speak before community members
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The specific order of speaking varies by community and context. But the clan mother's authority is always present — the chief speaks knowing she has supported his appointment and watches his conduct. The gathering is not just the chief's — it is the community's.

exercises.prompt: Skennón:'a — peace. How does the word peace describe the ideal state of a Kanien'kehá:ka gathering?
exercises.correct: A gathering at peace is one where everyone is heard, decisions are made by consensus, and the community's wellbeing is the purpose
options: A gathering at peace is one where everyone is heard, decisions are made by consensus, and the community's wellbeing is the purpose
Peace means no disagreement is allowed
Peace only applies to external relations
It means the gathering is short
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Skennón:'a in the context of a gathering means the conditions for good decision-making: clear minds, heard voices, shared purpose. It does not mean the absence of disagreement — it means the presence of right process. The Great Law provides that process.

exercises.prompt: Say the gathering opening aloud — greeting, then the Address name
exercises.meaning: Hello. The words that come before all else.
exercises.hint: Shé:kon (warm, open, directed at everyone present). Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen (five and three syllables — deliberate, formal, the signal that what comes next is the Address). A two-sentence opening that has opened gatherings in this territory for centuries.

exercises.prompt: The gathering
exercises.meaning: The words that come before all else
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen. Every unit in this course has ended with Onen' ki' wáhi — and you have said it many times now. But the Thanksgiving Address comes before, not after. It opens everything. It is the container the gathering happens in. The earth. The waters. The plants. The animals. The fish. The birds. The winds. The Thunderers. The sun. The moon. The stars. The Creator. You have met all of them in this language. You know their names. When you hear the Thanksgiving Address now, you hear the names of beings you have been introduced to. That is what learning a language gives you. Not just words — introductions.

lessons.title: Review — Community Life
lessons.sub: Bringing the whole unit together
intro: This review brings together the key vocabulary and ideas from this unit.
Use it to consolidate what you know before moving on.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation review: Nation (2001): spaced retrieval review across all lesson content produces deeper retention than reviewing categories separately.

exercises.prompt: Match the community word to its role

pairs.english: The longhouse — centre of governance and ceremony
The Great Law — the Good Path

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Longhouse, Great Law, wampum, lacrosse — four words from the core of Kanien'kehá:ka community life.

exercises.prompt: Rotinonhsyón:ni — People of the Longhouse. After this unit, what do you understand that name to mean beyond 'people who live in longhouses'?
exercises.correct: A whole way of life — governance by clan mothers and chiefs, the Great Law, condolence, ceremony, council, and community
options: A whole way of life — governance, law, ceremony, and community
Only that they lived in longhouses historically
A political category used in treaties
A colonial name imposed on the people
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Rotinonhsyón:ni names a complete social and political system — the clan mothers, the chiefs, the Great Law, the condolence, the council, the longhouse fire. The name holds all of it.

exercises.prompt: Complete: peace — Skennón:___
exercises.correct: 'a
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Skennón:'a — peace. The word the Great Law was built to establish. The word the condolence ceremony restores.
exercises.wm: Please try again — glottal stop then a.

exercises.prompt: The Good Path
exercises.meaning: The Great Law of Peace / the Good Path
exercises.storyPrompt: Kaià:ner — the Good Path. You have now met the full vocabulary of community governance: the longhouse, the clan mothers, the chiefs, the Great Law, the condolence, the social dances, the singers, the wampum, lacrosse, the community roles, the invitation, and the full gathering arc. This is not museum vocabulary. This is the language of a living governance system that still functions. Kaià:ner. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 17 — Numbers in Depth: Counting People, Objects and Time
desc: Counting people differently from objects, ordinal numbers, telling time, days of the week, months, and numbers in ceremony

lessons.title: Numbers 1–100 Consolidated
lessons.sub: The whole system — how Kanien’kéha builds numbers
intro: You learned numbers 1–10 in Unit 4 and extended to 100 in Unit 9. This lesson consolidates the whole system so you can move through any number with confidence.

Kanien’kéha numbers are built on a base-10 additive system. Once you know 1–10 and the tens (20, 30, 40…), you can build any number up to 100 by combining them: Tékeni óia’k tánon Áhsen — twenty and three — twenty-three.
The system is more transparent than English, which has irregular forms between 11 and 19. In Kanien’kéha, eleven is literally ‘ten one’ — Óia’k énska.
lessons.ped: Number system transparency: The regularity of the Kanien’kéha number system is a genuine learning advantage — once learners know the parts, they can generate any number. Nation (2001): productive control of a transparent system. Physical domain: generating numbers fluently.

exercises.prompt: Numbers 1–5
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! One through four — the foundation of the whole number system.

exercises.prompt: Numbers 6–10
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Five through ten. Óia’k — ten — is the base for eleven, twelve, twenty, and every compound number above ten.

exercises.prompt: Óia’k énska means eleven — literally ‘ten one’. How do you say thirteen?
exercises.sub: Apply the same pattern
exercises.correct: Óia’k áhsen — ten three
options: Óia’k áhsen — ten three
Áhsen óia’k — three ten
Óia’k tékeni — ten two
Áhsen énska — three one
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Óia’k áhsen — ten three — thirteen. The pattern: Óia’k + [number 1–9] for eleven through nineteen.
exercises.wm: Please try again — Óia’k comes first, then the ones digit.

exercises.prompt: Tékeni óia’k means twenty — two tens. How do you say forty?
exercises.correct: Kaié:ri óia’k — four tens
options: Kaié:ri óia’k — four tens
Tékeni óia’k — two tens
Wísk óia’k — five tens
Óia’k kaié:ri — ten four
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraé:ta’ne! Kaié:ri óia’k — four tens — forty. The tens: Tékeni óia’k (20), Áhsen óia’k (30), Kaié:ri óia’k (40)…
exercises.wm: Please try again — which number means four?

exercises.prompt: Wísk óia’k means fifty. How do you say fifty-three?
exercises.sub: Combine the tens with the ones
exercises.correct: Wísk óia’k tánon Áhsen — fifty and three
options: Wísk óia’k tánon Áhsen — fifty and three
Wísk Áhsen — five three
Áhsen Wísk óia’k — three fifty
Óia’k áhsen Wísk
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wísk óia’k tánon Áhsen — fifty and three. Tánon (and) joins the tens to the ones in compound numbers above twenty.
exercises.wm: Please try again — tens first, then tánon, then ones.

exercises.prompt: Write the word for one hundred
exercises.correct: Tewá:sen
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tewá:sen — one hundred. Its own word, not a compound.
exercises.wm: Please try again — it is its own word.

exercises.prompt: Count aloud from one to ten
exercises.word: Énska — Tékeni — Áhsen — Kaié:ri — Wísk — À:ia’k — Tsà:ta — Sha’té:kon — Tióhton — Óia’k
exercises.hint: Take each number at its own pace. When you can count to ten without hesitating, you own the building blocks of the whole system.

exercises.prompt: Counting in your language
exercises.storyPrompt: Énska. Tékeni. Áhsen. The first three numbers. In residential schools, children were required to count in English, do arithmetic in English, measure the world in English. Learning to count in Kanien’kéha is not just arithmetic. It is reclaiming the right to measure the world in your own language. How many things can you count right now, in the room or place where you are?

lessons.title: Counting People
lessons.sub: A different set of numbers — because people are counted differently
intro: One of the most interesting things about Kanien’kéha numbers: you count people differently from objects. When you count people, you use a different set of number forms.
Many languages do this — special counting forms for people, animals, or flat objects. In Kanien’kéha, the person-counting context marks a real distinction the language considers important.
This lesson introduces person-counting alongside familiar object-counting numbers so you can see both.
lessons.ped: Person vs object counting: The distinction reflects how Kanien’kéha categorises its world. Nation (2001): contrastive presentation of both forms together. Mental domain: understanding the system. Physical domain: using the correct form.

exercises.prompt: To nihá:ti? means ‘how many?’ When you ask this about people, the answer uses a different form than when counting objects. Why might Kanien’kéha treat counting people differently?
exercises.correct: People are a different category from objects — the language marks that distinction in how they are counted
options: People are a different category from objects — the language marks that distinction in how they are counted
It is historical only
The numbers are the same — only word order changes
Person-counting only applies to large groups
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanien’kéha marks the difference between people and objects through pronouns, prefix systems, and number forms. Counting a person with the same form as counting a stone flattens a distinction the language considers important.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehshón:’a — the people / human beings. This word groups all people together as a collective. How does this connect to Kanien’kéha’s way of counting people?
exercises.correct: Both reflect that people are understood as a category that deserves its own specific language — not just objects to be counted
options: Both reflect that people are understood as a category that deserves its own specific language — not just objects to be counted
They are unrelated
Onkwehshón:’a replaces number counting
The word is only used in the Thanksgiving Address
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Thanksgiving Address begins with Onkwehshón:’a — the people — acknowledged as a whole. And when individual people are counted, they get their own forms. People are not objects. The language knows this.

exercises.prompt: When counting two objects you say Tékeni. When counting two people in context, the form shifts. What does this shift signal to the listener?
exercises.correct: That the things being counted are people — the form itself carries that information
options: That the things being counted are people — the form itself carries that information
That it is a formal count
That the count is uncertain
Numbers always mean objects unless otherwise stated
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraé:ta’ne! The person-counting form tells the listener: these are people we are talking about. The number carries social meaning — not just quantity but category.

exercises.prompt: Five people are coming to the gathering. How do you signal in Kanien’kéha that you are counting people and not objects?
exercises.correct: Through context — using person nouns or pronouns alongside the number makes clear that people are being counted
options: Through context — using person nouns or pronouns alongside the number makes clear that people are being counted
By saying the number louder
Numbers always mean objects unless specified
There is no difference
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Context does significant work in Kanien’kéha counting. When you are clearly talking about people, the person-counting reading applies naturally.

exercises.prompt: Counting people
exercises.storyPrompt: Onkwehshón:’a — the people. In the Thanksgiving Address they are not counted. They are acknowledged as a whole. When individual people are counted, they get their own forms. This is the language saying: you do not count a person the way you count a stone. People matter. The language knows this.

lessons.title: Ordinal Numbers
lessons.sub: First, second, third — putting things in order
intro: Ordinal numbers — first, second, third — are built differently from cardinal numbers. You already know the start: Tsi naió:re — first. Tékeni tsi naió:re — second.
The phrase tsi naió:re means something like ‘that is the measure / the count’ — it turns a cardinal into an ordinal by specifying a position in a sequence.
Ordinals appear in daily life — the first frost, the second moon, the third clan — and in ceremony, where order matters deeply.
lessons.ped: Ordinals as sequencing vocabulary: Nation (2001): ordinals best learned after cardinals are solid, in functional contexts. Physical domain: producing ordinal expressions. Spiritual domain: the nations of the Confederacy have a traditional order.

exercises.prompt: First through fourth
exercises.sub: Pattern: [number] + tsi naió:re
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! First through fourth. The pattern is consistent: [number] + tsi naió:re = ordinal position.

exercises.prompt: Build the ordinal for fifth: ___ tsi naió:re
exercises.correct: Wísk
exercises.hint: W
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wísk tsi naió:re — fifth.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the word for five?

exercises.prompt: The Kanien’kehá:ka are often listed first — tsi naió:re — in the Confederacy council order. Does first mean most important?
exercises.correct: No — order of speaking does not mean rank. It creates a structure so every nation’s voice is heard in turn.
options: No — order of speaking does not mean rank. It creates a structure so every nation’s voice is heard in turn.
Yes — first nation is the most powerful
Order changes every meeting
Only guests speak first
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraé:ta’ne! The Haudenosaunee Confederacy is built on the equality of all nations. Speaking first is a role, not a rank. The Kanien’kehá:ka as Keepers of the Eastern Door often open, but every nation has equal standing.

exercises.prompt: Write ‘second’ in Kanien’kéha
exercises.correct: Tékeni tsi naió:re
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tékeni tsi naió:re — second.
exercises.wm: Please try again — two + the sequencing phrase.

exercises.prompt: Say the first three ordinals aloud
exercises.word: Tsi naió:re — Tékeni tsi naió:re — Áhsen tsi naió:re
exercises.hint: tsi na-IÓ:-re (4 syllables). Say them in sequence — the pattern will become automatic.

exercises.prompt: First
exercises.storyPrompt: Tsi naió:re — first. The first frost. The first goose to return in spring. The first green shoot of corn through the soil. In the ceremonial calendar, the first strawberry calls the Strawberry Ceremony. Firsts are not just positions in a sequence. They are signals. What is the first thing you notice in Kanien’kéha when you read a new sentence now?

lessons.title: Telling Time: Hours
lessons.sub: What time is it? — hours and the clock
intro: You learned Tsi niiohtonhátie? — what time is it? — in Unit 9. And you know the numbers. This lesson puts them together for the full time-telling system.
Énska niiohtonhátie — it is one o’clock. The word niiohtonhátie turns any number into an hour. It is the only new piece — and you have seen it since Unit 9.
Time-telling in Kanien’kéha uses the same numbers you already know. Number + niiohtonhátie = that hour.
lessons.ped: Time-telling as number application: Nation (2001): productive use of known vocabulary in a new functional frame. Physical domain: producing time expressions.

exercises.prompt: Énska niiohtonhátie — one o’clock. What does niiohtonhátie add to the number?
exercises.correct: It turns the number into a time expression — one o’clock, two o’clock, etc.
options: It turns the number into a time expression — one o’clock, two o’clock, etc.
It makes the number larger
It makes it a question
It adds ‘approximately’
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Niiohtonhátie is the frame that turns a number into an hour. Any number + niiohtonhátie = that hour.

exercises.prompt: Match the time expression
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! [Number] + niiohtonhátie = that o’clock. The same pattern every time.

exercises.prompt: Complete the question ‘What time is it?’: Tsi ___?
exercises.correct: niiohtonhátie
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsi niiohtonhátie? — what time is it? Now you can answer it with any hour.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which word completes the time question?

exercises.prompt: How do you say ‘it is seven o’clock’?
exercises.correct: Tsà:ta niiohtonhátie
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsà:ta niiohtonhátie — seven o’clock.
exercises.wm: Please try again — seven is Tsà:ta.

exercises.prompt: Ask and answer the time
exercises.meaning: What time is it? — It is three o’clock.
exercises.hint: Tsi nii-oh-ton-HÁ-tie? (rising, 6 syllables). Áhsen nii-oh-ton-HÁ-tie (firm, 8 syllables). Practise with different hours — substitute any number for Áhsen.

exercises.prompt: Keeping time
exercises.storyPrompt: Niiohtonhátie — the measure of the hour. Clock time arrived with colonisation and the demands of school schedules, work shifts, and government offices. Kanien’kéha has adapted to include it. The traditional daily structure was organised by sunrise, noon, sunset, the phases of the moon, the position of stars. What would it look like to measure your day by sunrise and moonrise instead of by the clock?

lessons.title: Days of the Week
lessons.sub: Seven day names — and the history they carry
intro: The seven-day week arrived in Kanien’kéha with European contact — specifically with Catholic missionaries. Some day names carry traces of saints’ names. Others are more descriptive.
Knowing them is practical — they are used every day in conversation. And knowing their history is part of knowing the language.
Seven names that carry a history of contact, adaptation, and the ongoing vitality of a language that keeps growing.
lessons.ped: Days as contact vocabulary: The day names are a site where colonial history is visible in the language itself. Presented with that context, not erased. García & Li Wei (2014): translanguaging acknowledges contact history. Nation (2001): high-frequency set vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Soséhne — Wednesday — carries traces of the name ‘Joseph’ inside it. What does this tell you about the day names?
exercises.correct: The day names were shaped by Catholic missionaries — European calendar vocabulary was adapted into Kanien’kéha sounds
options: The day names were shaped by Catholic missionaries — European calendar vocabulary was adapted into Kanien’kéha sounds
The names are purely traditional
Soséhne has no European connection
All day names are original
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Soséhne sounds like Kanien’kéha but carries Joseph inside it. This is how living languages absorb contact — they take what comes and speak it in their own voice.

exercises.prompt: Four of the seven days
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Sunday, Monday, Wednesday, Saturday. The Awent- root in three of these relates to day and light.

exercises.prompt: The remaining three days
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tuesday, Thursday, and Friday have some of the longest day names. Ronwaia’tanontaktónhne — Friday — is seven syllables of history.

exercises.prompt: Say all seven days in order
exercises.word: Awentatokenhtí:ke — Awententaón:ke — Ratironhia’kehronón:ke — Soséhne — Okarihstiiaháne — Ronwaia’tanontaktónhne — Awentákta
exercises.hint: Take your time. Some of these are long. Say each completely before moving on. When you can say all seven in order without stopping, you know the week.

exercises.prompt: The week
exercises.storyPrompt: Soséhne — Wednesday, carrying Joseph’s name inside it. Ronwaia’tanontaktónhne — Friday, seven syllables of history. The weekly calendar came with missionaries who wanted to impose their time structure. And yet Kanien’kéha absorbed it — through its own sounds, its own syllable patterns, its own roots. This is what a living language does. It takes what comes and speaks it in its own voice.

lessons.title: Months: The Traditional Moon Names
lessons.sub: Twelve moons — each one named for what is happening on the land
intro: Unlike the day names, the month names in Kanien’kéha are mostly traditional — they describe what is happening in the natural world during that moon. January is the great cold moon. August is the green corn moon. October is the harvest winding down moon.
You already know six of the month names from Unit 14’s seasonal vocabulary. This lesson consolidates all twelve.
Many month names use the -kó:wa suffix — great — which you have seen in Niawenhkó:wa and Skennón:wa. The great cold moon, the great summer moon.
lessons.ped: Month names as land-based calendar: The month names encode ecological and ceremonial knowledge. LaFever Spiritual domain: the calendar is embedded in the land. Nation (2001): twelve related words as a semantic set.

exercises.prompt: Tsiothorhkó:wa — January — means ‘the great cold moon’. The -kó:wa suffix means great. Where have you seen this before?
exercises.correct: In Niawenhkó:wa (great thanks), Skennón:wa (great peace), Kaià:ner’kó:wa (Great Law)
options: In Niawenhkó:wa (great thanks), Skennón:wa (great peace), Kaià:ner’kó:wa (Great Law)
Only in month names
Only in place names
It has no meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! -kó:wa amplifies whatever it follows. Tsiothorhkó:wa — the great cold — is January at its most intense.
exercises.wm: Please try again — where have you seen -kó:wa before?

exercises.prompt: Winter and spring months
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Winter cold → lengthening days → buds opening → great buds. The months tell the story of winter giving way to spring.

exercises.prompt: Summer and autumn months
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraé:ta’ne! Corn appears in two consecutive months — August and September — because the corn season is that central to the calendar.

exercises.prompt: Seskéha is August and Seskehkó:wa is September. Why does corn get two moons?
exercises.correct: The corn season spans both months — green corn appears in August, the full harvest in September
options: The corn season spans both months — green corn appears in August, the full harvest in September
The months were named by accident
Corn is only relevant in September
The green corn moon refers to a different plant
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Corn is so central to Kanien’kehá:ka life that two consecutive moons carry its name. The calendar is built around what the land is doing.

exercises.prompt: Say all twelve months in order — slowly
exercises.word: Tsiothorhkó:wa — Enníska — Enniskó:wa — Onerahtókha — Onerahtohkó:wa — Ohiaríha — Ohyarihkó:wa — Seskéha — Seskehkó:wa — Kenténha — Kentenhkó:wa — Tsiothórha
exercises.hint: Notice the pairs: cold moon and great cold moon, buds moon and great buds moon, corn moon and great corn moon, autumn moon and great autumn moon. When you know the pattern, twelve becomes six pairs.

exercises.prompt: The green corn moon
exercises.storyPrompt: Seskéha — the green corn moon. August. The corn is not yet fully dry — it is still green and sweet, tender enough to eat off the cob. The Green Corn Ceremony gives thanks for it at this moment — before the full harvest, when the corn is at its most alive. The month is named for this moment. Not for the harvest, not for the drying and grinding — for the corn in its living, growing, green state. The calendar honours the corn at its most itself.

lessons.title: Numbers in Ceremony
lessons.sub: What the numbers count in Kanien’kehá:ka life
intro: Numbers in Kanien’kehá:ka life are not just practical — they carry ceremonial and political meaning. Three clans. Five (then six) nations. The three sisters. The four sacred medicines. The fifty original chiefs.
This lesson connects the number vocabulary across this unit to its ceremonial and political uses. Numbers structure everything from kinship to governance.
You have the number vocabulary. Now see what it counts.
lessons.ped: Numbers as cultural structure: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): language vocabulary connected to its living cultural context. LaFever Spiritual domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Match the number to what it counts in Kanien’kehá:ka life

pairs.english: The Kanien’kehá:ka clans: Turtle, Bear, Wolf
The original five nations of the Confederacy
