The Confederacy after the Tuscarora joined
The four sacred medicines: tobacco, cedar, sweet grass, sage

exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraé:ta’ne! Three clans, five original nations, six nations today, four sacred medicines. Numbers organise Kanien’kehá:ka life at every level.

exercises.prompt: Áhsen — three. Three clans, three sisters, three broad sections of the Thanksgiving Address. What does three seem to represent in Kanien’kehá:ka structure?
exercises.correct: Completeness — three is a number of wholeness and balance across clan, agricultural, and ceremonial life
options: Completeness — three is a number of wholeness and balance across clan, agricultural, and ceremonial life
An arbitrary coincidence
The number of seasons
The number of longhouses in a community
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Three appears as a structural number across clan organisation, agriculture, and ceremony. Clans, sisters, sections of the Address — three signals a complete, balanced unit.

exercises.prompt: The Tuscarora came north after being displaced from their territory and the Haudenosaunee accepted them as the sixth nation around 1722. What does this tell you about the Confederacy?
exercises.correct: The Confederacy could grow — it adapted to welcome new members without abandoning its principles
options: The Confederacy could grow — it adapted to welcome new members without abandoning its principles
The original five nations were replaced
The number six was always planned
The Tuscarora were forced to join
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraé:ta’ne! The Great Law expanded to hold the Tuscarora. The number changed from five to six, and the Confederacy grew to include them. This is what a living governance system does.

exercises.prompt: Write the number six in Kanien’kéha — the number of nations in the Confederacy today
exercises.correct: À:ia’k
exercises.hint: À
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! À:ia’k — six. Kanien’kehá:ka, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca, Tuscarora. Six nations, one law, one fire.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which number word means six?

exercises.prompt: The Great Law established fifty original chieftainship titles. That is Wísk óia’k. What does having exactly fifty titles tell you about the Confederacy’s design?
exercises.correct: The number was carefully assigned — each nation and clan received specific titles to maintain balance across the Confederacy
options: The number was carefully assigned — each nation and clan received specific titles to maintain balance across the Confederacy
Fifty was chosen randomly
All fifty came from one nation
The number has no significance
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraé:ta’ne! The fifty chieftainship titles were distributed to create balance. The Onondaga as central nation and Keepers of the Fire hold the most. The system is precise — the numbers are part of the law.

exercises.prompt: Six
exercises.storyPrompt: À:ia’k — six. The Tuscarora came north after being displaced from what is now North Carolina, and the Haudenosaunee accepted them as the sixth nation around 1722. The Confederacy grew to hold them. The number changed from five to six and the Great Law expanded to include them. This is what a living governance system does — it adapts to welcome new members without abandoning its principles. The number six is not just a count. It is a record of inclusion.

lessons.title: Age and Time Elapsed
lessons.sub: Saying how old something is and how much time has passed
intro: You learned to ask and answer about age in Unit 12 — counting winters. This lesson extends that to duration: how long something has been happening, how many moons, how many years.
The age vocabulary combines number words with time words: niió:re’ (years / winters). The same numbers you already know, now measuring time elapsed.
You can now say almost anything about time in Kanien’kéha — what hour, which day, which moon, how many winters.
lessons.ped: Age and duration: Nation (2001): high-frequency time vocabulary. Known numbers plus known time words produce new functional expressions. Physical domain: producing age and duration sentences.

exercises.prompt: Niiawen’okón:’a niió:re’ asks ‘how old are you?’ What does niió:re’ mean?
exercises.correct: Year(s) / winters — the unit of time used to count age
options: Year(s) / winters — the unit of time used to count age
Months
Days
Seasons
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Niió:re’ — years / winters. The age question asks how many of these you are.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what unit does the age question use?

exercises.prompt: [Number] niió:re’ wakén:iate — I am [number] years old. If you are thirty, which number do you use?
exercises.correct: Áhsen óia’k — thirty
options: Áhsen óia’k — thirty
Tékeni óia’k — twenty
Kaié:ri óia’k — forty
Áhsen — three
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Áhsen óia’k niió:re’ wakén:iate — I am thirty years old. Thirty winters. The same number system, now measuring a life.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which is the word for thirty?

exercises.prompt: Complete: I am twenty-two years old — Tékeni óia’k tánon Tékeni ___ wakén:iate
exercises.correct: niió:re’
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tékeni óia’k tánon Tékeni niió:re’ wakén:iate — I am twenty-two years old. Number + niió:re’ + wakén:iate.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which word means years/winters?

exercises.prompt: ___ na’tewakohseriyá:konh — ___ is my age. How is this different from the niió:re’ pattern?
exercises.correct: It states your age as a fact about you — ‘that is my age’ — rather than counting the winters
options: It states your age as a fact about you — ‘that is my age’ — rather than counting the winters
It is exactly the same
It is only used for elders
It requires a different number system
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraé:ta’ne! Two ways to say your age — one counts the winters, one states it as a characteristic. Both are correct. A language with depth has multiple ways to express the same thing.

exercises.prompt: Ask and answer: how old are you?
exercises.word: Niiawen’okón:’a niió:re’? — Tékeni óia’k niió:re’ wakén:iate.
exercises.meaning: How old are you? — I am twenty years old.
exercises.hint: Niiawen’okón:’a niió:re’? (rising question). Tékeni óia’k niió:re’ wakén:iate (firm, clear). Substitute your own age.

exercises.prompt: Winters
exercises.storyPrompt: Niió:re’ — winters. Your age is measured in winters. Not in the turn of a calendar, but in the cycle that marks the Haudenosaunee year most deeply — winter, Midwinter Ceremony, the long gathering indoors, the storytelling nights, the cold that tests and the warmth that restores. Every winter you have lived through is one niió:re’. How many winters are you? And what has each one taught you?

lessons.title: A Full Time Conversation
lessons.sub: Putting it all together — time in a real exchange
intro: This is the final lesson of Unit 17. It brings together the time vocabulary from the whole unit into a real conversation: what time is it, what day is it, what month, how old are you, how long did that take.
You now have the vocabulary to talk about time in Kanien’kéha in nearly any context — hours, days, months, age, duration, ordinals, counting people and objects.
Use it. That is what it is for.
lessons.ped: Integration as unit endpoint: Nation (2001): productive synthesis of all unit vocabulary in a realistic conversational context. Physical domain primary: generating multiple time expressions fluently.

exercises.prompt: Someone asks: Tsi niiohtonhátie? It is quarter past eight in the morning. What is the closest answer in Kanien’kéha?
exercises.correct: Sha’té:kon niiohtonhátie — eight o’clock (the closest full hour)
options: Sha’té:kon niiohtonhátie — eight o’clock
Wísk niiohtonhátie — five o’clock
Tékeni niiohtonhátie — two o’clock
Tsà:ta niiohtonhátie — seven o’clock
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Sha’té:kon niiohtonhátie — eight o’clock. Kanien’kéha time-telling gives you the hour; minutes are expressed separately in fuller speech.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which number is eight?

exercises.prompt: Match the time question to a natural answer

pairs.english: Wísk óia’k niió:re’ wakén:iate — I am fifty years old

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! A range of time and number questions, each with its right answer. You can handle all of them now.

exercises.prompt: The Green Corn Ceremony happens in Seskéha — August. What information do you now have about August from this course?
exercises.sub: Think about everything you know about this month
exercises.correct: It is the green corn moon, the hottest part of summer, and the time of the Green Corn Ceremony — the season, the weather, and the ceremony are all connected
options: It is the green corn moon, the hottest part of summer, and the time of the Green Corn Ceremony — the season, the weather, and the ceremony are all connected
It is only a calendar month
The Green Corn Ceremony happens in winter
The month name has no cultural meaning
exercises.cm: Io’nikonhraé:ta’ne! Seskéha: green corn moon, hot weather (Iotontátsion), Green Corn Ceremony. Month name, weather vocabulary, and ceremonial calendar — all pointing to the same moment. The language holds them together.

exercises.prompt: Today is Monday. Complete: onkwa’nikón:ra ___
exercises.correct: Awententaón:ke
exercises.hint: A
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! onkwa’nikón:ra Awententaón:ke — today is Monday. Today (onkwa’nikón:ra from Unit 13) + day name.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which day name means Monday?

exercises.prompt: Say a complete time-and-date statement aloud
exercises.meaning: Today is Monday. It is three o’clock.
exercises.hint: Two sentences. Day first, then time. Say them clearly — you are giving someone the information they need to know when they are.

exercises.prompt: Time in the language
exercises.storyPrompt: Óia’k — ten. The base of the number system. Ten fingers, ten toes, ten as the foundation of how Kanien’kéha counts. You now know how to count to one hundred, how to count people and objects, how to say first and second and third, what time it is, what day it is, what moon it is, how old you are, and how many of the sacred nations there are. Time is not just a number. In Kanien’kéha, time is a season, a moon, a ceremony, a winter counted. Onen’ ki’ wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Numbers in Depth
lessons.sub: Bringing the whole unit together
intro: This review brings together the key vocabulary and ideas from this unit.
Use it to consolidate what you know before moving on.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation review: Nation (2001): spaced retrieval review across all lesson content produces deeper retention than reviewing categories separately.

exercises.prompt: Match the number to what it counts in Kanien'kehá:ka life

pairs.english: The original nations of the Confederacy
The Confederacy after the Tuscarora joined

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Three clans, five nations, six nations, twenty winters. Numbers organise Kanien'kehá:ka life at every level.

exercises.prompt: Énska niiohtonhátie — one o'clock. Seskéha — August. Tékeni óia'k niió:re' wakén:iate — I am twenty years old. What do these three sentences have in common?
exercises.correct: All three express time — the hour, the month, and an age — using the number system
options: All three express time using numbers
They are all greetings
They all use weather vocabulary
They are all about the Thanksgiving Address
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Hours, months, age — all measured with the same number words. Once you know the numbers, you can measure time in all its forms.

exercises.prompt: Complete: August (the green corn moon) — Sesk___
exercises.correct: éha
exercises.hint: é
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Seskéha — August, the green corn moon. The month named for the corn at its most alive.
exercises.wm: Please try again — éha closes the month name.

exercises.prompt: Numbers in the language
exercises.storyPrompt: The numbers you have learned in this unit count people, objects, hours, days, moons, years, and the nations of the Confederacy. They are not neutral tools. They measure what matters. Three clans. Six nations. One hundred years of the Indian Act. The number system is one of the most functional vocabularies in the language — and one of the most political. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 18 — The Verb in Depth I: Transitive Verbs & Object Agreement
desc: How Kanien'kéha verbs encode both the actor and the thing acted upon — transitive verbs, object prefixes, and agreement.

lessons.title: What Is a Transitive Verb?
lessons.sub: When a verb reaches out and affects something else
intro: So far most of the verbs you have learned are intransitive — they describe what someone does without specifying what they do it to. Ká:tiens — I am sitting. Katkáhthons — I am listening.

Transitive verbs describe actions that reach out and affect something else: I see it, she carries it, he cuts the wood. In Kanien'kéha, the object — the thing being acted upon — is encoded directly in the verb through a prefix, not as a separate word.
This lesson introduces what transitive verbs are and how to recognise them before practising their forms.
lessons.ped: Transitive vs intransitive as structural distinction: Mithun (1982): the transitive/intransitive distinction is one of the most fundamental organisational features of Kanien'kéha verb morphology. Nation (2001): known verbs (see, carry, cut) reappear in new structural frame.

exercises.prompt: Sahrón:ni — you make it. The 'it' is already in the verb. What does this tell you about transitive verbs in Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: The object (it) is encoded in the verb itself through a prefix — it does not float free as a separate word
options: The object is encoded in the verb itself through a prefix
'It' is a separate word after the verb
Transitive verbs have no object
Only animate objects can be in the verb
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Sahrón:ni — you make it. The 'it' is inside the verb. This is how transitive verbs work in Kanien'kéha: the object prefix is built into the verb form.

exercises.prompt: Sort these verbs: transitive (has an object) or intransitive (no object)?

pairs.english: Intransitive — no object, just the action

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Sit and run — intransitive. Make it and carry it — transitive. The 'it' marks the transitive object.

exercises.prompt: Satkatho — you look / look (command). Is this transitive or intransitive?
exercises.correct: It can be both — 'you look' is intransitive, 'you look at it' adds a transitive object prefix
options: It can be both — intransitive alone, transitive with an object prefix
It is always intransitive
It is always transitive
Stative verbs cannot be transitive
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Some verbs can be used both ways. Satkatho — you look (intransitive). With an object prefix: you look at it (transitive). The prefix changes the verb's relationship to the world.

exercises.prompt: In English: 'I see the bear'. In Kanien'kéha the bear does not float after the verb — instead, the object relationship is in the prefix. What advantage does this have?
exercises.correct: The verb is self-contained — it tells you both who acts and what is affected without needing word order to clarify
options: The verb is self-contained — it tells you both actor and object without word order
It is just a stylistic difference
Word order still determines meaning
The bear must always be named separately
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanien'kéha verbs are highly self-contained. A single verb form can express what English needs multiple words to say. The price is more complex verb morphology. The benefit is enormous expressive efficiency.

exercises.prompt: Reaching out
exercises.storyPrompt: Sahsénhawe — you carry it. Such a small verb. But it contains: you (the actor), the carrying action, and it (the thing being carried). Three pieces of information in one word. This is what transitive verbs do in Kanien'kéha — they reach out from the actor and pull the object into the verb itself. The sentence does not need a separate object word because the verb already holds the relationship. This is not just grammar. It is a way of seeing actions as inherently connected to the things they affect.

lessons.title: Object Prefixes: It, Him, Her, Them
lessons.sub: The prefixes that encode what is being acted upon
intro: In Kanien'kéha transitive verbs, the object is encoded by a prefix that appears before the verb root. The prefix changes depending on what is being acted upon: an inanimate object (it), a male person (him), a female person (her), a group (them).
You already know person prefixes for the actor. Object prefixes work on the same logic but from the other direction — they mark who is receiving the action.
This lesson introduces the most common object prefix patterns and practises them with verbs you already know.
lessons.ped: Object prefix system: Mithun (1982): the object prefix system is one of the most complex but most regular features of Kanien'kéha morphology. Nation (2001): known verbs in new structural frame. Krashen (+1): familiar roots, new prefix.

exercises.prompt: Sahrón:ni — you make it. The object is inanimate (it). When the object is a specific male person — you make him something — the prefix changes. What does this change signal?
exercises.correct: The object prefix encodes not just 'there is an object' but specifically what kind of object — animate vs inanimate, male vs female
options: The object prefix encodes what kind of object — animate/inanimate, male/female
The prefix change is cosmetic only
Only inanimate objects can be encoded
The verb root changes for animate objects
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Object prefixes carry information about the object — not just that something is being acted on, but who or what it is. Animate and inanimate, male and female — the grammar tracks all of these.

exercises.prompt: Match the object prefix type to what it encodes

pairs.english: You act on an inanimate thing

pairs.target: rake- / ako- (him / her acting on me)
pairs.english: A person acts on me specifically
You act on a male or female person

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Object prefixes: inanimate (it), animate male, animate female, plural. The system tracks who is affected with the same precision it tracks who acts.

exercises.prompt: Skwe'tarón:ko — you cut it up. The 'it' object is inanimate — you are cutting a thing. If you were cutting for someone (cutting his meat), the prefix would change. What does this grammatical requirement force the speaker to do?
exercises.correct: Specify exactly what is being cut and for whom — the grammar requires precision about the relationship
options: Specify exactly what is cut and for whom — the grammar requires relational precision
Nothing changes — context handles it
A separate word is added after the verb
The verb root changes entirely
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kanien'kéha grammar requires you to be specific about relationships. You cannot say 'cut' without saying what is being cut — and if a person is involved, you must mark that too. The grammar forces relational clarity.

exercises.prompt: Sahsénhawe — you carry it (inanimate). The sa- marks you (actor). What does the -ha- in -hawe encode?
exercises.correct: object
exercises.hint: o
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The -ha- (or similar) in transitive verb forms encodes the inanimate object — it. You carry it: you (sa-) + inanimate object marker + carry root.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does -ha- encode?

exercises.prompt: Raksatá:ti — he sees me. You learned this in Unit 11 as a relational prefix. How is this the same as the transitive object system?
exercises.correct: It is the same system — relational prefixes are how Kanien'kéha handles animate objects in transitive verbs
options: It is the same system — relational prefixes handle animate objects in transitive verbs
They are completely different systems
Relational prefixes only apply to kinship verbs
Raksatá:ti is intransitive
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The relational prefixes from Unit 11 are the transitive object system for animate objects. When you say 'he sees me', the rak- is the object prefix encoding me as the affected party. Unit 11 and Unit 18 are the same system from different angles.

exercises.prompt: The object in the verb
exercises.meaning: He acts on me / She acts on me (relational object prefix)
exercises.storyPrompt: Rak-. Ako-. These two-syllable prefixes hold a relationship. He sees me, she helps me, he gives me — in every case the prefix encodes both the actor and the person affected. The verb is a map of a relationship, not just an action. In a language built like this, you cannot talk about actions without talking about who is involved. Every transitive verb is a statement about relationship as much as about action.

lessons.title: Seeing, Giving, Telling: High-Frequency Transitive Verbs
lessons.sub: The most common transitive verbs in daily use
intro: Some transitive verbs are used so frequently in daily life that knowing them well gives you enormous conversational reach. Seeing, giving, telling, showing, helping, feeding — these are the verbs of everyday relationship.
You already know the seeing verb (raksatá:ti — he sees me) from Unit 11. This lesson adds the giving, telling, and showing verbs and practises them across object types.
Each of these verbs will appear in the conversation exercises that follow.
lessons.ped: High-frequency transitive verbs: Nation (2001): high-frequency vocabulary should be prioritised. The seeing, giving, telling, and helping verbs appear constantly in daily speech. Physical domain primary: production of transitive verb sentences.

exercises.prompt: Skahó:wi — you give (command). This is a transitive verb. What two things must the prefix system track for this verb?
exercises.correct: Who is giving (actor) and who or what is being given to (recipient/object)
options: Who is giving and who or what is being given to
Only who is giving
Only what is being given
The time of the giving
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Giving requires tracking both the giver and the recipient. The transitive prefix system handles both. Skahó:wi (you give) — to whom? The fuller form specifies.

exercises.prompt: Match the high-frequency transitive verb to its meaning

pairs.english: It makes our minds happy (gives thanks to us)

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four high-frequency transitive verbs — seeing, giving, giving-thanks, helping. All encode the relationship between actor and affected party.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — it makes our minds happy. This is from the Thanksgiving Address. Is it transitive?
exercises.correct: Yes — it acts on our minds (us), making it transitive. The ionkhi- prefix encodes 'our minds' as the object.
options: Yes — it acts on our minds. The ionkhi- prefix encodes us as the object.
No — it is intransitive because no person is named
Only ceremonial verbs can be transitive
The Thanksgiving Address uses a different system
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — it makes our minds happy. The ionkhi- is the object prefix encoding us as the ones being affected. You have been encountering transitive verbs since Unit 5 without having a name for them.

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'I see you'. In Kanien'kéha, which system handles the 'you' object?
exercises.correct: The relational prefix system — a prefix encodes both 'I' (actor) and 'you' (object) in one prefix
options: The relational prefix system encodes both I and you in one prefix
A separate word for 'you' follows the verb
Word order places 'you' after the verb
The verb root changes for this combination
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! 'I see you' uses a relational prefix that encodes I→you in one morpheme. The same system you learned in Unit 11 — now you understand why it exists: it is the transitive object system for animate objects.

exercises.prompt: Complete the giving command: skahó:___
exercises.correct: wi
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Skahó:wi — you give. The -wi ending closes the command form.

exercises.prompt: Giving
exercises.storyPrompt: Skahó:wi — give. The verb of generosity. In Kanien'kehá:ka culture, giving — to family, to guests, to community gatherings — is one of the most valued social acts. The Giveaway ceremony, practiced across many Haudenosaunee communities, distributes goods as an act of honour and gratitude. Skahó:wi. The grammar of this verb requires you to know who you are giving to. Generosity in Kanien'kéha is always specific: you give to someone, not just in general.

lessons.title: Building Transitive Sentences
lessons.sub: Putting actor, verb, and object together in complete sentences
intro: You now know the theory: transitive verbs encode both actor and object. This lesson practises building complete transitive sentences — specifying who does the action, what they do, and who or what is affected.
Kanien'kéha sentences with transitive verbs are extremely compact. What takes English six or seven words often takes Kanien'kéha two or three.
This lesson focuses on production — generating transitive sentences from known vocabulary.
lessons.ped: Transitive sentence production: Nation (2001): productive control requires sentence-level practice beyond isolated word knowledge. Physical domain primary: generating complete transitive sentences.

exercises.prompt: Wahró:nis — he makes it. You know sahrón:ni (you make it). What changed between 'you make it' and 'he makes it'?
exercises.correct: The actor prefix changed from sa- (you) to wa- (he) — the root and object encoding stayed the same
options: The actor prefix changed from sa- (you) to wa- (he)
The root changed
The object prefix changed
The verb is now intransitive
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Actor prefix sa- → wa-. Same root. Same object encoding. The paradigm shift for transitive verbs works the same way as for intransitive — change the actor prefix, keep everything else.

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'the bear eats the corn'. In Kanien'kéha, how is this expressed?
exercises.correct: The eating verb carries an object prefix encoding corn (inanimate), and the bear is the actor encoded in the prefix — the whole thing may be one complex verb
options: The eating verb carries both actor and inanimate object in its prefixes
Bear and corn are both separate words after the verb
Word order determines actor vs object
A separate object word follows the verb
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! In Kanien'kéha, a sentence like 'the bear eats the corn' can be expressed in a single highly complex verb form with bear as actor prefix and corn as object prefix — or with the nouns mentioned separately for clarity. Both options exist.

exercises.prompt: Raksakhó:wi — he gives it to me. The rak- encodes he→me. The -s- or -kh- encodes the giving action. What does this one word replace in English?
exercises.correct: 'He gives it to me' — five English words compressed into one Kanien'kéha verb form
options: 'He gives it to me' — five words in one verb form
'He gives' only
'To me' only
Two separate verbs
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Raksakhó:wi — he gives it to me. Five English words: he / gives / it / to / me. One Kanien'kéha word: actor (he) + object (me) + giving root. This compression is not unusual in Kanien'kéha — it is how the language works.

exercises.prompt: Match the transitive sentence to its English meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Make (you), make (he), make (I), give (you). The same roots across different actor prefixes. The paradigm is working.

exercises.prompt: Complete: I carry it — Akhsénha___
exercises.correct: we
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Akhsénhawe — I carry it. Akh- marks I as actor; -we closes the form.

exercises.prompt: Transitive sentences in daily life
exercises.meaning: You make it — He makes it — I make it
exercises.storyPrompt: Sahrón:ni — wahró:nis — akhró:nis. The same making action across three actors. This is the paradigm working in real life: the basket maker makes it (wahró:nis, if she is a woman: kahró:nis), the potter makes it, the hide tanner makes it. The verb changes its actor prefix with each maker. The root stays the same because the action — making — is the same. Transitive verbs in Kanien'kéha are like that: the root names the action, the prefixes name the relationship.

lessons.title: Asking Transitive Questions
lessons.sub: Who sees what? Who gives what to whom? Asking about transitive events
intro: Questions about transitive events require asking about both the actor and the object: who sees it? What do you give her? Who told him?
You know the question words from Unit 20: Ónhka (who), Nahò:ten (what), Ka' (where). This lesson applies them specifically to transitive verb questions.
Asking transitive questions is the conversational extension of everything you have built in this unit.
lessons.ped: Transitive questions as conversational skill: Nation (2001): question-answer pairs in functional context. Physical domain: producing transitive questions and answers.

exercises.prompt: Ónhka ne raónha? — Who is he? You know this from Unit 12. How do you use Ónhka to ask 'Who sees you?'
exercises.correct: Ónhka saksatá:ti? — Who sees you? (Ónhka + the seeing verb with you as object)
options: Ónhka saksatá:ti? — Who sees you?
Ónhka ne raónha saksatá:ti?
Nahò:ten saksatá:ti?
Ka' saksatá:ti?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ónhka (who) + the transitive verb with you as object = 'who sees you?' The question word replaces the actor; the verb carries the object.

exercises.prompt: Match the transitive question to its answer

pairs.target: Ónhka skahó:wi? (Who do you give to?)

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four transitive questions with natural answers. Who sees, what you make, who you give to, what he makes.

exercises.prompt: Nahò:ten — what. When you ask Nahò:ten wahró:nis? (what does he make?), what information does the answer need to provide?
exercises.correct: The inanimate object being made — the answer names the thing that is the object of the making verb
options: The inanimate object being made
Who is making it
When it was made
Whether it is big or small
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Nahò:ten asks for the object. The question targets the inanimate object prefix slot in the verb. The answer fills that slot with a noun.

exercises.prompt: Complete the who-question: ___ raksatá:ti? (Who sees me?)
exercises.correct: Ónhka
exercises.hint: Ó
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ónhka raksatá:ti? — who sees me? Ónhka (who) + the transitive seeing verb with me as object.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which question word asks 'who'?

exercises.prompt: Asking about relationships
exercises.storyPrompt: Ónhka saksatá:ti? — who sees you? A simple transitive question. But in Kanien'kéha, asking who sees you requires specifying that you are the object of the seeing. The question form encodes the relationship. Even a question about perception is a question about who is in relationship with whom. This is the language being consistent about what it values: not just actions, but the relationships those actions occur within.

lessons.title: Review — Transitive Verbs & Object Agreement
lessons.sub: Actor, verb, object — the full transitive system
intro: This review consolidates transitive verbs: what they are, how object prefixes work, the most common transitive verbs, building transitive sentences, and asking transitive questions.
The transitive system is the heart of how Kanien'kéha encodes relationships in verbs. Everything you have learned in this unit extends the verb knowledge from Units 10 and 11.
Use this lesson to consolidate before Unit 19 takes you into tense, aspect, and mood.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001): review across all lesson content. This unit bridges the relational prefix system (Unit 11) and the upcoming tense/aspect system (Unit 19).

exercises.prompt: Sort these verbs: transitive or intransitive?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Sit and run — intransitive. Make-it and he-sees-me — transitive. The distinction is in the prefix.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — it makes our minds happy. You have said this since Unit 5. Now that you understand transitive verbs, what do you recognise in this form?
exercises.correct: The ionkhi- prefix is a transitive object prefix — our minds (us) are the object being acted upon
options: The ionkhi- is a transitive object prefix — we are the object
The word is intransitive
The Thanksgiving Address uses a separate system
Ionkhi- marks the actor not the object
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — it makes our-minds happy. ionkhi- = object prefix encoding us. You have been using transitive verbs with object prefixes since Unit 5. Now you know what you were doing.

exercises.prompt: Skahó:wi (you give) vs Raksakhó:wi (he gives to me). What is the difference in the prefix structure?
exercises.correct: Skahó:wi has only an actor prefix (you). Raksakhó:wi has both actor (he) and object (me) encoded in the prefix.
options: Skahó:wi has actor only; Raksakhó:wi has actor + object
They are the same
Raksakhó:wi is intransitive
The root changes between them
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Transitive verbs can encode one person (actor only, with generic inanimate object) or two (actor + specific animate object). Raksakhó:wi is fully specified: he gives, to me.

exercises.prompt: Ask what someone makes: Nahò:ten ___?
exercises.correct: sahrón:ni
exercises.hint: s
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Nahò:ten sahrón:ni? — what do you make? Nahò:ten (what) targets the object slot of the transitive making verb.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which making verb takes 'you' as actor?

exercises.prompt: Say the making verb across three actors
exercises.meaning: I make it — You make it — He makes it
exercises.hint: akh-RÓ:-nis (3) — sa-HRÓ N:-ni (3) — wa-HRÓ:-nis (3). Same root -hrón:n-. Different actor prefixes: akh- (I), sa- (you), wa- (he). Three people, one root.

exercises.prompt: The transitive verb system
exercises.meaning: He sees me — She sees me
exercises.storyPrompt: Raksatá:ti. Akosatá:ti. He sees me, she sees me. The same seeing verb, two different relational object prefixes. In Kanien'kéha, seeing is never abstract — you always see something or someone, and the verb says who. The transitive system is the language insisting on specificity. You cannot just act; you act on something. You cannot just see; you see someone. Every transitive verb is a statement about relationship. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 19 — The Verb in Depth II: Tense, Aspect & Mood
desc: How Kanien'kéha verbs locate actions in time — past, future, conditional — and the aspect markers that shape how an action is understood.

lessons.title: Time in the Verb: How Kanien'kéha Locates Actions
lessons.sub: Aspect, not tense, is the primary way Kanien'kéha organises time
intro: English organises time primarily through tense: I walk (present), I walked (past), I will walk (future). Kanien'kéha organises time differently — aspect is primary, and tense markers are added when needed.

Aspect describes how an action relates to completion: is it ongoing (habitual), completed (punctual), or about to happen? You already know the habitual (-s suffix) from Unit 10. This unit adds the completed past and the future/intentional.
Understanding this distinction is the key to talking about yesterday, tomorrow, and everything you have already done or plan to do.
lessons.ped: Aspect-primary system: Mithun (1999): Kanien'kéha is an aspect-prominent language — the habitual/punctual distinction is more fundamental than tense. Nation (2001): aspect before tense in pedagogical sequence. This reframes what learners already know (habitual -s) as part of a larger system.

exercises.prompt: Ká:tiens — I am sitting (habitual). The -s marks the habitual aspect: ongoing or regular. What is the aspect called that marks a completed, one-time action?
exercises.correct: Punctual — it marks a specific, completed event rather than an ongoing or habitual one
options: Punctual — a specific, completed event
Progressive — still in progress
Future — about to happen
Stative — a condition
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Punctual aspect marks completed, specific events. Habitual marks ongoing or regular ones. These two aspects are the primary opposition in Kanien'kéha. Tense (past vs future) is added on top of this distinction.

exercises.prompt: Match the aspect marker to what it expresses

pairs.english: Ongoing or regular action — I sit (regularly), I am sitting
A specific completed event — I sat down (once, done)
An intended or future action — I will sit, I am going to sit
A condition being in — I am strong (state)

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Habitual, punctual, future, stative — four ways Kanien'kéha relates actions to time and completion. You already know all four; now you know their names.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address is spoken in the habitual. Why is the habitual the right aspect for the Address?
exercises.correct: The Address describes ongoing relationships and regular thanks — not past events, not future intentions, but what is continuously true
options: The Address describes ongoing relationships — what is continuously true
The Address is past tense — it describes what was given
The Address is future — what will be given
The Address uses all aspects equally
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The habitual aspect — the Address thanks the earth, waters, and sky beings for what they are continuously doing. Not what they did once (punctual), not what they will do (future), but their ongoing relationship with the world. The habitual is the aspect of lasting truth.

exercises.prompt: Wahrón:nis — he made it (punctual, completed). Wahró:nis could be he makes it (habitual). What is the difference in meaning?
exercises.correct: Punctual means it happened and is done. Habitual means it happens regularly or is currently happening.
options: Punctual = happened and done. Habitual = regular or current.
They mean the same thing
Punctual is more formal
Habitual refers to the past
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wahró:nis — he makes it (regularly, or is making it now). Wahrón:nis — he made it (once, it is done). The aspect suffix is the difference. This distinction replaces what English handles with 'makes' vs 'made'.

exercises.prompt: Aspect and time
exercises.meaning: I run (habitual) — You run (command) — You ran (punctual)
exercises.storyPrompt: Tekaráhtats — I run. Tesará:htat — you run (command). Tesaráhton — you ran (completed, done). Three forms of the same root. The root names the action. The prefix and suffix place it in time and relationship. In Kanien'kéha, time is not a separate dimension attached to an action — it is woven into the verb itself. Every time you speak a verb, you are also locating that action in its relationship to completion, to regularity, to intention. The language does not let you be vague about time.

lessons.title: The Completed Past
lessons.sub: How to say what has already happened
intro: The completed past — the punctual aspect — marks actions that happened and are finished. I sat down. She made the basket. The ceremony ended.
In Kanien'kéha, the completed past uses specific suffix patterns that differ across verb stem classes. The marker varies, but the meaning is consistent: this happened, it is done.
You already know one completed past form: Ka' iéhse'skwe'? — where did you go? The -skwe' suffix marked completed past on the going verb. This lesson names that pattern and extends it.
lessons.ped: Completed past (punctual) aspect: Mithun (1999): the punctual/completive aspect is the primary past reference form. Nation (2001): learners encounter the -skwe' form early (Unit 2) — this lesson names the pattern. Krashen (+1): known form, new label.

exercises.prompt: Ka' iéhse'skwe' — where did you go? The -skwe' marks completed past on the going verb. What does -skwe' signal to the listener?
exercises.correct: The going is finished — it happened and is done, not currently happening
options: The going is finished — happened and done
You are currently going
You plan to go
The direction is unknown
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! -skwe' marks completed, finished action. You have seen this form since Unit 2. Now you know it is the punctual aspect marker on this verb class.

exercises.prompt: Katiens'on — I sat down (completed, punctual). The -on or similar suffix marks completed past on A-stem verbs. How is this different from ká:tiens (I am sitting, habitual)?
exercises.correct: Ká:tiens is ongoing/regular. Katiens'on is completed — I sat down at a specific moment and the action is done.
options: Ká:tiens is ongoing; katiens'on is completed at a specific moment
They mean the same thing
Katiens'on is future tense
The difference is only formal
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Same root. Different aspect suffix. Ká:tiens — ongoing/habitual. Katiens'on — specific, completed, done. The suffix tells you everything about when the action sits in time.

exercises.prompt: Wahrón:ni — he made it (completed). The ceremony is over: what aspect would you use to describe it?
exercises.correct: Completed/punctual — the ceremony happened at a specific time and is now finished
options: Completed/punctual — happened at a specific time, now finished
Habitual — it happens regularly
Future — it is about to happen
Stative — it is a condition
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The ceremony is done — punctual. Wahrón:ni — he made it (completed, done). Punctual aspect is the natural choice for describing finished events.

exercises.prompt: The completed past marker on the going verb: Ka' iéhse'___?
exercises.correct: skwe'
exercises.hint: s
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ka' iéhse'skwe'? — where did you go? The -skwe' closes the completed past form.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which suffix marks completed past on this verb?

exercises.prompt: The completed past
exercises.storyPrompt: Iéhse'skwe' — you went. It happened. It is done. The going is in the past. In Kanien'kéha, the completed past is not just a grammatical category — it is a statement about closure. The action happened, had its moment, and is now part of what has been rather than what is. This matters in ceremony: the condolence ceremony marks the completed past of the chief who died before the community can move forward. In governance: a decision in council is completed past before the next discussion begins. The punctual aspect is the grammar of finality.

lessons.title: The Future and Intentional
lessons.sub: How to say what will happen and what you intend to do
intro: The future in Kanien'kéha is expressed through a prefix — typically wa'k- or similar — that marks an action as intended or about to happen. It is sometimes called the future-intentional because it covers both 'I will' and 'I am going to'.
You have already encountered future-leaning vocabulary: wà:ke (I am going) carries a sense of immediate future. This lesson formalises the future prefix and extends it to the verbs you know.
Future forms in Kanien'kéha are used for plans, intentions, predictions, and the inevitable — the seasons will change, the corn will grow.
lessons.ped: Future/intentional: Mithun (1999): the future/intentional prefix expresses both volitional future ('I will') and predicted future ('it will'). Nation (2001): future aspect as extension of known vocabulary. Physical domain: expressing plans and predictions.

exercises.prompt: Wà:ke — I am going. This already leans toward future. The intentional prefix makes the future explicit. When would you use the intentional rather than the habitual?
exercises.correct: When you are stating a specific plan or intention — not what you do regularly, but what you are going to do
options: When stating a specific plan or intention — not regular, but planned
They are interchangeable
Only in ceremonies
Only for the distant future
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Habitual: I go (regularly, that is my pattern). Intentional/future: I am going to go (this specific time, this plan). The distinction is between habit and intention.

exercises.prompt: Match the time expression to the aspect it calls for

pairs.english: Future/intentional — the seasonal change is predicted
Completed past — the season happened and ended
Habitual/stative — the current ongoing state of the season
Completed past — the arrival happened and is done

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Winter will come (future), autumn is over (completed), it is summer (habitual), spring arrived (completed). The seasons and the aspects work together.

exercises.prompt: Wahró:non — he will make it (future/intentional). You know wahró:nis (he made it, completed). What changed?
exercises.correct: The aspect suffix changed from completed past to future/intentional — the root stayed the same
options: The aspect suffix changed from completed to future/intentional
The root changed
The actor prefix changed
The verb became intransitive
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Same root. Same actor prefix. Different aspect suffix. Wahrón:nis — he made it (done). Wahró:non — he will make it (planned, coming). The suffix is the whole difference.

exercises.prompt: Kenién:ke — winter. To say 'winter will come', the verb takes a ___ prefix/suffix
exercises.correct: future
exercises.hint: f
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Winter will come — future/intentional aspect. The season is predicted, not yet arrived. The future marker makes that explicit.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which aspect marks future events?

exercises.prompt: The future
exercises.storyPrompt: Kenién:ke iahsenhstén:ion — winter will come. In Kanien'kehá:ka territory, the arrival of winter is not uncertain — it is predicted, planned for, prepared for. The future/intentional aspect is the grammar of preparation. Before the Midwinter Ceremony, before the hunting season, before the freezing of the rivers — the future is marked in the verb. The language does not just describe what is happening; it reaches forward and names what is coming. The future is not a blank. In Kanien'kéha, it is already being spoken.

lessons.title: Conditional and Hypothetical
lessons.sub: If, when, would — the language of possibility
intro: Conditional and hypothetical statements — if this happens, then that would happen; if I had known, I would have done it differently — require specific verb forms in Kanien'kéha.
The conditional is used for real conditions (if it rains, we will stay) and counterfactual hypotheticals (if I had a canoe, I would go).
This lesson introduces the conditional forms and the particles that introduce them, giving you the grammatical tools for nuanced expression.
lessons.ped: Conditional and hypothetical: Mithun (1999): conditional forms in Kanien'kéha are distinct from both habitual and punctual. This lesson provides conceptual introduction with simple examples. Physical domain: producing simple conditional sentences.

exercises.prompt: Tsi — where / at / when (locative). This particle can introduce time conditions: tsi Kenién:ke — when it is winter. How does tsi function in conditional sentences?
exercises.correct: It introduces the condition — 'when/if this situation exists' — before the consequence is stated
options: It introduces the condition — when/if this situation exists
It marks the future only
It negates the following clause
It is always translated as 'where'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsi introduces temporal and locative conditions. Tsi Kenién:ke — when it is winter. The condition is set, and the consequence follows. This is conditional logic in Kanien'kéha.

exercises.prompt: Onen' — now / then / at this point. In conditional sentences, onen' often introduces the consequence: if X, then onen' Y. What does onen' signal here?
exercises.correct: The consequence follows — 'then this happens' or 'at that point this occurs'
options: The consequence follows — 'then this happens'
It marks the condition
It negates the following
It marks habitual aspect
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Tsi [condition]... onen' [consequence]. If/when it is winter, then we gather inside. Tsi and onen' work together to structure conditional logic.

exercises.prompt: Tsi Kenién:ke, onen' Kanonsesné:hane' — when it is winter, then (people) gather in the longhouse. What type of conditional is this?
exercises.correct: A real conditional — if winter comes (and it will), then people gather. The condition is predictable and real.
options: A real conditional — predictable and real
A counterfactual — the opposite of what happens
A negative conditional
A future intention only
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Real conditionals describe predictable consequences. Tsi Kenién:ke — when winter comes (it will). Onen' the gathering happens. This is the conditional of seasonal knowledge and ceremonial planning.

exercises.prompt: Iah ka'niká:ien — never / not ever. This is a strong negation. How does it interact with conditional sentences?
exercises.correct: It produces negative conditionals: if X never happens, then Y cannot follow — strong denial of the condition
options: It produces strong negative conditionals — denying the condition
It is only used in affirmative sentences
It changes the verb to future
It replaces the if-particle
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Iah ka'niká:ien — never. Negative conditionals use this: if it never rains, the corn never grows. The negative condition produces a negative consequence. The logic is the same as positive conditionals, just negated.

exercises.prompt: If and when
exercises.storyPrompt: Tsi... onen'... If winter comes, then we gather. If the thunder sounds, then rain is coming. If the clan mother speaks, then the council listens. Conditional logic is the grammar of preparation and consequence. In Kanien'kehá:ka life, conditional knowledge is survival knowledge: if the ice is too thin, do not cross. If the ash borer comes, protect the trees. If the language is not spoken, it changes. The conditional sentence is not just grammar — it is the structure of thinking about what might happen and being ready for it.

lessons.title: Talking About the Past: Narratives and Stories
lessons.sub: Using completed and sequential past to tell what happened
intro: Telling a story in Kanien'kéha — about what happened yesterday, what occurred in the oral tradition, what the elder did — requires consistent use of completed past forms and narrative sequencing.
This lesson practises past narration using the verb knowledge from this unit: completed past forms, sequential past markers, and the particles that connect events in time.
You will practise telling a short narrative about a daily event, a craft being made, or a community gathering.
lessons.ped: Past narration as productive skill: Nation (2001): narrative is the most demanding productive language skill — it requires consistent aspect use over multiple sentences. Krashen (+1): known vocabulary in new discourse-level task. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Ki' — just / only / already. In past narratives, ki' can mark an event as just completed or recently done. How does this differ from the completed past suffix?
exercises.correct: Ki' adds recency — just now, already — while the completed past suffix simply marks that the action is done
options: Ki' adds recency (just now, already) while completed past just marks done
They are identical
Ki' marks the future
Ki' negates the action
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ki' narrows the time frame: just now, already, recently. The completed past says it happened and is done. Ki' adds that it happened very recently. Together they give you precise narrative time placement.

exercises.prompt: In the oral tradition, stories about the past are told in specific verb forms. The creation story is told in a narrative past style. Why does the aspect system matter for oral tradition?
exercises.correct: Consistent aspect use signals to listeners where each event sits in time — the narrative is organised by aspect as much as by sequence
options: Consistent aspect organises the narrative timeline for listeners
Aspect does not matter in oral tradition
Stories use only the habitual
The past form is identical to the present
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! In Kanien'kehá:ka oral tradition, the aspect system is the scaffolding of the narrative. Listeners follow the story through aspect markers as much as through content. Completed past: this happened and is done. Sequential: then this happened. The grammar is the architecture of the story.

exercises.prompt: You want to tell a story: 'First she made the fire, then she made the corn soup.' What aspect would you use for both verbs?
exercises.correct: Completed past for both — both actions happened in sequence and are finished
options: Completed past for both — sequential finished events
Habitual for both — these are regular activities
Future for both — this is planned
First habitual, then future
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Sequential completed past: she made the fire (done), then she made the soup (done). Both events are finished, one after the other. The completed past aspect holds the sequence together.

exercises.prompt: Arrange these narrative particles in sequence

pairs.english: Signals the next event in the sequence
Introduces a time frame or condition

pairs.target: Onen' ki' wáhi — that is all

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Narrative particles: tsi (when/if), ki' (just/already), onen' (then/at that point), onen' ki' wáhi (that is all). These are the connective tissue of Kanien'kéha storytelling.

exercises.prompt: A past narrative
exercises.meaning: Then... just / already... that is all
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen'. Ki'. Onen' ki' wáhi. Then. Just. That is all. These three phrases bracket a Kanien'kéha narrative. Then this happened. Just recently this occurred. That is all. The oral tradition of Kanien'kehá:ka storytelling has used this structure for as long as stories have been told. When elders tell the creation story, when families recount the previous day's events, when witnesses describe what happened at a council — the same particles, the same aspect markers, the same structure. The grammar of the story is as old as the story itself.

lessons.title: Review — Tense, Aspect & Mood
lessons.sub: Time in the verb — the complete system
intro: This review consolidates the aspect system: habitual (ongoing), punctual/completed past (done), future/intentional (coming), and conditional (if/when). Together they give you the grammatical tools to locate any action in time.
You also have the narrative particles — onen', ki', tsi — that connect events into sequences.
Use this lesson to consolidate before Unit 21, which takes you into complex sentence building.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001): review across all lesson content. This unit completes the verb grammar sequence begun in Unit 10.

exercises.prompt: Match the aspect to its meaning and marker

pairs.english: Ongoing or regular — I sit, I am sitting
Planned or predicted — I will sit, winter will come

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four aspects, four meanings. Habitual, completed, future, conditional — the complete time system of Kanien'kéha verbs.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address uses the habitual throughout. Why is this the right aspect for it?
exercises.correct: The Address describes ongoing relationships and continuous truth — what is always being given, not what was given once or will be given eventually
options: The Address describes ongoing, continuously true relationships
The Address is past tense
The Address is future tense
The Address uses all aspects
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The habitual is the aspect of ongoing truth. The earth sustains us — continuously, right now, always. The Thanksgiving Address is in the habitual because it describes what is perpetually true, not what happened once or will happen eventually.

exercises.prompt: Ka' iéhse'skwe' — where did you go? You have known this question since Unit 2. What do you now understand about the -skwe' that you did not before?
exercises.correct: It is the completed past marker — it places the going firmly in the finished past
options: It is the completed past marker — the going is finished
It marks the future
It is decorative
It marks respectful speech
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! -skwe' — you have been using completed past since Unit 2 without knowing its name. Now you know: it marks a finished, completed event. The grammar was always there. Now it has a name.

exercises.prompt: In a narrative, introduce the next event: ___ this happened
exercises.correct: Onen'
exercises.hint: O
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onen' — then. The most common narrative connector. Then this happened, then that happened. The sequence unfolds through onen'.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which particle introduces the next narrative event?

exercises.prompt: Say the going verb in all three aspects
exercises.meaning: I go (habitual) — You went (completed past) — I will go (future, approximate)
exercises.hint: Three aspects of the going verb. The root -ke/-ske carries the meaning. The prefix and suffix mark the time. Say all three and feel the difference.

exercises.prompt: Time in the verb
exercises.storyPrompt: Kenién:ke. Sé:ske. Ohiarí:ha. Ken'niká:ien. Winter, spring, summer, autumn. The four seasons in Kanien'kéha, each marking a different moment in the year. In the habitual they describe what is currently true. In the completed past they mark a season that has ended. In the future they name what is coming. The same words, the same seasons, the same land — but the aspect changes everything about when in time the statement sits. This is what the verb system gives you: the ability to locate everything you say in its right moment. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 20 — Putting It Together: Simple Sentences
desc: How to build a complete sentence in Kanien'kéha — saying what something is, where it is, whose it is, and how to ask and answer questions

lessons.title: How Kanien'kéha Builds a Sentence
lessons.sub: Word order, and why it feels different from English
intro: In English, the order of words in a sentence matters a lot. 'The bear sees the man' means something very different from 'The man sees the bear.' The order tells you who is doing what.
Kanien'kéha works differently. Because the verb already tells you who is doing what — through its prefixes — the words in a sentence can move around more freely. The verb carries the weight.

Most of the time in Kanien'kéha, the verb comes first or near the front of a sentence. But the most important thing to know is this: the verb does the work. Once you understand that, sentences start to make sense.
lessons.ped: Word order as conceptual reorientation: English speakers carry strong assumptions about sentence structure. This lesson names the difference plainly and early, using concrete examples rather than grammatical terminology. Nation (2001) identifies this kind of structural awareness as important for learners moving from vocabulary to sentence production. LaFever's Mental domain is primary.

exercises.prompt: In the sentence 'Ká:tiens ne Ohkwá:ri' (The bear is sitting), which part carries the most meaning?
exercises.correct: Ká:tiens — the verb — because it tells you that something is sitting
options: Ká:tiens — the verb — because it tells you that something is sitting
ne — the article
Ohkwá:ri — the word for bear
Both words carry equal weight
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The verb Ká:tiens already tells you that something is in the act of sitting. The word Ohkwá:ri (bear) adds who — but the verb is doing most of the work. This is how Kanien'kéha sentences are built.

exercises.prompt: You have seen ne throughout this course — in ne Ohkwá:ri (the bear), ne Kanehsatà:ke, ne Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen. What does ne do in a sentence?
exercises.correct: It points to a specific thing — like 'the' in English, it says 'that particular one'
options: It points to a specific thing — like 'the' in English, it says 'that particular one'
It means 'and'
It negates the sentence
It marks the verb
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! ne is the article that marks a specific noun — 'the bear', not just 'a bear'. You have been seeing it since Unit 1.

exercises.prompt: Why does Kanien'kéha have more freedom in word order than English?
exercises.correct: Because the verb's prefixes already show who is doing what — the sentence doesn't rely on word order to carry that meaning
options: Because the verb's prefixes already show who is doing what — the sentence doesn't rely on word order to carry that meaning
Because Kanien'kéha has no grammar rules
Because nouns always come first
Because verbs are always last
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The prefix system in the verb does the work that word order does in English. This is one of the things that makes Kanien'kéha feel different at first — but once the verb prefixes click, the whole language opens up.

exercises.prompt: A different way of putting things
exercises.storyPrompt: Ká:tiens — sitting. This one verb already tells you that something is in the act of sitting. In English you need a subject and a verb: 'the bear sits'. In Kanien'kéha, the verb often holds the whole picture. Think of the last time you walked into a room and understood what was happening before anyone said a word — you read the scene. Kanien'kéha verbs work a little like that. They hold the scene inside them.

lessons.title: That Is… / This Is… (Naming Things)
lessons.sub: The simplest way to name something in Kanien'kéha
intro: The simplest complete sentence in Kanien'kéha is a naming sentence: that is the bear, that's the corn.
You already know the pieces. Ní:'i — that's it / that's the one. Ne — the (pointing to something specific). Put them together: Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i — that's the bear.
This is the sentence you use when you point at something and name it. Children use it when they are learning the names of things. You are doing the same work — naming the world in Kanien'kéha.
lessons.ped: Naming as the first act of language: This lesson gives learners the simplest tool for naming in Kanien'kéha. The sentence structure is minimal but complete, building confidence before more complex forms. LaFever's Physical domain: learners produce naming sentences. Emotional domain: naming the world in your language is a small act of reclamation.

exercises.prompt: To say 'that is the bear', you say: Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i. What is ní:'i doing in this sentence?
exercises.correct: It points — it means 'that one' or 'that's it'
options: It points — it means 'that one' or 'that's it'
It is the verb 'to be'
It is the word for bear
It negates the sentence
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ní:'i as a pointer — 'that one, right there'. Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i: the bear — that's the one. You have seen ní:'i before as the pronoun 'I / me'. Here it works differently, as a pointer. Context tells you which use it is.

exercises.prompt: Match each naming sentence to what it means
exercises.sub: All nouns here are from earlier units
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! [Noun] ne ní:'i: that is the [noun]. You can build this sentence with any noun you know.

exercises.prompt: Complete the naming sentence: 'That's the bear'
exercises.correct: ní:'i
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i — that's the bear. [Noun] ne ní:'i is the naming sentence. Use it to point at anything and name it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what word points to the thing being named?

exercises.prompt: Say 'that's the turtle' in Kanien'kéha
exercises.correct: A'nó:wara ne ní:'i
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A'nó:wara ne ní:'i — that's the turtle. And of course — A'nó:wara is also the Turtle Clan. When you name the turtle, you name the clan.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Pattern: [noun] ne ní:'i

exercises.prompt: Say this naming sentence aloud
exercises.hint: oh-KWÁ:-ri (3 syllables) — ne (1) — NÍ:-'i (2). Point at something when you say ní:'i. The gesture and the word work together.

exercises.prompt: Naming the world
exercises.storyPrompt: Onón:ta ne ní:'i — that's the corn. One of the Three Sisters. When you point at an ear of corn and say its name in Kanien'kéha, you are doing something that has been done in this territory for thousands of years. The corn has a name. The beans have a name. The squash has a name. Every plant in the garden, every animal on the land — they all have names in this language. You are learning to say those names.

lessons.title: Describing Things (The Bear Is Big)
lessons.sub: How to say what something is like
intro: Kanien'kéha doesn't have adjectives the way English does. Instead, describing words are verbs — they describe a state something is in.
So instead of 'the bear is big' with an adjective, you say something closer to 'the bear — it is being big'. The describing word is a verb that expresses a state.

You have already seen many of these: owá:nen (it is big/large), ohsì:io (it is good/beautiful), kenní:io (it is good/beautiful), skén:nen (it is calm/peaceful). This lesson puts them to work in full sentences.
lessons.ped: Description through state verbs: This lesson names the pattern plainly — 'describing words are verbs here' — without requiring learners to understand technical terms. Nation (2001) identifies this kind of structural awareness as important for moving beyond vocabulary lists into sentence production. LaFever's Mental domain: understanding how description works. Physical domain: producing description sentences.

exercises.prompt: Owá:nen means 'it is big / large'. To say 'the bear is big', you say: Ohkwá:ri owá:nen. What is owá:nen doing in this sentence?
exercises.correct: It is the describing verb — it says what state the bear is in
options: It is the describing verb — it says what state the bear is in
It is a separate describing word added after the noun
It means 'the big bear' as one word
It is a question
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen: the bear — it is big. Owá:nen is a describing verb telling you the bear's state. No separate adjective needed — the verb does the describing.

exercises.prompt: Match the describing verb to what it means
exercises.sub: You have seen all of these before
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four describing verbs from earlier units. Each one describes a state. Pair any of them with a noun and you have a complete describing sentence.

exercises.prompt: Build this sentence: 'The bear is big'
exercises.correct: owá:nen
exercises.hint: o
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — the bear is big. Noun + describing verb. A complete Kanien'kéha sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what describing verb means 'it is big'?

exercises.prompt: Kenní:io means 'it is good / it is beautiful'. How do you say 'the corn is good'?
exercises.correct: Onón:ta kenní:io
options: Onón:ta kenní:io
Kenní:io ne Onón:ta ní:'i
Onón:ta ne ohsì:io ní:'i
Kenní:io Onón:ta ne
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Onón:ta kenní:io — the corn is good. Noun first, then the describing verb. The same pattern you just practised.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + describing verb.

exercises.prompt: Say 'the wolf is calm' in Kanien'kéha
exercises.correct: Okwáho skén:nen
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Okwáho skén:nen — the wolf is calm. And skén:nen also means peace — so you could hear this as 'the wolf is at peace'. The Wolf Clan carries the responsibility of keeping peace between nations.

exercises.prompt: Say this describing sentence aloud
exercises.hint: o-NÓN-ta (3 syllables) — oh-SÌ:-io (3 syllables). A simple, complete sentence. You can say this at a meal, in a garden, at a ceremony.

exercises.prompt: Skén:nen
exercises.storyPrompt: Skén:nen — peaceful, calm. This word appears in greetings, in the names of leaders, in the Great Law of Peace. When you describe something as skén:nen, you are using a word that has been at the centre of Haudenosaunee life for centuries. Peace is not just the absence of conflict — it is a state of balance and right relationship. What in your life right now is skén:nen?

lessons.title: There Is a Bear (Saying Something Exists)
lessons.sub: How to say that something is present or exists
intro: Sometimes you want to say that something is simply there — 'there is a bear', 'there are geese', 'there is water'. In Kanien'kéha, the phrase E'tho ká:yen — 'it is there' — is a key building block.
Combined with a noun, it says: that thing is present, right there. Ohkwá:ri — e'tho ká:yen — the bear is there / there is a bear.
This is the kind of sentence you say when you spot something — an animal, a place, food ready on the table.
lessons.ped: Existence sentences as functional vocabulary: The ability to assert that something exists underlies animal sightings, food descriptions, weather reports, and more. Nation (2001) identifies high-frequency functional patterns as priority acquisition targets. The lesson uses culturally relevant content — animals, land, water. LaFever's Spiritual domain is touched in the story exercise connecting existence sentences to the land.

exercises.prompt: E'tho ká:yen means 'it is there'. What does this build when you add a noun in front?
exercises.correct: An existence sentence — 'there is a [thing]' or '[thing] is there'
options: An existence sentence — 'there is a [thing]' or '[thing] is there'
A question
A command
A negative sentence
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! E'tho ká:yen — 'it is there'. Add a noun: Ohkwá:ri — e'tho ká:yen: the bear is there. Simple and complete.

exercises.prompt: Match the existence sentence to what it means

pairs.english: The bear is there / There is a bear
The beaver is there / There is a beaver
The water is there / There is water
The river is there / There is a river

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! [Noun] — e'tho ká:yen. A bear, a beaver, water, a river — you can announce the presence of any of them with this pattern.

exercises.prompt: Build an existence sentence: 'There is water'
exercises.correct: Ónhka
exercises.hint: Ó
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ónhka — e'tho ká:yen: there is water. Ónhka is from Unit 3 and also appears in the Thanksgiving Address — the waters that sustain us.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the Kanien'kéha word for water?

exercises.prompt: Kaniatarà:ke means 'river / on the river'. It already ends in -ke. What does that ending do?
exercises.sub: You learned this in Unit 12
exercises.correct: It is a locative ending — it means 'at' or 'on' that place
options: It is a locative ending — it means 'at' or 'on' that place
It is decorative
It marks the plural
It is only used in place names
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The -ke ending is a locative — it builds 'at' or 'on' into the name itself. Kaniatarà:ke means 'on the river'. The place name tells you where you are standing.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does the -ke ending do?

exercises.prompt: Say this existence sentence aloud
exercises.meaning: The beaver is there / There is a beaver
exercises.hint: Tsa-NÍ:-to (3 syllables) — pause — e'-tho KÁ:-yen (4 syllables). Say it like you've just spotted something — because that's exactly when you'd use it.

exercises.prompt: Noticing what is there
exercises.storyPrompt: Tsaní:to — e'tho ká:yen. The beaver is there. Before there were roads and fences, the people knew the land by what lived in it — where the beaver dams were, where the deer came to drink, where the geese landed in autumn. To say 'the beaver is there' in Kanien'kéha is to read the land the way generations read it before you. What is there, in the land around you right now, that you could name in Kanien'kéha?

lessons.title: Where Is It? (Location Sentences)
lessons.sub: The bear is at the river — putting things in place
intro: A location sentence puts something in a specific place: the bear is at the river, the canoe is on the water, the drum is in the longhouse.

In Kanien'kéha, the word tsi — meaning 'where / at' — connects a noun to its location. You have seen it in phrases like tsi niakonónhsoten (where I live). Now you use it to place things and people on the land.

Many location words already carry their 'at' meaning built in — words like Kaniatarà:ke (at the river), Ohnekánoron (on the lake), kanonhséhsne (at the longhouse). These are not just names — they are places.
lessons.ped: Locative vocabulary as land-based learning: This lesson teaches location sentences through concrete places in Kanien'kehá:ka territory — the river, the lake, the longhouse, the field. Every location word is also a description of the land. LaFever's Spiritual domain: the land has language. Physical domain: learners produce location sentences about real places.

exercises.prompt: Tsi means 'where / at'. In the sentence 'Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke' (The bear is at the river), what is tsi doing?
exercises.correct: It connects the bear to its location — 'the bear, at the river'
options: It connects the bear to its location — 'the bear, at the river'
It asks a question
It negates the sentence
It is an article like ne
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tsi links the subject to its place. Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke: the bear, at the river.

exercises.prompt: Match the location word to where it puts you
exercises.sub: These are real places on Kanien'kehá:ka territory

pairs.english: At the river / on the river
On the water / on the lake
In the field / on the meadow

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four places on Kanien'kehá:ka territory. These are real — not invented examples.

exercises.prompt: Build this location sentence: 'The bear is at the river'
exercises.correct: Kaniatarà:ke
exercises.hint: K
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke — the bear is at the river.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the word for 'at the river'?

exercises.prompt: Say 'the canoe is on the water' in Kanien'kéha
exercises.correct: Kaná:tsihs tsi Ohnekánoron
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kaná:tsihs tsi Ohnekánoron — the canoe on the water. Kaná:tsihs from Unit 3 now has a home in a full sentence.

exercises.prompt: Ohnekánoron means 'on the water / on the lake'. You have seen a water root before in this course. Where?
exercises.correct: Ónhka (water, Unit 3) — the same water root appears in different water words
options: Ónhka (water, Unit 3) — the same water root appears in different water words
Nowhere — Ohnekánoron is unique
Only in the word for rain
Only in place names
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The water root runs through many Kanien'kéha words. When you know a root, you start seeing it in new words — and new words become readable.
exercises.wm: Please try again — where have you seen a water word before?

exercises.prompt: Say this location sentence aloud
exercises.meaning: The bear is at the river
exercises.hint: oh-KWÁ:-ri (3) — tsi (1) — Ka-nia-ta-RÀ:-ke (5). The grave accent on rà: means a falling pitch. A full sentence in six syllables.

exercises.prompt: Putting things in their place
exercises.storyPrompt: Kaniatarà:ke — at the river. Rivers are at the centre of Kanien'kehá:ka life — for travel, for food, for ceremony. The St. Lawrence River, the Ottawa River, the Grand River — these are not just geographic features. They are relatives, acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address. When you say 'the bear is at the river', you are placing a living being alongside another living being. The river is not just a backdrop.

lessons.title: Whose Is It? (Possession Sentences)
lessons.sub: That is my shoe — saying who something belongs to
intro: Possession in Kanien'kéha is built into the noun through prefixes. You have already been using this — iakóha (my mother), Ónia'karé:'a (my clan). The possessive is part of the word.
To say 'that is my shoe', you take the possessed form of the noun and use the naming sentence from Lesson 72. Ak-áhta' — my shoe. Ak-áhta' ne ní:'i — that is my shoe.
Ak- marks 'my' on most nouns. Sa- marks 'your'. These two prefixes cover most of the possession sentences you will need in daily life.
lessons.ped: Possession as practical grammar: Possessive constructions are among the highest-frequency structures in daily conversation. Nation (2001) identifies high-frequency, high-utility structures as priority targets. The lesson draws on familiar nouns so learners focus on the new possessive prefix rather than new vocabulary. Emotional domain: possession is also the language of home and belonging.

exercises.prompt: Áhta' means 'shoe'. To say 'my shoe', you add ak- to get Ak-áhta'. How does 'that is my shoe' look as a full sentence?
exercises.correct: Ak-áhta' ne ní:'i
options: Ak-áhta' ne ní:'i
Áhta' ak ne ní:'i
ne ní:'i Ak-áhta'
Ak-áhta' tsi ne
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ak-áhta' ne ní:'i — that is my shoe. The possessed noun + ne ní:'i: the same naming pattern from Lesson 72, now with a possessive prefix on the noun.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is the naming sentence pattern?

exercises.prompt: Match the possessed item to its meaning
exercises.sub: Ak- marks 'my' on these nouns
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ak- + [noun] = my [noun]. The prefix attaches to the front of the noun.

exercises.prompt: Complete the possession sentence: 'That is my soap'
exercises.correct: ní:'i
exercises.hint: n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ak-atsihnhétste' ne ní:'i — that is my soap. The same naming sentence ending, now with a possessed noun.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what word ends a naming sentence?

exercises.prompt: To say 'that is your book', you use sa- (your) instead of ak- (my). How would you say it?
exercises.sub: Apply the same pattern with sa-
exercises.correct: Sa-kahiatónhsera ne ní:'i
options: Sa-kahiatónhsera ne ní:'i
Ak-kahiatónhsera ne ní:'i
Kahiatónhsera ne ní:'i
ne ní:'i sa-kahiatónhsera
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Sa-kahiatónhsera ne ní:'i — that is your book. Ak- = my, Sa- = your. Two prefixes, two people, the same sentence pattern.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which prefix marks 'your'?

exercises.prompt: Say this possession sentence aloud
exercises.hint: ak-ÁH-ta' (3 syllables) — ne (1) — NÍ:-'i (2). The ak- prefix is quick and unstressed. The stress falls on AH in the noun.

exercises.prompt: What belongs to you
exercises.storyPrompt: Ak-áhta' ne ní:'i — that is my shoe. Such a small sentence. But the word 'mine' in any language carries a lot. In residential schools, children's clothes were taken and replaced with uniforms. Personal belongings were not allowed. To say 'my shoe' in Kanien'kéha is to claim something. Small, ordinary, yours.

lessons.title: Saying No (Negative Sentences)
lessons.sub: Iáh — the word for 'not' — and how to use it
intro: You learned Iáh — no / not — in Unit 3. This lesson takes it further: how do you use Iáh to make any sentence negative?

Iáh comes before the verb or describing word. Sometimes it also triggers a small change in the verb — often you will see a te- added. You have already seen this in phrases you know: Iáh tó:kens (not so well), Iáh waké:ka' (I don't like it), Yah tewake'nikonhrayén:ta's (I don't understand).
Yah is the same as Iáh — just a common spoken version. Both are correct.
lessons.ped: Negation as high-frequency grammar: The ability to say 'no', 'not', and 'I don't' is essential from the earliest stages. Nation (2001) identifies negation as a priority structure. This lesson builds on Iáh, which learners have encountered repeatedly since Unit 3, making the pattern explicit without technical grammar terms.

exercises.prompt: You know Iáh means 'no / not'. In Iáh tó:kens (not so well), what is Iáh doing?
exercises.correct: It flips the meaning — turning 'well' into 'not well'
options: It flips the meaning — turning 'well' into 'not well'
It asks a question
It adds emphasis
It marks the past
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iáh flips the meaning. Tó:kens — well. Iáh tó:kens — not well. Iáh is the switch.

exercises.prompt: Match the negative phrase to what it means
exercises.sub: You have seen all of these before
