exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four negative phrases from earlier units — now you can see the pattern they share. Iáh / Yah comes first, and often a te- appears in the verb after it.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'I don't understand'
exercises.correct: Yah
exercises.hint: Y
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Yah tewake'nikonhrayén:ta's — I don't understand. Yah and Iáh are both correct — Yah is common in everyday speech.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what word starts a negative sentence?

exercises.prompt: Iah ka'niká:ien means 'never'. What two parts do you see in it?
exercises.correct: Iah (not) + ka'niká:ien (ever) — not + ever = never
options: Iah (not) + ka'niká:ien (ever) — not + ever = never
It is a single word with no parts
It means 'sometimes'
Iah here means 'yes'
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Iah ka'niká:ien — not ever = never. Kanien'kéha builds complex meanings from smaller pieces. When you can see the pieces, longer expressions become readable.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does Iah always do?

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I don't understand'?
exercises.correct: Yah tewake'nikonhrayén:ta's
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Yah tewake'nikonhrayén:ta's — I don't understand. This is one of the most useful phrases you can know. No one expects a learner to understand everything. Say it, and a speaker will help you.

exercises.prompt: Say this negative phrase aloud
exercises.hint: IÁH (1 syllable) — wa-KÉ:-ka' (3 syllables). Short, clear, direct. Iáh lands firmly — it is the word of refusal and negation.

exercises.prompt: The power of no
exercises.storyPrompt: Iáh — no. Such a small word. The ability to say no in your own language — no, I don't agree; no, that's not right; no, I don't understand; no, that is not mine — is not small. In residential schools, Indigenous children were not allowed to say no in their own language. Reclaiming Iáh is reclaiming the right to refuse, to disagree, to draw a line, in the language that belongs to you.

lessons.title: Yes or No? (Asking Simple Questions)
lessons.sub: How to turn a statement into a yes/no question
intro: The simplest way to turn a statement into a yes/no question in Kanien'kéha is to add the word ken — often translated as 'is it?' or 'right?'.
You have already seen ken in action: Satonhkaria'ks ken? (Are you hungry?), Iawekon ken? (Is it delicious?). Ken sits after the verb and raises the question.
The answers are simple: Hen' — yes. Iáh — no. These two words, with ken questions, cover a huge range of everyday exchanges.
lessons.ped: Yes/no questions as immediate communicative function: The ability to ask and answer yes/no questions enables participation in conversation at the earliest stages. Nation (2001) identifies question formation as a priority structure. This lesson is deliberately short and focused: one pattern (ken), two answers (Hen' / Iáh), practised through familiar vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: What is Hen'?
exercises.correct: Yes — the affirmative answer
options: Yes — the affirmative answer
No
Maybe
I don't know
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Hen' — yes. Hen' and Iáh are the two answers to every ken question.

exercises.prompt: Match the ken question to what it is asking
exercises.sub: You have seen all of these before

pairs.english: Is it wonderful? / Is it pleasing?

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Remove ken and you have a statement. Add ken and you have a question. That's the whole rule.

exercises.prompt: Turn this into a yes/no question: 'Are you hungry?'
exercises.correct: ken
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Satonhkaria'ks ken? — Are you hungry? Ken turns any statement into a yes/no question.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what word makes it a question?

exercises.prompt: Someone asks you Iawekon ken? (Is it delicious?). You think it is. What do you say?
exercises.correct: Hen' — yes
options: Hen' — yes
Iáh — no
Onen' — now
tánon — and
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Hen' — yes. Simple and satisfying.

exercises.prompt: Say this question and answer aloud
exercises.sub: Both sides of the exchange
exercises.hint: Sa-ton-hka-RIA'-ks ken? — rising at the end. Hen' — one clear syllable. Practise both sides — you will ask and answer.

exercises.prompt: Yes
exercises.storyPrompt: Hen' — yes. The simplest response in the language. And yet — to say yes in Kanien'kéha, to a question asked in Kanien'kéha, is to participate. It is to be in the conversation, not outside it. Every time you answer Hen' to a question in this language, you are one step further inside. What question would you most like to be able to say yes to, in Kanien'kéha?

lessons.title: Who? What? Where? When? (Information Questions)
lessons.sub: The six question words you need to ask about anything
intro: Yes/no questions get you a yes or a no. Information questions get you an answer. This lesson covers the main question words in Kanien'kéha.
You have seen most of these scattered across the course. This lesson brings them together so you can see them as a set and use them with confidence.
Ónhka — who. Nahò:ten — what. Ka' — where. Kátke — when. Oh nontyé:ren — why. To nihá:ti — how many. Six question words that open almost any conversation.
lessons.ped: Question words as conversational keys: Nation (2001) identifies question words as high-frequency vocabulary that must be taught as a set — they form a paradigm together. This lesson presents them together for exactly that reason, using already-known content vocabulary so the focus stays on the question words themselves.

exercises.prompt: Match the question word to what it asks
exercises.sub: Learn these six together — they go everywhere
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four of the six question words. Ónhka (who), Nahò:ten (what), Ka' (where), Kátke (when). These four will carry most of your information questions.

exercises.prompt: The last two question words
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Oh nontyé:ren (why) and To nihá:ti (how many) complete the set. Now you have all six.

exercises.prompt: Nahò:ten means 'what'. You have seen it in Nahò:ten katkáhthos? (What do you see?). Which food question also uses Nahò:ten?
exercises.correct: Naho:ten ni:se' enseke? — What will you eat?
options: Naho:ten ni:se' enseke? — What will you eat?
Kanié:ri iken'? — What is your name?
Ka' wáhse'? — Where are you going?
Kátke? — When?
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Nahò:ten turns up in many question frames. It is one of the busiest question words in the language.

exercises.prompt: To nihá:ti means 'how many'. Which question in Unit 12 also asked about a number?
exercises.correct: Kahiatónhkwa tsi ní:se'? — How many winters are you?
options: Kahiatónhkwa tsi ní:se'? — How many winters are you?
Kanié:ri iken'? — What is your name?
Ka' nontáhse'? — Where did you come from?
Satonhkaria'ks ken? — Are you hungry?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kahiatónhkwa carries the 'how many' meaning in that age question. To nihá:ti is the standalone 'how many' you can use before any noun.

exercises.prompt: What is the Kanien'kéha question word for 'where'?
exercises.correct: Ka'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ka' — where. Short and common. You have seen it in Ka' wáhse'? and Ka' nontáhse'? since Unit 2.
exercises.wm: Please try again — a short word, just two characters.

exercises.prompt: Say the six question words aloud — one at a time
exercises.word: Ónhka — Nahò:ten — Ka' — Kátke — Oh nontyé:ren — To nihá:ti
exercises.meaning: Who — What — Where — When — Why — How many
exercises.hint: Say each one clearly with a pause between. ÓN-hka. Na-HÒ:-ten. Ka'. KÁT-ke. Oh non-TYÉ-ren. To ni-HÁ:-ti. These six words open every conversation.

exercises.prompt: The questions that matter
exercises.storyPrompt: Ónhka — who? This is always the first question. Who are you? Who are these people? Who came before? In Kanien'kehá:ka life, knowing who someone is — their name, their clan, their community, their family — is the foundation of every relationship. Ónhka is not a small question. It is an invitation. Who are you?

lessons.title: Putting Weight on a Word
lessons.sub: How to stress what matters most in a sentence
intro: Sometimes you want to put extra weight on part of a sentence — not just 'the bear is there' but 'the bear — yes, right there — is there'. Or not just 'I am going' but 'I am the one going'.

Kanien'kéha does this with small particles you have already met: Wáhi (truly / so it is), ki' (just / only / already), and ón:ne' (it's done / finished). These little words shift what the listener pays attention to.
You have said Onen' ki' wáhi at the end of every unit. Now you know what each piece means and what it is doing.
lessons.ped: Emphasis and focus as communicative precision: This lesson keeps the content familiar while teaching a new function — adding weight and focus. Nation (2001) recommends this kind of functional extension of known vocabulary. LaFever's Physical domain: learners practise producing focused sentences. Emotional domain: emphasis is the language of what matters most.

exercises.prompt: Ki' means 'just / only / already'. In 'Ohkwá:ri ki' ne ní:'i' (It's just the bear — only the bear), what is ki' adding?
exercises.correct: It narrows the focus — 'only this, nothing else'
options: It narrows the focus — 'only this, nothing else'
It makes the sentence a question
It negates the bear
It adds a time meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ki' narrows: just the bear, only the bear, nothing more. You have seen ki' in Onen' ki' wáhi — 'that's all, nothing more'. The same narrowing at work.

exercises.prompt: Wáhi means 'truly / so it is'. What does adding Wáhi to a sentence do?
exercises.correct: It adds weight and certainty — 'it is truly so'
options: It adds weight and certainty — 'it is truly so'
It makes the sentence negative
It asks a question
It weakens the statement
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Wáhi confirms and closes. Onen' ki' wáhi — 'now that's truly all'. Wáhi gives a sentence finality.

exercises.prompt: Match the particle to what it does

pairs.english: Just / only — narrows to one thing
Okay then — closes or accepts something

exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Four small particles that make speech sound natural. They are the difference between a word list and a real sentence.

exercises.prompt: Ioiá:ner means 'it is good / right'. To say 'it is truly good', which particle do you add?
exercises.correct: Wáhi — Ioiá:ner wáhi — it is truly good
options: Wáhi — Ioiá:ner wáhi — it is truly good
Iáh — Iáh ioiá:ner — it is not good
ki' — Ioiá:ner ki' — it is only good
ken — Ioiá:ner ken — is it good?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ioiá:ner wáhi — it is truly good. Wáhi strengthens and confirms.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which particle adds certainty?

exercises.prompt: Say this phrase aloud — and mean it
exercises.meaning: Now that's truly all / I am finished
exercises.hint: O-nen' (2) — ki' (1) — WÁ-hi (2). Three particles working together: now (onen') + just/only (ki') + truly (wáhi). You have said this many times. Now you understand every piece of it.

exercises.prompt: Finishing
exercises.storyPrompt: ón:ne' — it's done. There is something satisfying about a word for completion. In a language that was silenced for generations, every finished lesson is its own small ón:ne' — done, completed, real. You said it. It happened. The language moved through you. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Sentences About the World Around You
lessons.sub: Using simple sentences about what you can see right now
intro: Every lesson in this unit has given you a sentence type. This lesson asks you to use all of them — naming, describing, locating, possessing, questioning, negating — about the world right in front of you.
Look around where you are. What do you see? What is big? What is there? What is yours? Can you ask a question about it in Kanien'kéha?
This is not a test. It is an invitation to use what you know with real things. That is how a language becomes yours.
lessons.ped: Transfer to real-world use: Nation (2001) identifies the move from practised production to real use as the final step in acquisition. This lesson moves away from controlled exercises toward open-ended production — using sentence types with the learner's immediate environment. LaFever's four domains converge: knowing the patterns (Mental), using them with real objects (Physical), connecting language to the learner's own life (Emotional), understanding this as a step in the relationship with the language (Spiritual).

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'that is a bear'. Which sentence pattern do you use?
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i — the naming pattern
options: Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i — the naming pattern
Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — the describing pattern
Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke — the location pattern
Ohkwá:ri — e'tho ká:yen — the existence pattern
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri ne ní:'i — naming. All four are correct Kanien'kéha sentences — but each does a different job.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which pattern names a thing?

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'the corn is there'. Which pattern?
exercises.correct: Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen — the existence pattern
options: Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen — the existence pattern
Onón:ta ne ní:'i — naming
Onón:ta kenní:io — describing
Onón:ta ken? — question
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen. E'tho ká:yen announces that something is present — right there.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which pattern announces that something is there?

exercises.prompt: Match each sentence to its type
exercises.sub: Five sentence types from this unit
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Possession, negation, question, description — four different tools. A sentence is just a tool. You choose which one you need.

exercises.prompt: Look out a window or step outside. Say what you see — in Kanien'kéha.
exercises.sub: Use any sentence pattern you know
exercises.hint: Name what you see. Describe it. Say where it is. Say if it is big or small, good or old. Ask yourself a question about it. Any sentence you make — in any pattern — is real Kanien'kéha.

exercises.prompt: Saying yes to the language
exercises.storyPrompt: Hen' — yes. You can now build simple sentences in Kanien'kéha. You can name things, describe them, say where they are, say whose they are, ask about them, say no to them. These are not just exercises. They are the tools you need to speak. To be in conversation. To be present in the language. You started without knowing a single Kanien'kéha sentence. Look at where you are now. Hen' — yes. You are here.

lessons.title: Reading a Short Kanien'kéha Text
lessons.sub: A few sentences together — reading and understanding
intro: You have been building sentences one at a time. This lesson puts several together into a short text — a few sentences you can read, understand, and respond to.
The text uses only words and sentence patterns from this course. You will not find anything you haven't seen before. What you will find is all of it working together.
Reading Kanien'kéha — seeing it on the page and understanding it — is its own skill. This lesson gives you your first real reading practice.
lessons.ped: Reading as integration: Nation (2001) identifies reading as the activity that most consolidates vocabulary — encountering known words in new combinations, in connected text rather than isolated exercises. This is the unit's integration point. LaFever's Mental domain: reading and comprehension. Physical domain: responding to comprehension questions.

exercises.prompt:
Read this and answer the question:

'Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke. Ohkwá:ri owá:nen. Ohkwá:ri — e'tho ká:yen.'

Where is the bear?
exercises.correct: At the river — Kaniatarà:ke
options: At the river — Kaniatarà:ke
In the field
At the longhouse
On the lake
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke — the bear is at the river. You read a Kanien'kéha sentence and understood it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look for the location word.

exercises.prompt: From the same text: 'Ohkwá:ri owá:nen.' What does this tell you about the bear?
exercises.correct: The bear is big / large
options: The bear is big / large
The bear is peaceful
The bear is there
The bear is mine
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — the bear is big. A describing sentence. You read it and understood it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does owá:nen mean?

exercises.prompt:
Read this and answer:

'Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen. Ohsahéta' — e'tho ká:yen. Otá:ra — e'tho ká:yen.'

What are these three sentences about?
exercises.correct: The Three Sisters — corn, beans, and squash are all present
options: The Three Sisters — corn, beans, and squash are all present
Three animals at the river
Three people being named
Three seasons
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Three existence sentences, three sisters: Onón:ta (corn), Ohsahéta' (beans), Otá:ra (squash). They are there. You read three complete Kanien'kéha sentences.
exercises.wm: Please try again — you know these three nouns from Unit 7.

exercises.prompt: One more sentence: 'Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — ohsì:io.' What does this mean?
exercises.sub: You know both of these
exercises.correct: The Thanksgiving Address is good / beautiful
options: The Thanksgiving Address is good / beautiful
The Thanksgiving Address is there
The Thanksgiving Address is mine
The Thanksgiving Address — where is it?
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — ohsì:io. You can now read and understand a sentence about the Thanksgiving Address — in Kanien'kéha.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does ohsì:io mean?

exercises.prompt: Read this short text aloud
exercises.sub: Take your time — read for meaning, not speed
exercises.word: Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke. Ohkwá:ri owá:nen. Onón:ta — e'tho ká:yen. Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — ohsì:io.
exercises.meaning: The bear is at the river. The bear is big. The corn is there. The Thanksgiving Address is good.
exercises.hint: Four sentences. Four patterns — location, description, existence, description. Say each one clearly. You are reading Kanien'kéha.

exercises.prompt: Reading the language
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. You just read a Kanien'kéha text. Maybe you stumbled on a word, maybe you had to check something. That is exactly what reading a new language feels like. You did it anyway. The bear was at the river. The corn was there. The Thanksgiving Address was good. These things were true in Kanien'kéha, and you understood them. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Putting It Together
lessons.sub: Bringing the whole unit together
intro: This review brings together the key vocabulary and ideas from this unit.
Use it to consolidate what you know before moving on.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation review: Nation (2001): spaced retrieval review across all lesson content produces deeper retention than reviewing categories separately.

exercises.prompt: Match the sentence type to its structure

pairs.english: Description — the earth that sustains us

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Question, existence, negative, description — four sentence types you can now build.

exercises.prompt: Owá:nen — it is big. This is a stative describing verb. What sentence structure does it create when paired with a noun?
exercises.correct: Noun + describing verb — the noun first, then the state it is in (Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — the bear is big)
options: Noun + describing verb — noun then state
Verb + noun — verb first
Noun only — no verb needed
A completely different structure
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — the bear is big. Noun + describing stative verb. This is the Kanien'kéha description sentence.

exercises.prompt: Complete: it is good — Ioia'tá___
exercises.correct: wen
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ioia'táwen — it is good. A stative description of the world's condition.

exercises.prompt: Simple sentences
exercises.storyPrompt: Simple sentences. You can now name things, describe them, locate them, deny them, and ask about them. You can say who something belongs to, what it looks like, and whether it exists. These are not small skills. This is language. Every great speech in Kanien'kéha — every condolence address, every Thanksgiving Address, every council speech — is built from simple sentences, connected. You have the building blocks. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 21 — Sentences & Clauses II: Complex Sentences
desc: Combining simple sentences into complex ones — relative clauses, embedded sentences, and multi-clause expression.

lessons.title: Combining Sentences: What Changes
lessons.sub: How simple sentences join into complex ones
intro: You can now build simple sentences: The bear is big. The basket is there. I went to my grandmother's house. This unit shows how to combine them.
Kanien'kéha uses embedding, sequential particles, and relative constructions to link clauses. The result is richer, more natural speech.
This unit builds directly on Unit 20 (simple sentences), Unit 18 (tense/aspect), and Unit 11 (relational prefixes).
lessons.ped: Complex sentence building: Nation (2001): complex sentences emerge naturally from known simple sentence types combined with linking particles. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Tánon — and / also. This is the most basic sentence connector. Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Okwáho onhkwé:ton — the bear is big and the wolf is small. What does tánon do?
exercises.correct: It joins two equal clauses — and connects them without making one depend on the other
options: It joins two equal clauses equally
It makes one clause depend on the other
It marks the second clause as past
It negates the second clause
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tánon — and. The simplest connective. Two complete clauses, joined as equals. The bear is big AND the wolf is small.

exercises.prompt: Tsi introduces a clause that gives the time or place of the main action. Tsi ká:tiens, katkáhthons — while I am sitting, I am listening. How is this different from tánon?
exercises.correct: Tsi makes one clause the time/place frame for the other — it is subordinating, not coordinating
options: Tsi subordinates one clause to the other as its time/place frame
Tsi is the same as tánon
Tsi only introduces questions
Tsi negates the following clause
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Tánon coordinates (and). Tsi subordinates (when/while/where). These are the two fundamental clause-linking strategies in Kanien'kéha.

exercises.prompt: Match the connector to how it links clauses

pairs.english: When/where/while — one clause frames the other
Just/only/already — narrows or limits the clause

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four clause connectors: and, when/where, then, just/already. Together they give you the building blocks of complex sentences.

exercises.prompt: Building complexity
exercises.storyPrompt: Tánon. Tsi. Onen'. Three small words that turn simple sentences into stories. The bear is big and the wolf is small. When winter comes, then the people gather. She made the basket, then she carried it to the market. Simple clauses, linked into narratives. This is how language builds toward meaning that individual sentences cannot carry alone. Every complex sentence you will ever say in Kanien'kéha will use these connectors — or their more elaborate kin. Start here. The rest follows.

lessons.title: Relative Clauses: The Bear That Runs
lessons.sub: Describing nouns with embedded clauses
intro: A relative clause modifies a noun by adding information about it: the bear that runs, the basket she made, the man who speaks Kanien'kéha. In Kanien'kéha, relative clauses are formed through specific embedding patterns.
The key is the nominaliser — a form that turns a verb phrase into a noun-modifier. You have seen this pattern in compound words: the one who makes pots, the one who tans hides.
This lesson shows how the same nominalising pattern works in full relative clauses.
lessons.ped: Relative clauses as nominalisers: Mithun (1982): Kanien'kéha uses nominalisation as its primary relative clause strategy. Nation (2001): relative clauses emerge from known vocabulary through the nominaliser.

exercises.prompt: Iakoriwá:ienstha — she who makes pots. This is a nominalised verb — a verb turned into a noun-modifier. What does this tell you about how Kanien'kéha forms 'the person who...' expressions?
exercises.correct: It uses nominalised verbs — the verb form itself describes the person, without needing a separate relative pronoun like 'who' or 'that'
options: It uses nominalised verbs — no separate 'who' is needed
It uses 'who' as a separate word
It requires a special relative clause marker
Only kinship verbs can be nominalised
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanien'kéha forms relative clauses through nominalisation. The verb that describes the action becomes the name of the person who does it. No separate 'who' or 'that' is needed — the verb form carries both.

exercises.prompt: Match the nominalised verb to its relative clause meaning

pairs.english: She who makes pots / the woman who makes pots
Those who make baskets / the basket makers

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four nominalised verbs — four relative clauses without a single 'who' or 'that'. The verb form is the relative clause.

exercises.prompt: The bear that runs — how would Kanien'kéha express this?
exercises.correct: By nominalising the running verb with the bear as actor — the running bear becomes one complex nominal expression
options: By nominalising the running verb with the bear as actor
By using 'that' as a relative pronoun
By placing 'runs' after bear as a separate sentence
By using a special adjective form
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The running bear — a nominalised verb phrase with bear as actor. The complex nominal packages the noun and the relative clause into one unit. No separate relative pronoun is needed.

exercises.prompt: The one who...
exercises.meaning: She who makes pots — Those who make baskets
exercises.storyPrompt: Iakoriwá:ienstha. Ronataierénhthon. She who makes pots. Those who make baskets. These are relative clauses — descriptions embedded inside a noun phrase. In English you need 'who' or 'that' to build them. In Kanien'kéha, the verb form itself carries the description. The maker words you learned in Unit 15 were relative clauses all along. Now you know what they are. Every maker word is a grammar lesson hiding in the vocabulary.

lessons.title: Causal and Purposive Clauses
lessons.sub: Because, so that, in order to — linking cause and purpose
intro: Causal clauses explain why something happens. Purposive clauses explain what something is for. Both are common in daily speech and in the Thanksgiving Address.
In Kanien'kéha, these are formed through specific particles and verb forms that you will recognise from earlier units.
This lesson gives you the tools to say: I am learning because the language matters. She made the basket in order to carry the corn. The ceremony is held so that the people give thanks.
lessons.ped: Causal and purposive clauses: Nation (2001): causal and purposive clauses are high-frequency in natural speech. Physical domain: producing explanations and purpose statements.

exercises.prompt: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am learning Kanien'kéha. To say WHY, you add a purposive clause. The reason: because the language matters. How do causal/purposive clauses in Kanien'kéha typically work?
exercises.correct: A particle or verb form introduces the reason or purpose as a dependent clause after or before the main action
options: A particle introduces the reason as a dependent clause
'Because' is borrowed from English
The reason comes as a separate sentence only
Causal clauses do not exist in Kanien'kéha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Causal and purposive clauses use specific particles and verb forms to link reason to action. The structure: main action + linking particle + reason/purpose clause.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address gives reasons throughout: the waters are thanked because they sustain us, the sun because it gives warmth. What clause type is this?
exercises.correct: Causal — each acknowledgement states the being and the reason (because they do X) for thanking them
options: Causal — the being + because they do X
Purposive — the being in order to thank them
Relative — the being that does X
Temporal — when the being does X
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Thanksgiving Address is structured around causal clauses: we thank the waters because they sustain us. Cause and acknowledgement, linked throughout the most important text in the language.

exercises.prompt: Match the clause type to an example

pairs.english: We give thanks because the waters sustain us
She made the pot in order to cook corn
She made the fire, then she cooked the corn

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Causal, purposive, temporal, sequential — four clause types, four ways of explaining relationships between events.

exercises.prompt: Because
exercises.storyPrompt: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha. I am learning Kanien'kéha. Because the language matters. Because it carries the world. Because learning it is an act of relationship. Because every word you carry is a word that stays in the language. Every time you explain why you do something in Kanien'kéha, you are using a causal clause. Every time you say what something is for, you are using a purposive clause. The language of reason is not abstract — it is the language of motivation, of ceremony, of the Thanksgiving Address itself. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Complex Sentences
lessons.sub: Connectors, relative clauses, and complex sentence building
intro: This review consolidates complex sentence building: tánon (and), tsi (when/where), onen' (then), ki' (just), relative clauses through nominalisation, and causal/purposive clauses.
Complex sentences are not grammatical luxuries — they are how real communication happens. You need them to tell stories, explain reasons, describe people, and sequence events.
Use this lesson to consolidate before continuing.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001): review through new combinations.

exercises.prompt: Match the connector to its function
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four connectors, four linking strategies. These are the grammar of connected speech.

exercises.prompt: Iakoriwá:ienstha is a nominalised verb used as a relative clause. What does knowing this tell you about the maker words you learned in Unit 15?
exercises.correct: They were all relative clauses — she who makes pots, those who weave baskets — embedded in noun form
options: They were all relative clauses embedded in noun form
They are simple nouns with no embedded structure
Only some of them are relative clauses
Relative clauses require 'who' in Kanien'kéha
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Every maker word in Unit 15 was a nominalised relative clause. The potter is she-who-makes-pots. The basket makers are those-who-make-baskets. Vocabulary and grammar were the same thing all along.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address contains causal, purposive, and relative clauses throughout. What does this tell you about its grammatical sophistication?
exercises.correct: The Address is one of the most grammatically complex texts in the language — it uses the full clause system to express nuanced gratitude
options: The Address uses the full clause system — it is grammatically sophisticated
The Address is simple and repetitive
The Address only uses simple sentences
Complex clauses only appear in the Address
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Thanksgiving Address uses nominalisation, causal clauses, relative constructions, and sequential linking throughout. Learning complex sentence building is, among other things, learning to understand the Address more deeply.

exercises.prompt: Complex sentences
exercises.storyPrompt: Tánon, tsi, onen', ki'. Four small words that connect the world. She made the basket AND carried it. WHEN winter came, THEN the people gathered. The ceremony JUST ended. Every complex sentence in Kanien'kéha is built from connectors like these, plus the nominalisation strategies that embed relative clauses, plus the aspect markers that place everything in time. You now have the complete toolkit. The sentences you build with it are yours. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Complex Sentences
lessons.sub: Connectors, relative clauses, causal and purposive clauses
intro: This review consolidates the complex sentence tools from this unit: tánon (and), tsi (when/where), onen' (then), ki' (just), relative clauses through nominalisation, and causal/purposive clauses.
Use it to consolidate before Unit 22.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001).

exercises.prompt: Match the connector to its function
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four connectors, four linking strategies.

exercises.prompt: Iakoriwá:ienstha is a nominalised verb used as a relative clause. What does knowing this tell you?
exercises.correct: The maker words from Unit 15 were all relative clauses — she who makes pots, those who make baskets — embedded in noun form
options: They were all relative clauses embedded in noun form
They are simple nouns
Only some are relative clauses
Relative clauses require 'who'
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Every maker word in Unit 15 was a nominalised relative clause. The grammar and the vocabulary were the same thing all along.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address contains causal, purposive, and relative clauses throughout. What does this tell you?
exercises.correct: The Address uses the full clause system — it is grammatically sophisticated
options: The Address uses the full clause system
The Address is simple
The Address only uses simple sentences
Complex clauses only appear in the Address
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Learning complex sentence building is, among other things, learning to understand the Address more deeply.

exercises.prompt: Complex sentences
exercises.storyPrompt: Tánon, tsi, onen', ki'. Four small words that connect the world. She made the basket AND carried it. WHEN winter came, THEN the people gathered. The ceremony JUST ended. Every complex sentence in Kanien'kéha is built from connectors like these. The sentences you build with them are yours. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 22 — Grammar in Action: Putting the Verb System Together
desc: Consolidating everything from the verb system units — active/stative, aspect, transitive verbs, relational prefixes — in connected, realistic speech.

lessons.title: The Verb System: A Map
lessons.sub: Seeing the whole system at once
intro: You have now learned the core of the Kanien'kéha verb system across four units: active vs stative (Unit 10), relational prefixes (Unit 11), transitive verbs and object agreement (Unit 18), and tense, aspect, and mood (Unit 19).
This unit brings those pieces together into connected, realistic speech. The goal is not to review grammar in isolation but to use it in combination — the way real Kanien'kéha speakers do.
Think of this as the unit where the grammar becomes language.
lessons.ped: Integration of verb system: Nation (2001): integrative practice across grammatical subsystems produces deeper acquisition than reviewing each in isolation. This unit is the payoff of the four preceding grammar units.

exercises.prompt: Ká:tiens — I am sitting (active, habitual). Sohkwá:ren — you are strong (stative). Sahrón:ni — you make it (transitive, habitual). Wahrón:nis — he made it (transitive, completed). What do these four forms show together?
exercises.correct: The full range of the verb system: active/stative distinction, transitive/intransitive, and aspect — all in four words
options: The full range: active/stative, transitive/intransitive, and aspect — all in four words
They are all the same type of verb
They all use the same prefix
They all describe the same action
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four forms, four grammatical dimensions. This is the verb system working simultaneously.

exercises.prompt: Match each verb to its grammatical type

pairs.english: Collective transitive — it makes our minds happy

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Active, stative, relational, collective transitive — four types in four verbs.

exercises.prompt: Sahsénhawe — you carry it. What are the three things the verb system is encoding in this one word?
exercises.correct: Actor (you), object (it/inanimate), and habitual aspect — three grammatical dimensions in one verb
options: Actor (you), object (inanimate it), habitual aspect — three dimensions
Actor only
Actor and aspect only
Object only
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Sahsénhawe: sa- (you, actor) + -hawe (carry with inanimate object encoding) + habitual. Three dimensions. One word.

exercises.prompt: The verb system at full capacity
exercises.storyPrompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase'. You have said this phrase since Unit 5. Now look at what it contains: a collective object prefix (our minds/us), a making/causing root, an aspect suffix, and a subject prefix (it). One word holds a transitive relationship between the world's gifts and our collective minds. This is the verb system at full capacity. Not grammar for its own sake — grammar in service of gratitude.

lessons.title: Active and Stative in the Same Sentence
lessons.sub: Using both verb types together
intro: Real speech mixes active and stative verbs constantly. She is sick but she is making the basket. He is strong and he runs every day. The basket is there and it is beautiful.
This lesson practises combining active and stative verbs in the same sentence or passage, using the connectors from Unit 21.
The result is richer, more natural Kanien'kéha.
lessons.ped: Active/stative integration: Nation (2001): vocabulary integration in sentence context. Physical domain: producing mixed active/stative sentences.

exercises.prompt: Saiosé:ren — you are sick (stative). Tánon ká:tiens — and you are sitting (active). What grammatical contrast is in this combined sentence?
exercises.correct: A stative condition (being sick) combined with an active ongoing action (sitting) — two verb types, one sentence
options: Stative condition + active ongoing action — two verb types in one sentence
Both are stative
Both are active
The verbs cannot be combined
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Stative + active in one sentence. The grammar works exactly as it does for each separately — combining them is just natural speech.

exercises.prompt: Ioia'táwen — it is good (stative). Tánon wahró:nis — and he made it (active, completed). What does combining these two tell the listener?
exercises.correct: The thing is good AND it was made — the quality (stative) and the origin (active completed) together
options: Quality and origin together — what it is and how it came to be
They contradict each other
Only the second clause matters
The stative always overrides the active
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ioia'táwen tánon wahró:nis — it is good and he made it. The quality of the object and the act of its making, in one sentence.

exercises.prompt: Match the combined sentence to its grammatical description

pairs.english: She is strong and she runs (stative + active habitual)
She is sick while she is sitting (stative + active simultaneous)
It is good — then he made it (stative + active sequential)
It is big and it is good (two statives)

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four combinations of active and stative verbs.

exercises.prompt: Complete: she is strong AND she runs — Sohkwá:ren ___ tesará:htat
exercises.correct: tánon
exercises.hint: t
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Tánon — and. The connector that joins two equal clauses.

exercises.prompt: Strength and movement
exercises.storyPrompt: Sohkwá:ren. You are strong. Tánon tekaráhtats. And I run. One stative. One active. Two people. A complete picture of health and movement in two short clauses. This is what integrated grammar sounds like — not grammar exercises, but the description of a real state and a real action, joined by tánon. The grammar is transparent. The people are visible through it.

lessons.title: Aspect in Narrative
lessons.sub: Telling a story using all three aspects
intro: Telling even a short story requires moving between aspects: what was happening (habitual), what happened and finished (completed past), and what was planned or about to happen (future/intentional).
This lesson practises aspect-switching in a short narrative — describing a day, an event, a ceremony — using the aspect markers from Unit 19.
The result is connected, time-aware speech.
lessons.ped: Aspect in narrative: Nation (2001): narrative is the most demanding productive task. Physical domain primary: generating multi-sentence aspect-consistent narratives.

exercises.prompt: Ká:tiens — habitual. Ka' iéhse'skwe' — completed past. Iahsé:ke — future. A story uses all three. Which aspect opens a typical narrative?
exercises.correct: Habitual — setting the scene of what was regularly happening before the specific events
options: Habitual — setting the scene of regular background activity
Completed past — the events have already happened
Future — what will happen
Stative — describing conditions
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Narratives typically open with habitual aspect: this is what was regularly happening. Then the specific events (completed past) break through. Then future aspect closes with what followed.

exercises.prompt: Match the aspect to its role in narrative structure

pairs.english: Background, scene-setting — what was regularly true
The specific events — what happened and is done
What followed or was planned next

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Habitual (background), completed (events), future (what followed), closing. The narrative arc in four aspects.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address uses habitual throughout. Why is this the right narrative mode for it?
exercises.correct: The Address describes the world as it continuously is — not past events or future plans, but the ongoing state of relationships
options: The Address describes ongoing relationships — the habitual is the aspect of continuous truth
The Address is a story of past events
The Address is about future hopes
The Address mixes all aspects equally
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Thanksgiving Address is not narrative in the story sense — it is the habitual of truth. The earth sustains us — always. The water quenches thirst — always. Habitual for what is continuously true; completed past for what happened once; future for what is coming.

exercises.prompt: In a narrative, introduce the next event: ___' this happened
exercises.correct: Onen
exercises.hint: O
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onen' — then. The narrative connector that sequences events.

exercises.prompt: Narrative in winter
exercises.storyPrompt: Kenién:ke — winter. The storytelling season. In a story set in winter: Kenién:ke tánon kanonsesné:hane' — it is winter and people gather in the longhouse (habitual, background). Onen' Ratiwennahní:ion sennhiáhkhw — then the singers begin to sing (completed, event). Onen' ki' wáhi — that is all. Three sentences, three aspects, one complete scene. The grammar does not call attention to itself. It holds the story in time.

lessons.title: Full Verb System Practice
lessons.sub: Using everything together in production
intro: This lesson is pure production practice — generating sentences that use multiple parts of the verb system simultaneously: actor + object, aspect, active or stative, connected with particles.
There is no new grammar here. The goal is fluency with what you have already learned.
The exercises push you to generate rather than recognise.
lessons.ped: Productive fluency: Nation (2001): productive control — being able to generate forms, not just recognise them — is the highest vocabulary achievement. This lesson is entirely production-focused.

exercises.prompt: You want to say: 'She made the basket and she carries it now.' What aspects do you need?
exercises.correct: Completed past for made (it's done) and habitual for carries (ongoing now)
options: Completed past for 'made', habitual for 'carries'
Habitual for both
Completed for both
Future for both
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Made = completed past (it's done). Carries = habitual (ongoing now). Two actions, two aspects, one sentence.

exercises.prompt: You want to say: 'He sees her and she helps him.' What grammatical system handles these two verbs?
exercises.correct: Relational prefixes — he→her for seeing, she→him for helping — both encoded in the verb prefixes
options: Relational prefixes encode both relationships in the verb prefixes
Separate object words after each verb
Word order alone
The verbs do not need to mark the objects
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Raksatá:ti (he sees me) and Akohronnión:ni (she helps him) — relational prefixes handle both. The actor and the object are in the prefix. No separate words needed.

exercises.prompt: You want to say: 'I am strong and I will make the canoe.' What combination do you need?
exercises.correct: Stative for 'strong' (condition) + future/intentional for 'will make' (planned)
options: Stative for 'strong' + future/intentional for 'will make'
Active habitual for both
Completed past for both
Stative for both
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Strong = stative (the condition you are in). Will make = future/intentional (the plan). One condition, one intention.

exercises.prompt: Complete: I make it — Akh___
exercises.correct: ró:nis
exercises.hint: r
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Akhró:nis — I make it. Akh- (I, actor) + making root + inanimate object encoding.

exercises.prompt: Making, carrying, giving
exercises.storyPrompt: Sahsénhawe. Sahrón:ni. Skahó:wi. You carry it. You make it. You give. Three transitive verbs. Three actions reaching out from the actor to affect something or someone else. This is the verb system at work in daily life — not in a grammar exercise, but in the making and carrying and giving that community life is made of. Every time you use a transitive verb in Kanien'kéha, you are encoding a relationship. That is what the verb system is for.

lessons.title: Review — Grammar in Action
lessons.sub: The verb system as a whole
intro: This review consolidates the verb system integration from this unit and brings together the grammar from Units 10, 11, 18, 19, and 21.
You now have the complete Kanien'kéha verb system: active and stative, habitual and completed and future, transitive and intransitive, relational prefixes, conditional, narrative aspect use.
Use this lesson to consolidate before continuing.
lessons.ped: Unit and multi-unit consolidation: Nation (2001): major consolidation review at the end of the grammar sequence.

exercises.prompt: One more time: match each form to its grammatical type
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Active, stative, transitive, relational — the four main verb types.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' has been in this course since Unit 5. Now that you understand the verb system, what do you recognise in it?
exercises.correct: A collective transitive: ionkhi- is the object prefix (our minds/us), the root is making-happy, the aspect is habitual — it continuously makes our minds happy
options: ionkhi- is the object prefix, the root is making-happy, habitual — continuously true
It is a simple intransitive
It is past tense
It is a stative condition
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — object prefix (us) + causing/making root + habitual. The Thanksgiving Address phrase is a fully specified transitive verb. You have understood it all along. Now you know why.

exercises.prompt: Ka' iéhse'skwe'? — where did you go? You have known this since Unit 2. What is the -skwe' now that you understand the aspect system?
exercises.correct: The completed past marker — the going happened and is finished
options: The completed past marker — the going is done
A question particle
A future marker
A politeness marker
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! -skwe' — completed past. You have been using it since Unit 2. Now you know its name and its place in the aspect system. The grammar was there all along.

exercises.prompt: Close this unit: Onen' ki' ___
exercises.correct: wáhi
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onen' ki' wáhi. The closing phrase of every unit, every speech, every ceremony. You have known it since Unit 1.

exercises.prompt: The grammar is complete
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen' ki' wáhi. The verb system is complete. Active and stative, habitual and completed and future and conditional, transitive and intransitive, relational prefixes, narrative aspect, integrated production. Four units of grammar, now brought together in one. The grammar is not an obstacle between you and the language. It is the architecture the language lives in. Now that you know the architecture, you can build in it. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 23 — Describing Things: Colours, Sizes, Feelings and More
desc: How to describe anything in Kanien'kéha — colour, size, shape, quality, taste, feeling — using describing verbs

lessons.title: There Are No Adjectives Here
lessons.sub: A different way of describing — and why it makes sense
intro: In English, adjectives are their own kind of word. 'The big bear.' Big sits beside the noun, describing it.

Kanien'kéha does it differently. Describing words are verbs — they describe a state something is in. So instead of 'the big bear', you say something closer to 'the bear — it is being big'. The description is an action, not a label.

You have already been using this without knowing it had a name. Owá:nen (it is big), ohsì:io (it is good), skén:nen (it is peaceful) — all of these are describing verbs. This unit puts them to work across colour, size, texture, taste, and feeling.
lessons.ped: Describing verbs as a reorientation: The concept that adjectives don't exist as a separate word class is best introduced once rather than embedded silently. Plain language explanation — 'describing words are verbs here' — is used throughout. Nation (2001): known vocabulary (owá:nen, ohsì:io) reappears in a new structural frame. LaFever Mental domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Owá:nen means 'it is big'. To say 'the bear is big' in Kanien'kéha, you say: Ohkwá:ri owá:nen. What is owá:nen doing?
exercises.correct: It is describing the bear — saying what state it is in
options: It is describing the bear — saying what state it is in
It is a separate naming word placed after the noun
It asks a question about the bear
It tells you where the bear is
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Owá:nen is a describing verb. It tells you what state the bear is in — the state of being big. No separate adjective needed.

exercises.prompt: Match the describing verb to its meaning
exercises.sub: These are all states something can be in
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four describing verbs you already know. Each one names a state. This is how Kanien'kéha describes the world.

exercises.prompt: Kenní:io means 'it is good / beautiful'. How do you say 'the maple tree is beautiful'?
exercises.sub: Wahtá: is the maple tree
exercises.correct: Wahtá: kenní:io
options: Wahtá: kenní:io
kenní:io ne Wahtá: ní:'i
Wahtá: ne kenní:io ní:'i
kenní:io Wahtá: ne
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wahtá: kenní:io — the maple tree is beautiful. Noun first, describing verb after. A complete sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + describing verb.

exercises.prompt: States, not labels
exercises.storyPrompt: Owá:nen — it is big. In Kanien'kéha, to describe something is to say what state it is in right now. The bear is in the state of being big. The corn is in the state of being good. This is not just a grammar difference — it is a different way of seeing. Things are not permanently labelled; they are in states. A river can be calm today and not tomorrow. A child is small now and will not be small forever. What would change about how you see the world if you described it as states instead of labels?

lessons.title: Colours
lessons.sub: Colour words in Kanien'kéha — and what they are really saying
intro: Colour words in Kanien'kéha are describing verbs, like everything else in this unit. But they are also often descriptive in a deeper way — the word for green means something like 'it is plant-coloured'. The word for red means something like 'it is blood-coloured'.
The colours were introduced in Unit 4. This lesson brings them back in full sentences — describing the natural world, the land, the animals, the foods you already know.
Eleven colours: red, blue, green, yellow, black, white, brown, pink, grey, orange, purple. All of them describing verbs.
lessons.ped: Colour vocabulary in cultural context: The descriptive roots of colour terms connect language to worldview. This is introduced simply — 'the word for green means plant-coloured' — without academic framing. Nation (2001): colours from Unit 4 reappear in sentence context. Physical domain: learners produce colour description sentences.

exercises.prompt: Óhonte' means 'green'. Its root means something like 'plant-coloured' — the colour of living plants. To say 'the leaf is green', which sentence is correct?
exercises.sub: Ohná:ken is the word for leaf
exercises.correct: Ohná:ken Óhonte'
options: Ohná:ken Óhonte'
Óhonte' ne Ohná:ken ní:'i
Ohná:ken ne Óhonte'
Óhonte' Ohná:ken ne
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohná:ken Óhonte' — the leaf is green. The same pattern: noun + describing verb.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun first, then the colour word.

exercises.prompt: Match the colour word to its meaning
exercises.sub: Eight of the eleven colours
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Red, blue, black, white — four of the colours you first saw in Unit 4.

exercises.prompt: Three more colours
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Grey, orange, purple, yellow. All eleven colours are now in reach.

exercises.prompt: Say 'the strawberries are red' — Ken' niiohontésha' ___
exercises.correct: Onekwénhtara
exercises.hint: O
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ken' niiohontésha' Onekwénhtara — the strawberries are red. The first berries of summer, red and ripe for the Strawberry Ceremony.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which colour word means red?

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the raven is black'? (Ká:ka is the word for crow/raven)
exercises.correct: Ká:ka Kayé:ri
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ká:ka Kayé:ri — the crow is black. A short, complete sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + colour word.

exercises.prompt: Oròn:ia means blue. The sky and the river can both be described as Oròn:ia. What does this tell you about how Kanien'kéha groups colours?
exercises.correct: Blue covers both sky-colour and water-colour — the same word describes different shades of blue
options: Blue covers both sky-colour and water-colour — the same word describes different shades of blue
The sky and river are considered the same thing
There are two different words for sky-blue and water-blue
Oròn:ia only applies to the sky
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Every language draws colour boundaries differently. Kanien'kéha uses Oròn:ia for the blue of the sky and the blue-green of water. This is not a gap in the language — it is a different way of grouping the colour spectrum.

exercises.prompt: Say this colour sentence aloud
exercises.meaning: The flower is green / The earth is plant-coloured
exercises.hint: oh-WÉN:-tsia (3 syllables) — ÓH-on-te' (3 syllables). Two words, a complete sentence. Say it like you're looking at something growing.

exercises.prompt: White
exercises.storyPrompt: Ó:wira' — white. The white of snow in Kenién:ke (winter). The white of wampum shells in a belt. The white of a birch tree on the riverbank. Colours in Kanien'kéha come from the world — from the things that are those colours. When you learn a colour in this language, you learn it alongside what it describes. What colour do you most associate with Kanien'kehá:ka territory?

lessons.title: Size and Shape
lessons.sub: Big, small, and everything in between
intro: You already know owá:nen (it is big) and Onhkwa'shón:'a (it is small) from Unit 20. This lesson adds more size and shape descriptions: heavy, light, fast, slow, old, new.
These are all describing verbs — they say what state something is in. Used together, they let you give a fuller picture of anything you are talking about.
You can stack them: Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Okwáhson — the bear is big and heavy. Tánon (and) links two descriptions of the same thing.
lessons.ped: Combining descriptions: This lesson introduces the tánon connector as a way to link two describing verbs — a simple move toward richer sentences. Nation (2001): known words (owá:nen, tánon) reappear in new combinations. Physical domain: learners produce multi-description sentences.

exercises.prompt: Match the size and weight word to its meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Big, small, heavy, light — four physical descriptions. All describing verbs, all saying what state something is in.

exercises.prompt: Match the speed and age words
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Fast, slow, new, old — four more describing verbs. Notice Iohskatá:ien means old for a thing, not for a person. A person who is old uses a different form.

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'The drum is heavy' — Kanén:tara ___
exercises.correct: Okwáhson
exercises.hint: O
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanén:tara Okwáhson — the drum is heavy. A describing sentence about something from Unit 3.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which word means heavy?

exercises.prompt: To say 'the canoe is big and new', you use tánon to link two describing verbs: Kaná:tsihs owá:nen tánon asé:tsion. What is tánon doing here?
exercises.correct: Connecting two describing verbs — 'big and new'
options: Connecting two describing verbs — 'big and new'
Negating the second description
Asking a question
Marking the past
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Tánon links — you saw it joining nouns in Unit 12, and now it joins descriptions. Kaná:tsihs owá:nen tánon asé:tsion — the canoe is big and new.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does tánon always do?

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the rabbit is fast'? (Kwà'ien is rabbit)
exercises.correct: Kwà'ien Ioia'táwen
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kwà'ien Ioia'táwen — the rabbit is fast. A short, true sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + describing verb.

exercises.prompt: Say this description aloud
exercises.hint: Ka-non-ses-NÉ:-ha (5 syllables) — ra-TÓN:-kwe (3 syllables). The longhouse has stood for a long time. Say it like you mean the whole history of it.

exercises.prompt: Old and new
exercises.storyPrompt: Ratón:kwe — old, ancient. Asé:tsion — new, fresh. The longhouse is ratón:kwe. This app is asé:tsion. The language is ratón:kwe and asé:tsion at the same time — ancient, and being renewed right now, in you. What is the oldest thing you know in Kanien'kéha? What is the newest?

lessons.title: Good, Bad, Beautiful, Ugly
lessons.sub: Quality words — saying how good or right something is
intro: Some of the most useful describing words are quality words — good, bad, beautiful, right, dangerous, different. These show up in everyday conversation, in greetings, and in the Thanksgiving Address.
You have seen several of these before. Ohsì:io — good, beautiful. Ioiá:ner — it is right, good. Skén:nen — peaceful. Ióthteron — it is dangerous. This lesson brings them together and adds a few more.
Quality words are also how you give an opinion. 'That is good.' 'That is not right.' 'That is different.' These are the words of everyday judgment and response.
lessons.ped: Quality vocabulary as conversational function: Expressing approval, disapproval, and observation is among the most frequent functions in daily conversation. Nation (2001): known words recycled in new sentence contexts. Emotional domain: quality descriptions are the language of opinion and response.

exercises.prompt: Match the quality word to its meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four quality words. Ohsì:io and Ioiá:ner both mean 'good' — but ohsì:io leans toward beautiful/nice, while Ioiá:ner leans toward right/correct.

exercises.prompt: Iáh ohsì:io means 'not good / bad / ugly'. You learned Iáh as the negation word. What does it do to ohsì:io here?
exercises.correct: It flips ohsì:io to its opposite — not good
options: It flips ohsì:io to its opposite — not good
It asks a question about it
It means 'very good'
It changes the subject
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iáh + ohsì:io = not good. The negation pattern works the same with describing verbs as with any other verb.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does Iáh always do?

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'It is dangerous' — ___
exercises.correct: Ióthteron
exercises.hint: I
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ióthteron — it is dangerous. A single describing verb makes a complete sentence. Used to warn, to caution, to describe a situation.
exercises.wm: Please try again — starts with Ió.

exercises.prompt: Iowen'niáton means 'it is expensive'. To say 'the car is expensive', which sentence is correct? (Kà:sere' is car)
exercises.correct: Kà:sere' iowen'niáton
options: Kà:sere' iowen'niáton
Iowen'niáton ne Kà:sere' ní:'i
Kà:sere' ne iowen'niáton
iowen'niáton Kà:sere' tánon
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kà:sere' iowen'niáton — the car is expensive. Same pattern every time: noun + describing verb.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + describing verb.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the bread is cheap / inexpensive'? (Kanà:taros is bread)
exercises.correct: Kanà:taros io'tiesénha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanà:taros io'tiesénha — the bread is inexpensive. A practical sentence for everyday life.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + describing verb.

exercises.prompt: Say this aloud — and mean it
exercises.meaning: It is right / It is good
exercises.hint: io-IÁ:-ner — three syllables. Firm and clear. This word confirms, agrees, approves. Say it like you are nodding yes.

exercises.prompt: Different
exercises.storyPrompt: Tewatkaráhton — it is different. Kanien'kéha is different from English. Different from French. Different from every other language you know. That difference is not a problem — it is the point. Every language carries a different way of seeing and naming the world. You have been learning that difference, one word at a time. What is the most different thing you have discovered in Kanien'kéha so far?

lessons.title: Texture, State, and Physical Feel
lessons.sub: Wet, dry, dirty, open, closed — describing how things feel and are
intro: This lesson covers the describing verbs for physical states — wet, dry, dirty, open, closed, full. These are the words you need for talking about the world as you move through it.
All of them are describing verbs. All follow the same pattern: noun + describing verb = complete sentence.
Some of these have a direct connection to daily life on the land — wet ground, dry wood, an open door, full water. They are practical words.
lessons.ped: Physical state vocabulary: Texture and state words are high-frequency in land-based and domestic contexts. Nation (2001): new vocabulary introduced in a well-established sentence pattern to reduce cognitive load. Physical domain primary: learners practise these in real-world description sentences.

exercises.prompt: Match the physical state word to its meaning
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wet, dry, dirty, open — physical states. Use them to describe what you find on the land, in the house, in the river.

exercises.prompt: Iotkahrón:ni means 'it is closed'. To say 'the door is closed', which sentence is correct? (Kanonhsatón:ni is door)
exercises.correct: Kanonhsatón:ni Iotkahrón:ni
options: Kanonhsatón:ni Iotkahrón:ni
Iotkahrón:ni ne Kanonhsatón:ni ní:'i
ne Kanonhsatón:ni Iotkahrón:ni ní:'i
Kanonhsatón:ni ne Iotkahrón:ni
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kanonhsatón:ni Iotkahrón:ni — the door is closed. Noun + describing verb. The same every time.

exercises.prompt: The ground is wet after rain. Complete: 'Ohwén:tsiake ___'
exercises.correct: Oiónni
exercises.hint: O
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohwén:tsiake Oiónni — the ground is wet. Ohwén:tsiake (on the earth/ground) from the locative vocabulary in Unit 13.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which word means wet?

exercises.prompt: Ionkwahserón:ni means 'it is full'. You want to say 'the basket is full'. What do you need besides the describing verb?
exercises.sub: À:there' is the word for basket
exercises.correct: À:there' Ionkwahserón:ni — the basket is full
options: À:there' Ionkwahserón:ni — the basket is full
Ionkwahserón:ni ne À:there' ní:'i — naming pattern
À:there' e'tho ká:yen — existence pattern
À:there' Oiónni — wrong describing verb
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! À:there' Ionkwahserón:ni — the basket is full. One more correct Kanien'kéha sentence from you.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which sentence has the right pattern and the right describing verb?

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'the wood is dry'? (Karón:ta is tree / wood)
exercises.correct: Karón:ta Ioskaié:ri
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Karón:ta Ioskaié:ri — the wood is dry. Good wood for a fire.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + describing verb.

exercises.prompt: Say this aloud
exercises.hint: Ka-non-hsa-TÓN:-ni (5 syllables) — io-hwén-tsia-te' (4 syllables). Two words, one complete sentence about something right in front of you.

exercises.prompt: Open
exercises.storyPrompt: Iohwéntsia'te' — it is open. A door left open is an invitation. In many Kanien'kehá:ka communities, a door left open meant there was food, there was welcome, there was room. Hospitality is woven into the culture. The word for open is a physical description — but it is also a state of relationship. What does it mean to you, in your community, to leave the door open?

lessons.title: Taste and Smell
lessons.sub: Describing food and what the nose finds
intro: The glossary has a full set of food describing verbs — and you have probably already wanted some of them. Is the soup hot? Is the meat done? Is it delicious? Is it burnt?
These are all describing verbs, following exactly the same pattern you have been using all unit. The food vocabulary from Units 7 and 13 comes to life when you can describe it.
Smell gets two words: Iatewátkon — it smells good. Ioriwá:nente' — it stinks. Between them, you can describe most of what the nose finds.
lessons.ped: Taste and smell as high-frequency practical vocabulary: Food description drives a huge proportion of daily conversation. Nation (2001): new describing verbs applied to already-known food vocabulary. Emotional domain: food and smell carry strong memory and cultural connection.

exercises.prompt: Match the food describing verb to its meaning
exercises.sub: The first four
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! From delicious to burnt — the full range of what food can be. All describing verbs, all stating a condition.

exercises.prompt: Four more food states

pairs.english: It is cool / cold (food or drink)

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Hot, cold, boiled, spicy — four more states food can be in. You can now describe almost anything that comes off the fire.

exercises.prompt: Someone gives you a bowl of corn soup. You want to tell them it is delicious. What do you say?
exercises.sub: Onennó:ton onhkwa'shón:'a is corn soup
exercises.correct: Onennó:ton onhkwa'shón:'a yawékon
options: Onennó:ton onhkwa'shón:'a yawékon
Onennó:ton onhkwa'shón:'a yotsha'áhton
Onennó:ton onhkwa'shón:'a okáhte
Onennó:ton onhkwa'shón:'a yowíhsto
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Yawékon — delicious. You just gave someone one of the best responses you can give after a meal.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which word means delicious?

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'It smells good' — ___
exercises.correct: Iatewátkon
exercises.hint: I
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iatewátkon — it smells good. One word, a complete sentence.
exercises.wm: Please try again — starts with Ia.

exercises.prompt: Ioriwá:nente' means 'it stinks'. This is useful for more than just bad smells — it can describe something that has gone wrong, figuratively. When might you use it outside of a smell context?
exercises.correct: When describing a situation that is bad or wrong — 'that stinks' as a way of saying something is not right
options: When describing a situation that is bad or wrong — 'that stinks' as a way of saying something is not right
Only when talking about actual smell
Only for describing animals
It has no figurative use
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Like in English, 'it stinks' can describe more than smell. Languages allow their speakers to borrow from sensory vocabulary to talk about how situations feel. This kind of flexibility is a sign of a living language.

exercises.prompt: Say this aloud — after a meal
exercises.hint: ya-WÉ-kon — three syllables. Warm, satisfied. Say it like you just ate something that made you happy.

exercises.prompt: A meal together
exercises.storyPrompt: Yawékon — it is delicious. Food shared between people is one of the most basic acts of community. Corn soup, the Three Sisters together, bannock, roasted meat — these foods carry history and relationship. To say yawékon in Kanien'kéha after eating food made by someone in your community is to connect the eating to the language that belongs there. What food, made by someone you love, would you describe as yawékon?

lessons.title: Describing Animals
lessons.sub: Using everything you know to describe an animal
intro: This lesson is a putting-it-together lesson. You know the animals from Units 6 and earlier. You now have colour, size, speed, quality, and physical state words. This lesson asks you to describe an animal fully — not just name it.
A full description might say: the bear is big, dark, and slow. The rabbit is small, white, and fast. The eagle is large and brown. You have all the words. You just need to put them together.
Use tánon (and) to connect descriptions. Use the noun + describing verb pattern for each one.
lessons.ped: Extended description as productive output: This lesson asks learners to produce multi-element descriptions, combining several known patterns. Nation (2001): productive use of known vocabulary in open-ended tasks deepens retention. Physical domain primary. Emotional domain: animals carry clan identity and cultural meaning.

exercises.prompt: You want to say 'the bear is big and dark'. Big is owá:nen. Dark/black is Kayé:ri. What is the full sentence?
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Kayé:ri
options: Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Kayé:ri
Ohkwá:ri Kayé:ri owá:nen
owá:nen tánon Kayé:ri ne Ohkwá:ri ní:'i
Ohkwá:ri tánon owá:nen Kayé:ri
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Kayé:ri — the bear is big and dark. Two descriptions linked by tánon. The noun stays at the front.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun first, then the describing verbs joined by tánon.

exercises.prompt: Match the animal to its most fitting description
exercises.sub: Use what you know about these animals
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Four animals, four descriptions. The turtle is slow — and the Turtle Clan is the keeper of the earth. The eagle is fast and large — and it is honoured in the Thanksgiving Address. The descriptions carry meaning.

exercises.prompt: Describe the wolf: 'The wolf is big and grey' — Okwáho owá:nen tánon ___
exercises.correct: Oseráhne
exercises.hint: O
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Okwáho owá:nen tánon Oseráhne — the wolf is big and grey. The Wolf Clan is responsible for keeping peace between nations — a big responsibility.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which colour word means grey?

exercises.prompt: Describe the goose in Kanien'kéha. Use at least two describing verbs. (Káhonk is goose)
exercises.correct: Káhonk owá:nen tánon Ó:wira'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Káhonk owá:nen tánon Ó:wira' — the goose is big and white. The geese return every spring — one of the signs the land is waking up.

exercises.prompt: Say this animal description aloud
exercises.meaning: The bear is big and dark
exercises.hint: oh-KWÁ:-ri (3) — o-WÁ:-nen (3) — TÁ-non (2) — Ka-YÉ:-ri (3). Say it steadily — four words in a row describing one animal.

exercises.prompt: The bear
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — the bear is big. The Bear Clan is the keeper of medicine and health in Kanien'kehá:ka society. The clan name and the animal name are the same word. When you describe the bear — its size, its colour, its weight — you are also describing the clan that carries its name. What would it mean to describe yourself by the qualities of your clan animal?

lessons.title: Describing People
lessons.sub: How to describe a person — what is said and how it is said
intro: Describing a person in Kanien'kéha is done with the same describing verbs — but the subject changes. Instead of the bear being big, a person is being strong, or happy, or tired.
The emotional state verbs you saw in the glossary — Wakatshennón:ni (I am happy), Wake'nikonhráksen (I am sad) — already have the person built in. These are full sentences on their own.

When describing another person's appearance, Kanien'kéha speakers are often careful about what is said and how — physical descriptions that would seem rude or intrusive are avoided. This is built into the culture, not just the grammar.
lessons.ped: Cultural norms around description: This lesson acknowledges that describing people is culturally shaped — some descriptions that feel neutral in English carry different weight in Kanien'kehá:ka social norms. This is introduced plainly, not as a restriction but as cultural knowledge. Emotional domain primary: these words describe inner states and relationships.

exercises.prompt: Wakatshennón:ni means 'I am happy'. This is a complete sentence on its own. Why doesn't it need a separate word for 'I'?
exercises.correct: The person is built into the verb — the wakat- prefix already means 'I am in this state'
options: The person is built into the verb — the wakat- prefix already means 'I am in this state'
It is not a complete sentence
The word for I comes after the verb
Kanien'kéha has no word for I
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wakat- already carries 'I'. The prefix does the work that a separate pronoun does in English. This is the same principle you learned in Unit 1 — Kanien'kéha builds the subject into the verb.

exercises.prompt: Match the emotional state to its meaning
exercises.sub: All first-person — 'I am...'

pairs.english: I feel good inside / I am content

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Four states of the heart. Notice how many of them contain -nikonhr- — the root connected to mind and heart in Kanien'kéha. The mind and heart are one thing in this language.

exercises.prompt: Four more ways to say how you are
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Afraid, angry, busy, ready — four more states. You now have the vocabulary to honestly say how you are.

exercises.prompt: Wake'nikonhrí:io — I feel good inside. The root -nikonhr- appears in many Kanien'kéha words. Where have you seen it before?
exercises.correct: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne — it makes my mind happy (from Unit 1)
options: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne — it makes my mind happy (from Unit 1)
Kanié:ri iken'? — what is your name?
Ka' nontáhse'? — where did you come from?
Tho niioht — that's right
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! -nikonhr- is the mind/heart root — one of the most important roots in the language. Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne, Wake'nikonhrí:io, Wake'nikonhráksen — the same root in different emotional states. Once you know a root, you see it everywhere.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which familiar phrase contains -nikonhr-?

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I am happy' in Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: Wakatshennón:ni
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wakatshennón:ni — I am happy. A complete sentence in one word.

exercises.prompt: Say this aloud — and check in with yourself
exercises.sub: Is it true right now?
exercises.meaning: I feel good inside / I am content
exercises.hint: wa-ke'-ni-kon-hrÍ:-io — six syllables. The -nikonhr- is the heart-root — say it from there. Is this true for you right now?

exercises.prompt: Heavy-hearted
exercises.storyPrompt: Wake'nikonhrén:ton — I am heavy-hearted. There is a word in Kanien'kéha for this feeling. Not just 'sad' — heavy-hearted. The weight of something sitting in the chest. Grief, loss, worry, the weight of history. Many Kanien'kehá:ka people carry a heavy-heartedness about the language — knowing what was lost, knowing what is still being lost. And yet they keep teaching. Keep learning. Keep speaking. Even with a heavy heart. What do you carry that is heavy, as you learn this language?

lessons.title: Making It More or Less (Intensifiers)
lessons.sub: Very, quite, a little — adding degree to a description
intro: Sometimes big is not enough — it is very big. Sometimes good is the wrong strength — it is only a little good. Kanien'kéha has words for adding degree to a description.
You have already met owá:nen — big. And -owá:nen can also be a suffix that adds 'great' to a word: Niawenhkó:wa — thank you greatly. Skennón:wa — great peace.
This lesson looks at how degree works in Kanien'kéha — the words that strengthen or soften a description.
lessons.ped: Degree as nuance: Degree words move learners from basic description to more expressive and natural speech. Nation (2001): degree words are best learned alongside the words they modify. Physical domain: learners produce intensified descriptions. Emotional domain: degree is the difference between saying how you feel and how you really feel.

exercises.prompt: Niawenhkó:wa — thank you very much. The -kó:wa ending means 'great / large'. Where else have you seen -kó:wa (or -owá:nen) adding greatness to a word?
exercises.sub: Think of greetings and peace words you know
exercises.correct: Skennón:wa — great peace (Skennen + kó:wa). Also in Rotinonhsyón:ni — People of the Longhouse
options: Skennón:wa — great peace (Skennen + kó:wa). Also in Rotinonhsyón:ni — People of the Longhouse
Only in Niawenhkó:wa
It only appears at the end of names
-kó:wa has no connection to other words
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! -kó:wa / -owá:nen is a greatness suffix that runs through the language. Niawenhkó:wa — great thanks. Skennón:wa — great peace. Kaià:ner'kó:wa — Great Law of Peace. Once you see this suffix, you start noticing it everywhere.

exercises.prompt: To say something is very good, you can say ohsì:io owá:nen — literally 'it is good, greatly'. What does adding owá:nen after another describing verb do?
exercises.correct: It strengthens the description — 'greatly good', 'very good'
options: It strengthens the description — 'greatly good', 'very good'
It creates a new word
It negates the first description
It asks a question
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Owá:nen can follow another describing verb to strengthen it — 'it is good, greatly'. This is one way Kanien'kéha adds degree. The more natural the combination becomes to your ear, the more fluent you are becoming.

exercises.prompt: Match the strengthened expression to its meaning
exercises.sub: You know all the parts — now see them combined

pairs.english: The Great Law / Great Good Path

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The -kó:wa / -owá:nen pattern is not just a grammar note — it is at the heart of some of the most important words in Kanien'kéha. The Great Law, great peace, great thanks.

exercises.prompt: To say the eagle is very large: Á:kwe'ks owá:nen ___
exercises.correct: owá:nen
exercises.hint: o
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Á:kwe'ks owá:nen owá:nen — the eagle is very large. Repeating owá:nen, or following one describing verb with owá:nen, intensifies the description. The eagle is large, greatly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what word adds 'greatly' after a description?

exercises.prompt: Say this aloud and feel its weight
exercises.hint: Nia-wen-KÓ:-wa — four syllables. The -kó:wa is the greatness — it is not decorative. You are expressing great thanks. Say it like you mean the greatness of it.

exercises.prompt: Skennón:wa
