exercises.storyPrompt: Skennón:wa — great peace. This is the word the Peacemaker brought to the nations. Not just quiet. Not just absence of war. Great peace — a living, active, maintained state of right relationship between people and between nations. The Haudenosaunee Confederacy is built on this word. It is the -kó:wa that makes peace not just small and personal but vast and communal. What would great peace look like in your community, in your family, in yourself?

lessons.title: Saying It Is Not (Negating Descriptions)
lessons.sub: It is not red — making any description negative
intro: You learned Iáh (not) in Unit 3 and used it with verbs throughout the course. Negating a describing verb works the same way — Iáh comes before the describing word.
Iáh ohsì:io — not good. Iáh owá:nen — not big. Iáh Onekwénhtara — not red. The pattern is consistent: Iáh + describing verb = negative description.
You can also say what something is not, and then say what it is: Iáh Kayé:ri — Ó:wira'. Not black — white. This kind of contrast is natural in real conversation.
lessons.ped: Negation of descriptions as complete productive skill: Learners who can negate descriptions can participate in correction, contrast, and clarification — all common communicative functions. Nation (2001): negation extends productive control of the full description system. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: To say 'the corn is not red', you say: Onón:ta Iáh Onekwénhtara. Where does Iáh go?
exercises.correct: Before the describing verb — Iáh Onekwénhtara
options: Before the describing verb — Iáh Onekwénhtara
At the end of the sentence
Before the noun
Iáh is not used with colour words
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Iáh always comes before what it negates. Onón:ta Iáh Onekwénhtara — the corn is not red.
exercises.wm: Please try again — where does Iáh go?

exercises.prompt: Match the negative description to its meaning
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Iáh + [describing verb] = the negative. Any description can be flipped this way.

exercises.prompt: The wolf is not black. Complete: Okwáho Iáh ___
exercises.correct: Kayé:ri
exercises.hint: K
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Okwáho Iáh Kayé:ri — the wolf is not black. What colour is it? Oseráhne — grey.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which colour word means black?

exercises.prompt: Say 'the snow is not black — it is white' in Kanien'kéha. (Óniehte is snow)
exercises.sub: Use the contrast pattern: not [this] — [that]
exercises.correct: Óniehte Iáh Kayé:ri — Ó:wira'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Óniehte Iáh Kayé:ri — Ó:wira'. Not black — white. A contrast sentence using negation. The snow is obviously white — but the pattern works with any contrast.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Pattern: [noun] Iáh [first colour] — [second colour]

exercises.prompt: A child says 'Kwà'ien owá:nen' (the rabbit is big). You know rabbits are small. How do you gently correct this?
exercises.correct: Iáh owá:nen — Onhkwa'shón:'a — not big, it is small
options: Iáh owá:nen — Onhkwa'shón:'a — not big, it is small
Iáh Kwà'ien — not a rabbit
Kwà'ien Iáh — the rabbit is no
Onhkwa'shón:'a ne ní:'i — that is small
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Iáh owá:nen — Onhkwa'shón:'a. A gentle correction using the contrast pattern. Not big — small. This is how language learning works — noticing, correcting, trying again.
exercises.wm: Please try again — how do you say 'not big'?

exercises.prompt: Say this negative description aloud
exercises.hint: IÁH (firm) — O-nek-wén-tha-ra — pause — o-RÒN:-ia. Two words in contrast. The pause between them is important — it gives the listener time to process the correction before the new information arrives.

exercises.prompt: Not
exercises.storyPrompt: Iáh — not. There is something freeing about negation. When you can say what something is not, you can be precise. You can correct misunderstandings. You can disagree. You can say: that is not what I meant. That is not what happened. That is not who I am. The word Iáh is small and it is powerful. What would you like to say 'not' to, in Kanien'kéha?

lessons.title: Writing a Full Description
lessons.sub: Putting it all together — a scene described in Kanien'kéha
intro: This is the final lesson of Unit 23. It brings together everything from the unit — colour, size, quality, texture, taste, feeling, negation, degree — in a putting-it-all-together challenge.
You will read a short scene, answer questions about it, and then try to describe something of your own. The scene uses only vocabulary from this course.
A description in Kanien'kéha is built one sentence at a time: one noun, one describing verb, perhaps a tánon and a second description. Stack them and you have a picture.
lessons.ped: Extended description as unit endpoint: Nation (2001): productive synthesis of all unit vocabulary in connected, meaningful text. LaFever all four domains: knowing the vocabulary (Mental), producing descriptions (Physical), connecting to real experience (Emotional), situating description within a living language (Spiritual).

exercises.prompt:
Read this scene and answer:

'Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke. Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Kayé:ri. Kaniatarà:ke Oiónni. Ohwén:tsia Óhonte'.'

What colour is the bear?
exercises.correct: Dark / black — Kayé:ri
options: Dark / black — Kayé:ri
White — Ó:wira'
Brown — Athéhsa
Grey — Oseráhne
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Kayé:ri — the bear is big and dark. You read a Kanien'kéha description and pulled the right detail from it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look for the colour word in the sentence about the bear.

exercises.prompt: From the same scene: 'Kaniatarà:ke Oiónni.' What does this sentence tell you?
exercises.correct: The river is wet
options: The river is wet
The river is big
The river is cold
The river is there
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Kaniatarà:ke Oiónni — the river is wet. Of course it is — and yet the sentence is complete and correct. The ordinary things are worth naming.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does Oiónni mean?

exercises.prompt: From the same scene: 'Ohwén:tsia Óhonte'.' What is Ohwén:tsia?
exercises.sub: You know this word from Unit 3
exercises.correct: Flower / earth — the flower (or earth) is green
options: Flower / earth — the flower (or earth) is green
The river
The bear
The corn
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ohwén:tsia — flower / earth / ground. Ohwén:tsia Óhonte' — the earth is green. The scene is coming together: a dark bear at a wet river beside green earth.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what is Ohwén:tsia?

exercises.prompt: Now add a sentence to the scene about corn. Which is the best full description sentence?
exercises.correct: Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr — the corn is beautiful and yellow
options: Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr — the corn is beautiful and yellow
Onón:ta ne ní:'i — that is the corn
Onón:ta e'tho ká:yen — the corn is there
Onón:ta Iáh ohsì:io — the corn is not good
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr — beautiful and yellow. Two describing verbs joined by tánon. The scene now includes the corn.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which sentence describes what the corn looks like?

exercises.prompt: Read the full scene aloud
exercises.sub: Take your time — you are describing something real
exercises.word: Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke. Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Kayé:ri. Kaniatarà:ke Oiónni. Ohwén:tsia Óhonte'. Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr.
exercises.meaning: The bear is at the river. The bear is big and dark. The river is wet. The earth is green. The corn is beautiful and yellow.
exercises.hint: Five sentences. Five pictures. Say each one with a small pause between — you are drawing something for the listener with your words.

exercises.prompt: A scene in Kanien'kéha
exercises.storyPrompt: Onón:ta ohsì:io — the corn is beautiful. You have described a scene in Kanien'kéha. A bear, a river, the earth, the corn. This is not an exercise anymore — it is a description of a real place, in the language of the people who have described that place for generations. The bear was always big. The river was always wet. The corn was always beautiful. And now you have said so, in the language. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Describing Things
lessons.sub: Bringing the whole unit together
intro: This review brings together the key vocabulary and ideas from this unit.
Use it to consolidate what you know before moving on.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation review: Nation (2001): spaced retrieval review across all lesson content produces deeper retention than reviewing categories separately.

exercises.prompt: Match the describing word to what it describes
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Colour, size, emotion, quality — four types of description you can now produce.

exercises.prompt: Onhkwa'shón:'a — it is small (as a description). You have also seen this word as the noun for beads. What does this tell you about Kanien'kéha vocabulary?
exercises.correct: The same root can function as a noun and a describing verb — meaning shifts with grammatical context
options: The same root can be noun or describing verb — context determines meaning
There are two different words
Beads are always small
The word is only used for beads
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Onhkwa'shón:'a — beads (noun) and small (describing verb). The same root, two functions. Kanien'kéha vocabulary is dense with these productive roots.

exercises.prompt: Complete: green — Óhonte___
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Óhonte' — green. The glottal stop closes the colour word.
exercises.wm: Please try again — a glottal stop closes it.

exercises.prompt: Description
exercises.storyPrompt: You can now describe the world in Kanien'kéha. The bear is big. The snow is white. The corn is green. She is happy. He is heavy-hearted. The river smells good. The night is dark. Description is how we share what we see with others. And seeing the world through the descriptions of Kanien'kéha — through colour words rooted in blood and plants and sky, through size words that are stative verbs, through emotional words built on the mind-heart root — is seeing it differently. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 24 — Spatial Language: Location, Direction & the Land
desc: Where things are, where they go, and how Kanien'kéha encodes the physical world — locative expressions, directional verbs, and land vocabulary.

lessons.title: Locative Expressions: Where Things Are
lessons.sub: E'tho ká:yen — it is there. The language of place.
intro: You know E'tho ká:yen — it is there — from Unit 20. This lesson extends the locative vocabulary: above, below, inside, outside, beside, across.
Kanien'kéha encodes location through specific locative particles and suffixes that attach to place nouns. The system is regular once you know the patterns.
Spatial language in Kanien'kéha is connected to the physical territory — the waterways, the mountains, the longhouse, the land.
lessons.ped: Locative system: Mithun (1982): locative suffixes in Kanien'kéha are highly productive. Nation (2001): locative vocabulary is among the highest-frequency spatial vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Ohwén:tsiake — on the earth / at ground level. The -ke suffix marks 'on/at'. Where else have you seen -ke as a locative suffix?
exercises.correct: In Kanehsatà:ke (at Kanehsatà:ke) and Kaniatarà:ke (at the river) — the -ke suffix is the standard locative
options: In Kanehsatà:ke and Kaniatarà:ke — -ke is the standard locative suffix
Only in Ohwén:tsiake
The suffix is decorative
Only on nature nouns
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! -ke marks 'at/on' throughout the language. Kanehsatà:ke — at Kanehsatà:ke. Kaniatarà:ke — at the river. Ohwén:tsiake — at ground level. One suffix, many places.

exercises.prompt: Match the locative expression to its meaning

pairs.english: On the earth / at ground level
In the mountains / where the mountains are

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ground, sky, river, mountains — four locative expressions covering the vertical and horizontal landscape of Kanien'kehá:ka territory.

exercises.prompt: Awé:ri — down the bay / toward the water. This is a directional locative specific to Kanehsatà:ke. What does having a place-specific directional tell you?
exercises.correct: Locative language is grounded in specific landscapes — the directions are not abstract but tied to actual geographic features
options: Locative language is grounded in actual geographic features
Directionals are always abstract
Awé:ri could mean any direction
Only Kanehsatà:ke has directional vocabulary
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Awé:ri — down the bay. This direction is specific to the geography of Kanehsatà:ke, where the bay is a prominent landmark. Local directionals encode the landscape the speakers live in. Language and land are inseparable.

exercises.prompt: Complete the ground-level locative: Ohwén:tsia___
exercises.correct: ke
exercises.hint: k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohwén:tsiake — on the earth, at ground level. The -ke locative suffix on the earth noun.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which suffix marks 'at/on'?

exercises.prompt: The land
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohwén:tsia — earth, ground. The same word that appears in the Thanksgiving Address as the earth that sustains us. In spatial language it is the ground underfoot, the reference point for all the other locatives: above the earth, below the water, at the river, in the mountains. The land is not just a place in Kanien'kéha — it is the spatial anchor for all location. Every locative expression in the language is oriented around the features of the land the language grew in.

lessons.title: Direction Verbs Extended
lessons.sub: Going, coming, crossing — the full vocabulary of movement
intro: You know the core direction verbs from Unit 2: going, coming, where did you go. This lesson extends the direction verb vocabulary to cover crossing water, going uphill, going downhill, going inside, and going out.
These directions are tied to the actual territory: crossing the river, going up the mountain, entering the longhouse.
Direction verbs in Kanien'kéha are among the most spatially specific verbs in the language.
lessons.ped: Extended direction verbs: Nation (2001): direction verbs are high-frequency functional vocabulary. Physical domain primary: production of direction sentences.

exercises.prompt: Match the direction question-answer pair
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The three core direction question-answer pairs you learned in Unit 2. Still working. Still useful.

exercises.prompt: Kanonhséhsne — at the longhouse. This is the locative form of the longhouse noun. You are going to the longhouse. What direction verb would you use?
exercises.correct: wà:ke — I am going — combined with the longhouse locative to say 'I am going to the longhouse'
options: wà:ke — I am going — combined with the longhouse locative
A completely different verb
A future form is always needed
The locative alone is enough
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! wà:ke thi Kanonhséhsne — I am going to the longhouse. Direction verb + article thi (motion/direction) + destination locative. The pattern from Unit 2 still works.

exercises.prompt: Kaniatarà:ke — at the river. To say 'I am going to the river', you use wà:ke thi Kaniatarà:ke. Why is thi the right article here rather than ne?
exercises.correct: Thi marks motion toward a destination — ne marks a stationary reference. Going requires thi.
options: Thi marks motion toward a destination — ne marks stationary reference
They are interchangeable
Ne is used with water nouns
Thi is only for the longhouse
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Thi vs ne — the article of direction vs the article of reference. You are going toward the river (thi). The river is there (ne). You have known this since Unit 3.

exercises.prompt: Going
exercises.storyPrompt: Wà:ke — I am going. The simplest direction verb. Combined with a destination locative, it becomes: I am going to the longhouse, to the river, to my grandmother's house, to Kanehsatà:ke. Direction is how you place yourself in the territory — not just where you are, but where you are moving. Wà:ke is not just a verb. It is a declaration of direction. And in Kanien'kehá:ka territory, knowing your direction — knowing where you are going — has always been a matter of survival, of ceremony, of relationship.

lessons.title: Land Features and Place Names
lessons.sub: The vocabulary of the physical landscape
intro: Kanien'kehá:ka place names are descriptions of the land. Kanehsatà:ke — where the crust of snow on top of the earth is. Kahnawà:ke — at the rapids. Akwesasne — where the partridge drums.
This lesson extends the land feature vocabulary: river, lake, mountain, field, forest, rapids, swamp. Each names a feature of the territory where the language lives.
Knowing these words means knowing how to talk about the land in the language that grew from it.
lessons.ped: Land vocabulary as territorial knowledge: Tuck & Yang (2012): land vocabulary is sovereignty vocabulary. LaFever Spiritual domain: the land is not backdrop but relationship.

exercises.prompt: Match the land feature to its name
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! River, lake, mountains, rapids — four features of the Kanien'kehá:ka landscape, each with its own name.

exercises.prompt: Kaniá:tara — lake. Kaniatarà:ke — at the river. They share the Kaniat- root. What does this shared root suggest?
exercises.correct: Both relate to water bodies — the root carries the sense of a significant body of water, with suffixes specifying lake vs river location
options: Both relate to water bodies — the root carries that sense
The words are unrelated
One is a verb and one is a noun
The -ara suffix changes the meaning entirely
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Kaniat- appears in both lake and river names. The root carries the sense of a significant water feature. Suffixes and locatives specify the type and location. Word families in Kanien'kéha often work this way.

exercises.prompt: Kanehsatà:ke means 'where the crust of snow on top of the earth is'. Why is a description of snow part of the community's name?
exercises.correct: Place names describe what is actually there at a particular season or condition — the name captures a real, recurring feature of the land
options: Place names describe real, recurring features of the land
The name was assigned randomly
Snow is the most important thing about the community
The name is no longer meaningful
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanien'kehá:ka place names describe the land as it is experienced. The snow crust at Kanehsatà:ke, the rapids at Kahnawà:ke, the partridge drumming at Akwesasne — each name is a moment of the land captured in language.

exercises.prompt: Place names
exercises.storyPrompt: Kanehsatà:ke. Kahnawà:ke. Akwesasne. Three communities, three descriptions of the land. The snow crust. The rapids. Where the partridge drums. These are not arbitrary labels — they are observations, made by people living closely enough to the land to notice its specific qualities and name them. When you say these names in Kanien'kéha, you are using the land's own language about itself. Colonial names — Oka, Caughnawaga, St. Regis — overlay the land with the names of colonisers. The original names remain underneath. Saying them is an act of return. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Spatial Language
lessons.sub: Location, direction, and the land
intro: This review consolidates locative expressions, direction verbs, and land feature vocabulary.
Spatial language in Kanien'kéha is territorial language — it names the physical world that the community lives in and moves through.
Use this lesson to consolidate.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001): review through new combinations.

exercises.prompt: Match the spatial expression to its meaning

pairs.english: On the earth / at ground level
Down the bay / toward the water

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Ground, sky, lake, bay — spatial expressions across the vertical and horizontal landscape.

exercises.prompt: Wà:ke thi Kanehsatà:ke — I am going to Kanehsatà:ke. What makes this a complete direction sentence?
exercises.correct: Direction verb (wà:ke) + motion article (thi) + destination locative (Kanehsatà:ke) — three pieces make the complete sentence
options: Direction verb + motion article + destination — three pieces make it complete
The verb alone is enough
The destination alone is enough
Only two pieces are needed
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wà:ke (going) + thi (toward) + Kanehsatà:ke (destination). The three-piece direction sentence, working exactly as it did in Unit 2.

exercises.prompt: The river
exercises.meaning: At the river / on the river
exercises.storyPrompt: Kaniatarà:ke — at the river. The St. Lawrence River runs through the heart of Kanien'kehá:ka territory. People have been travelling it by canoe for thousands of years. Its waters are acknowledged in the Thanksgiving Address. Its fish sustained communities for generations. Its contamination at Akwesasne is one of the most direct environmental injustices faced by any Indigenous community in North America. The river has a name in Kanien'kéha. That name has been said on its banks since before European contact. Kaniatarà:ke. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Moving Through the Territory
lessons.sub: Direction, location, and the land together
intro: This lesson combines the locative expressions, direction verbs, and land feature vocabulary from this unit in connected sentences about moving through the Kanien'kehá:ka landscape.
Going to the river, coming from the mountains, arriving at the longhouse — this is spatial language in use.
lessons.ped: Spatial integration: Nation (2001): vocabulary integration in sentence context. Physical domain.

exercises.prompt: Match the direction sentence to its meaning

pairs.english: I am going to the longhouse

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Going, coming, where — the core direction vocabulary in use.

exercises.prompt: Awé:ri — down the bay / toward the water. You are at Kanehsatà:ke and heading toward the water. What two pieces of information does this direction give?
exercises.correct: Your starting point (Kanehsatà:ke) and your direction (toward the water/bay)
options: Starting point and direction toward the water
Only direction
Only location
It gives no spatial information
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Awé:ri encodes local geographic direction — toward the bay that is part of Kanehsatà:ke's specific landscape. Local directionals are not abstract; they are tied to the actual land.

exercises.prompt: Complete: I am going to the river — ___ thi Kaniatarà:ke
exercises.correct: wà:ke
exercises.hint: w
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Wà:ke thi Kaniatarà:ke — I am going to the river.

exercises.prompt: Kaniá:tara — lake. You are describing a journey: from the mountains, to the lake, to the longhouse. What spatial vocabulary do you need?
exercises.correct: Tsi iononté:nion (mountains), Kaniá:tara (lake), kanonhséhsne (at the longhouse) — locatives for each place
options: Locatives for each place: mountains, lake, longhouse
Only direction verbs
Only location words
Only the direction verb wà:ke
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A journey needs locatives for each place along the way, plus direction verbs to connect them. Spatial language is the vocabulary of movement through a real landscape.

exercises.prompt: The land beneath the language
exercises.storyPrompt: Kanehsatà:ke — where the crust of snow on top of the earth is. Every direction in this unit, every locative, every land feature — they all exist in relationship to a specific place on a specific land. The spatial language of Kanien'kéha is not abstract. It grew in this territory. Awé:ri (toward the bay) is only meaningful if you know which bay. Kanehsatà:ke is only meaningful if you know the land. Language and land are the same thing. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 25 — Technology & the Modern World
desc: How Kanien'kéha names the modern world — technology, media, digital life, and the language of contemporary community.

lessons.title: Naming New Things: The Language Adapts
lessons.sub: How Kanien'kéha extends its own patterns to name what is new
intro: You saw this principle in Unit 3: Kà:sere' (car), Kahiatónhsera (paper/book), Karihtón:ni (clock). Kanien'kéha names new things by extending its own noun class and descriptive systems rather than borrowing foreign words.
This lesson surveys how modern technology is named in Kanien'kéha: computers, phones, radio, television, internet. Each name is a description of what the thing does.
A language that can name everything its speakers encounter is a language that will survive into every era.
lessons.ped: Productive naming as language vitality: García & Li Wei (2014): language vitality depends on the ability to name contemporary life. Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): language pedagogy must include contemporary contexts.

exercises.prompt: Karihtón:ni — clock. The name describes what it does: the measuring/counting thing. How does this naming strategy apply to modern technology?
exercises.correct: Modern objects are named by what they do — a phone is 'the voice-carrying thing', a computer is 'the calculating thing' — using the same Ka-class descriptive strategy
options: Modern objects are named by what they do — using the same descriptive strategy
Modern objects use borrowed English names
Technology names do not follow any pattern
Only traditional objects can be named this way
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Ka-class naming strategy extends to any object: name it by what it does. Clock, car, book — and phone, computer, internet. The pattern is the same. The language grows without losing itself.

exercises.prompt: Tsi waterennótha — radio station. The name describes what it does: where voices/words go out. What does this naming strategy reveal about how Kanien'kéha speakers understood radio?
exercises.correct: They understood it as a technology that sends voices/words out — the name captures the function from the perspective of the speaker
options: The name captures the function from the speaker's perspective
The name is borrowed from English
Radio was named before it was understood
The name is unrelated to the function
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Tsi waterennótha — where the words/voices go out. The radio station is described from the inside: it is a place where voices are sent. This perspective — naming from function, not from form — is consistent throughout Kanien'kéha naming.

exercises.prompt: Match the modern institution to its Kanien'kéha name

pairs.english: Radio station (where the voices go out)

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Radio station, band office, school, clock — each named by what it does or what happens there. Modern and traditional institutions alike follow the same naming principle.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehón:we — Indigenous person / original person. Why does Indigenous identity vocabulary matter in a unit about technology?
exercises.correct: Indigenous communities are active users and producers of technology — Kanien'kehá:ka people make apps, podcasts, social media content in their language
options: Indigenous communities are active technology users and producers in their language
Technology is irrelevant to Indigenous identity
Indigenous people do not use modern technology
The two topics are unrelated
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanien'kehá:ka communities are using technology for language revitalization: apps, YouTube channels, podcasts, social media, radio stations. The language that names the radio station also names the podcaster. Technology is a tool for language survival, not a threat to it.

exercises.prompt: Technology and language
exercises.storyPrompt: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha. You are learning this language — on a device, through an app, in the twenty-first century. This is technology in service of a language that is thousands of years old. The same radio stations that missionaries once used to broadcast English and French into Kanien'kehá:ka homes are now being used to broadcast Kanien'kéha back. The same internet that spreads dominant languages is also hosting Kanien'kéha YouTube channels, podcasts, and community learning platforms. Technology does not determine language fate. People do. Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha.

lessons.title: Language Revitalization and Media
lessons.sub: Radio, video, apps — technology in the service of language
intro: Kanien'kehá:ka communities have been using media for language revitalization for decades. The Kahnawà:ke radio station has broadcast in Kanien'kéha since the 1970s. Immersion schools use digital tools. Language apps like this one are part of a long tradition of using available technology to keep the language alive.
This lesson introduces the vocabulary of media and communication — radio, television, recording, broadcasting — and connects it to the language revitalization context.
The language adapts to survive. Technology is one of the tools it uses.
lessons.ped: Media and revitalization: McCarty & Lee (2014): language revitalization requires using contemporary media. This lesson situates the app itself within the broader revitalization context.

exercises.prompt: Tsi waterennótha — radio station. Kanien'kehá:ka radio broadcasting began in Kahnawà:ke in the 1970s. What made radio particularly valuable for language revitalization?
exercises.correct: Radio reaches people in their homes without requiring them to attend a class — it puts the language in daily domestic space
options: Radio reaches people in their homes — it puts the language in daily life
Radio only reaches young people
Radio is more formal than speaking
Radio replaced the written language
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Radio brings the language into kitchens, cars, and living rooms. You do not need to seek the language out — the language comes to you. This is why radio was one of the earliest and most effective revitalization tools.

exercises.prompt: Ionteriwahrónnion — school / place of learning. Kanien'kéha immersion schools teach all subjects in the language. Why is immersion the most effective language learning environment?
exercises.correct: When every part of the day happens in the language, learners cannot avoid using it — immersion creates the conditions of a first language
options: Immersion makes the language unavoidable — it recreates first language conditions
Immersion is only for young children
Immersion requires no previous knowledge
Immersion focuses on grammar only
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Immersion works because the language becomes the medium of life, not just a subject of study. When you learn math, art, gym, and lunch conversation in Kanien'kéha, the language is everywhere. This app is one piece of a much larger revitalization ecosystem.

exercises.prompt: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am learning Kanien'kéha. This verb is in the habitual aspect. What does this tell you about language learning?
exercises.correct: It is ongoing — not a one-time event but a continuous, regular practice that is always happening
options: It is ongoing — a continuous practice always in progress
It happened once and is done
It will happen in the future
It is a stative condition
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The habitual. I am learning — continuously, regularly, right now. Language learning is not something you finish. It is something you keep doing. The habitual aspect is exactly right.

exercises.prompt: The language on the airwaves
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen on the radio. The words that come before all else, broadcast across the St. Lawrence Valley every morning. The Thanksgiving Address has been spoken at the start of every gathering in Haudenosaunee communities for centuries. Now it is also spoken at the start of radio broadcasts, at the beginning of YouTube videos, in podcasts and apps and classroom recordings. The words travel new pathways. The words themselves have not changed. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Technology & the Modern World
lessons.sub: Naming the contemporary world in Kanien'kéha
intro: This review consolidates the technology and modern world vocabulary from this unit.
Kanien'kéha is a living language. It names the contemporary world using its own productive strategies. It has always done this.
Use this lesson to consolidate.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001): review through new combinations.

exercises.prompt: Match the contemporary vocabulary to its name
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Contemporary institutions and tools named in Kanien'kéha — all using the same descriptive naming strategies as traditional vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha. You are learning this language through technology. What does this say about the relationship between technology and Indigenous language survival?
exercises.correct: Technology is a tool — it can serve language survival or undermine it, depending on how communities choose to use it
options: Technology is a tool — communities choose how it serves the language
Technology automatically undermines Indigenous languages
Technology has no effect on language
Only traditional methods work for language learning
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Technology is not inherently for or against any language. Kanien'kehá:ka communities have consistently chosen to use available technology in service of their language. Radio, video, apps. The choice to use technology for the language rather than against it is a community decision. You are part of that choice right now.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehón:we in the digital age
exercises.storyPrompt: Onkwehón:we — original person. In the digital age, original people are everywhere: making apps, running podcasts, creating social media content, building language learning platforms, broadcasting on community radio. The stereotypes that imagine Indigenous peoples as belonging to the past are contradicted every day by Onkwehón:we people living fully in the present — and using the present's tools to carry their languages forward. Onkwehón:we. Original. Here. Now. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Language on the Internet
lessons.sub: Digital spaces and the language
intro: Kanien'kéha is present online: YouTube channels, podcasts, social media accounts, language learning apps. This lesson explores the vocabulary of digital communication and online language use.
The language has been adapting to new media for decades — from radio to video to digital. The naming strategies are the same; the medium changes.
lessons.ped: Digital language use: Contemporary language revitalization uses digital tools. This lesson connects the naming-by-function strategy to digital vocabulary.

exercises.prompt: Kahiatónhsera — paper / book. The same word is used for a webpage or document on a screen. What does this tell you about how Kanien'kéha handles digital documents?
exercises.correct: The function is the same — it carries written information — so the same word applies regardless of medium
options: The function is the same so the same word applies
A new word is invented for digital documents
The word is borrowed from English
Digital documents are not named in Kanien'kéha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kahiatónhsera — paper, book, document, webpage. The function (carrying written information) is what the word names. The medium (paper vs screen) does not change the word.

exercises.prompt: Tsi waterennótha — radio station. A podcast is a similar technology: recorded voices sent out. How would the naming strategy apply to a podcast?
exercises.correct: The podcast would be named by what it does — recorded voices that go out — using the same descriptive function-based strategy
options: Named by what it does — recorded voices that go out
The word would be borrowed from English
A completely new naming system would be needed
Podcasts cannot be named in Kanien'kéha
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The naming strategy — describe what the thing does — works for any technology. A podcast is a place where recorded voices go out. The language can name it without borrowing.

exercises.prompt: Complete: I am learning Kanien'kéha — Kateweyénhstha ___
exercises.correct: Kanyen'kéha
exercises.hint: K
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — on a device, through an app, right now. The language meets you where you are.

exercises.prompt: Original people, online
exercises.storyPrompt: Onkwehón:we on the internet. Original people, everywhere, making content in their languages. TikTok videos in Kanien'kéha. YouTube immersion lessons. Instagram captions. Twitter/X posts. The language is where the people are. It was always where the people are. The technology changes. The people — and their language — continue. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Technology & the Modern World
lessons.sub: Naming the contemporary world
intro: This review consolidates the technology vocabulary and the naming strategy that underlies it.
Use it to consolidate before continuing.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001).

exercises.prompt: Match the contemporary institution to its name

pairs.english: Radio station — where voices go out
Band office — where business is conducted

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Each named by what it does or what happens there.

exercises.prompt: Karihtón:ni — clock. Kahiatónhsera — book/webpage. Tsi waterennótha — radio station. What do all three names have in common?
exercises.correct: All describe the object or place by its function — what it does or what happens there
options: All describe by function — what it does or what happens there
All are borrowed from English
All are Ka-class nouns
All were invented recently
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Function-based naming is the consistent strategy. It works for clocks, books, radio stations, and every technology yet to be invented.

exercises.prompt: Technology in service of language
exercises.storyPrompt: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — on this device, in this app, right now. You are using twenty-first century technology to learn a language that is thousands of years old. The technology serves the language. The language survives into the technology. Both are tools. The people who use them — and why they use them — are what matters. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 26 — Health & the Body in Depth
desc: The language of health, illness, healing, and the medicine traditions of Kanien'kehá:ka culture.

lessons.title: Feeling Well and Feeling Ill
lessons.sub: The vocabulary of health and sickness
intro: You know some health vocabulary already: saiosé:ren (you are sick), sohkwá:ren (you are strong), the emotion states from Unit 23. This unit deepens health vocabulary — illness, healing, medicine, the body in sickness and recovery.
Health in Kanien'kehá:ka understanding is not just physical. It is mental, emotional, and spiritual — the four domains of LaFever's Medicine Wheel that have structured this entire curriculum.
The medicine vocabulary connects to the sacred medicines (Unit 15), the medicine woman (Iakoiánehr tsi oiontátkon), and the Bear Clan's medicine keeping responsibility.
lessons.ped: Health as holistic: LaFever (2016): health encompasses all four domains. This unit centres that holistic understanding. McCarthy & Lee (2014): health vocabulary must be culturally grounded.

exercises.prompt: Match the health state to its Kanien'kéha expression
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Strong, sick, cold, afraid — physical and emotional health states. Both are part of health in Kanien'kehá:ka understanding.

exercises.prompt: Iakoiánehr tsi oiontátkon — she makes medicine / medicine woman. The medicine woman's role connects to which clan?
exercises.correct: The Bear Clan — Rotihskeré:wake — which carries the medicine keeping responsibility
options: Bear Clan — Rotihskeré:wake — the medicine keepers
Wolf Clan
Turtle Clan
All clans equally
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Bear Clan — Rotihskeré:wake — carries the medicine knowledge. The bear knows where the medicine plants grow. The medicine woman carries that knowledge into practice.

exercises.prompt: Onkwatà:wen — native tobacco — is used in healing protocols. How does tobacco function in a healing context?
exercises.correct: It opens communication with the spirit world, carries the patient's need, and begins the healing ceremony
options: It opens communication with the spirit world and begins the healing
It replaces other medicines
It is only used for prevention
Tobacco is not used in healing
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! In Kanien'kehá:ka healing, native tobacco opens the protocol. Before medicines are prepared, before the ceremony begins, tobacco is put down to open the channel. The healing is not purely physical — it is spiritual communication too.

exercises.prompt: Wake'nikonhrén:ton — I am heavy-hearted. Is this a physical or emotional health symptom?
exercises.correct: Both — heavy-heartedness affects both emotional wellbeing and can manifest physically. The four domains of health are not separate.
options: Both — the four domains of health are not separate
Emotional only
Physical only
It is not a health symptom at all
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Medicine Wheel teaches that physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual health are interconnected. Heavy-heartedness (Wake'nikonhrén:ton) is the emotional state the condolence ceremony addresses — and it has physical manifestations too. The four domains are always in relationship.

exercises.prompt: The medicine woman
exercises.storyPrompt: Iakoiánehr tsi oiontátkon — she makes medicine. She knows the plants: which ones heal fever, which ones soothe pain, which ones must be prepared in specific ways at specific times of year. She knows the protocols: the tobacco, the words, the ceremony that accompanies the physical medicine. Her knowledge cannot be learned from a book — it is learned from practice, from elders, from the land itself. Residential schools targeted this knowledge directly, removing children from the grandmothers who would have taught them. The medicine woman's knowledge survived anyway, carried by those who resisted. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Healing Practices and Sacred Medicines
lessons.sub: The medicine traditions of Kanien'kehá:ka culture
intro: You learned the four sacred medicines in Unit 15: tobacco, cedar, sweet grass, sage. This lesson extends into the healing practices that use them — the False Face Society, the Longhouse medicine ceremonies, plant medicine knowledge.
Healing in Kanien'kehá:ka tradition is ceremony as much as it is medicine. The False Face masks, the rattles, the songs — all are part of the healing practice.
This lesson introduces the vocabulary of healing without reproducing restricted ceremonial content.
lessons.ped: Healing practices with appropriate boundaries: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): language pedagogy must honour community-defined boundaries around restricted knowledge. This lesson introduces healing vocabulary without reproducing ceremonial content that belongs to specific knowledge keepers.

exercises.prompt: Iotsiskwatáties — false face mask. The False Face Society uses these masks in healing ceremonies. What is appropriate for a language learner to know about the False Face Society?
exercises.correct: That it exists, that it performs healing ceremonies, that its masks and songs are restricted knowledge — and that this boundary deserves respect
options: That it exists, performs healing, and that its content is restricted — respect that boundary
All ceremonial details should be shared
The Society's masks and songs are public
There are no restrictions on this knowledge
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The False Face Society performs healing ceremonies with specific masks, rattles, and songs. This knowledge is restricted — it belongs to members of the Society. A language learner needs to know the word, what it names, and where the boundary is. That boundary is not a barrier; it is a form of care.

exercises.prompt: Match the healing item to its role

pairs.english: Drives the rhythm of healing ceremony

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Tobacco, cedar, rattle, medicine woman — four elements of a healing practice. Each plays a specific role.

exercises.prompt: Okwéskons — rattle. The rattle is used in healing ceremonies. Why does sound play such an important role in healing?
exercises.correct: Sound in Kanien'kehá:ka healing connects the physical ceremony to the spiritual world — it calls on helpers and marks the ceremonial space
options: Sound connects the physical ceremony to the spiritual world
Sound is decorative only
The rattle is just a musical instrument
Only the medicine matters, not the ceremony
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The rattle's sound is not incidental — it is part of the healing itself. It marks the ceremonial space, calls on spiritual helpers, and drives the ceremony forward. The physical and spiritual dimensions of healing work together in Kanien'kehá:ka practice.

exercises.prompt: Cedar
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen'takwenhténtsha — cedar. The purification plant. Before a healing ceremony begins, cedar clears the space and the participants. Its smoke removes what does not belong — illness, negative energy, spiritual interference. The medicine woman prepares the cedar. She knows how much to use, how to burn it, what words to say. The cedar does not work alone — it works within a protocol that the medicine woman holds. The tree, the knowledge, the ceremony. Three things that must be held together for the healing to work.

lessons.title: Review — Health & the Body in Depth
lessons.sub: Health, healing, and the medicine traditions
intro: This review consolidates health vocabulary: physical states, emotional states, medicine women, sacred medicines, healing practices.
Health in Kanien'kehá:ka understanding is holistic — physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual.
Use this lesson to consolidate.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001): review through new combinations.

exercises.prompt: Match the health concept to its expression
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Physical strength, emotional heaviness, the healer, the opening medicine. Health in four dimensions.

exercises.prompt: The medicine woman holds restricted knowledge. This lesson has introduced her title and role. What has it deliberately not done?
exercises.correct: It has not reproduced restricted ceremonial content — the boundary around specific healing knowledge is respected
options: It has not reproduced restricted ceremonial content
It has not named the medicines
It has not connected healing to the community
It has not mentioned the Bear Clan
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Language pedagogy must respect community-defined boundaries. The medicine woman's title is shareable. The specific songs, protocols, and restricted knowledge of healing ceremonies are not. Knowing where the boundary is — and respecting it — is part of learning the language responsibly.

exercises.prompt: Health
exercises.storyPrompt: Sohkwá:ren — you are strong. Health in Kanien'kehá:ka life is this: strong in body, clear in mind, whole in heart, grounded in spirit. The Medicine Wheel that has structured this course — physical, mental, emotional, spiritual — is a framework for health as much as it is a framework for learning. When the Thanksgiving Address thanks the earth for sustaining us, it is thanking the source of physical health. When the condolence ceremony restores the grieving nation's clarity, it is healing the mental and emotional domains. Health is not separate from culture. In Kanien'kéha, it never was. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Mental and Emotional Health
lessons.sub: The inner life — emotions as health
intro: The Medicine Wheel teaches that health is physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual. This lesson focuses on the mental and emotional domains — the states of the inner life that Kanien'kéha names with such precision.
You learned these states in Unit 23 (describing things). This lesson reframes them as health vocabulary — recognising that emotional states are health states in Kanien'kehá:ka understanding.
lessons.ped: Mental/emotional health as vocabulary: LaFever (2016): the Medicine Wheel explicitly includes mental and emotional domains as health domains. This lesson names that explicitly.

exercises.prompt: Match the emotional state to its expression

pairs.english: I feel good inside / I am content

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Content, heavy-hearted, happy, sad — four states of the inner life in Kanien'kéha.

exercises.prompt: Wake'nikonhrén:ton — I am heavy-hearted. The condolence ceremony addresses this state directly. What does this tell you about how Haudenosaunee governance treats emotional health?
exercises.correct: Emotional health is a prerequisite for governance — you cannot lead or decide clearly when heavy-hearted, and the ceremony addresses that before governance resumes
options: Emotional health is a prerequisite for governance — the ceremony restores it before decisions are made
Emotion is separate from governance
The ceremony is only about grief
Only physical health matters for governance
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Great Law specifies that governance cannot proceed when grief has not been addressed. Emotional health is a governance requirement, built into the law.

exercises.prompt: Skennón:'a — peace. In a health context, how is peace a health state?
exercises.correct: Peace is the condition of a clear mind and whole spirit — the state the condolence ceremony restores and the Thanksgiving Address maintains
options: Peace is the condition of a clear mind and whole spirit
Peace is only political
Peace only refers to the absence of war
Peace is unrelated to health
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Skennón:'a as health: when the mind is clear, when the spirit is whole, when the emotions are restored — that is peace. The condolence ceremony restores it. The Thanksgiving Address maintains it.

exercises.prompt: Feeling good inside
exercises.meaning: I feel good inside / I am content
exercises.storyPrompt: Wake'nikonhrí:io — I feel good inside. This is the emotional health goal of the condolence ceremony. Not happiness in the performative sense — but genuine inner wellness. The heaviness lifted. The clarity returned. The spirit grounded. When someone who has been heavy-hearted reaches Wake'nikonhrí:io, the ceremony has done its work. Health is not just the absence of sickness. In Kanien'kehá:ka understanding, it is the presence of this: Wake'nikonhrí:io.

lessons.title: Review — Health & the Body in Depth
lessons.sub: Holistic health in Kanien'kéha
intro: This review consolidates health vocabulary: physical states, emotional states, the medicine woman, sacred medicines, healing practices, and the four-domain understanding of health.
Use it to consolidate.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001).

exercises.prompt: Match the health concept to its expression

pairs.english: Peace — the condition of restored health

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Physical, emotional, healer, restored peace — health in four dimensions.

exercises.prompt: Onkwatà:wen — native tobacco. It opens healing protocols. What does this tell you about the relationship between ceremony and medicine in Kanien'kehá:ka health practice?
exercises.correct: Ceremony and medicine are not separate — healing begins with ceremony and proceeds through it
options: Ceremony and medicine are not separate — healing is ceremonial
Medicine is purely physical
Tobacco replaces other medicines
The ceremony is only symbolic
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The tobacco opens the channel. The cedar purifies. The rattle drives. The medicine woman holds the knowledge. Healing in Kanien'kehá:ka tradition is a complete ceremonial and physical practice — the two are inseparable.

exercises.prompt: Health
exercises.storyPrompt: Sohkwá:ren — you are strong. Health in Kanien'kehá:ka life is this: strong in body, clear in mind, whole in heart, grounded in spirit. The Medicine Wheel that has structured this course is a framework for health as much as a framework for learning. When the Thanksgiving Address thanks the earth for sustaining us, it is thanking the source of physical health. When the condolence restores the grieving nation's clarity, it is healing the mental and emotional domains. Health is not separate from culture. In Kanien'kéha, it never was. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 27 — Agriculture & the Land: The Three Sisters in Depth
desc: Corn, beans, and squash — the agriculture, ceremony, and ecology of the Three Sisters in Kanien'kehá:ka life.

lessons.title: The Three Sisters: Companions in the Garden
lessons.sub: Corn, beans, and squash — how they grow together
intro: The Three Sisters — Onón:ta (corn), Ohsahéta' (beans), Otá:ra (squash) — are not just crops. They are companions. They grow together because each one helps the others: corn provides a stalk for beans to climb, beans fix nitrogen in the soil, squash leaves shade the ground and keep moisture in.

This companion planting system is one of the most sophisticated agricultural technologies developed in the eastern woodlands. It is also the foundation of the ceremonial calendar: the planting, the Green Corn, the harvest.
This lesson introduces the Three Sisters in their agricultural, ecological, and ceremonial context.
lessons.ped: Three Sisters as ecological and ceremonial knowledge: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): land-based knowledge should be central. The Three Sisters are a complete agricultural and ceremonial system. LaFever Spiritual domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Match the Sister to her role in the companion planting system

pairs.english: Provides the stalk for beans to climb
Fix nitrogen in the soil, feeding corn and squash
Leaves shade the ground, keeping moisture and suppressing weeds

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Three Sisters work as a system. None of them is as productive alone as they are together. This is not a metaphor — it is ecology.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — it makes our minds happy / we give thanks. The Thanksgiving Address gives thanks for the Three Sisters. Why are they thanked as a group rather than individually?
exercises.correct: They work as a unit — thanking them as a group reflects how they actually exist, in relationship with each other
options: They work as a unit — the group reflects their actual ecological relationship
It is more efficient
Individual thanksgiving is not possible
Only corn is thanked in the Address
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Three Sisters are thanked as Three Sisters because that is how they exist. The Address is precise: it names beings by what they actually are and do. The Sisters, together, sustain the people. They are thanked together.

exercises.prompt: Seskéha — August, the green corn moon. The Green Corn Ceremony happens in Seskéha. What does naming August after the green corn tell you about the relationship between the calendar and the crops?
exercises.correct: The calendar is built around the crops — the month is named for what the land is doing, not for an abstract number
options: The calendar is built around the crops — months are named for what the land does
The name is coincidental
Only August is named after a crop
The Green Corn Ceremony is not connected to the month name
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Seskéha — the green corn moon. The calendar and the crops are the same system. When you know the month name, you know what is happening in the garden.

exercises.prompt: The Three Sisters
exercises.storyPrompt: Onón:ta. Ohsahéta'. Otá:ra. Corn, beans, squash. The Three Sisters have been grown together in Kanien'kehá:ka gardens for thousands of years. Together they provide complete nutrition: the corn provides carbohydrates, the beans provide protein and amino acids the corn lacks, the squash provides vitamins. The plants sustain the people the same way the people tend the plants. This is not coincidence — it is thousands of years of careful observation, selection, and cultivation. The Three Sisters are a technology. They are also a philosophy: things work better in relationship than alone.

lessons.title: Planting, Growing, Harvesting
lessons.sub: The vocabulary of the agricultural cycle
intro: The agricultural cycle has its own vocabulary: preparing the ground, planting the seeds, tending the growing plants, harvesting the mature crops, storing and processing the harvest.

Each stage has Kanien'kéha words and associated ceremonial practices. Planting happens after the Strawberry Ceremony signals the growing season. Harvest happens before the Harvest Ceremony gives thanks.
This lesson introduces the key agricultural vocabulary across the full cycle.
lessons.ped: Agricultural cycle vocabulary: Nation (2001): agricultural vocabulary is among the most culturally specific a language carries. Physical domain: production of agricultural sentences.

exercises.prompt: Sanerénhton — you mow the lawn (command). This is a True E-stem verb. What agricultural action might a similar True E-stem verb describe for the corn garden?
exercises.correct: Clearing the ground before planting — using a similar physical motion to mowing
options: Clearing the ground before planting — a similar motion
Harvesting the corn — a different motion
Planting the seeds
Watering the plants
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! True E-stem verbs often describe actions with tools or with specific physical motion. The mowing verb points toward the class of agricultural clearing and preparation verbs.

exercises.prompt: Senonnhéron — you shuck corn (command). What stage of the agricultural cycle does shucking belong to?
exercises.correct: Harvest processing — after the corn is picked, it is shucked (husks removed) before drying and storage
options: Harvest processing — after picking, before drying
Planting
Growing season tending
Seed selection
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Senonnhéron — you shuck corn. This is the processing stage, when the harvested corn has its outer husks removed. The corn then dries before being ground into flour or stored whole. Each stage of the agricultural process has its verb.

exercises.prompt: Sé:ske — spring. The Strawberry Ceremony signals the start of the growing season. Why does the agricultural year begin with strawberries, not with planting corn?
exercises.correct: The first strawberries signal that the growing season has arrived — they are the first fruits and their appearance means the soil is warm enough to plant
options: Strawberries signal the growing season is ready — they appear when soil is warm enough to plant
Strawberries are planted before corn
The Strawberry Ceremony has no agricultural function
Spring begins when corn is planted
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ken' niiohontésha' — strawberries — appear in Sé:ske as a natural signal. Their arrival means the frost risk is passing. The Strawberry Ceremony honours the first fruit and announces: the growing season has begun.

exercises.prompt: Match the agricultural stage to its ceremonial marker

pairs.english: Green Corn Ceremony — thanks for the first corn
Harvest Ceremony — thanks for the full harvest

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Planting season, green corn, harvest, winter rest — four agricultural stages, four ceremonies. The ceremonial calendar IS the agricultural calendar.

exercises.prompt: Shucking corn
exercises.storyPrompt: Senonnhéron — you shuck corn. A harvest processing verb. After the ears are pulled from the stalks, the outer husks are peeled back — sometimes saved for corn husk dolls (Ionkwaiénhton), sometimes for basket weaving, sometimes left to dry with the corn inside. Nothing from the harvest is wasted. The corn husk doll's material comes from the processing of the corn. The basket's material may too. The three sisters, the husk, the doll, the basket — a circle of use in which everything has a purpose and a word.

lessons.title: Review — Agriculture & the Three Sisters
lessons.sub: Corn, beans, squash — the full agricultural and ceremonial picture
intro: This review consolidates the Three Sisters vocabulary, the agricultural cycle, and the ceremonial calendar that accompanies it.
Agriculture and ceremony are one system in Kanien'kehá:ka life. The language holds both.
Use this lesson to consolidate.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001): review through new combinations.

exercises.prompt: Match the Sister to her name

pairs.english: Strawberries — signal of the growing season

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Corn, beans, squash, strawberries. Three Sisters and the herald of their season.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — it makes our minds happy. The Three Sisters are thanked in the Thanksgiving Address with this phrase. What makes the Three Sisters different from other agricultural products?
exercises.correct: They are acknowledged as beings that sustain life — not as commodities but as relatives whose gifts are given and received
options: They are acknowledged as relatives whose gifts sustain life — not as commodities
They are acknowledged because they taste good
They are acknowledged because they are the most profitable crops
Only corn is acknowledged in the Address
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Three Sisters are not products in Kanien'kehá:ka understanding — they are beings who give. The Thanksgiving Address thanks them as such: their gifts sustain us. The relationship is reciprocal: we tend them, they feed us. We acknowledge them, they continue to give.

exercises.prompt: Onón:ta
exercises.storyPrompt: Onón:ta — corn. The most central food of Kanien'kehá:ka life. Corn soup (Onón:ta o'wá:ron — corn with meat) has been made in these communities for longer than any written record. The Green Corn Ceremony thanks it in August. The Midwinter Ceremony uses it in the feasts of renewal. The mortar (Ohwèn:ton) pounds it into flour. The corn husk doll (Ionkwaiénhton) is made from its discarded husks. Corn is everywhere in the vocabulary because corn was everywhere in the life. It still is. Onón:ta. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Corn Soup and Daily Food
lessons.sub: How the Three Sisters become food
intro: The Three Sisters are not just crops — they become food. Corn soup (Onón:ta o'wá:ron) is one of the most important dishes in Kanien'kehá:ka life. Beans are stored and cooked. Squash is roasted.
This lesson connects the agricultural vocabulary to the food vocabulary from Unit 7 — closing the loop from planting to table.
lessons.ped: Agricultural to food vocabulary: Nation (2001): known vocabulary (Three Sisters, food) in new connected context (from garden to table).

exercises.prompt: Onón:ta o'wá:ron — corn with meat. This is the name of corn soup. What does o'wá:ron add?
exercises.correct: O'wá:ron means meat — corn soup is corn combined with meat (bear, deer, or other)
options: O'wá:ron means meat — corn soup includes meat
O'wá:ron means water
It adds no meaning
O'wá:ron means beans
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta o'wá:ron — corn + meat. The name is a description of the dish. The naming-by-description strategy works for food just as it works for tools and technology.

exercises.prompt: Ohwèn:ton — mortar. The mortar pounds dried corn into flour for bread. You know Kanà:taros — bread. What process connects the mortar to the bread?
exercises.correct: Dried corn → mortar pounds it to flour → flour is mixed and baked/cooked → bread (Kanà:taros)
options: Dried corn pounded to flour → flour cooked → bread
The mortar makes the bread directly
The bread requires no processing
The mortar is not used in bread making
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The mortar is the processing step between dried corn and bread. Ohwèn:ton pounds the corn. The flour becomes Kanà:taros. The agricultural vocabulary and the food vocabulary are one continuous chain.

exercises.prompt: Match the food/tool to its role in the meal
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Corn, beans, squash, mortar — the Three Sisters and the tool that processes them.

exercises.prompt: Corn soup
exercises.storyPrompt: Onón:ta o'wá:ron — corn soup. It has been made in Kanien'kehá:ka kitchens for a very long time. The corn is dried and stored through the winter. In spring it is taken out, soaked, and pounded in the mortar. Mixed with meat — bear, deer, whatever was hunted — and simmered. The smell of corn soup is the smell of home for many Kanien'kehá:ka people. It is served at feasts, at funerals, at ceremonies, at community gatherings. The food that comes from the garden connects back to the garden, to the planting, to the Green Corn Ceremony, to the Three Sisters themselves. Onón:ta o'wá:ron. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Agriculture & the Three Sisters
lessons.sub: The full agricultural and ceremonial picture
intro: This review consolidates the Three Sisters vocabulary, the agricultural cycle, and the ceremonial calendar.
Agriculture and ceremony are one system. The food that comes from the garden is the same food thanked in the Address and served at the feast.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001).

exercises.prompt: Match the Sister (and herald) to her name

pairs.english: Strawberries — herald of the growing season

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Three Sisters and the first fruit that signals their season.

exercises.prompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase' — the Three Sisters are thanked in the Thanksgiving Address. What makes the Three Sisters different from other crops?
exercises.correct: They are acknowledged as beings who give — relatives whose gifts are received with gratitude, not commodities
options: They are acknowledged as relatives whose gifts sustain life
They are thanked because they taste good
They are the most profitable crops
Only corn is acknowledged
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Three Sisters are not products — they are beings who give. The Address thanks them as such. The relationship is reciprocal: we tend them, they feed us.

exercises.prompt: Corn
exercises.storyPrompt: Onón:ta — corn. The most central food of Kanien'kehá:ka life. Corn soup in the pot. Green corn on the stalk in August. Dried corn in the storage. Corn flour in the bread. The Green Corn Ceremony in Seskéha. The mortar pounding all winter. The Three Sisters in the garden all summer. Onón:ta is everywhere in the vocabulary because it was everywhere in the life. It still is. Onón:ta. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 28 — Oral Tradition: Stories, Legends & the Spoken Word
desc: The oral tradition of the Kanien'kehá:ka — creation stories, legendary beings, storytelling protocols, and the language that carries them.

lessons.title: The Storytelling Season
lessons.sub: When stories can be told — and why
intro: In Kanien'kehá:ka tradition, certain stories can only be told in winter — when the ground is frozen, when the snakes and insects sleep, when the spiritual world has settled into the cold season.

This is not arbitrary. The stories of the creation, of the legendary beings, of the Sky World — these carry power. Telling them in summer, when those beings are awake and active, could attract unwanted attention or misalign the forces the stories invoke.
This lesson introduces the storytelling tradition and its protocols before entering the vocabulary of the stories themselves.
lessons.ped: Storytelling protocols as cultural knowledge: The when and how of storytelling is as important as the stories themselves. This lesson honours that by introducing protocols before content. Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): restricted knowledge boundaries.

exercises.prompt: Kenién:ke — winter. Stories of the creation and legendary beings are told in winter. What does the frozen ground signal that makes winter the storytelling season?
exercises.correct: The snakes and insects that might overhear the powerful stories are sleeping — the spiritual world is quieter and safer for telling certain stories
options: Snakes and insects sleep — the spiritual world is quieter and safer
Stories are easier to remember in winter
Winter has longer nights
The stories are about cold weather
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The storytelling protocol is grounded in a specific understanding of the spiritual world. Certain beings are active in summer and must not overhear stories that concern them. In winter, those beings sleep. The protocol is not superstition — it is a specific cosmological practice.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehshón:'a tsi onnhéhton — the little people. These are beings who live in the rocks and forests and can be helpful or mischievous. Why might their existence be acknowledged in a storytelling lesson?
exercises.correct: They are part of the cosmological world the stories describe — knowing they exist is knowing the audience for whom the protocol matters
options: They are part of the cosmological world the stories describe
They are only mentioned in children's stories
The little people are purely fictional characters
They have no role in the storytelling protocol
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The little people, the thunderers, the stone giants — these are not fictional characters in Kanien'kehá:ka understanding. They are beings who exist. The storytelling protocol exists partly because of this: powerful stories about real beings require care.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address is said before storytelling gatherings. Why does the Address matter in this context?
exercises.correct: The Address acknowledges all the beings who might be present or affected — it sets a respectful frame before stories that involve those beings are told
options: The Address acknowledges all beings present — it frames the stories respectfully
The Address is said before all gatherings regardless
The Address replaces the stories
Storytelling has no connection to the Address
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Before powerful stories are told, the Address acknowledges the beings those stories are about. The earth, the sky, the Creator — all are named and thanked. The stories can then be told within that respectful frame.

exercises.prompt: The storytelling season
exercises.storyPrompt: Kenién:ke — winter. The storytelling season. The fire is lit. The people gather. The elder begins. In Kanien'kehá:ka tradition, certain stories belong to this moment — the long winter night, the gathered community, the fire at the centre. The protocols are clear: only in winter, never lightly, always with respect for the beings whose stories are being told. When the elder says Onen' ki' wáhi at the end, the story is finished. The firelight holds what was spoken. The listeners carry it forward. That is how oral tradition works.

lessons.title: The Creation Story: Key Vocabulary
lessons.sub: Words from the beginning — Sky World, Sky Woman, the turtle
intro: The Haudenosaunee creation story begins in the Sky World — Otsikonhríhson — where people lived before the earth existed. Sky Woman falls, geese carry her, the turtle provides a surface, and the animals build the world from mud.

You have encountered parts of this story throughout the course: the sky world (Unit 14), the turtle (Unit 6), the muskrat who dove for mud, the earth that was built. This lesson assembles the creation story vocabulary.
This is not a retelling of the story — that belongs to knowledge keepers. This is an introduction to the key vocabulary.
lessons.ped: Creation story vocabulary with appropriate boundaries: The full creation story belongs to knowledge keepers. This lesson introduces key vocabulary without attempting to retell the story in full. Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): community boundaries respected.

exercises.prompt: Match the creation story element to its Kanien'kéha name

pairs.english: The sky / where the Sky World is
Turtle — whose back became the foundation of the earth
Muskrat — who dove and brought up the earth
Earth — built on the turtle's back

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Sky, turtle, muskrat, earth — four elements from the beginning of the world. Four words from this course that you can now place in the story they belong to.

exercises.prompt: A'nó:wara — turtle. The Turtle Clan (Rotiniáhton) takes its name from the turtle. Now that you know the creation story connection, what does the Turtle Clan name carry?
exercises.correct: The memory of the turtle whose back became the foundation of the world — the clan name holds the creation story
options: The memory of the turtle whose back became the foundation — the name holds the story
The turtle is only an animal symbol
The clan was named after a specific historical turtle
The creation story has no connection to the clan names
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Rotiniáhton — Turtle Clan. The turtle held the weight of the world. The clan carries that. The clan name is the creation story, compressed into a social identity. Every time someone says Rotiniáhton, they are speaking a piece of the creation.

exercises.prompt: Shonkwaiá:tison — the Creator. The Creator is acknowledged at the end of the Thanksgiving Address, after all other beings. In the creation story, the Creator is present at the beginning. What does this tell you?
exercises.correct: The Creator brackets the whole of existence — present at the beginning in the story, acknowledged at the end of the Address that thanks the created world
options: The Creator brackets existence — beginning of the story, end of the Address
The Creator is only in the Address
The Creator only appears once in Kanien'kehá:ka tradition
The story and the Address are unconnected
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shonkwaiá:tison — at the beginning in the story, at the end of the Address. The Creator is the frame of the whole world. The Address gives thanks for everything between those two poles: the created world, from earth to stars.

exercises.prompt: The muskrat
exercises.storyPrompt: Tanón:kereh — muskrat. In the creation story, every animal dove to the bottom of the primordial water to bring up earth for Sky Woman. Each one failed — the loon, the beaver, the otter — until the smallest dove last and came back floating on the surface, dead, a tiny bit of mud in its paw. From that mud the world was built. The smallest creature succeeded where the largest failed. This is not just a creation story — it is a teaching about humility and persistence, about how the one who is overlooked may be the one who makes the world possible. Tanón:kereh.

lessons.title: Legendary Beings
lessons.sub: The beings of the oral tradition — what they are and what they mean
intro: Kanien'kehá:ka oral tradition is populated with beings beyond humans and animals: the stone giants, the flying head, the little people, the water serpent, the skeleton, the fireball.
These beings are not 'monsters' in the horror sense — they are part of a rich cosmological world with specific roles, relationships, and teachings. Some are threats. Some are helpers. Some are both.
This lesson introduces the key legendary beings by name, without attempting to retell their stories (which belong to knowledge keepers and the winter season).
lessons.ped: Legendary beings as cosmological vocabulary: The beings of oral tradition are part of the worldview the language carries. Naming them is appropriate; retelling their full stories belongs to knowledge keepers. Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023).

exercises.prompt: Match the legendary being to its name
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Water serpent, Stone Giant, little people, ghost — four beings of the Kanien'kehá:ka oral tradition, each named.

exercises.prompt: Tsi nihononterá:kehre' — the Stone Giant. Stone Giants are beings in the oral tradition who threatened communities. In the stories, they are defeated through specific means. What is the appropriate way to learn about those means?
exercises.correct: From knowledge keepers, in the storytelling season — the full stories belong to those contexts, not to a language app
options: From knowledge keepers in the storytelling season
From this lesson
From any available source
The stories are freely shareable
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The stories of the Stone Giants and how they are defeated belong to knowledge keepers and the winter storytelling tradition. This lesson introduces the name. The story belongs elsewhere. Knowing the boundary is part of learning the language responsibly.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehshón:'a tsi onnhéhton — the little people. They are said to live in rocks and forests and can be helpful or tricky. What does having a specific name for the little people tell you?
exercises.correct: They are recognised beings in the Kanien'kehá:ka cosmological world — specific enough to have their own name
options: They are recognised beings with their own specific name
The little people are fictional only
All cultures have the same beings under different names
The name is borrowed from European folklore
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onkwehshón:'a tsi onnhéhton — the little people who are underground / who live below. They have a specific name in Kanien'kéha because they are specific beings in the cosmological world. The language names what exists.

exercises.prompt: The water serpent
exercises.storyPrompt: Kanonhkwatshéri:io — the large water serpent. In the oral tradition, the great serpent lives in the deep water. The Peacemaker's journey required crossing the lake where the serpent lived. The serpent is one of the beings whose presence marks the deep, dangerous, transformative waters. Learning its name is a small step into the vast cosmological world of Kanien'kehá:ka tradition. The full story belongs to winter, to knowledge keepers, to the gathered community around a fire. This word is a door to that world — not the world itself. Kanonhkwatshéri:io.

lessons.title: Review — Oral Tradition
lessons.sub: Stories, legends, and the spoken word
intro: This review consolidates oral tradition vocabulary: the storytelling season, creation story elements, and legendary beings.
The oral tradition is the deepest repository of Kanien'kéha language — the most complex grammar, the oldest vocabulary, the richest metaphor.
Use this lesson to consolidate.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001): review through new combinations.

exercises.prompt: Kenién:ke — winter. Why is it significant that the storytelling season is a specific season, not 'whenever you feel like it'?
exercises.correct: The protocol reflects a specific cosmological understanding — certain stories have power that must be handled carefully and at the right time
options: The protocol reflects a specific cosmological understanding — stories have power
Winter nights are just longer
It is only a social convention
The protocol has no specific reason
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The storytelling protocol is not arbitrary convention — it reflects a specific understanding of how stories, power, and the spiritual world relate. The season matters. The context matters. The care matters.

exercises.prompt: Match the creation story element to its role

pairs.english: Provided the foundation for the world
Brought up the earth from the water
Present at the beginning; acknowledged at the end of the Address
Built on the turtle's back from the muskrat's mud

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Turtle, muskrat, Creator, earth — four creation story elements, now placed in their roles.

exercises.prompt: Oral tradition and the Address
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. The creation story tells how the world came to be. The Thanksgiving Address thanks the world that was created. Together they form a complete cosmology: here is how the world was made; here is how we acknowledge it every day. Learning Kanien'kéha means entering this cosmology — not as an outsider studying it, but as a person building a relationship with it, word by word. Every lesson has been a step deeper in. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Oral Tradition
