lessons.sub: Stories, legends, and the spoken word
intro: This review consolidates oral tradition vocabulary: the storytelling season, creation story elements, legendary beings, and the protocols that govern when and how stories are shared.
Oral tradition is the deepest repository of Kanien'kéha language — the oldest vocabulary, the richest metaphor, the most complex grammar.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001).

exercises.prompt: Kenién:ke — winter. Why does the storytelling season being specifically winter matter?
exercises.correct: The protocol reflects a specific cosmological understanding — certain stories have power that must be handled at the right time
options: The protocol reflects cosmological understanding — stories have power
Winter nights are longer
It is only social convention
The protocol has no specific reason
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The storytelling protocol is not arbitrary — it reflects the understanding that certain stories have power requiring care.

exercises.prompt: Match the creation story and legend element to its role

pairs.english: Provided the foundation for the world
Brought up the earth from the water
Present at beginning; acknowledged at end of the Address
Being of the deep waters in oral tradition

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Turtle, muskrat, Creator, water serpent — from the creation and the oral tradition.

exercises.prompt: The Thanksgiving Address and the creation story together form a complete cosmology. What are their two different functions?
exercises.correct: The creation story tells how the world came to be; the Address thanks the world that was created — origin and gratitude, beginning and daily practice
options: Creation story = origin; Address = daily gratitude for the created world
They have the same function
The Address tells the creation story
The creation story replaces the Address
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Creation story: here is how the world was made. Thanksgiving Address: here is how we acknowledge it every day. Beginning and practice. The two together are a complete relationship with existence.

exercises.prompt: Origin and gratitude
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen. The creation story tells how the world came to be — Sky Woman, the turtle, the muskrat, the earth built from mud. The Thanksgiving Address thanks the world that was made. Together: origin and gratitude. The beginning, and the daily practice of acknowledging it. You have been learning both throughout this course. The creation story's vocabulary — the sky world, the turtle, the muskrat — was scattered across many units. Now you can see how it fits together. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 29 — Ceremony in Language: Singing, Prayer & Ritual Speech
desc: The language of ceremony — the formal register, ceremonial song vocabulary, prayer, and the speech forms used in Haudenosaunee ritual life.

lessons.title: Ceremonial Language: A Different Register
lessons.sub: How formal ceremonial speech differs from everyday Kanien'kéha
intro: Kanien'kéha has a ceremonial register — a style of speaking used in formal gatherings, condolence ceremonies, and longhouse protocols. This register is not a different language, but it uses specific vocabulary, specific verb forms, and specific oratorical patterns not common in everyday speech.

You have encountered ceremonial language throughout this course: the Thanksgiving Address, the condolence vocabulary, the council speech formulas. This unit names that register explicitly and explores its distinctive features.
This is among the most advanced content in the course. It is also among the most important — ceremonial language is where the language is richest and deepest.
lessons.ped: Ceremonial register as advanced content: The ceremonial register requires familiarity with everyday Kanien'kéha before it becomes accessible. This unit is placed late in the course for this reason. LaFever Spiritual domain primary throughout.

exercises.prompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the Thanksgiving Address. You have known this text since Unit 5. What makes it an example of ceremonial register rather than everyday speech?
exercises.correct: Specific vocabulary not used in daily life, formal oratorical structure, specific phrase patterns, and the expectation of completeness — it is a complete text, not a conversation
options: Specific vocabulary, formal structure, complete text form — not everyday conversation
The language is different from everyday Kanien'kéha
It is simpler than everyday speech
Only chiefs can say it
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ceremonial register is recognisable by specific vocabulary, formal structure, and the expectation that the full text will be delivered. Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen is the clearest example.

exercises.prompt: Skennón:'a — peace. In the condolence ceremony, this word carries enormous weight. In everyday speech it is also usable. What is the difference between ceremonial and everyday use of the same word?
exercises.correct: Ceremonial use carries the full weight of the Great Law and the condolence tradition; everyday use is lighter. Context elevates the word.
options: Ceremonial use carries the full cultural and legal weight; everyday use is lighter — context elevates the word
The word changes meaning entirely in ceremony
Ceremonial use is wrong
The word cannot be used in everyday speech
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Skennón:'a is the same word in both registers. But in the condolence ceremony, surrounded by the formal text, the full weight of what peace means in Haudenosaunee governance is invoked. Words carry more or less weight depending on context. Ceremonial context is the heaviest.

exercises.prompt: Iakoiánehr — clan mother. In ceremonial context, how does addressing a clan mother differ from everyday address?
exercises.correct: Ceremonial address uses the formal title in full, often with respectful acknowledgement of her role — the formal register marks the occasion
options: Ceremonial address uses the full formal title with acknowledgement of role
There is no difference
Her name is used instead in ceremony
The formal title is only used in writing
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ceremonial register elevates address terms. The clan mother's full title, her role acknowledged, her name used with the formality the occasion demands. The register signals that this is not everyday conversation — it is ceremony.

exercises.prompt: Ceremonial language
exercises.meaning: It makes our minds happy / We give thanks
exercises.storyPrompt: Ionkhi'nikonhraién:tase'. You have said this phrase dozens of times in this course. In everyday learning, it is a feedback phrase — excellent, wonderful. In the Thanksgiving Address, it is the hinge of the whole structure — the phrase that closes each acknowledgement and gathers everyone's minds in shared gratitude. The same words. The same language. Different registers. This is what ceremonial language is: the everyday language of the people, elevated, structured, and made whole for the occasion. You have been learning ceremonial language all along.

lessons.title: Songs and Singing in Ceremony
lessons.sub: The role of song in Kanien'kehá:ka ceremony — and what can be shared
intro: Songs are central to every Kanien'kehá:ka ceremony. Social dance songs, medicine songs, Thanksgiving songs, condolence songs — each has its specific context, its specific singers, and its specific protocols for sharing.

Some songs are public and can be heard by anyone. Some songs belong to specific societies or ceremonies and are restricted. This lesson introduces the vocabulary of ceremonial singing without reproducing restricted content.
The singers — Ratiwennahní:ion — carry the songs. Knowing their title and their role is appropriate. Knowing which songs they carry requires community relationship, not a language app.
lessons.ped: Ceremonial song vocabulary with boundaries: The vocabulary of singing and ceremony is appropriate. Specific ceremonial songs are not appropriate to reproduce. Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): community boundaries apply.

exercises.prompt: Match the ceremonial music element to its role

pairs.english: The singers — who carry and lead the ceremony
Water drum — the heartbeat of the ceremony
Native tobacco — opens the ceremonial space

exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Singers, drum, rattle, tobacco — the elements that make a ceremony possible. Each plays a specific role that cannot be substituted.

exercises.prompt: Ratiwennahní:ion — the singers. Some of their songs are public; some are restricted. How does a learner know which is which?
exercises.correct: By being in community relationship with knowledge keepers who can share what is shareable — a language app cannot make this determination
options: By being in community relationship with knowledge keepers
By looking it up in a reference
All songs are either fully public or fully private
The language determines what is shareable
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The distinction between shareable and restricted ceremonial content is held by community knowledge keepers, not by language learners or apps. Relationship is how you learn what can be shared. This lesson models that boundary.

exercises.prompt: Tesenón:niahkhw — you dance (command). Social dances are public — anyone can participate. How do social dances differ from restricted ceremonial dances?
exercises.correct: Social dances are open invitations — anyone present can join. Restricted dances belong to specific societies or ceremonies with specific membership.
options: Social dances are open invitations to all. Restricted dances belong to specific societies.
All dances are the same
Social dances are more important
Restricted dances are not real ceremonies
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Social dances like the Friendship Dance are explicitly open — the singers invite everyone to join. Restricted dances belong to specific medicine societies or longhouse ceremonies. The line is clear: open vs restricted, not public vs private.

exercises.prompt: The singers
exercises.storyPrompt: Ratiwennahní:ion — the singers. They arrive at the gathering before it begins. They set up the drum, prepare the rattles, speak quietly among themselves. When the ceremony is ready, they begin. The songs have been learned over years — from other singers, from recording sessions, from ceremony attendance, from elders who taught them. Some songs they will teach freely. Some they will not. They know the difference. When they sing, the ceremony becomes possible. Ratiwennahní:ion. The language holds their name. Their songs hold the ceremony.

lessons.title: Review — Ceremony in Language
lessons.sub: Ceremonial register, song, and the language of ritual
intro: This review consolidates ceremonial language: the formal register, the difference between everyday and ceremonial use, the vocabulary of ceremonial singing, and the appropriate boundaries around restricted content.
Ceremonial language is the deepest level of Kanien'kéha. It is where the language has been kept most carefully and where it is most alive.
Use this lesson to consolidate. Onen' ki' wáhi.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001): review through new combinations.

exercises.prompt: Match the ceremonial element to what makes it ceremonial

pairs.english: Formal oratorical structure, complete text, specific vocabulary
Everyday word elevated to full legal and spiritual weight by ceremonial context
Singers who carry both restricted and shareable songs
The closing phrase that marks the end of ceremony and learning alike

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Formal structure, contextual elevation, restricted knowledge, and the universal closing. Ceremonial language in four elements.

exercises.prompt: You have now encountered the Thanksgiving Address in Units 5, 14, 15, 16, 19, 20, 24, 27, 28, and 29. Why does it keep appearing?
exercises.correct: Because it is the connective tissue of the language and culture — it touches every domain, every topic, every vocabulary area in the course
options: Because it is the connective tissue of language and culture — it touches every domain
Because it is the most important vocabulary to memorise
It appears by coincidence
It replaces other content when nothing else is available
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen appears in every unit because it is the frame that holds the language together. It acknowledges the earth and all its beings. Every unit of this course has been about the earth and its beings. The Address was never background. It was always the point.

exercises.prompt: Onen' ki' wáhi
exercises.meaning: That is all / I am finished
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen' ki' wáhi. You have reached Unit 29. Twenty-nine units of Kanien'kéha — pronouns, kinship, nouns, verbs, colours, body, the Thanksgiving Address, animals, clans, food, ceremony, tools, grammar, fish, birds, numbers, conversation, the verb systems, introductions, daily routine, the natural world, making and building, community life, numbers in depth, simple sentences, describing things, spatial language, technology, health, agriculture, oral tradition, and ceremony. Onen' ki' wáhi. That is all — for now. The language continues. You continue. What you have built here is a beginning. The rest of the language is out there, in the community, in the ceremony, in the conversations you have not yet had. Go have them. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: The Language of Prayer
lessons.sub: Speaking to the spirit world in Kanien'kéha
intro: Prayer in Kanien'kehá:ka tradition is not petition in the way it is understood in colonial religious contexts — it is acknowledgement, gratitude, and relationship. The Thanksgiving Address is itself the primary form of prayer.
This lesson explores the vocabulary of speaking to the spirit world: tobacco as the carrier, the words that accompany it, and the formal language of acknowledgement.
Some of this content is restricted and belongs to specific knowledge keepers. This lesson introduces what is appropriate to introduce.
lessons.ped: Prayer vocabulary with appropriate boundaries: Chew, Leonard & Rosenblum (2023): restricted ceremonial content boundaries respected.

exercises.prompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. How is this a form of prayer?
exercises.correct: It acknowledges every being the Creator made — a comprehensive act of gratitude that is the foundation of all that follows
options: It acknowledges every being the Creator made — comprehensive gratitude before all else
It is not prayer — only a greeting
It is only said by elders
It replaces other prayer forms
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The Thanksgiving Address is the primary prayer form: every being acknowledged, every gift named, gratitude expressed collectively. Not petition — acknowledgement. Not asking — thanking.

exercises.prompt: Onkwatà:wen — tobacco is put down before making a request or beginning a ceremony. What is the theology of tobacco as prayer medium?
exercises.correct: Tobacco carries the intention to the spirit world — it opens the channel of communication and marks the request as sincere
options: Tobacco carries the intention — it opens communication and marks sincerity
Tobacco is decorative
Tobacco replaces words
Tobacco only matters in medicine contexts
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Tobacco is the carrier. You put it down, you speak your intention, the tobacco carries it. This is a specific theological understanding of how communication with the spirit world works in Kanien'kehá:ka practice.

exercises.prompt: Shonkwaiá:tison — the Creator. The Thanksgiving Address acknowledges the Creator last, after all the created beings. In prayer, what does this order mean?
exercises.correct: You acknowledge all the Creator's gifts first — then you arrive at the Creator, having named everything that was given
options: You acknowledge the gifts first, then arrive at the Creator having named what was given
The Creator is less important than other beings
It is an arbitrary order
The Creator is acknowledged first in some versions
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The order of the Address is a form of prayer logic: name what was made, thank each being for their specific gift, and arrive at the Creator having named the whole of creation. The prayer is comprehensive before it is personal.

exercises.prompt: Prayer through tobacco
exercises.storyPrompt: Onkwatà:wen — tobacco. Put it down. Speak. The tobacco carries what you have said to where it needs to go. This is the simplest description of prayer in Kanien'kehá:ka tradition: put the tobacco down, speak your intention or your gratitude, and trust the channel is open. No elaborate formula. No institution required. Just tobacco, words, and the relationship that makes the carrying possible. Onkwatà:wen. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Ceremony in Language
lessons.sub: Ceremonial register, song, and prayer
intro: This review consolidates ceremonial language: the formal register, ceremonial singing vocabulary, prayer, and the appropriate boundaries around restricted content.

Ceremonial language is the deepest level of Kanien'kéha. You have now been introduced to its vocabulary, its register, and its boundaries. The rest belongs to community relationship and lived practice.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation: Nation (2001).

exercises.prompt: Match the ceremonial element to what makes it ceremonial

pairs.english: Formal oratorical structure — the primary prayer form
Everyday word elevated by ceremonial context
Singers who carry restricted and shareable songs
Tobacco — opens the ceremonial and prayer space

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Prayer form, elevated word, singers, tobacco — ceremonial language in four elements.

exercises.prompt: You have encountered the Thanksgiving Address across many units. Why does it keep appearing?
exercises.correct: Because it is the connective tissue of the language and culture — it touches every domain, every topic, every vocabulary area
options: It is the connective tissue — it touches every domain and topic
It is repeated for memorisation
It replaces other content
It appears by coincidence
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The Address has appeared in almost every unit because it acknowledges almost every being this course has introduced. The earth, the waters, the plants, the animals, the sky beings — you have met all of them. When you hear the Address now, you hear the names of beings you know.

exercises.prompt: The end — and the beginning
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen' ki' wáhi. You have reached Unit 29. Twenty-nine units of Kanien'kéha. Pronouns, kinship, nouns, verbs, colours, body, the Thanksgiving Address, animals, clans, food, ceremony, tools, grammar, fish, birds, numbers, conversation, the verb systems, introductions, daily routine, the natural world, making and building, community life, numbers in depth, sentences, describing, spatial language, technology, health, agriculture, oral tradition, and ceremony. Onen' ki' wáhi. That is all — for now. The language continues. You continue. What you have built here is a beginning. The rest is out there, in the community, in the ceremony, in the conversations you have not yet had. Go have them. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit 30 — Reading and Writing Kanien'kéha
desc: The marks that make Kanien'kéha readable — long vowels, glottal stops, stress, and nasals — and why every one of them matters

lessons.title: The Writing System: Where It Came From
lessons.sub: How Kanien'kéha came to be written — and why it matters
intro: Kanien'kéha is a spoken language first. It lived in the mouths and ears of the Kanien'kehá:ka people for thousands of years before anyone wrote it down.

The writing system used in this app — and in most Kanien'kehá:ka communities today — was developed by linguists and community members working together. It uses the same alphabet as English but adds marks: accent marks, long vowel markers, and special letters to capture sounds English cannot.

There is more than one way to write Kanien'kéha. Different communities use slightly different spelling conventions. None is wrong. This unit focuses on the Kanehsatà:ke / Kahnawà:ke system used throughout this app.
lessons.ped: Orthography as political and cultural act: The history of writing systems for Indigenous languages is entangled with colonial documentation practices. This lesson names that history plainly — missionaries and linguists wrote down Indigenous languages for their own purposes — while foregrounding the community-led work of developing a usable, respected orthography. LaFever Spiritual domain: writing the language is a form of asserting its existence and permanence. Mental domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Kanien'kéha was spoken for thousands of years before it was written. What does this tell you about how to think about the writing system?
exercises.correct: It is a tool to help people read and learn — the real language lives in speech
options: It is a tool to help people read and learn — the real language lives in speech
Written Kanien'kéha is the correct form
Speaking without writing is informal and incorrect
The writing system replaced the spoken language
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The writing is a tool — useful and important, but it serves the spoken language. When you learn to read and write Kanien'kéha, you are learning a way to study and remember the spoken language. The real home of the language is in the voice.

exercises.prompt: Oh ni:ioht tsi enshi:ron ___? means 'How do you say ___?' This is a learner's tool. When would you use it?
exercises.correct: When you want to know the Kanien'kéha word for something you don't know yet
options: When you want to know the Kanien'kéha word for something you don't know yet
When you already know the word
Only in formal ceremonies
Only in writing
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Oh ni:ioht tsi enshi:ron ___? — how do you say ___? Keep this question close. Every speaker you meet is a teacher.

exercises.prompt: Oh naho:ten ken:ton ne ___? means 'What does ___ mean?' Why is having this question in your toolkit so important?
exercises.correct: It lets you ask for help when you don't understand — and keeps you in the conversation instead of stopping
options: It lets you ask for help when you don't understand — and keeps you in the conversation instead of stopping
It is only useful in writing class
Fluent speakers never need to ask this
It is considered rude to ask
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Oh naho:ten ken:ton ne ___? — what does ___ mean? A learner who can ask this question can keep learning from every conversation. You do not need to understand everything. You need to be able to ask.

exercises.prompt: Match the learner's tool to what it does
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Four tools every learner needs. How do you say it? What does it mean? Say it again. I don't understand. These four phrases can carry you through many conversations even when your vocabulary is still growing.

exercises.prompt: Writing the language
exercises.storyPrompt: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am studying Kanien'kéha. At one point in history, people who wanted to suppress this language did not bother writing it down — they just forbade it. Every time Kanien'kéha is written — in a textbook, on a sign, in an app like this one — it is one more place the language exists in the world. Writing is not the whole language. But it is a form of saying: this language is here. It is real. It is not going away. What does it mean to you to see Kanien'kéha written?

lessons.title: Short and Long Vowels
lessons.sub: The colon (:) — the most important mark in Kanien'kéha writing
intro: Every vowel in Kanien'kéha comes in two lengths: short and long. A long vowel is held about twice as long as a short one. The colon (:) after a vowel is what tells you it is long.
This is not a decoration. Changing a vowel's length can completely change the meaning of a word. The colon is as important as any letter.
You have been seeing it since the first lesson: í:se' (you), Shé:kon (hello), Tho niioht, Niawenhkó:wa. This lesson makes the pattern visible.
lessons.ped: Vowel length as phonemic contrast: Mithun (1982) establishes vowel length as fully contrastive in Kanien'kéha. This is introduced through minimal pairs drawn from known vocabulary — words learners have been using throughout the course — so the point lands with words they already care about. Nation (2001): orthographic awareness through familiar examples. Physical domain: learners practise distinguishing and producing length contrast.

exercises.prompt: In í:se' (you), the colon after í tells you the vowel is long. How does a long vowel sound different from a short one?
exercises.correct: It is held longer — about twice the length of a short vowel
options: It is held longer — about twice the length of a short vowel
It is louder
It has a different sound entirely
It is higher in pitch
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A long vowel is held longer. Not louder, not higher — just longer. í: in í:se' is held about twice as long as a plain i would be. Stress (loudness and pitch) is marked separately with an accent mark.

exercises.prompt: Find the long vowel in each word
exercises.sub: The colon (:) marks it

pairs.english: Long vowel on í — held double
Long vowel on é — held double
Long vowel on ó — held double
Long vowel on é — held double

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Long vowels are everywhere in Kanien'kéha. Once you see the colon, you know to hold that vowel. Missing the length can change the meaning of a word.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look for the colon.

exercises.prompt: Shé:kon has a long vowel on é:. What happens if you shorten it — if you say Shékon without the length?
exercises.correct: You might be misunderstood — length is part of the word, not optional
options: You might be misunderstood — length is part of the word, not optional
Nothing — length does not affect meaning
The word becomes more formal
The word becomes a question
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Vowel length in Kanien'kéha is not optional — it is part of the word. Shé:kon is a specific word because of that long é:. Speakers who know the language will notice if you shorten it, just as an English speaker notices if you say a word wrong.

exercises.prompt: Mark the long vowel: Sh___:kon
exercises.correct: é
exercises.hint: é
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon — the long vowel is é:. The colon comes right after the vowel it lengthens.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which vowel does the colon follow in Shé:kon?

exercises.prompt: Count the long vowels in: Onen' ki' wáhi
exercises.sub: Look for colons — remember wáhi has an accent but no colon
exercises.correct: Zero — none of the vowels in Onen' ki' wáhi are long
options: Zero — none of the vowels in Onen' ki' wáhi are long
One — the á in wáhi
Two — the o and the i
Three — one in each word
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! No colons in Onen' ki' wáhi — all short vowels. The accent mark on wáhi (á) marks stress, not length. Accent and length are two different marks.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look only for colons (:), not accent marks.

exercises.prompt: Say these two words — feel the length difference
exercises.hint: Shé:kon: hold the é: for a full beat. Shékon: cut it short. Can you feel the difference? The first is the word. The second is a mistake. Train your ear to hear it.

exercises.prompt: The colon
exercises.storyPrompt: A single small mark — the colon. It changes everything it touches. Without it, words blur into each other, meanings shift. With it, Kanien'kéha becomes readable, teachable, transmittable. The people who worked to develop this writing system chose to put the colon in because they respected what it represents — the precision of a language that has always known the difference between short and long. When you honour the colon, you honour that precision.

lessons.title: The Glottal Stop
lessons.sub: The apostrophe (') — a real consonant, not punctuation
intro: The apostrophe (') in Kanien'kéha is not punctuation. It is a real consonant — the glottal stop. Your throat closes briefly, then opens again. You do this in English too, in the middle of 'uh-oh' — that little catch between the two sounds.
In Kanien'kéha the glottal stop appears at the beginning, middle, and end of words. It is as real as k, t, or n. Leaving it out can change the meaning of a word.
You have been seeing it since Unit 1: í:'i (I), iken', Onen', Raksá:'a. This lesson makes it explicit.
lessons.ped: Glottal stop as phoneme: The glottal stop is consistently the hardest orthographic feature for English-background learners to take seriously — they read the apostrophe as punctuation. This lesson addresses that head-on with familiar examples. Mithun (1982): the glottal stop is a full phoneme in Kanien'kéha. Physical domain primary: learners practise producing and identifying it.

exercises.prompt: In í:'i (I / me), the apostrophe between í: and i is a glottal stop. What does a glottal stop feel like to produce?
exercises.correct: A brief closure of the throat — like the catch in the middle of 'uh-oh' in English
options: A brief closure of the throat — like the catch in the middle of 'uh-oh' in English
A silent pause with no throat movement
A puff of air like the h in hello
A sound like the letter k
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The glottal stop is produced by briefly closing the vocal cords — the same catch you feel in 'uh-oh'. In í:'i, you hold the í:, close briefly, then open onto the final i. It takes practice but it is a learnable sound.

exercises.prompt: Find the glottal stop in each word
exercises.sub: Look for the apostrophe (')

pairs.english: Glottal stop between í: and i
Glottal stop before the final a

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Glottal stops appear at the start, middle, and end of words. In every position, the apostrophe marks a real throat closure. It is not decoration.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look for the apostrophe (').

exercises.prompt: í:se' ends with a glottal stop (the ' at the end). What would happen to the meaning if you dropped it — saying í:se instead of í:se'?
exercises.correct: The word would be incorrect — the glottal stop is part of the word
options: The word would be incorrect — the glottal stop is part of the word
Nothing — it is optional
The word would become a question
The word would become plural
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The glottal stop in í:se' is part of the word — leaving it out produces an incorrect form. Kanien'kéha speakers will notice. Honour the apostrophe.

exercises.prompt: Write the pronoun 'I / me' correctly with its glottal stop
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! í:'i — the glottal stop sits between the long vowel and the final i. One small mark, but the word is not complete without it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what mark goes in the middle of í:'i?

exercises.prompt: Ieksá:'a (girl) has a glottal stop before the final 'a. In writing, how do you know a glottal stop is there rather than just an apostrophe used for English-style punctuation?
exercises.correct: Context — in Kanien'kéha writing, every apostrophe represents a glottal stop. There is no other use for it.
options: Context — in Kanien'kéha writing, every apostrophe represents a glottal stop. There is no other use for it.
It is only a glottal stop if it appears at the end of a word
Glottal stops are marked with a different symbol
You cannot know — it is ambiguous
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! In Kanien'kéha orthography, every apostrophe is a glottal stop. There is no ambiguity — it is not used for contractions or possessives the way English uses it. Every ' you see is a sound.

exercises.prompt: Say these two words — feel the glottal stop
exercises.hint: í:'i: hold the long í:, close the throat briefly, then say the final i. iken': say it, then close the throat at the end — don't let the word trail off. The glottal stop closes the word like a period closes a sentence.

exercises.prompt: The throat's consonant
exercises.storyPrompt: í:'i — I. The very first pronoun you learned has a glottal stop in its heart. The word for 'I' in Kanien'kéha closes briefly on itself — a small pause, a catch, before it opens again. Think about that. The word for yourself has a built-in moment of pause. Not silence — a closure that then reopens. What does it mean to say 'I' with that catch in it?

lessons.title: Stress and Accent Marks
lessons.sub: The acute (á) and grave (à) — where the voice rises and falls
intro: Stress in Kanien'kéha is marked with accent marks over vowels. The acute accent (á) marks the syllable where your voice rises and gets a little stronger. The grave accent (à) marks a falling pitch.
You have been seeing these throughout the course: Shé:kon, wáhi, Kanehsatà:ke. The accent tells your ear — and your mouth — where to land.
Stress in Kanien'kéha can change meaning. It is not decoration and it is not optional. But it is learnable — and the more you listen, the more naturally it comes.
lessons.ped: Stress as meaningful — not decorative: English speakers often treat accent marks as optional in foreign languages. This lesson makes clear that in Kanien'kéha they change meaning, using familiar words as examples. Nation (2001): orthographic features best taught through known vocabulary. Physical domain: listening and production.

exercises.prompt: In Shé:kon, the acute accent (é) is on the first syllable. What does the acute accent tell you to do with your voice?
exercises.correct: Rise slightly on that syllable — it is a little louder and higher
options: Rise slightly on that syllable — it is a little louder and higher
Lower your voice
Pause before it
Lengthen the vowel
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The acute accent (á, é, í, ó) marks where your voice rises — slightly louder, slightly higher. It is the stressed syllable. SHÉ-kon: the stress is on SHÉ.
exercises.wm: Please try again — what does the acute accent do to pitch?

exercises.prompt: Kaniatarà:ke has a grave accent (à) in the middle. The grave marks a falling pitch on that syllable. How is this different from the acute accent?
exercises.correct: The grave (à) is a falling pitch — the voice drops; the acute (á) is a rising pitch — the voice rises
options: The grave (à) is a falling pitch — the voice drops; the acute (á) is a rising pitch — the voice rises
They mean the same thing
The grave marks a long vowel
The grave marks a glottal stop
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Acute (á) = rising, stressed. Grave (à) = falling. Kaniatarà:ke: Ka-nia-ta-RÀ:-ke — the voice falls on the rà:. Both marks give you direction for your voice.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which way does each accent direct the pitch?

exercises.prompt: Find the stressed syllable in each word
exercises.sub: The acute accent (') marks it
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Stress moves around in Kanien'kéha — it is not always on the first syllable the way English defaults. The accent marks show you exactly where it lands.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look for the acute accent (').

exercises.prompt: Kanehsatà:ke has a grave accent on the à. Write the accented vowel: Kanehsat___:ke
exercises.correct: à
exercises.hint: à
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kanehsatà:ke — the grave accent sits on à, and the colon immediately follows it, marking vowel length. So this syllable has both a falling pitch and a long vowel. Two marks working together on one syllable.
exercises.wm: Please try again — the grave accent goes over the a before the colon.

exercises.prompt: Niawenhkó:wa — count the syllables and find the stressed one.
exercises.correct: Four syllables: Nia-wen-KÓ:-wa — stress on the third syllable KÓ:
options: Four syllables: Nia-wen-KÓ:-wa — stress on the third syllable KÓ:
Three syllables: stress on the first
Five syllables: stress on the last
Two syllables: stress on the second
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Nia-wen-KÓ:-wa — four syllables, stress on KÓ:. That syllable has both the stress (acute accent) and a long vowel (colon). It carries a lot of weight — and Niawenhkó:wa is a word of great weight.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count the syllables first, then find the acute accent.

exercises.prompt: Say this word and feel where the stress lands
exercises.hint: Nia-wen-KÓ:-wa. The KÓ: carries the stress and the length together. Let your voice rise and hold on that syllable. The word is not in a hurry — it is an expression of real gratitude.

exercises.prompt: The name on the land
exercises.meaning: Kanehsatà:ke — where the crust is on the sand
exercises.storyPrompt: Kanehsatà:ke. Say it slowly. Ka-neh-sa-tà:-ke. The falling pitch on tà:, the long vowel, the final ke that tells you this is a place. Every accent mark and every colon in this word is part of how the name has been said for generations. The place name has a sound, and the writing tries to capture that sound faithfully. When you read Kanehsatà:ke and say it correctly — with the falling pitch and the long vowel in the right place — you are saying the name the way it has always been said.

lessons.title: Nasal Vowels
lessons.sub: ę and ǫ — vowels that come through the nose
intro: Kanien'kéha has two nasal vowels: ę and ǫ. When you say these, air passes through both your mouth and your nose at the same time. It is a different sound from any vowel in English.
ę sounds something like 'an' — but the n is not a separate sound. It just tells you air is going through the nose. ǫ is similar but rounder, like 'on' without the separate n.
These two letters are easy to miss when you are reading — they look like o and e with a little hook underneath. But they sound different from o and e, and using them correctly matters.
lessons.ped: Nasal vowels as a distinct phonological category: Nasal vowels are a core feature of Kanien'kéha that have no parallel in English. The lesson introduces them through feel and contrast — air through nose and mouth simultaneously — rather than through IPA description. Nation (2001): new phonemes best taught through familiar word environments. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: The letter ę (with a hook underneath) is a nasal vowel. What makes it different from a regular e?
exercises.correct: Air flows through both the mouth and the nose at the same time — it is a nasal vowel
options: Air flows through both the mouth and the nose at the same time — it is a nasal vowel
It is longer than a regular e
It is said louder
It is the same as e but only in formal speech
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ę is a nasal vowel — the mouth stays open but air also comes through the nose, giving the vowel a nasal quality. It is a sound that does not exist in English as a single vowel, which is why it needs its own letter.

exercises.prompt: ǫ is the nasal version of o. If ę sounds roughly like 'an' (with air through the nose), what does ǫ sound roughly like?
exercises.correct: 'on' — but with air through the nose, not a separate n sound
options: 'on' — but with air through the nose, not a separate n sound
'oo' as in moon
'ow' as in how
Exactly like the regular o
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! ǫ is the rounded nasal vowel — somewhere between 'on' and 'oh' but with nasal air flowing through. ę and ǫ are both nasal; ę is the front (mouth more open), ǫ is the back (lips more rounded).

exercises.prompt: You see the letter ę in a Kanien'kéha word. What should you do?
exercises.correct: Say the vowel with air coming through the nose as well as the mouth
options: Say the vowel with air coming through the nose as well as the mouth
Say it like a regular e
Skip it — it is a spelling variant only
Say it like the letter n
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! ę = nasalise. Every time you see the hooked e or hooked o, air goes through the nose. It takes practice, but the more you do it, the more natural it becomes.

exercises.prompt: Kanien'kéha has two nasal vowels: ę and ǫ. English has no nasal vowels as single sounds. What does this mean for English speakers learning Kanien'kéha?
exercises.correct: It is a new sound that requires practice — your mouth needs to learn to do something it has not done in English
options: It is a new sound that requires practice — your mouth needs to learn to do something it has not done in English
English speakers cannot learn nasal vowels
Nasal vowels are optional in Kanien'kéha
They sound exactly like English vowels with an n after them
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Learning a new sound takes physical practice — your mouth, throat, and nose learning to coordinate in a new way. This is normal. Every new language asks your body to do new things. The nasal vowels are one of Kanien'kéha's most distinctive sounds.

exercises.prompt: Say the two nasal vowels — feel where the air goes
exercises.hint: For both: keep your mouth open and let air come through your nose at the same time. ę: like 'an' said with a relaxed, open jaw — but no separate n. ǫ: lips slightly rounded, like 'on' — but the n is just nasal airflow, not a separate consonant. Practise in front of a mirror if you can.

exercises.prompt: A sound your body learns
exercises.storyPrompt: ę and ǫ. Two sounds that exist in this language and not in English. Your mouth may resist them at first — it has spent years doing something different. That is normal. Learning a language means teaching your body new sounds. Babies do this without thinking. Adults do it with effort and repetition. Every time you practise ę and ǫ, you are retraining something physical — not just memorising a fact. The language is entering your body.

lessons.title: Reading Practice: Short Words
lessons.sub: Seeing all the marks together — and reading what they mean
intro: This lesson puts all four orthographic features together: long vowels (:), glottal stops ('), accent marks (á / à), and nasal vowels (ę / ǫ). Short, familiar words — read carefully, with attention to every mark.
The goal here is not speed. It is accuracy. Reading Kanien'kéha slowly and correctly is better than reading it quickly and wrong. Every mark is there for a reason.
You will see words from Units 1 through 23 — words you know by now. The task is to read them as they are written, not as you expect them to look.
lessons.ped: Reading accuracy before reading speed: Nation (2001) identifies orthographic accuracy — reading the written form correctly — as a prerequisite for using written text as a learning resource. This lesson provides controlled practice with known vocabulary. Physical domain primary: eyes and mouth working together.

exercises.prompt: Read this carefully: í:se' — How many distinct phonological features are present in this one word?
exercises.sub: Count: long vowel, glottal stop, accent, nasal vowel
exercises.correct: Three — a long vowel (í:), an acute accent (í), and a glottal stop (') at the end
options: Three — a long vowel (í:), an acute accent (í), and a glottal stop (') at the end
One — just the accent
Two — accent and glottal stop only
Four — including a nasal vowel
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! í:se' — three features in one short word: the acute accent on í, the long vowel marker : after it, and the glottal stop ' at the end. This two-syllable word is doing a lot of orthographic work.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count each mark separately.

exercises.prompt: Match each word to the orthographic features it contains
exercises.sub: Look carefully at each mark

pairs.english: Acute accent + long vowel + glottal stop

exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Every word in Kanien'kéha carries its own combination of marks. Reading carefully means attending to all of them.
exercises.wm: Please try again — look at each mark in the word.

exercises.prompt: Write the correct vowel with its accent and length marker for Niawenhk___:wa
exercises.correct: ó
exercises.hint: ó
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Niawenhkó:wa — the ó carries the acute accent (stress) and is followed by the colon (length). One syllable doing two jobs at once.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which accented vowel goes before the colon?

exercises.prompt: Type the word for bear exactly as it is written in Kanien'kéha
exercises.sub: Every mark matters
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri — acute accent on á, colon after it. Two marks on one syllable. The bear's name in Kanien'kéha, written exactly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — include both the accent mark and the colon.

exercises.prompt: Read: Onón:ta. How many syllables does this word have, and where is the stress?
exercises.correct: Three syllables: O-NÓN:-ta — stress on the second syllable NÓN:
options: Three syllables: O-NÓN:-ta — stress on the second syllable NÓN:
Two syllables: ON-ta
Four syllables: O-no-on-ta
Three syllables: stress on the first
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! O-NÓN:-ta — three syllables, stress on NÓN:. The long vowel and the stress both sit on the second syllable. Corn — Onón:ta — named and said correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count the vowels, then find the accent.

exercises.prompt: Read these five words aloud — one by one, carefully
exercises.hint: Take each word on its own. Find the accent, feel the length of the long vowel, produce any glottal stop. Speed comes later. Accuracy comes first.

exercises.prompt: Reading your own language
exercises.storyPrompt: í:se' — you. Every mark in this word has been placed there carefully by people who wanted the language to be readable, learnable, transmittable. The people who developed this writing system were thinking of you — the learner, the one coming back to the language. When you read í:se' and say it correctly, you are using a gift that was made for you. You are the person this writing was written for.

lessons.title: Reading Practice: Full Sentences
lessons.sub: Putting it together — reading sentences aloud from the page
intro: Short words are one thing. Full sentences are where reading really gets tested. This lesson gives you full Kanien'kéha sentences to read aloud — sentences you have already seen in this course.

Your job is to read them as written. Notice every colon, every apostrophe, every accent mark. Say the long vowels long. Close your throat on the glottal stops. Let the stress land where the accent tells you.
This is what it looks like to read Kanien'kéha.
lessons.ped: Sentence-level reading as consolidation: Nation (2001) identifies reading connected text as the highest-value orthographic practice — it integrates phonological awareness with meaning. All sentences in this lesson are drawn from earlier units, so learners can focus on the reading task without new vocabulary demands. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Read this sentence: 'Shé:kon, tó:kens?' — How many long vowels are in this sentence?
exercises.sub: Count every colon (:)
exercises.correct: Two — é: in Shé:kon and ó: in tó:kens
options: Two — é: in Shé:kon and ó: in tó:kens
One — only in Shé:kon
Three — one in each syllable
None — the colons are punctuation
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon, tó:kens? — two long vowels: é: and ó:. A simple greeting, and already two length marks to honour.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count every colon that follows a vowel.

exercises.prompt: Read: 'Onen' ki' wáhi' — How many glottal stops are in this phrase?
exercises.sub: Count every apostrophe (')
exercises.correct: Two — one at the end of Onen' and one at the end of ki'
options: Two — one at the end of Onen' and one at the end of ki'
One — only at the end of Onen'
Three — one in each word
None — the apostrophes are punctuation
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Onen' ki' wáhi — two glottal stops. Both close the words they end. Say Onen' with a slight throat closure, then ki' with another, then wáhi open and clear.
exercises.wm: Please try again — count every apostrophe.

exercises.prompt: Read this description: 'Ohkwá:ri owá:nen tánon Kayé:ri.' — How many long vowels?
exercises.correct: Four — á: in Ohkwá:ri, á: in owá:nen, á: in tánon, é: in Kayé:ri
options: Four — á: in Ohkwá:ri, á: in owá:nen, á: in tánon, é: in Kayé:ri
Two
Three
Five
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Four long vowels in one sentence — the bear is big and dark. Kanien'kéha is rich with vowel length. When you hold every long vowel, the language sounds like itself.
exercises.wm: Please try again — go word by word and count each colon.

exercises.prompt: Complete the name of the Thanksgiving Address — the word before Karihwatéhkwen
exercises.correct: Karihwatéhkwen
exercises.hint: K
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. You have known this name since Unit 5. Now you can read every mark in it correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — the second word of the Thanksgiving Address name.

exercises.prompt: Type this sentence exactly as written: The corn is beautiful and yellow
exercises.sub: Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr
exercises.correct: Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta ohsì:io tánon Otsì:nekwahr — every accent mark and colon in place. The corn is beautiful and yellow, written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — copy the sentence exactly, including every accent mark and colon.

exercises.prompt: Read this full sentence aloud — every mark honoured
exercises.meaning: The Thanksgiving Address is good and great.
exercises.hint: OH-én:-ton (3 syllables) — Ka-ri-hwa-TÉH-kwen (5 syllables) — oh-SÌ:-io (3 syllables) — TÁ-non (2 syllables) — o-WÁ:-nen (3 syllables). Fourteen syllables in one sentence. Say it slowly. Every colon is a held vowel. Every accent is a lift.

exercises.prompt: Reading the words that come before all else
exercises.storyPrompt: Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen — the words that come before all else. This name has lived in the mouths of Kanien'kehá:ka people for generations — spoken at the opening of every gathering, ceremony, and meeting. Now it is written. Now you can read it. The marks on the page — é:, á:, é — try to capture what the voice has always known. They are an attempt to write down something that was made to be spoken. When you read Ohén:ton Karihwatéhkwen correctly, you are reaching through the writing to the speech it came from.

lessons.title: Dictation: Listening and Writing
lessons.sub: Hearing Kanien'kéha and writing what you hear
intro: Dictation is one of the oldest and most effective tools for learning to write a language. You listen, you write what you hear — including every mark.
This lesson gives you the guidance for dictation practice, and presents short word and sentence challenges that ask you to write from memory and from sound.
You do not need to be perfect. You need to try. Every attempt teaches you something about where your ear and your hand agree — and where they do not yet.
lessons.ped: Dictation as integrative practice: Nation (2001) identifies dictation as a high-value integrative activity — it requires phonological processing, orthographic knowledge, and memory simultaneously. The lesson acknowledges that learners may not have audio playback in the app and redirects toward writing from memory of known words. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: When you do dictation in Kanien'kéha, which four things do you need to listen and write for?
exercises.correct: Long vowels (:), glottal stops ('), accent marks (á/à), and nasal vowels (ę/ǫ)
options: Long vowels (:), glottal stops ('), accent marks (á/à), and nasal vowels (ę/ǫ)
Only the consonants
Only the vowels
Vowels and consonants but not marks
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! All four orthographic features must be captured in dictation. Missing the colon, the apostrophe, the accent, or the nasal hook changes the word.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: Hello
exercises.sub: Include every mark
exercises.correct: Shé:kon
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon — the first word of every lesson. Acute accent on é, colon after it. You have seen this hundreds of times. Now you can write it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — include the accent mark and the colon.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: Thank you very much
exercises.sub: Include every mark
exercises.correct: Niawenhkó:wa
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Niawenhkó:wa — four syllables, stress and length both on kó:. Well done.
exercises.wm: Please try again — four syllables: Nia-wen-kó:-wa.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: That is all / I am finished
exercises.sub: Three words — every mark
exercises.correct: Onen' ki' wáhi
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onen' ki' wáhi — two glottal stops (Onen' and ki') and one acute accent (wáhi). You have said this at the end of every unit. Now you can write it.
exercises.wm: Please try again — three words, two apostrophes, one accent mark.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: bear
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri — acute accent on á, colon after it. The Bear Clan written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — don't forget both the accent and the colon on á:.

exercises.prompt: Write from memory: corn
exercises.correct: Onón:ta
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta — acute accent on ó, colon after it. One of the Three Sisters, written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — the stress and length are on the second syllable: nón:.

exercises.prompt: Writing yourself
exercises.storyPrompt: í:'i — I. Can you write it without looking? Acute accent on í, colon after it, apostrophe in the middle, then i. Four marks and two letters — the word for yourself, in the language of your people. If you can write í:'i correctly from memory, you can write anything. Start there. Start with yourself.

lessons.title: Writing Your Own Sentences
lessons.sub: From reading to writing — making sentences in Kanien'kéha
intro: Reading someone else's Kanien'kéha and writing your own are different skills. Reading follows marks that are already there. Writing means making those marks yourself — placing the colon, the apostrophe, the accent, in exactly the right places.
This lesson asks you to write short Kanien'kéha sentences from what you know. Use the sentence patterns from Units 20 and 23: naming, describing, locating, possessing.
Write slowly. Check every word. Kanien'kéha written correctly is a form of respect for the language.
lessons.ped: Writing as productive orthographic control: Nation (2001) identifies writing production as the highest-demand orthographic task. Learners who can write correctly have fully internalised the orthographic system. This lesson provides structured writing prompts with known vocabulary — the challenge is orthographic, not lexical. Physical domain primary.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: That is the turtle
exercises.sub: Use the naming pattern from Unit 20
exercises.correct: A'nó:wara ne ní:'i
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! A'nó:wara ne ní:'i — glottal stop at the start, acute accent and long vowel on nó:, glottal stop in ní:'i. A short sentence with four orthographic features.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Pattern: [noun] ne ní:'i. Don't forget the glottal stop at the start of A'nó:wara.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: The bear is big
exercises.sub: Use the describing pattern from Unit 23
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri owá:nen
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri owá:nen — both words have an á: that needs both accent and length. Noun + describing verb, both written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again. Both words have á: — acute accent followed by colon.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: The corn is good
exercises.correct: Onón:ta ohsì:io
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onón:ta ohsì:io — both words have long vowels: nón: and sì:. The grave accent on sì: means the pitch falls there.
exercises.wm: Please try again — check the vowel length markers in both words.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: The bear is at the river
exercises.sub: Use the location pattern from Unit 20
exercises.correct: Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohkwá:ri tsi Kaniatarà:ke — three words, multiple marks. The grave accent on Kaniatarà: and the colon after it. A complete location sentence, written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — noun + tsi + location. Check the grave accent and colon in Kaniatarà:ke.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: The leaf is green
exercises.sub: Ohná:ken is leaf
exercises.correct: Ohná:ken Óhonte'
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Ohná:ken Óhonte' — long vowel in Ohná:ken, acute accent and glottal stop at the end of Óhonte'. Green — plant-coloured — written correctly.
exercises.wm: Please try again — Ohná:ken has ná: (accent + length). Óhonte' ends with a glottal stop.

exercises.prompt: Write then say: The wolf is calm
exercises.sub: Write it first, then read what you wrote aloud
exercises.hint: ok-WÁ-ho (3 syllables) — SKÉN:-nen (2 syllables). Write both words from memory, then check your marks: á in Okwáho, é: in skén:nen. Then say what you wrote.

exercises.prompt: Writing the language into the world
exercises.storyPrompt: Okwáho skén:nen — the wolf is calm. You wrote that in Kanien'kéha. Think about what it means to write in a language that was, for generations, not allowed to be written in schools — not allowed to be spoken in schools. Every sentence you write in Kanien'kéha is a small act of presence. The language is here. You are putting it on the page. The wolf is calm.

lessons.title: Dialect Spelling: Kahnawà:ke, Akwesasne, and Six Nations
lessons.sub: Different communities write some things differently — and all are right
intro: You have been learning the spelling conventions of Kanehsatà:ke and Kahnawà:ke throughout this app. But Akwesasne and Six Nations / Tyendinaga communities sometimes spell things differently.
The biggest differences you have already seen: in Akwesasne, words with r are often spelled with l (raónha / laónha). In Ontario dialects, some words with i are spelled with y (í:se' / ý:se').
These are not mistakes. They are different communities writing the sounds they actually say. When you encounter a different spelling, your job is to recognise it — not correct it.
lessons.ped: Dialect variation as orthographic asset: Flores & Rosa (2015): the ideology that one spelling is correct and others are errors is a form of language standardisation that has historically disadvantaged minority communities. This lesson names dialect spelling variation plainly and teaches recognition without judgment. Nation (2001): encountering variation in known words deepens orthographic awareness.

exercises.prompt: In Akwesasne Kanien'kéha, raónha (he) is often spelled laónha. Why?
exercises.correct: Because in Akwesasne speech, the r sound is pronounced as l — the spelling reflects how the community actually says it
options: Because in Akwesasne speech, the r sound is pronounced as l — the spelling reflects how the community actually says it
Because Akwesasne has a different alphabet
It is a spelling error
The two words mean different things
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Akwesasne speakers say l where Kanehsatà:ke speakers say r. The spelling follows the speech. laónha is fully correct — it is the right spelling for the Akwesasne pronunciation.

exercises.prompt: Match the Kanehsatà:ke spelling to the Akwesasne spelling
exercises.sub: r becomes l in Akwesasne
exercises.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! The r → l alternation is consistent across all words with an r-initial prefix in Akwesasne. Once you know the pattern, you can read Akwesasne spelling even if you learned the Kanehsatà:ke form.
exercises.wm: Please try again — r in Kanehsatà:ke becomes l in Akwesasne.

exercises.prompt: In Six Nations and Tyendinaga (Ontario) dialects, í:se' (you) is sometimes written ý:se'. What sound does y represent in this context?
exercises.correct: A palatal glide — the y-sound at the start, where Kanehsatà:ke uses an i-sound
options: A palatal glide — the y-sound at the start, where Kanehsatà:ke uses an i-sound
A different vowel entirely
The same sound as i — just a different letter
A consonant cluster
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! In Ontario dialects, some positions where Kanehsatà:ke uses i (as a vowel-like sound) use y instead (as a glide). ý:se' and í:se' are the same word, said slightly differently in two communities, spelled to reflect those differences.

exercises.prompt: You are reading a text in Six Nations Kanien'kéha and see ý:'i instead of í:'i. What do you do?
exercises.correct: Recognise it as the Ontario spelling of the same word — 'I / me'
options: Recognise it as the Ontario spelling of the same word — 'I / me'
Conclude the text has an error
Look it up as if it is a different word
Skip it — it cannot be the same word
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Dialect reading requires this kind of recognition: same word, different community spelling. ý:'i and í:'i are both correct. Both mean I / me. A fluent reader of Kanien'kéha can move between community spellings.

exercises.prompt: Why does this app accept multiple spellings as correct answers in typing exercises?
exercises.correct: Because all community forms of the language are valid — no learner should be marked wrong for using their own community's spelling
options: Because all community forms of the language are valid — no learner should be marked wrong for using their own community's spelling
Because spelling does not matter in Kanien'kéha
To make the exercises easier
Because there is no agreed spelling for anything
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The app accepts dialect variants precisely because no community's spelling is more correct than another's. If you learned Akwesasne Kanien'kéha, you should not be penalised for writing laónha. If you learned Six Nations, ý:se' should be accepted. The language belongs to all its communities.

exercises.prompt: One language, many spellings
exercises.storyPrompt: í:se' — ý:se'. The same word, two communities, two ways of writing it. Both correct. The Kanien'kehá:ka people span what is now the Canada-US border — Akwesasne, Kanehsatà:ke, Kahnawà:ke, Six Nations, Tyendinaga, Wahta. Each community has its own history, its own dialect, its own way of saying some things. The language holds all of them. When you see a spelling you do not recognise, lean toward it with curiosity rather than correction. It is probably someone else's home.

lessons.title: Writing Has Power
lessons.sub: What it means to write Kanien'kéha today
intro: This is the final lesson of Unit 30. It does not introduce new orthographic rules. It asks you to sit with what writing this language means.

Kanien'kéha has been spoken for thousands of years. It was suppressed, forbidden in schools, almost silenced. It is now being written on signs, in apps, in textbooks, on social media, in government documents.
Every correctly written Kanien'kéha sentence is part of that return.
lessons.ped: Writing as revitalization: The TRC Calls to Action, the Indigenous Languages Act (2019), and UNDRIP Article 13 all affirm the right to transmit Indigenous languages. This lesson connects writing as a skill to writing as a political and cultural act — without jargon, in plain language. LaFever Spiritual domain primary. All four domains present.

exercises.prompt: Onkwehón:we means 'Indigenous person / original person'. The word is built from roots meaning the original people of this land. What does this tell you about the relationship between the language and the land?
exercises.correct: The language names people by their relationship to the land — the original people are named as such in the language itself
options: The language names people by their relationship to the land — the original people are named as such in the language itself
It is a historical term no longer in use
It refers only to people from Kanehsatà:ke
It is a borrowed word from French
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Onkwehón:we — the original people. The name is built into the language. The language remembers who was here first and names them accordingly. When you write Onkwehón:we correctly, you are writing that memory.

exercises.prompt: Rotinonhsyón:ni means 'People of the Longhouse' — the name the Haudenosaunee use for themselves. Why does it matter to write this name in Kanien'kéha rather than only in English translation?
exercises.correct: Because the Kanien'kéha name carries meaning and identity that the English translation cannot fully hold
options: Because the Kanien'kéha name carries meaning and identity that the English translation cannot fully hold
It does not matter — both names mean the same thing
English translation is more accurate
The Kanien'kéha name is only used in ceremonies
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Rotinonhsyón:ni — People of the Longhouse. This is how the Haudenosaunee name themselves, in their own language. Writing it in Kanien'kéha rather than only in English is a form of using the name that belongs to them. English can translate it. But it cannot be it.

exercises.prompt: Write in Kanien'kéha: I am learning Kanien'kéha
exercises.sub: This sentence is in your glossary
exercises.correct: Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! Kateweyénhstha Kanyen'kéha — I am learning Kanien'kéha. Written by you, about you, in the language you are learning. That is something.
exercises.wm: Please try again — check the accent mark on yén and the glottal stop in Kanyen'.

exercises.prompt: Say this aloud — then write it from memory
exercises.hint: Ro-ti-non-hsyÓN:-ni — five syllables. Stress and length on yÓN:. This is a name. Names deserve to be said correctly.

exercises.prompt: Onen' ki' wáhi
exercises.meaning: That is all / I am finished
exercises.storyPrompt: Onen' ki' wáhi. You have now worked through Unit 30. You can read Kanien'kéha. You can write it. You know the marks — the colon, the apostrophe, the accent, the nasal vowel — and you know what each one means. You can read a sentence in this language and understand it. You can write a sentence and have it be correct. This is not small. The Canadian government passed the Indigenous Languages Act in 2019, affirming the right to reclaim, revitalize, and transmit Indigenous languages. You are doing that right now. In this lesson. In this language. Onen' ki' wáhi.

lessons.title: Review — Reading and Writing
lessons.sub: Bringing the whole unit together
intro: This review brings together the key vocabulary and ideas from this unit.
Use it to consolidate what you know before moving on.
lessons.ped: Unit consolidation review: Nation (2001): spaced retrieval review across all lesson content produces deeper retention than reviewing categories separately.

exercises.prompt: Short vowels and long vowels are marked differently in Kanien'kéha. A long vowel is written with a colon (:) after the vowel letter. What does the colon tell you?
exercises.correct: Hold this vowel longer — length changes meaning in Kanien'kéha
options: Hold this vowel longer — length changes meaning
It marks a question
It marks a glottal stop
It is decorative
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! The colon marks a long vowel. Ó:ia'k (ten) vs Óia'k — the length matters. A short vowel and a long vowel can change the meaning of a word entirely.

exercises.prompt: The glottal stop is written with an apostrophe ('). You have seen it in áhta' (shoe), Onen' (now), and many other words. What does it sound like?
exercises.correct: A brief catch in the throat between sounds — the sound between the syllables of 'uh-oh' in English
options: A brief catch in the throat — like the middle of 'uh-oh'
A long pause
The letter h
It is silent
exercises.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! The glottal stop — ' — is a consonant made by briefly closing the throat. You have been producing it since Lesson 1 without knowing its name. Now you know how to read it.

exercises.prompt: In Akwesasne spelling, the letter r is replaced with ___
exercises.correct: l
exercises.hint: l
exercises.cm: Tho niioht! In Akwesasne, r becomes l. Kanien'kéha becomes Kanien'kéha with l-sounds. Both spellings are correct — they represent the same word in different community orthographies.
exercises.wm: Please try again — which letter replaces r in Akwesasne?

exercises.prompt: Writing has power
exercises.storyPrompt: Kanien'kéha. Kanyen'kéha. The same language, different community spellings. Writing systems are not neutral — they encode choices about what sounds matter, whose pronunciation is standard, which community's voice shapes the page. Kanien'kéha has been written by missionaries, by linguists, and now by community members themselves. When communities control their own orthography, they control how their language is seen on the page. That is power. Onen' ki' wáhi.

title: Unit TEST — Dialogue Exercises
desc: Test unit for verifying dialogue exercise functionality. Not part of the curriculum sequence.

lessons.title: Dialogue Test 1 — Community Gathering
lessons.sub: Testing: akaónha/aónha distinction in a live exchange
intro: This is a test lesson. It contains only the first dialogue exercise from Unit 1, Lesson 7.
Use it to verify that the dialogue engine loads, turns advance correctly, the Submit button responds, and the closing reflection appears at the end.
lessons.ped: Test lesson: Isolated dialogue exercise for QA purposes. Remove this unit before deployment.

exercises.prompt: A community gathering
exercises.sub: Use what you know
exercises.scene: You are at a community gathering with your cousin. An elder man — Rokstén:ha — approaches and greets you both. He introduces the woman standing beside him, then turns and asks you a question.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Rokstén:ha
turns.gloss: Greetings.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Greet him back.
options: Shé:kon.
Niawenhkó:wa.
Tho niioht.
Onen'.
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Shé:kon is the standard greeting — you return it in kind.
turns.wm: When someone greets you with Shé:kon, you return the same greeting.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Rokstén:ha
turns.gloss: Greetings. She is known to us — she is an elder woman.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He is speaking about the elder woman beside him. How do you refer to her to your cousin?
options: Akaónha kanónhsa tsi ionkwarihó:ten.
Aónha kanónhsa tsi ionkwarihó:ten.
Raónha kanónhsa tsi ionkwarihó:ten.
Í:se' kanónhsa tsi ionkwarihó:ten.
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! akaónha — the respectful she. An Akokstén:ha requires akaónha. aónha would be grammatically wrong here.
turns.wm: She is an elder woman — which pronoun encodes respect for a woman of her standing?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Rokstén:ha
turns.gloss: Where are you going?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Tell him where you are going — to your grandmother's house.
options: Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke.
Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' non nontà:ke.
Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' wà:ke.
Ka' nontáhse'?
turns.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke — I am going to my grandmother's house. wà:ke marks going; nontà:ke marks coming from.
turns.wm: You are going — which verb is for going, not coming from? And whose house are you going to?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Rokstén:ha
turns.line: Tho niioht. Aksótha — akaónha ká:tiens kanónhsa.
turns.gloss: I see. Your grandmother — she is sitting at home.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He has told you your grandmother is home. Take your leave.
options: Niawenhkó:wa. Onen'.
Shé:kon. Onen'.
Tho niioht. Ka' wáhse'?
Én. í:'i.
turns.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Thank you — and then Onen', meaning 'now' or 'that's it for now.' A natural way to close a conversation.
turns.wm: You want to thank him and say goodbye. Niawenhkó:wa is thank you; Onen' closes the exchange.

exercises.closing: You moved through a real community exchange — greeting, recognising an elder, and saying where you are going. The pronoun akaónha is not optional here. The language requires you to see who she is before you can speak about her.
exercises.dialect_note: In Akwesasne, raónha becomes laónha and Rokstén:ha becomes Lokstén:ha. All community forms are accepted.

lessons.title: Dialogue Test 2 — Grandmother's Door
lessons.sub: Testing: singular/dual/plural + type input
intro: This is a test lesson. It contains the Unit 1 Review dialogue, which includes a type-mode learner turn.
Verify that the type input renders, accepts answer variants, and advances correctly.
lessons.ped: Test lesson: Dialogue with mc and type turns. QA purposes only.

exercises.prompt: At your grandmother's door
exercises.sub: Three conversations at once
exercises.scene: You arrive at your grandmother's house. Two women are sitting on the step. Your grandmother calls from inside. Three people want to speak with you.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Aksótha (from inside)
turns.gloss: Greetings! Is that you?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Confirm it's you — just you, alone.
options: Ó:nen, í:'i.
Ó:nen, teiakeniiáhshe.
Ó:nen, iakwakwé:kon.
Ó:nen, raónha.
turns.cm: Tho niioht! í:'i — I, me. You are alone at the door. In Ontario dialects this appears as ý:'i — both accepted.
turns.wm: You are alone. Which pronoun means 'I' — just you, singular?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Younger woman on the step
turns.line: Shé:kon. Teiakeniiáhshe wà:ke — í:se' wáhse' nahe'?
turns.gloss: Greetings. You and I are going — are you coming along?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: She wants you both to go somewhere together. Agree — say 'you and I, yes.'
options: Én, teiakeniiáhshe.
Én, iakwakwé:kon.
Én, í:'i.
Én, raónha.
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! teiakeniiáhshe — you and I, the inclusive dual. She invited you in, and you accepted with the same pronoun.
turns.wm: She used teiakeniiáhshe — you and I, two people together. Which pronoun matches that?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Elder woman on the step
turns.gloss: Where are you going?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Tell her where you are going.
turns.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! wà:ke — I am going. Short, complete, correct.
turns.wm: The verb 'I am going' — it starts with w. Try: wà:ke

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Elder woman on the step
turns.gloss: I see. Thank you — take care.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Thank her in return.
options: Niawenhkó:wa, akaónha.
Niawenhkó:wa, aónha.
Ka' wáhse', akaónha?
Shé:kon, akaónha.
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! Niawenhkó:wa — thank you. And akaónha, the respectful address, because she is an elder woman. The grammar of respect holds even in a single closing line.
turns.wm: Thank her — and remember which pronoun an elder woman requires.

exercises.closing: Singular, dual, and plural all appeared in one scene. The pronoun system is not a list to memorise — it is a map of who is here, and who belongs to whom.
exercises.dialect_note: In Akwesasne: í:'i stays the same. raónha becomes laónha. All are accepted.

lessons.title: Dialogue Test 3 — Walking through the Community
lessons.sub: Testing: multi-turn dialogue with two type exercises
intro: This is a test lesson. It contains the Unit 2 direction-verb dialogue, the most complex of the three.
Verify that all six turns (three other, two mc, two type) advance in correct order and the closing dialect note renders.
lessons.ped: Test lesson: Longest dialogue. QA purposes only.

exercises.prompt: Walking through the community
exercises.sub: A morning exchange
exercises.scene: You are walking through the community on a quiet morning. A neighbour stops to talk. The conversation moves through where you are going, where they came from, and where someone else went yesterday.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Neighbour
turns.gloss: Greetings. Where are you going?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: You are going to your grandmother's house. Tell them.
options: Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke.
Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' wà:ke.
Ake'nisténha tsi tiakonónhsote' wà:ke.
Aksótha tsi tiakonónhsote' non nontà:ke.
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Aksótha = my grandmother; wà:ke = I am going. nontà:ke would mean coming from, not going to.
turns.wm: You are going — not coming from. And it is your grandmother's house, not your grandfather's or mother's.

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Neighbour
turns.line: Tho niioht. Í:'i, Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' non nontà:ke.
turns.gloss: I see. I — I came from my grandfather's house.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: They came from their grandfather's house. Say the Kanien'kéha word for 'my grandfather.'
options: Raksótha
Aksótha
Rake'sótha
Rake'níha
turns.cm: Niawenhkó:wa! Raksótha — my grandfather, used in a sentence. Compare Aksótha (my grandmother) — same -sótha root, ra- marks male.
turns.wm: The ra- prefix marks male elder kin. Aksótha is grandmother. Which form has ra-?

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Neighbour
turns.gloss: Where did your father go?

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Type the question they just asked: 'Where did (someone) go?'
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Ka' iéhse'skwe' — where did (someone) go? The -skwe' suffix marks past tense.
turns.wm: The past-tense direction question — starts with Ka' and ends with -skwe'. Try: Ka' iéhse'skwe'

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Your father went to your grandfather's house. Type: 'I went (there).'
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! iè:ke'skwe' — I went. Same -skwe' suffix as the question. All three direction pairs complete: wà:ke / nontà:ke / iè:ke'skwe'.
turns.wm: The past-tense answer — shares the -skwe' suffix with the question. Try: iè:ke'skwe'

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Neighbour
turns.line: Tho niioht. Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' wà:ke ó:nen.
turns.gloss: I see. I'm going to my grandfather's house now.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He is leaving. Say goodbye — type the Kanien'kéha word for 'now / that's all.'
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Onen' — now, that's all, goodbye for now. A simple and complete closing.
turns.wm: The word that closes a Kanien'kéha conversation — short, two syllables. Try: Onen'

exercises.closing: Ka' wáhse' — Ka' nontáhse' — Ka' iéhse'skwe'. Three questions, three tenses, built into the verb itself. Where you go and who you go to are not separate in Kanien'kéha. The language holds them together.
exercises.dialect_note: wà:ke, nontà:ke, and iè:ke'skwe' are stable across community dialects — no r→l alternation in these forms.

lessons.title: Dialogue Test — All Three in Sequence
lessons.sub: Testing: all three dialogues as a single lesson
intro: This lesson runs all three dialogue exercises back to back.
Use it to verify that the engine resets cleanly between dialogues and the lesson completion screen scores correctly.
lessons.ped: Test lesson: Full sequence. QA purposes only.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: Your father went to your grandfather's house. Type: 'I went (there).'
turns.cm: Io'nikonhraién:ta'ne! iè:ke'skwe' — I went. Same -skwe' suffix as the question. All three direction pairs complete: wà:ke / nontà:ke / iè:ke'skwe'.
turns.wm: The past-tense answer — shares the -skwe' suffix with the question. Try: iè:ke'skwe'

turns.speaker: other
turns.name: Neighbour
turns.line: Tho niioht. Raksótha tsi thonónhsote' wà:ke ó:nen.
turns.gloss: I see. I'm going to my grandfather's house now.

turns.speaker: learner
turns.instruction: He is leaving. Say goodbye — type the Kanien'kéha word for 'now / that's all.'
turns.cm: Tho niioht! Onen' — now, that's all, goodbye for now. A simple and complete closing.
turns.wm: The word that closes a Kanien'kéha conversation — short, two syllables. Try: Onen'

exercises.closing: Ka' wáhse' — Ka' nontáhse' — Ka' iéhse'skwe'. Three questions, three tenses, built into the verb itself. Where you go and who you go to are not separate in Kanien'kéha. The language holds them together.
exercises.dialect_note: wà:ke, nontà:ke, and iè:ke'skwe' are stable across community dialects — no r→l alternation in these forms.
